### [Probing Tensor Singularities and Their Euler-Class Descendants via Non-Abelian Quantum Geometry Measurement](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17977v1)

**Authors:** Zhe Wang, Yan-Qing Zhu, Xinsheng Tan, Giandomenico Palumbo, Lichang Ji, Wei Xin, Shi-Liang Zhu, Yang Yu  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17977v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · 🔥 `analog quantum simulation` **4/5** · `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

**Summary.** This paper reports the experimental observation of 4D tensor singularities and their 3D descendants using a superconducting qubit array. By implementing a specialized hybrid measurement protocol, the authors successfully mapped non-Abelian quantum geometry and demonstrated how high-dimensional topological objects dictate the physics of lower-dimensional systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for researchers in many-body dynamics and quantum simulation as it demonstrates a robust framework for exploring high-order gauge theories and non-Abelian topology using programmable superconducting circuits.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to probe and characterize high-dimensional 4D tensor singularities and their 3D Euler-class descendants using non-Abelian quantum geometry measurements.

**Main result.** The researchers experimentally observed a 4D point-like tensor monopole evolving into a nodal ring with a quantized invariant of 2, and demonstrated that 4D singularities reduce to 3D Euler dipoles.

**Method.** A hybrid analog-digital protocol was used, employing a modified transitionless quantum driving (mTQDA) technique to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio during adiabatic evolution.

**Model / system.** The platform is a superconducting qubit array consisting of a 2x3 lattice of six transmon qubits with tunable couplers, simulating a 4D minimal Hamiltonian with six bands.

**Key observables.** Non-Abelian quantum metric, Tensor Berry curvature, 4D Tensor Monopole charge (Q_T), First Euler number (chi_1), and the nodal-ring invariant (nu_T).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Energy offset (Lambda), excitation densities (n_ex), and hyperspherical coordinates (Omega, gamma, theta, phi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is protected by chiral and spacetime-inversion symmetries; the analysis assumes the ability to decompose adiabatic evolution into discrete circuit gates.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 introduces the 4D model and symmetry protection; Figure 2 compares measured and simulated non-Abelian quantum metrics and shows the topological phase transition; Figure 3 demonstrates the 3D Euler dipole and Berry curvature distribution.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the 4D tensor singularity concept, describes the superconducting circuit implementation and the hybrid measurement protocol, presents the experimental reconstruction of the 4D monopole and its evolution into a nodal ring, and concludes with the dimensional reduction to 3D Euler dipoles.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report the theoretical prediction and experimental observation of a new class of four-dimensional (4D) tensor singularities and their three-dimensional (3D) Euler-class descendants, protected by chiral and spacetime inversion symmetries on a superconducting circuit platform. The 4D point-like singularity/monopole, characterized by the Dixmier-Douady class of a real bundle gerbe associated with tensor gauge fields, is observed to evolve into a nodal ring carrying an additional first Euler class charge under symmetry-preserving perturbations. Dimensional reduction reveals 3D Euler and Euler curvature dipoles, exhibiting nontrivial Euler topology and a topological sum rule that ensures zero-energy flat bands inherit nontrivial topology even without interactions. Crucially, these high-dimensional degenerate systems are mapped and reconstructed using a hybrid analog-digital protocol designed for non-Abelian quantum geometry measurement within a superconducting qubit array. Our work not only expands the family of topological monopoles but also establishes a robust experimental framework for exploring high-order gauge theory and real-bundle topology across diverse quantum platforms.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper provides an exact solution for the steady state of a boundary-driven XYZ quantum circuit. It identifies a new class of robust, chiral, and separable steady states called elliptic spin helices. These states are particularly interesting because they are computationally tractable and potentially easy to implement in experimental settings.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides an exact analytical solution for a non-equilibrium system without U(1) symmetry, identifying robust, experimentally accessible chiral states that could be useful for studying many-body dynamics and open quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Finding the exact many-body density operator for the nonequilibrium steady state (NESS) of a boundary-driven XYZ quantum circuit, generalizing previous XXZ solutions to the fully anisotropic case.

**Main result.** The authors derive an exact solution using a spatially inhomogeneous matrix product ansatz and discover a family of robust, separable, chiral steady states known as 'elliptic brickwork spin helices'.

**Method.** The study employs a two-replica Matrix Product Ansatz (MPA) with an infinite bond dimension, utilizing the algebraic structure of inhomogeneous Lax operators and Jacobi theta functions.

**Model / system.** A 1D brickwork quantum circuit of N+2 qubits with XYZ-type unitary gates, where boundary qubits are subject to reset quantum channels that project them toward arbitrary pure target states.

**Key observables.** Local magnetization profiles (x, y, z components), one-point correlations at the boundary, and helix indicators (f1, f2) measuring purity and distance to the helix state.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Anisotropy parameter (eta), Trotter time step (u), boundary reset parameters (alpha_L, alpha_R), and the modulus of the elliptic theta function (tau).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation assumes the validity of the proposed matrix product ansatz and relies on the algebraic properties of the Yang-Baxter RLL-type relations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the circuit architecture; Figure 5 and 6 show helix indicators (f1, f2) as functions of anisotropy, identifying the presence of pure helices, descendants with kinks, and different chiralities.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the XYZ circuit model, presents the Matrix Product Ansatz solution, describes the discovery of elliptic spin helices, and provides rigorous mathematical proofs in the appendices.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We obtain the exact many-body density operator of a boundary-driven XXZ quantum circuit via a spatially inhomogeneous matrix product Ansatz. The Ansatz has formally infinite bond-dimension and generalizes authors' previous construction \cite{2025XXZcircuit} for the XXZ interactions. The boundary qubits are coupled to reset quantum channels that project them toward arbitrary pure target states. We find and describe a family of relatively robust separable chiral nonequilibrium steady states (NESS), which are elliptic analogs of spin helices for the circuit, and which   are particularly attractive from an experimental perspective.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Experimental observation of entropic-singularity-induced nonadditive quantum communication in a qutrit platypus channel](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17418v1)

**Authors:** Yu Guo, Bo-Xuan Wang, Xiao-Min Hu, Yun-Feng Huang, Chuan-Feng Li, Guang-Can Guo, Bi-Heng Liu  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17418v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · 🔥 `quantum optics experiment` **4/5**

<img src="2605.17418_figures/2605.17418_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Experimental setup. (a) Preparation of the 6-dimensional entangled state. A 404 nm pump laser is expanded into a 2×3 parallel beam array, which coherently pumps a BBO crystal to generate entangled photon pairs via SPDC. The two photons are separated by a PBS, after which one photon undergoes a local unitary operation to achieve the target input states for the channels. (b) Realization of the channel M3 ⊗A1/2 using PBSAs, HWPAs, and a PBS. (d) Quantum state tomography of the 6-dimensional quantum state. (e) Encoding strategy of the six-dimensional path modes. PBS, polarizing beam splitter; HWP, half-wave plate; IF, interference filter; RM, reflection mirror; SPD, single-photon...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17418_figures/2605.17418_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3: Experimental observation of coherent information nonadditivity for the joint channel M3 ⊗A1/2. For the input state ρ(r1, r2, r3), we vary one parameter while fixing the other two at their respective optimal values and experimentally measure the corresponding value of ∆(r1, r2, r3) (blue bars). The error bars are obtained via Monte Carlo simulations.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper reports the first experimental observation of nonadditive quantum communication induced by entropic singularities. By using a photonic setup to combine a qutrit platypus channel and an amplitude damping channel, the authors showed that the joint capacity is greater than the sum of individual capacities. This demonstrates that specific types of noise can be exploited to enhance information transmission.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides experimental evidence for how singular entropy landscapes in noisy channels can be leveraged to enhance quantum communication rates beyond additive limits.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the experimental observation of nonadditivity in quantum channel capacity, specifically whether entropic singularities can drive superadditive quantum communication.

**Main result.** The researchers experimentally demonstrated a violation of additivity by more than 20 standard deviations, showing that the joint coherent information of a qutrit platypus channel and a qubit amplitude damping channel exceeds the sum of their individual capacities.

**Method.** The study uses a photonic platform to implement the channels, employing quantum state and process tomography on 6-dimensional entangled photon states to measure von Neumann entropies and coherent information.

**Model / system.** A photonic implementation using a 6-dimensional entanglement source generated via Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC) in a BBO crystal, implementing a qutrit platypus channel (M3) and a qubit amplitude damping channel (A1/2).

**Key observables.** Coherent information (Q1), von Neumann entropies of the channel output and environment, and the nonadditivity parameter (Delta).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Damping parameter gamma = 1/2 for the amplitude damping channel; dimension d=3 for the platypus channel; process fidelities exceeding 0.98.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The experiment assumes that the reconstructed density matrices from tomography accurately represent the true quantum states and that the purification method correctly captures the environment's entropy.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the schematic of the 6D entanglement source, the implementation of the combined M3 and A1/2 channels using optical components, the setup for quantum state tomography, and the encoding strategy for spatial modes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nonadditivity, describes the experimental photonic setup and channel implementation, details the tomography and measurement methods, presents the results showing the violation of additivity and the role of entropic singularities, and concludes with the implications of the findings.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The nonadditivity of channel capacity is a defining feature that distinguishes quantum communication from classical communication. In the quantum realm, the channel capacity is determined by coherent information, which is defined through the von Neumann entropies of the output and its environment. Despite its fundamental importance, experimental evidence of such nonadditive quantum communication has been elusive because of the complexity of the required quantum channel. Here, we experimentally observe entropic-singularity-induced coherent-information nonadditivity using the qutrit platypus channel implemented on a photonic platform. By preparing six-dimensional photonic entanglement, we directly measure the coherent information of a platypus channel, a qubit amplitude damping channel, and their joint uses, revealing a clear violation of additivity. Quantum process tomography further reveals the entropic singularity responsible for this effect, demonstrating how singular entropy landscapes in low-dimensional channels can enhance quantum communication beyond additive limits.

</details>

**Summary.** The researchers have developed a new way to manufacture quantum photonic chips by bonding III-V materials directly onto silicon nitride wafers. This platform allows for the integration of lasers, nonlinear light sources, and high-efficiency detectors on a single, large-scale chip. This advancement is a major step toward building complex, large-scale quantum computers and communication networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it provides a scalable hardware architecture for generating and detecting complex quantum states like squeezed light and entangled photons with extremely high fidelity and low loss.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of creating scalable, wafer-scale quantum photonic circuits that simultaneously provide ultra-low-loss passive routing, high optical gain, and strong nonlinearities without the losses or scalability issues of previous integration methods.

**Main result.** The demonstration of a wafer-scale III-V-on-SiN platform that integrates high-efficiency nonlinear elements, lasers, and detectors, achieving 15x brighter entanglement sources and near-unity quantum efficiency in photodetectors.

**Method.** Wafer-scale manufacturing using direct bonding of III-V epitaxial stacks onto foundry-fabricated SiN circuits, utilizing adiabatic inverse tapers for interlayer coupling and self-injection locking for laser noise reduction.

**Model / system.** A heterogeneous photonic platform consisting of ultra-low-loss Silicon Nitride (SiN) waveguides integrated with III-V materials (InGaP and InP) for gain, nonlinearity, and detection.

**Key observables.** SiN waveguide loss, InGaP waveguide loss, interlayer coupling loss, SHG efficiency, entangled-photon brightness, two-photon entanglement fidelity, laser linewidth, and photodetector quantum efficiency.

**Important parameters / regimes.** SiN loss < 1 dB/m, InGaP SHG efficiency > 35,000 %/W, entanglement fidelity of 99.9%, photodetector quantum efficiency > 99%, and laser linewidth in the hertz-level regime.

**Figures summary.** Figure 4b shows the integrated homodyne detector setup and a model for squeezing degradation; Figure 4c displays photodetector bandwidth and noise; Figure 5a illustrates the vision for a fully integrated system.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of scalable quantum photonics, describes the fabrication and architecture of the III-V-on-SiN platform, details the characterization of nonlinear sources and active components, analyzes noise and efficiency in the detection system, and concludes with a roadmap for large-scale integration.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Heterogeneous integration of gain and strongly nonlinear materials with ultra-low-loss silicon nitride (SiN) photonics offers a route to scalable quantum circuits, but concurrent wafer-scale manufacturability, low interlayer loss, and high performance have been challenging to realize. Here we demonstrate a wafer-scale III-V-on-SiN quantum photonic platform that directly integrates III-V layers to foundry-fabricated SiN circuits. The SiN layer provides 200-300 nm thick waveguides with $<1$ dB/m loss and a mature passive photonics ecosystem, while III-V materials provide large $χ^{\left(2\right)}$ and $χ^{\left(3\right)}$ nonlinearities for parametric gain, frequency conversion and quantum light generation. Adiabatic interlayer couplers yield $<25$ mdB loss to InGaP waveguides and resonators with intrinsic quality factors exceeding $10^6$, enabling $15\times$ brighter entanglement sources and efficient nonlinear conversion on SiN. Integrated components--including low-loss beam splitters, waveguide crossers, and tunable interferometers--are complemented by III-V lasers and InP photodetectors with amplifiers achieving up to $99^{+1}_{-12}\%$ quantum efficiency and $3$ GHz bandwidth. This architecture unites ultra-efficient sources, nonlinear elements and detectors on a wafer-scale, low-loss platform, establishing a path toward large-scale, low-noise quantum photonic systems.

</details>

**Authors:** Yihan Wang, Xiaofeng Jin, Yuchuan Ming, Jianxiang Miao, Xiao-Ming Lu, M. W. Mitchell, Jia Kong  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17784v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(b) Squared instantaneous estimation error (∆2ω, red solid line) and the variance estimated by the AEKF (black solid line). (c) Mean squared error (MSE) as a function of the estimation performance used in the EKF and AEKF. We evaluate the average instantaneous variance of simulation data and calculate the...</sub>

Under such low-SNR conditions, the time-domain magnetic signal is obscured by noise, making commonly-employed time-domain fitting challenging. (c)-(d) Random-walk and SM-like magnetic field generated by the signal generator (gray solid line) respectively, together with the corresponding AEKF estimates (red solid line) obtained without prior knowledge of the field-evolution model.</sub>

**Summary.** The paper presents an adaptive filtering framework designed to track dynamic magnetic fields in real-time using spin-noise spectroscopy. By dynamically estimating measurement noise, the method overcomes the limitations of traditional filters and can reconstruct weak signals even when the noise levels change abruptly. This allows for the detection of magnetic field variations that are otherwise hidden below the intrinsic sensitivity of standard spectroscopic methods.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum sensing and open quantum systems as it provides a robust way to perform real-time parameter estimation in the presence of non-stationary noise and unknown signal dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Extracting weak, time-varying magnetic field signals from high-noise environments where the signal-to-noise ratio is extremely low and the noise statistics may be non-stationary.

**Main result.** The development of an Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (AEKF) that successfully tracks dynamic magnetic fields, even with massive measurement noise mismatch and without prior knowledge of the signal waveform, outperforming conventional spin-noise spectroscopy.

**Method.** An Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (AEKF) utilizing the Sage-Husa algorithm and innovation-based adaptive estimation (IAE) with a sliding window to dynamically update measurement noise covariance.

**Model / system.** An 87Rb atomic vapor cell in a magnetic shield using Faraday rotation for detection, where the spin dynamics are governed by the Bloch equations.

**Key observables.** Photocurrent (capturing polarization fluctuations via the Faraday effect) and Larmor precession frequency.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Larmor frequency, relaxation term, spin-noise fluctuation matrix, and measurement noise intensity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The magnetic field is assumed to be a slowly time-varying variable, and measurement noise is assumed to be Gaussian-distributed.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the experimental setup and agreement between experimental and simulated spin noise spectra; Fig 2 demonstrates AEKF robustness to noise mismatch and sudden disturbances; Fig 3 shows tracking of various field models; Fig 4 presents experimental reconstruction of random-walk and earthquake-like magnetic signals.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of weak signal extraction, proposes the AEKF framework, validates the method through numerical simulations of various field dynamics, and concludes with experimental demonstration using Rb vapor cells.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The extraction of weak signals plays a crucial role in quantum precision measurement, where the estimation results are often limited by low signal-to-noise ratios. Here, we demonstrate a parameter-estimation framework based on the adaptive extended Kalman filter for dynamic magnetic-field estimation in quantum systems using spin-noise measurements -- a challenging regime characterized by weak signals. By modeling the magnetic field as an unknown parameter, the proposed approach alleviates model dependence in state estimation. Furthermore, by introducing an adaptive algorithm with real-time noise estimation, our method overcomes the measurement noise intensity constraints of conventional extended Kalman filtering and enhances its practical applicability. Numerical simulations covering three representative magnetic-field dynamics validate the capability of the proposed framework, while experimental results demonstrate successful tracking of a seismo-magnetic-like signal beyond the intrinsic sensitivity of conventional spin-noise spectroscopy.

</details>

**Authors:** Mohamed Hatifi, Brian Stout

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `interference shaping light` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `free-space correlated emission` **2/5**

At the balanced point γm = γe, the dark-channel rate vanishes, γd = 0, while the bright channel remains coupled. **Summary.** This paper proposes a generalized theory of photodetection that includes both electric and magnetic field sensitivities. It demonstrates that by engineering these responses, one can actively control the measurement basis of single photons and create detectors that absorb light without emitting detectable radiation. This offers a new design principle for advanced quantum sensors and metamaterial-based detectors.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a new way to think about detector engineering in quantum optics and circuit QED, showing that the detector architecture itself can be used as a tool to select measurement bases and control light-matter interaction channels.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Standard quantum photodetection models (Glauber theory) rely solely on electric-field response, which fails to account for modern engineered detectors that are sensitive to both electric and magnetic fields.

**Main result.** The authors develop a generalized framework where the detector's electric and magnetic responses can be used to rotate the measurement basis and selectively suppress specific radiative output channels.

**Method.** The study uses an extension of Glauber's theory of optical coherence, POVM formalism, and input-output theory to analyze detection probability and absorption.

**Model / system.** The framework is applied to three scenarios: a far-field two-source dipole geometry, a two-mode single-photon Hilbert space, and a lossy resonant detector model with electric and magnetic coupling.

**Key observables.** Detection probability, first-order visibility, path distinguishability, and absorption fraction.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The complex parameter zeta (ratio of magnetic to electric response), radiative linewidths (gamma_e, gamma_m), and internal decay rate (gamma_i).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model is presented as an effective description of engineered electromagnetic responses.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the two-dipole geometry and detection amplitude cancellation; Figure 2 illustrates detector-defined interference, visibility/path-bias relations, and Bloch sphere representation; Figure 3 shows radiative rates and the relationship between output channels and absorption.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a generalized detection operator, applies it to far-field interference, extends the analysis to single-photon basis rotation, investigates resonant absorption and channel selection, and concludes with a design principle for engineered detectors.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Photodetection converts optical quantum states into measurement events, but the usual electric-field response model becomes restrictive when the detector response is shaped by cavity, superconducting, or metamaterial engineering. We develop a generalized quantum photodetection framework in which electric and magnetic field amplitudes contribute coherently to the detection operator, and analyze it in a far-field two-source geometry, a two-mode single-photon setting, and a lossy resonant detector model. The far-field reference case exhibits complete detector-amplitude cancellation, absent in the electric-only Glauber response, while the single-photon model shows that the detector continuously rotates the effective measurement basis and controls the first-order visibility via an exact closed-form law. In the resonant realization, a monitored radiative output channel can be dark while the detector remains internally excited and absorptive, with unit absorption of the matched input mode at critical coupling. These results identify basis-selective readout and channel-selective absorption as experimentally relevant signatures of engineered electric-magnetic photodetection.

</details>

<sub>Fig. 2. (a) A representative graph marking the MIS (red) and two independent sets (IS) of size |MIS| −1: a connected IS, which is subset of the MIS (green), and a disconnected IS, which is not (yellow). The shaded nodes indicate vertices that are not common to all three sets. (b) Eigenvalue spec- trum of H [Eq. (1)] at tf, sorted by the number of Rydberg excitations. The degenerate IS band in the traditional AQC is shaded in gray, while the non-degenerate IS band in the LD-AQC is shaded in yellow. Connected and disconnected IS states are denoted by green and orange circles respectively.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.16944_figures/2605.16944_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. (a) Benchmarking performance of the traditional and LD-AQC algorithms. Plotting the average success probability PMIS = |⟨ψMIS|ψ(tf)⟩|2 (right axis) and approximation ratio error 1 −R with R ≡⟨ψ(tf)|ˆn|ψ(tf)⟩(left axis) as a function of protocol duration tf, averaged over hundreds of instances of 11-node MIS problems. (b) Success probability metric, SPM ≡−log(1 −PMIS), evaluated against the hardness pa- rameter HP for the graph ensemble from (a). (c) The SPM of LD-AQC vs. the traditional algorithm, for the data from (a,b).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.16944_figures/2605.16944_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. (a) Average infidelity ⟨1 −PMIS⟩versus total protocol time for specific hardest graphs subset (2.2 &lt; HP ≤4.0). The traditional homogeneous AQC (purple) is compared against local detuning utilizing exponential (green) and inverse-power (red) functions. (b) Dependence of the local detuning function fi(a) on the control parameter a. The green curves correspond to the exponential profile fi(a) = exp(−dia), while red represent the power-law profile fi(a) = (1 + di)−a, evaluated for di = 0, . . . , 5. (c) Average infidelity ⟨1 −PMIS⟩for tf · Ωmax = 25π for a graph ensemble, comparing six scaling functions, fi(a).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.16944_figures/2605.16944_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5. Statistical analysis of the data presented in Fig.3. (a) Probability density of the logarithmic error ratio, log10[(1 − Ptrad)/(1 −PLD)]. (b) Probability density of the hardness parameter ratio, HPtrad/HPLD.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper introduces a new adiabatic quantum algorithm for the Maximum Independent Set problem using local, degree-dependent detuning in Rydberg atom arrays. By engineering the Hamiltonian to favor low-degree nodes, the method suppresses trap states and significantly improves the success probability and scaling compared to traditional global control methods.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for researchers in many-body dynamics and quantum control, as it demonstrates how spatially-dependent Hamiltonian engineering can be used to manipulate spectral gaps and suppress transitions into unwanted many-body states.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Improving the efficiency of Adiabatic Quantum Computation (AQC) for solving the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem by addressing the shrinking spectral gap and the presence of trap states.

**Main result.** The proposed Local Degree-dependent AQC (LD-AQC) outperforms traditional global controls, achieving higher success probabilities and a 25% reduction in the fidelity decay rate as problem hardness increases.

**Method.** The authors implement a Hamiltonian engineering approach using degree-dependent local detuning profiles that preferentially excite atoms with low local degrees, combined with a classical preprocessing algorithm (Algorithm 0) to optimize detuning parameters.

**Model / system.** The study uses Rydberg atom arrays (neutral atom arrays) where the MIS problem is mapped to the preparation of a state with maximal Rydberg excitations under a Hamiltonian with Rydberg-Rydberg interactions.

**Key observables.** Success probability (P_MIS), approximation ratio error, average infidelity, and the hardness parameter (HP_trad and HP_LD).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Local degree (d_i), detuning profiles (linear, exponential, power-law), protocol duration (t_f), and the energy difference function (D_k(a)).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The existence of optimal parameters (a*) is assumed for general detuning functions; the Rydberg blockade interaction is assumed to be sufficiently strong (U > delta_0^2 * f_max) to prevent unwanted excitations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the energy spectrum lifting degeneracy in the IS band, benchmarking plots showing superior success probability and lower error for LD-AQC, and scaling analysis of the success probability metric against hardness parameters.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the MIS problem in AQC, describes the LD-AQC Hamiltonian engineering method, presents a classical algorithm for parameter optimization, provides mathematical proofs for energy gap maintenance, and concludes with numerical simulations and scaling analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a hybrid adiabatic algorithm for maximum independent set (MIS) using Rydberg atom arrays. We engineer local controls that preferentially excite atoms with few neighbors, which represent graph nodes with small degrees. Numerical simulations show that the designed pulses accelerate convergence to the MIS state and suppress population in trap states. We obtain higher success probabilities than traditional global controls and a $25\%$ reduction in fidelity decay rate as problem hardness increases.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.18112"></a>
### [Linear-optical test of quantum contextuality with sequential measurements](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18112v1)

**Authors:** Jiaqi Liu, Bita Olamaei, Lijian Zhang, Ali Asadian, Saleh Rahimi-Keshari  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18112v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · 🔥 `quantum optics experiment` **4/5** · `quantum measurements` **3/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18112_figures/2605.18112_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. (a) Setup for the implementation of the KCBS non- contextuality test. Our system consists of a single photon distributed over M modes, which can model any quantum sys- tem with an M-dimensional Hilbert space. Measurement A(j)</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18112_figures/2605.18112_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Experimental setup. A pulsed Ti:Sapphire laser is frequency doubled via parametric up-conversion in the first β- barium borate (BBO) crystal; the resulting second harmonic then pumps a second BBO crystal to generate photon pairs via spontaneous parametric down-conversion. One photon is routed through a Glan polarizer and a polarizing beam splitter (PBS) into the optical path, while the other (heralding photon) is directly coupled into a single-photon counting module (SPCM). The device within the dashed box is used to simulate photon loss in the optical path. The first three beam displacers (BDs) implement the unitary transformation U† j , and the subsequent two beam displacers...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18112_figures/2605.18112_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. The extent of violation of the KCBS inequality is quantified by how much the KCBS value S exceeds the clas- sical bound of 2. The dependence of this quantity on the photon-loss rate is shown, where values above and below 2 correspond to violation and adherence to the inequality, re- spectively. Here, the “Ideal S Value” denotes the theoretical prediction S(ρ1) for the input state ρ1 = ρ00|0⟩⟨0|+ρ11|1⟩1⟨1|, where ρ00 represents the photon-loss rate. The “Experimental S Value” corresponds to the measured data obtained in our experiment. The “Classical Bound” indicates the threshold value in the inequality that separates the classical and quan- tum regimes.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper presents an experimental implementation of a Kochen-Specker contextuality test using sequential measurements in a linear-optical setup. By utilizing 'no-click' detection events, the researchers successfully demonstrated a violation of the KCBS inequality. The setup also functions as a practical tool for verifying the quality of single-photon sources and probing photon-number statistics.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it provides a practical method for certifying non-classicality and single-photon statistics using a robust, loss-tolerant sequential measurement protocol.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Demonstrating Kochen-Specker quantum contextuality in a linear-optical setup using sequential measurements while ensuring that the same observable is implemented identically across different contexts.

**Main result.** The authors experimentally demonstrated a clear violation of the KCBS inequality with a value of 2.2363 ± 0.0041 and showed that the setup can serve as a tool for verifying single-photon sources and photon-number statistics.

**Method.** A sequential measurement scheme is implemented using linear-optical networks and on-off photodetectors, where 'no-click' events are used to preserve the photon for subsequent measurements.

**Model / system.** A single-photon system distributed among M bosonic modes, implemented via a linear-optical setup using a pulsed Ti:SAG laser, BBO crystals for SPDC, and a hybrid encoding of spatial paths and polarization.

**Key observables.** The KCBS parameter S, which quantifies the violation of the Kochen-Specker inequality.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The single-photon population density (rho_11), photon-loss rate (rho_00), and the amplitude of coherent states (alpha).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The fair-sampling assumption and the trusted-device assumption for the experimental components.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the experimental setup and the sequential measurement scheme; Figure 2 shows the physical components like beam displacers and wave plates; Figure 3 depicts the dependence of the KCBS quantity on the photon-loss rate.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of contextuality, describes the proposed sequential measurement scheme and mathematical framework, details the experimental implementation and setup, presents the results of the KCBS violation and robustness to loss, and concludes with applications for photon-number verification.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum contextuality provides a fundamental signature of nonclassical behavior that cannot be explained by noncontextual hidden-variable models. We propose and experimentally implement a linear-optical setup for demonstrating Kochen-Specker contextuality via a violation of the KCBS inequality using single photons. Our scheme employs sequential measurements realized with linear-optical networks and on-off photodetectors. The construction ensures that each co-measured observable is implemented by the same physical operation across different contexts. Our experimental results demonstrate a clear violation of the KCBS inequality and robustness against photon loss. Beyond fundamental investigations, the proposed setup provides a practical tool for probing non-classicality and photon-number statistics of quantum states, which in turn enables the verification of single-photon sources.

</details>

### [Quantum magic of strongly correlated fermions $-$ the Hubbard dimer](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18494v1)

**Authors:** Edoardo Zavatti, Gabriele Bellomia, Massimo Capone

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

The hexagon represents the stabilizer polytope STABN for N = 1, i.e. all possible convex combinations of single qubit stabilizer states. Its vertices correspond to pure stabilizer states. The highlighted points correspond to the two (mixed) stabilizer states σ± such that the decomposition ρ = (1 + s)σ+ −sσ−is optimal. This is analogous to the formulation given in Eq. (1).</sub>

While the system is clearly devoid of local magic, this is correctly captured only by LR, while the 2-SRE is everywhere non-zero. Bottom panel: local non-Gaussianity. Unlike magic and entanglement, non-Gaussianity is zero only at U = 0, highlighting the different nature of these quantities.</sub>

In (a) we display the U vs T phase diagram, showing that non-stabilizerness disappears in the region of large temperature and small Coulomb repulsion. The dashed white line represents the line of critical temperature Tc(U/t) where the magic drops to zero, while the dashed azure line marks the maximum of non-stabilizerness at each temperature. We further display the evolution of LR(ρ) and of the double occupancy ⟨d⟩as functions of temperature for fixed Coulomb repulsion in (b) and (c), while in (d) and (e) as functions of U/t in log-scale.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.18494_figures/2605.18494_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5: Dynamics of non-stabilizerness after a quench at t = 0. Panels (a) and (e) display the evolution of LR and the 2-SRE, (b) and (f) of the inter-site entanglement under SSRs, (c) and (g) of the non-Gaussianity, and finally (d) and (h) of the double occupancy, after a quench from U = 100 to U = 5. When dephasing is absent (Γ = 0), non-stabilizerness oscillates periodically, while in presence of dephasing (Γ &gt; 0) it saturates to a constant value. Panels (i) and (j) show the evolution of LR and of the 2-SRE respectively, for different values of the final value of the quench Uf. While the 2-SRE always saturates to non-zero values, LR can become zero after some time, due to the mixing...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates the 'quantum magic' of the Hubbard dimer, demonstrating that non-stabilizerness is a fundamental resource distinct from entanglement and non-Gaussianity. It shows that this magic peaks during electron localization and can be destroyed by temperature or dephasing. The work also highlights the unreliability of certain entropy-based measures for mixed fermionic states.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a deep look into how quantum complexity resources behave under thermalization and decoherence, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to quantify 'quantum magic' (non-stabilizerness) in strongly correlated fermionic systems and determine if it serves as a distinct quantum resource compared to entanglement and non-Gaussianity.

**Main result.** Non-stabilizerness is identified as a unique resource that peaks during the transition to electron localization and can vanish due to thermal or dynamical processes, providing insights missed by traditional measures.

**Method.** The authors use exact analytical solutions for the Hubbard dimer to evaluate various complexity metrics, including robustness of magic, stabilizer Renyi entropy, and fermionic non-Gaussianity.

**Model / system.** The system is the Hubbard dimer, a two-site Hubbard model at half-filling characterized by a hopping amplitude (t) and local Coulomb repulsion (U).

**Key observables.** Non-stabilizerness (robustness of magic and stabilizer Renyi entropy), fermionic non-Gaussianity, inter-site entanglement under superselection rules, and double occupancy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction strength ratio (U/t), temperature (T), and dephasing rate (Gamma) during quantum quenches.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is limited to the two-site Hubbard model, which is a simplified version of more complex many-body systems.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a geometric interpretation of the robustness of magic; Figure 2 compares non-stabilizerness, entanglement, and non-Gaussianity; Figure 7 illustrates how magic vanishes in certain density matrix mixtures.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the concept of magic, presents exact solutions for the Hubbard dimer, analyzes zero- and finite-temperature properties, explores non-equilibrium quench dynamics with decoherence, and compares magic to other complexity measures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the non-stabilizerness (quantum magic) content of the Hubbard dimer, an analytically solvable, yet completely non-trivial, model of strongly correlated fermions. We can access zero- and finite-temperature properties as well as the time evolution in a quantum quench protocol. We evaluate local and nonlocal non-stabilizerness using both the robustness of magic and the stabilizer Renyi entropy, demonstrating how the latter often fails in detecting the mixed stabilizer states that are typically found in this kind of systems. Finally, we compare the non-stabilizerness with other genuine resources of quantum-state complexity, i.e., the fermionic non-Gaussianity and the superselected two-site entanglement. Our findings corroborate the notion of non-stabilizerness as a fundamentally different quantum resource, able to give profound insights that are missed by more traditional information-theoretic quantities.

</details>

### [Zeno-Assisted Quantum Heat Engines](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18367v1)

**Authors:** Selma Memić, Rafael Wagner, Susana F. Huelga, Martin B. Plenio  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18367v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18367_figures/2605.18367_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Four-stroke quantum heat engine operating an Otto cycle. (Left) A single working system HS interacts alternately with two thermal baths, HBhot and HBcold, at inverse temperatures βh = T −1 h and βc = T −1 c , respectively. The labeled arrows indicate the average heat and work flows according to the sign convention adopted later in the text. (Right) The cycle consists of four strokes: an adiabatic compression, a hot isochoric thermalization, an adiabatic expansion, and a cold isochoric thermalization. During the work strokes the working medium is isolated from the baths and evolves unitarily; during each heat stroke it interacts with only one bath.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18367_figures/2605.18367_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Quantum Zeno drive. An interval 0 ≤t(1) ≤t ≤ t(n) is subdivided into n intervals δt(k) = t(k+1)−t(k) = (t(n)− t(1))/n for k = 1, . . . , n. At each instant t(k) a projective measurement M is applied to the system. During the intervals δt(k) we drive the system according to a time-dependent Hamiltonian H(t) yielding a unitary U(t(k+1), t(k)). These are sometimes referred to as unitary kicks or pulses. We thus alternate between unitary kicks of duration δt(k) and instantaneous projective measurements M. We refer to this as a time-dependent Zeno-drive.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18367_figures/2605.18367_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Four strokes Zeno-assisted quantum heat engine. The engine shown in Fig. 1 is modified by adding an auxiliary single-qubit system HL = C2 to the working medium, referred to as the quantum lubricant. The engine is operated according to a four-stroke Otto cycle in which the unitary strokes are replaced by those generated by a Zeno- drive: strong time-dependent coupling between system and lubricant, together with frequent monitoring of the lubricant’s state. The heat strokes remain unchanged, with only the system HS brought into contact with the baths.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.18367_figures/2605.18367_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Instantaneous coherence in the working system after strong-coupling and Zeno-assisted drives. (a) Coherence quantifier Cℓ1(ρS(t1)|HS(t1)) of the final state ρS(t1) at the end of the compression stroke, relative to the final Hamiltonian HS(t1) = Hhot, as a function of the compression-stroke duration τcomp. The curve is obtained from simulated values sampled every 0.05 in τcomp. The total evolution of the system and the lubricant is generated unitarily by Htot(t) in Eq. (69); the coupling strength is varied by changing Γ from 0 (the non-lubricated case) to 50, approaching the strong-coupling limit. (b) Same coherence quantifier for a finite-time Zeno drive. The curve is obtained from...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.18367_figures/2605.18367_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 6. Efficiency, power, and extracted work gains from lubrication via the Zeno effect without accounting for thermodynamic costs. The non-lubricated case is shown for comparison, illustrating the detrimental effects of coherence when the work strokes are fast. Panels (a)-(c) show the gains obtained from lubrication in the strong-coupling limit. Panels (d)-(f) show the gains obtained when strong coupling is combined with frequent monitoring of the lubricant. In these, a single trajectory with n measurements is shown for each choice of lubrication parameter. The duration of the cold thermalization stroke is τcold = 12, and that of the hot thermalization stroke is τhot = 5. The duration of...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new method for 'lubricating' quantum heat engines using Quantum Zeno Dynamics. By coupling the engine to an auxiliary system and monitoring it frequently, the authors show it is possible to suppress quantum friction and achieve high efficiency even during fast, finite-time operations.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a unified framework connecting quantum control (shortcuts to adiabaticity) with quantum thermodynamics and the Zeno effect, offering a new way to suppress dissipation in driven open systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** How to mitigate 'quantum friction' (non-adiabatic transitions and coherences) in finite-time quantum heat engines to maintain high efficiency and power.

**Main result.** The authors propose a 'Zeno-assisted' lubrication protocol using strong coupling and frequent monitoring to implement a shortcut to adiabaticity, effectively recovering Otto efficiency at finite driving speeds.

**Method.** The study uses Quantum Zeno Dynamics (QZD) and the framework of adiabatic transporters to derive an effective Hamiltonian in the strong-coupling and frequent-monitoring limits.

**Model / system.** A tripartite quantum Otto engine consisting of a single-qubit working medium coupled to two thermal baths and an auxiliary 'lubricant' qubit system.

**Key observables.** Work extraction, efficiency, power, entropy production, and heat exchange.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Cycle duration (tau), coupling strength (Gamma), energy gaps (omega, Omega), and temperature (Th, Tc).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Markovian Lindblad dynamics for thermalization, the strong-coupling limit (Gamma to infinity), and the Zeno limit (frequent monitoring).

**Figures summary.** Figure 14 illustrates the competition between the strong-coupling regime and the Zeno limit, showing how monitoring in the computational basis can suppress the lubrication effect.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of quantum friction, describes the Zeno-assisted protocol, compares it to dephasing-assisted methods, analyzes thermodynamic costs, and provides mathematical proofs in the appendices.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Finite-time quantum heat engines (QHEs) typically extract less work than their quasistatic counterparts because fast driving generates coherences and non-adiabatic transitions during the work strokes, a phenomenon commonly referred to as quantum friction. Quantum lubrication denotes a broad class of strategies that use auxiliary systems or controls to mitigate this loss. In this work, we introduce a lubrication protocol based on the quantum Zeno dynamics (QZD). By coupling the working medium to an auxiliary lubricant system and frequently monitoring the lubricant, we confine the joint evolution to a Zeno subspace and obtain an effective shortcut to adiabaticity during the work strokes of a QHE running an Otto cycle. In the ideal Zeno limit, the protocol reproduces the transitionless dynamics required to preserve populations in the instantaneous energy basis and recover the Otto efficiency at finite stroke duration. We also analyze several implementation-dependent thermodynamic costs, including switching, driving, monitoring, and imperfect thermalization, in order to assess how these costs constrain the practical gains in efficiency and power. Our results identify QZD as a conceptually distinct route to quantum lubrication and highlight quantum heat engines as a useful setting in which to study the interplay between strong coupling, measurement, and quantum thermodynamic control.

</details>

### [Coherent spectroscopy of collective excitations in superfluid helium far from equilibrium](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17835v1)

**Authors:** Gabriel Voith, Alexander A. Milner, Valery Milner  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17835v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `interference shaping light` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5** · `quantum optics experiment` **2/5**

The pulses are linearly polarized at 45◦with respect to one another, combined in a collinear geometry and focused into the bulk liquid helium, kept at the temperature of 1.4 K and saturated vapor pressure (SVP). A Michelson interferometer splits pump pulses into pairs with a variable delay τ and relative amplitude. P: polarizer, M: mirror, A: variable attenuator, BS: beam splitter, DM: dichroic mirror, L:lens, λ/2, λ/4: zero-order half- and quarter-wave plates.</sub>

<sub>Figure 2 shows the time-dependent optical birefrin- gence of superfluid helium induced by a single femtosec- ond pump pulse. The oscillatory signal is dominated by the two-roton response around 350 GHz, consistent with our earlier observations [12]. As reported previ- ously, the instantaneous frequency of this signal is not constant but changes on a picosecond timescale. Our re- cent observation that the signal scales linearly with the pump pulse energy [18], with the initial roton frequency remaining unchanged, suggests that the frequency evolu- tion is not driven by laser-induced temperature changes. Instead, we show that the roton signal can be described very well by a superposition of...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17835_figures/2605.17835_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Laser-induced optical birefringence of superfluid he- lium at T = 1.4 K and saturated vapor pressure (solid red line). The insets demonstrate the quality of a numerical fit (dashed black) at both early and late times (upper and lower inset, respectively) by a three-frequency model consisting of two roton components and one maxon component (see text for details).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17835_figures/2605.17835_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Power spectrum (solid red, log scale) and the spectral phase (dashed blue) of the time-dependent signal shown in Fig. 2. The three pronounced peaks correspond to rotons, maxons, and Pitaevskii plateau, and are labeled with ‘R’, ‘M’, and ‘P’, respectively.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.17835_figures/2605.17835_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. (a) Birefringence signal (red solid line) recorded after the excitation by two pump pulses, separated in time by τ = 1.45 ps (as indicated with two vertical arrows). For reference, the response to the second pulse alone (analogous to that in Fig. 2) is shown by the dashed black curve. The power spectrum of the residual signal is shown in panel (b), with the roton (‘R’), maxon (‘M’), and Pitaevskii plateau (‘P’) peaks labeled accordingly.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper uses ultrafast laser pulses to perform spectroscopy on collective excitations in superfluid helium far from equilibrium. By using a 'double-kick' method to suppress the dominant roton signal, the authors successfully identified and characterized previously inaccessible maxon and Pitaevskii plateau excitations. The study reveals new insights into the binding energy, lifetime, and phase dynamics of these quasiparticles.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work demonstrates advanced coherent control techniques to isolate weak many-body excitations from a dominant background, which is highly relevant for studying non-equilibrium dynamics and quasiparticle interactions in any strongly interacting quantum system.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Exploring the ultrafast, non-equilibrium dynamics of collective excitations in superfluid helium beyond the well-studied roton regime, specifically identifying maxon and Pitaevskii plateau excitations which are obscured by the dominant roton response.

**Main result.** The researchers successfully isolated maxon pairs and Pitaevskii plateau excitations using coherent control, revealing that maxon pairs have a surprisingly large binding energy and an extremely short lifetime compared to rotons.

**Method.** Ultrafast coherent control using sequences of femtosecond infrared pump pulses and a frequency-doubled probe pulse to measure time-resolved optical birefringence via a Michelson interferometer.

**Model / system.** Superfluid Helium-4 at 1.4 K and saturated vapor pressure, studied in a far-from-equilibrium regime using an optical cryostat.

**Key observables.** Time-dependent optical birefringence, power spectrum of the signal, and the spectral phase of the excitations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pulse duration of ~70-100 fs, dynamics on picosecond timescales, frequencies in the GHz range (~350-680 GHz), and maxon decay constant of ~3 ps.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The birefringence signal can be modeled as a superposition of decaying sinusoidal components representing different quasiparticle branches.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the experimental setup; Fig 2 displays time-dependent birefringence and its numerical fit; Fig 3 presents the power spectrum and spectral phase identifying R, M, and P peaks; Fig 4 shows the suppression of rotons via a 'double kick' and the resulting residual spectrum; Fig 5 shows the isolated maxon signal contribution.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of studying non-equilibrium excitations, describes the experimental setup and coherent control method, presents the identification of maxon and plateau features through signal suppression, analyzes the energy and phase properties of these excitations, and concludes with the implications for studying strongly interacting quantum fluids.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ultrafast dynamics of collective excitations in superfluids remains largely unexplored beyond the roton regime, despite its importance for understanding nonequilibrium processes in these systems. Here, we employ ultrafast coherent control with sequences of femtosecond pulses to perform spectroscopy of multiple branches of the Landau excitation spectrum in superfluid helium far from equilibrium. By measuring the time-resolved optical birefringence, we track the nonequilibrium dynamics of maxon pairs and Pitaevskii plateau excitations alongside the previously studied roton pairs, revealing surprisingly strong binding energy of maxon pairs, their extremely short lifetime, and the influence of the quasiparticle effective mass on the phase of the coherent response. These results demonstrate the ability to extract previously inaccessible information about collective excitations in a strongly interacting quantum fluid by probing its nonequilibrium dynamics on femtosecond and picosecond timescales.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates an integrated 4H-SiC microring resonator that overcomes the traditional trade-off between photon generation efficiency and spectral bandwidth. By optimizing the material's nonlinearity and the resonator's geometry, the authors achieved high-fidelity time-bin entanglement suitable for high-speed quantum networks. This provides a scalable pathway for developing efficient, broadband quantum light sources on a chip.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information because it presents a scalable, integrated hardware solution for generating high-rate, broadband entangled photons, which is a critical component for high-speed quantum communication and networking.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The efficiency-bandwidth trade-off in cavity-enhanced quantum light sources, where high quality factors increase generation efficiency but narrow the spectral bandwidth, limiting high-speed quantum communication.

**Main result.** The researchers developed an integrated 4H-SiC microring resonator that achieves high photon-pair generation rates and a 1.0 GHz bandwidth, demonstrating time-bin entanglement with 94.37% fidelity and a Bell inequality violation of over 138 standard deviations.

**Method.** The study uses spontaneous four-wave mixing (SFWM) in a 4H-SiC microring resonator, optimizing material nonlinearity and device geometry to decouple efficiency from bandwidth, and characterizes entanglement via quantum state tomography and unbalanced Mach-Zehnder interferometry.

**Model / system.** An integrated 4H-silicon carbide (4H-SiC) microring resonator (MRR) on a photonic integrated circuit, utilizing a 1555.75 nm pulsed pump to generate time-bin entangled photon pairs via third-order nonlinearity.

**Key observables.** Photon-pair generation rate (PGR), coincidence-to-accidental ratio (CAR), interference visibility, Bell inequality violation (CHSH), and quantum state tomography fidelity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Loaded Q-factors of 1.1-1.9 x 10^5, spectral bandwidth of 1.0-1.7 GHz, photon-pair generation rate of 1.35 x 10^7 s^-1 mW^-2, and a 160 MHz repetition rate.

**Figures summary.** The figures likely illustrate the device geometry, the experimental setup for photon generation and detection, the scaling of photon-pair generation with pump power, and the reconstruction of the density matrix and interference fringes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the efficiency-bandwidth trade-off, describes the 4H-SiC microring resonator platform and optimization strategy, details the experimental setup and photon generation process, presents the characterization of entanglement and generation rates, and concludes with the implications for scalable quantum networks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Integrated time-bin-entangled photon-pair source with cavity-enhanced nonlinear optical processes is essential for quantum information technologies. However, microcavities with a high quality factor inherently introduce a trade-off between generation efficiency and photon bandwidth, which hinders the development of high-speed quantum networks with an integrated source. Here, we address this challenge by optimizing the nonlinearity property of the material and the geometry of the integrated microring resonator with a 4H-silicon carbide platform. Operating at a loaded quality factor of 1.9 $\times$ 10^5 - spectral bandwidth of 1.0 GHz and pumped with 300-ps double pulses separated by 1.25 ns at a repetition rate of 160 MHz, the device achieves a time-bin-entangled photon-pair generation rate of 1.35 $\times$ 10^7 s^-1 mW^-2. A raw visibility of 95.55 $\pm$ 0.18% is measured, showing a violation of Bell's inequality by more than 138 standard deviations, and a fidelity of 94.37 $\pm$ 0.22% is obtained by quantum state tomography. These results provide a scalable pathway to an efficient and broadband time-bin entangled quantum light source, overcoming intrinsic limitations of cavity-based designs and advancing integrated platforms for future quantum communication networks.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a mathematical framework to study the 'collapse' of sequential quantum measurements into simpler, commutative forms. It characterizes the information lost (such as off-diagonal coherence) when a sequence of measurements is reduced to its essential surviving components. The work provides a rigorous classification of measurement sequences that are equivalent under this residual transformation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum measurement and open quantum systems, as it provides a rigorous framework for understanding how information is lost or preserved in sequential, non-commuting measurement protocols and how complex measurement sequences can be reduced to simpler, classical-like structures.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the residual structure of ordered Positive Operator-Valued Measures (POVMs) and characterizes how sequential measurement processes can be simplified via a 'residual collapse' map.

**Main result.** The author establishes an equivalence relation on ordered POVM realizations, showing that a collapse map can reduce complex sequential measurements to a commutative, orthogonal set of effects plus a terminal 'escape' effect, while identifying the specific off-diagonal coupling data that is lost during this process.

**Method.** The study uses operator-theoretic methods, including Naimark dilation, residual recursion, and fixed-point analysis of a residual transform, to track the evolution of measurement effects through sequential testing.

**Model / system.** The mathematical model consists of discrete POVMs acting on a Hilbert space, representing a sequence of quantum measurements where each subsequent test is applied to the residual state left after previous tests have failed.

**Key observables.** The effects (outcomes) of the POVM, the residual operators (remaining state mass), and the escape effect (accumulated terminal mass).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The driving positive contractions (A_n) that determine measurement steps, and the spectral gap condition required for norm convergence.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The sequence of contractions must satisfy R_n -> 0 for normalization; norm convergence in infinite dimensions requires a spectral gap condition; and the escape effect must have no spectral mass at 1 for certain iterative limits.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the residual recursion and transform, moves to analyzing the Naimark dilation and dimension discrepancies in the residual chain, investigates the iterative convergence of the transform to a collapsed POVM, and concludes by characterizing the range and fiber of the collapse map.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ordered realizations of discrete POVMs are studied through a residual transform generated by sequential tests. One application of the transform replaces each coordinate by the effect obtained after all earlier tests have failed, and appends the remaining mass as a terminal outcome. Under natural hypotheses, iterating the transform produces a collapsed POVM whose non-escape coordinates are the parts of the original effects that survive all earlier tests. The resulting collapse map gives an equivalence relation on ordered POVM realizations. Its range and fibers are characterized. The range consists of collapsed POVMs, whose non-escape coordinates are mutually orthogonal and whose support projections strongly sum to the identity. The fiber over a collapsed POVM consists of all ordered realizations with the same residually visible compressions. In particular, different ordered realizations, including ones with different off-diagonal coupling data, can have the same collapsed image. After collapse, the non-escape coordinates are fixed under further residual iteration. The remaining dynamics takes place in the escape effect, which is fragmented by a universal scalar functional calculus.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces new theoretical methods to model the quantum evolution of optical solitons beyond the standard linearized regime. By treating the soliton as an open system, the authors successfully capture complex non-Gaussian dynamics and explain why traditional theories underestimate photon loss in the presence of high-order dispersion.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides powerful computational tools for analyzing non-Gaussian, non-perturbative dynamics in open quantum systems, specifically addressing the breakdown of linearization in mesoscopic bosonic systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Modeling the quantum dynamics of optical chi(3) solitons in non-perturbative, non-Gaussian regimes where traditional linearization-based methods fail.

**Main result.** The authors develop two effective approaches (LSM and ME) that capture quantum-induced phase shifts and photon loss, and identify that dispersive-coupling-induced soliton broadening causes significant underestimation of dissipation in standard theories.

**Method.** Two approaches: a discrete Lanczos Supermode (LSM) expansion and a continuous Markovian Master Equation (ME) derived via a Born-Markov approximation.

**Model / system.** A quantum chi(3) nonlinear waveguide system described by a field Hamiltonian containing quadratic dispersion and Kerr nonlinearity, treated as an open system where the field is partitioned into a soliton component and a residual reservoir.

**Key observables.** Soliton phase shifts, photon loss (dissipation/evaporation), soliton stability, and quantum-induced soliton diffusion.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Photon number (mesoscopic regime), nonlinearity strength, group velocity dispersion (GVD), and third-order dispersion (TOD).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The ME approach assumes a Markovian reservoir, while the LSM approach assumes dynamics can be localized to a few-supermode basis.

**Figures summary.** Schematic of field partitioning; plots of ME dissipator matrix elements; Wigner function showing non-classical negativity; and comparisons of photon loss rates across LSM, ME, and GSSF models.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the open-system framework, details the LSM and ME formalisms, validates them against MPS benchmarks, analyzes stability and phase shifts, and investigates higher-order dispersion effects.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce two approaches to modeling the quantum dynamics of optical $χ^{(3)}$ solitons. Taking an open-system viewpoint, we project the underlying quantum field into system (soliton) and residual reservoir components. The reservoir is treated as either (i) a discrete ``Lanczos supermode'' (LSM) expansion which localizes dynamics to a few-supermode basis, or (ii) a non-local environment which can be traced out by deriving a Markovian master equation (ME). Using these methods, we analyze and identify the quantum structure of both the soliton's stability and its hierarchy of perturbations. Through numerical simulations, we confirm both methods effectively capture quantum-induced soliton phase shifts in a concise few-mode (single-mode for ME) basis, and the LSM approach also captures photon loss which arises from non-Markovian dispersive couplings. As neither method is limited to the linearized regime, our approaches provide powerful computational tools to analyze complex non-Gaussian quantum dynamics of solitons where other commonly-used methods fail, providing insight into such non-perturbative regimes. We also investigate radiation that occurs in the presence of higher-order dispersion with ultrashort pulses, deriving a ME that predicts photon loss consistent with classical theory, but find that both classical and ME theory dramatically underestimate the actual amount of dissipation, which we explain in terms of dispersive coupling-induced soliton broadening.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new regularized variational method for Counterdiabatic driving that prevents mathematical divergences in systems with unbounded bosonic modes. By applying this to the Quantum Rabi Model, the authors demonstrate robust, high-fidelity state preparation across strong and deep-strong coupling regimes. The proposed control terms are shown to be implementable using Floquet engineering.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a scalable solution for quantum control in continuous-variable systems and offers a way to achieve high-fidelity state preparation in strongly interacting light-matter platforms like superconducting circuits and NV centers.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Standard variational approaches for Counterdiabatic (CD) driving fail in the Quantum Rabi Model because the unbounded bosonic Hilbert space causes trace-based cost functions to diverge or become unphysical.

**Main result.** The authors develop a regularized variational framework using physically motivated renormalization schemes that successfully extends CD driving across the entire Rabi parameter landscape, including the deep-strong coupling regime.

**Method.** The method introduces a regularized action functional using weighted traces (coherent states, superradiant states, or filtered traces) and demonstrates implementation via Floquet engineering through parametric modulation.

**Model / system.** The Quantum Rabi Model (QRM), which describes the interaction between a two-level system (qubit) and a single bosonic mode (cavity) across strong, ultrastrong, and deep-strong coupling regimes.

**Key observables.** Final ground-state fidelity, infidelity, cavity occupation, atomic polarization, and Spearman's rank correlation coefficient between action and fidelity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Normalized coupling (eta), frequency ratio (Gamma), and the driving frequency for Floquet engineering.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on coherent unitary dynamics, neglecting dissipative effects and assuming a zero-temperature limit for the bosonic reference state.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the QRM regime map; Figure 4 compares fidelity across different regularization schemes; Figure 5 illustrates the fidelity landscape; Figure 6 compares analytical vs. optimized CD protocols; Figure 7 shows time evolution of cavity and atomic observables under Floquet engineering; Figures 8 and 9 analyze the optimization landscape and the predictive power of the regularized action.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the failure of standard trace-based CD methods in unbounded spaces, introduces a regularized variational framework with multiple renormalization schemes, applies this to the QRM to identify new CD terms, proposes a fidelity-based alternative, and demonstrates experimental implementability via Floquet engineering.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Counter-diabatic (CD) driving provides a powerful route to fast and robust state preparation by suppressing diabatic excitations during finite-time evolution. Yet, deriving analytical CD protocols for complex systems remains challenging, motivating the development of variational approaches. These methods typically rely on minimizing trace-based functionals to construct approximate control Hamiltonians. However, in unbounded systems, such functionals can become ill-defined because of the unbounded bosonic Hilbert space, leading to divergent cost functions and unphysical variational coefficients. Here, we introduce a variational optimization framework equipped with physically motivated renormalization schemes that regularize the trace-based metric by restricting it to relevant displaced and low-energy subspaces. As a paradigmatic example, we apply our method to the quantum Rabi model beyond the dispersive approximation and identify two distinct CD contributions that couple the atomic degree of freedom to the position and momentum quadratures of the field. These terms suppress diabatic excitations across coupling regimes ranging from strong to deep-strong light--matter interaction. We further formulate a fidelity-based quantum optimal-control strategy that bypasses the limitations of trace-based variational methods. Finally, we show that the resulting CD terms can be implemented via Floquet engineering through parametric modulation of the native Hamiltonian. Our results demonstrate that CD driving can be consistently extended to continuous-variable systems with unbounded Hilbert spaces, providing a controlled and scalable framework for quantum control in strongly interacting light-matter platforms.

</details>
