<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.16614_figures/2605.16614_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Standard MSS on ibm marrakesh (4096 shots). (a) Measured C(ρC) (scatter, 1σ bootstrap error bars) against the theory curve C(φ) = (| sin φ| + | cos φ| −1)/2 (line). All four φ values are faithful to within 0.034. (b) C(ρB) &lt; 10−6 in every case — noise- robust security from the I/2 invariance argument.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents 'Magic Secret Sharing,' a method to distribute the non-Clifford resources required for universal quantum computing such that only a specific coalition can access them. By using GHZ states and phase gates, the authors show that computational power can be shared with a threshold structure, even providing a device-independent way to certify the delivery of magic. The protocol was successfully demonstrated on a large-scale IBM quantum processor.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper introduces a new quantum cryptographic primitive, Magic Secret Sharing (MSS), which aims to distribute the computational capability (non-Cliffordness) of a quantum state using a threshold structure.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate a (n-1, n) threshold protocol where an authorized coalition can reconstruct magic content, while unauthorized parties hold stabilizer states. This was experimentally validated on the 156-qubit IBM Heron processor with high fidelity and near-exact magic recovery.

**Method.** The protocol uses GHZ states and local phase gates, employing the resource theory of magic (Wigner distance) to quantify non-stabilizerness. Certification is achieved via one-sided device-independent (1SDI) steering inequalities.

**Model / system.** The protocol is implemented on the 'ibm_marrakesh' (156-qubit IBM Heron r2) quantum hardware using a 3-party (2,3) GHZ state configuration.

**Key observables.** Magic content (Wigner distance C), state fidelity, security metric (C of the unauthorized party), and steering inequality violations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The phase parameter phi (controlling magic injection), the (n-1, n) threshold structure, and the 15-to-1 magic state distillation threshold.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The security relies on the invariance of the maximally mixed state under local unitaries; the 1SDI setting assumes the recipient does not need to trust the coalition's devices.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2(a) is mentioned in the notes, and Table 1 summarizes experimental results including theoretical vs. measured magic content, error bars, and security metrics.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the MSS primitive, defines the resource theory framework, provides mathematical proofs for the threshold structure and gate uniqueness, presents the 1SDI certification method, and concludes with experimental validation on IBM hardware.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce Magic Secret Sharing (MSS), a quantum cryptographic primitive in which the secret is the computational capability of a quantum state rather than its classical description. In the resource theory of magic, non-stabilizer states fuel universal quantum computation via non-Clifford gates; MSS distributes this resource with an (n-1,n) threshold structure using a pre-shared GHZ state and a single local phase gate P(phi) = diag(1, exp(i*phi)). Any individual party holds the maximally mixed state I/2, with Wigner distance C(I/2) = 0, so no local operation can yield non-Clifford computational advantage regardless of what operations are applied or what noise acts on the device. The authorised coalition reconstructs magic content C(phi) = (|sin(phi)| + |cos(phi)| - 1)/2 exactly, enabling a logical T gate via gate teleportation in multi-server blind quantum computation (BQC). Among diagonal parametric gates, phase gates are the unique class satisfying the security condition, characterised via an exact column-sum condition. The protocol is elevated to a one-sided device-independent (1SDI) setting via a steering inequality: the assemblage produced on the recipient's side certifies magic delivery without trusting the coalition's devices. We demonstrate the (2,3) instance on ibm_marrakesh (156-qubit IBM Heron): security (C(rho_Bob) = 0.000, below LP reconstruction tolerance) holds in all runs, and state fidelity reaches 0.959-0.986 for the authorised party, with faithfulness confirmed for all four test values of phi including near-exact recovery (C = 0.154 vs theory 0.153) for phi = pi/8.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.17196"></a>
### [Maxwell's Demon](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17196v1)

**Authors:** R. E. Kastner  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17196v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17196_figures/2605.17196_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1. The basic Szilard Engine setup</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17196_figures/2605.17196_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3. A real, delocalized quantum molecule</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper re-evaluates the Maxwell's Demon paradox by arguing that the Second Law of Thermodynamics is preserved by the physical consequences of quantum measurement. It shifts the focus from the entropy cost of memory erasure to the unavoidable entropy increase caused by the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle during the measurement process.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental link between quantum measurement theory, information theory, and thermodynamics, specifically addressing how the uncertainty principle enforces the Second Law in open quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge posed by Maxwell's Demon to the Second Law of Thermodynamics and whether the demon can violate entropy increase through microscopic manipulation.

**Main result.** The Second Law is upheld by quantum mechanics because the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle ensures that the physical act of measurement or localization incurs an unavoidable entropy cost, precluding the demon's success.

**Method.** A historical and theoretical analysis comparing classical epistemic uncertainty with quantum ontological uncertainty, utilizing the Quantum Information Uncertainty Relation (QIUR) and Shannon entropy.

**Model / system.** A single-molecule gas system in a one-dimensional box (Szilard Engine) where a partition is used to manipulate or measure the particle's position and momentum.

**Key observables.** Entropy (S), position uncertainty (delta x), momentum uncertainty (delta p), and transition rates (r_ij).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The classical limit (high temperature), the regime of low-energy photon measurement, and the variance of the probability distribution.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of Gaussian wavefunctions as an approximation for delocalized particles and the assumption of fundamental (ontological) stochasticity in quantum transitions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the Szilard Engine; Figure 2 depicts a localized classical particle; Figure 3 depicts a delocalized quantum molecule.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with a historical review of the Demon challenge (Szilard, Brillouin, Bennett), critiques the Bennettian view of information erasure, and concludes by proposing a quantum-mechanical resolution based on the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This work provides an overview of key historical developments in the formulation of the Second Law of Thermodynamics, focusing on the notorious challenge of ``Maxwell's Demon'', a hypothetical creature who could presumably violate that law. It begins by recalling Maxwell's challenge and discussing the apparent loophole in the Second Law that appears to make such a violation possible. An alternative formulation of the Demon challenge by Szilard is considered, along with his attempted defeat of the Demon through reference to measurement. A similar effort by Brillouin is also analyzed. The proposal of Bennett to defeat the Demon through the requirement of memory erasure is critically discussed. Finally, it is proposed that the Second Law gains a firm foundation through neglected features of quantum theory. In particular, an application of the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle is shown to decisively defeat the Demon, as well as to serve as justification for Landauer's Principle, albeit in terms distinct from the usual computational formulation.

</details>

### [Universal interface fluctuations in absorbing-state phase transitions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17781v1)

**Authors:** Yohsuke T. Fukai, Keiichi Tamai, Tetsuya Hiraiwa  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17781v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium universality` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5**

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a universal crossover between two fundamental non-equilibrium universality classes: absorbing-state phase transitions and KPZ growth. It shows that at long time scales, the fluctuations of the active interface in APT models are governed by KPZ physics. This provides a fundamental link between the bulk dynamics of phase transitions and the scale-invariant growth of interfaces.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the relationship between universal fluctuations in absorbing-state phase transitions (APTs) and Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) interface growth, specifically seeking to establish how bulk scale invariance relates to interface fluctuations.

**Main result.** The authors identify a universal crossover where short-time fluctuations governed by APT exponents transition into long-time fluctuations belonging to the KPZ universality class, with height distributions converging to the GOE-TW distribution.

**Method.** Numerical simulations of (2+1)-dimensional discrete models and Langevin equations, combined with scaling analysis and parameter estimation using the Levenberg-Marquardt and Nelder-Mead algorithms.

**Model / system.** The study utilizes discrete models such as Bond Percolation (DP class) and the Biased Voter Model (CDP class), alongside continuum stochastic Fisher-Kolmogorov-Petrovsky-Piskunov (sFKPP) Langevin equations.

**Key observables.** Interface height h(x, t), probability density functions of height, cumulants (mean, variance, skewness, kurt1), spanwise-averaged density, and spatial covariance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Distance from criticality (epsilon), APT correlation time (xi_parallel) and length (xi_perp), and the dimensionless parameter D/sigma^2 for CDP models.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that height fluctuations follow the Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble Tracy-Widom (GOE-TW) distribution for KPZ fitting purposes.

**Figures summary.** Figures include schematics of interface height definitions, snapshots of active sites, plots of mean density and height probability distributions, and scaling collapses of cumulants and spatial covariance.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of APT-KPZ connection, describes the discrete and continuum models used, details the numerical simulation and parameter estimation methods, presents the results of the universal crossover and scaling collapse, and concludes with discussion on parameter universality and potential experimental candidates.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Despite similarities between models exhibiting absorbing phase transitions (APTs) and those showing Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) growth, the relationship between these universal fluctuations has remained elusive. We numerically study (1+1)-dimensional interfaces of (2+1)-dimensional models showing APTs of directed percolation (DP) and compact directed percolation (CDP) classes with an active boundary, finding a universal crossover from short-time APT-governed fluctuations to long-time KPZ fluctuations. Upon rescaling time and length by the APT correlation time and length, the cumulants of the interface height distributions collapse onto a single scaling function. The fluctuation properties of the discrete Domany-Kinzel model and the continuum stochastic Fisher-Kolmogorov-Petrovsky-Piskunov (sFKPP) equation coincide, indicating that the KPZ growth parameters are determined solely by fundamental properties of the APT. For the CDP sFKPP equation, a dimensionless parameter tunes both the critical interface distribution and the KPZ parameters, with the interface properties of the biased voter model recovered in a limiting case. These results uncover a universal crossover in which KPZ fluctuations emerge from APT fluctuations at long times, linking paradigmatic universality classes of nonequilibrium scale-invariant phenomena.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper demonstrates a method for universal control of a high-dimensional quantum system encoded in a microwave cavity. By optimizing sequences of Jaynes-Cummines interactions and qubit rotations, the researchers achieved high-fidelity control of qutrits, ququarts, and ququints. This establishes a scalable and efficient framework for implementing bosonic quantum computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a practical route to programmable bosonic processors by turning a known physical interaction (JC) into a universal gate set, while demonstrating how dispersive shifts can be leveraged as a computational resource.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving universal control of a high-dimensional bosonic oscillator (qudit) using the Jaynes-Cummings interaction, specifically overcoming the challenges of dispersive shifts and circuit depth scaling.

**Main result.** Demonstrated universal qudit control with a mean post-selected process fidelity of 96% for qutrit gates and successful implementation of ququart and ququint shift gates.

**Method.** Decomposing arbitrary unitaries into sequences of JC interactions and qubit rotations, using gradient descent optimization for pulse parameters, and utilizing frequency chirping for error mitigation.

**Model / system.** A superconducting circuit-QED platform consisting of a transmon ancilla qubit coupled to a high-quality factor microwave cavity mode, operating in a weakly dispersive regime.

**Key observables.** Joint qubit-oscillator Wigner tomography, qudit process tomography, and state fidelity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** JC interaction rate (g_JC), dispersive shift (chi_f), photon number cutoff (d), and transmon coherence times (T1, T2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes a decoherence-free evolution model for some comparisons; neglects higher-order energy shifts and cavity self-Kerr; assumes the ability to stabilize temperature to prevent frequency fluctuations.

**Figures summary.** Energy level diagrams of the JC interaction; scaling analysis of gate infidelity and circuit depth; demonstration of qutrit/ququart/ququint shift gates via Wigner functions; and process tomography matrices for universal qutrit gates.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the JC control problem; description of the circuit-QED platform and Hamiltonian; presentation of the control protocol and optimization methods; experimental demonstration of universal qutrit gates; scaling analysis for higher-dimensional qudits; and error budget/robustness analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Jaynes-Cummings (JC) interaction-the coherent exchange of excitations between a two-level system and a harmonic oscillator-is one of the fundamental interactions of quantum optics, realized across platforms such as cavity quantum electrodynamics, trapped ions, mechanical resonators, and superconducting circuits. Although JC interactions and qubit rotations form a universal gate set for oscillator control, practical implementations have not been demonstrated. Here we develop and experimentally demonstrate universal JC-based oscillator control by compiling arbitrary unitary gates into sequences of JC interactions and qubit rotations. In our experiment, the oscillator is realized using a mode of a high quality factor microwave cavity and the ancilla qubit using a superconducting transmon circuit, with the JC interaction implemented by a sideband interaction enabled by the Josephson nonlinearity. The native gates are constructed to be closed below a chosen cutoff photon number, encoding a qudit with suppressed leakage errors, while ancilla relaxation errors are detectable. We further find that the dispersive shift serves as a compilation resource that reduces circuit depths. We demonstrate universal qudit control and implement a single-qutrit gate set with a mean post-selected process fidelity of 96%, as well as ququart and ququint shift gates. These results establish Jaynes-Cummings control as a practical route to universal oscillator control, enabling programmable bosonic processors across a variety of quantum platforms.

</details>

### [Open quantum dynamics without Complete Positivity: a criticism](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18639v1)

**Authors:** Fabio Benatti, Dariusz Chruściński, Saverio Pascazio  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18639v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** The paper investigates whether one can describe open quantum systems using maps that are positive but not completely positive by restricting the allowed initial states. It proves that this 'compatibility' approach fails in high dimensions because the set of allowed entangled states shrinks to zero, suggesting that Complete Positivity is a necessary requirement for consistent high-dimensional dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it addresses a fundamental consistency condition (CP) and shows that non-CP models may be physically inconsistent with large-scale entanglement.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper critiques the validity of abandoning the requirement of Complete Positivity (CP) in open quantum dynamics by using a 'compatibility-based' approach that restricts initial states.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that as the system dimension increases, the volume of compatible entangled states vanishes, revealing an intrinsic weakness in non-CP dynamical models.

**Method.** The authors use spectral analysis of the lifted map and volume calculations of the parameter interval for isotropic states to evaluate the compatibility of non-CP maps.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on finite d-level quantum systems interacting with an environment, specifically analyzing a family of positive but non-CP maps (Lambda_mu) and isotropic qudit states.

**Key observables.** The volume of compatible entangled states (V_comp) and the fidelity (F) of isotropic states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System dimension (d), map parameter (mu), and state fidelity (F).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is primarily focused on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces and specific families of channels and states.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by framing the dilemma of CP vs. non-CP dynamics, evaluates the pros and cons of the CP requirement, analyzes the 'compatibility-based' strategy using isotropic states, and concludes with the scaling of compatible state volumes in high dimensions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The requirement of complete positivity is very often regarded as a fundamental consistency condition for the description of open quantum dynamics. We critically examine this requirement and discuss both its physical motivations and its limitations. We analyze proposals based on restricting the domain of non-completely positive maps to subsets of compatible initial states. Using isotropic states as a concrete example, we show that such domain restrictions become increasingly severe with growing system dimension, revealing an intrinsic weakness of the compatibility-based approach.

</details>

### [Shapiro steps of superfluid Fermi gases in a ring trap across the BCS--BEC crossover](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17940v1)

**Authors:** Hikaru Kuriki, Masaya Kunimi, Tetsuro Nikuni  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17940v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The nearly vertical depletion near the barrier corresponds to the suppression of the order parameter by the barrier, while the slanted depletion structures propagating away from it correspond to emitted solitons. (c) Time evolution of the winding number W(t) defined...</sub>

**Summary.** This paper explores how Shapiro steps emerge in a driven superfluid Fermi gas in a ring trap. It demonstrates that these quantized steps are caused by the synchronization of soliton emissions at a moving barrier. The findings provide a microscopic understanding of transport properties across the BCS-BEC crossover.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides microscopic insights into phase-locking and nonequilibrium phase dynamics in topological atomtronic systems, bridging the gap between fundamental BCS-BEC crossover physics and driven Josephson junction dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the nonequilibrium dynamics and transport properties of Shapiro steps in a superfluid Fermi gas within a ring trap across the BCS-BEC crossover.

**Main result.** The study observes quantized Shapiro-step plateaus in the chemical potential difference, which originate from synchronized phase slips mediated by periodic soliton generation at the barrier.

**Method.** Numerical simulations using the time-dependent Bogoliable-de Gennes (tdBdG) equations solved via the split-step Fourier method and a modified Broyden's method for self-consistency.

**Model / system.** A superfluid Fermi gas in a 1D ring geometry containing a moving Gaussian potential barrier that acts as a Josephson junction biased by DC and AC currents.

**Key observables.** Chemical potential difference (delta-mu), topological winding number, order parameter amplitude and phase, local current density, and particle number density.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction strength (kF a inverse), DC barrier velocity (v_DC), AC amplitude (x_AC), AC frequency (omega), and the Stewart-McCumber parameter (beta_c).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is treated in an effective 1D geometry at zero temperature, and the midpoint order parameter is assumed to evolve smoothly during time steps.

**Figures summary.** Schematic of the ring trap setup; dynamics of the order parameter and winding number; relationship between barrier velocity and chemical potential difference showing Shapiro steps; and the evolution of local velocity at the barrier.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical setup and problem, details the numerical BdG framework and self-consistent loop, presents results on the superflow-to-voltage transition, analyzes the microscopic soliton-mediated mechanism of Shapiro steps, and discusses the breakdown in the BCS regime.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the transport properties of a superfluid Fermi gas confined in a ring trap with a moving potential barrier across the Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) to Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) crossover. Employing time-dependent Bogoliubov--de Gennes (BdG) equations, we simulate the dynamics of a Josephson junction biased by both DC and AC currents. Over a wide range of interaction strengths, we observe clear low-order Shapiro-step plateaus in the barrier-velocity--chemical-potential-diffenrence, within the phase-coherent regime, where the time-averaged chemical potential difference is quantized in units of $\hbarω/2$. This factor of $1/2$ reflects our convention of defining the chemical potential per single fermion in the BdG framework. Microscopic analysis reveals that these fundamental steps originate from synchronized phase slips mediated by periodic soliton generation at the barrier. Our findings clarify the role of interaction regimes in the nonequilibrium phase dynamics of ring-trapped fermionic superfluids and provide microscopic insights relevant to future studies of atomtronic systems with nontrivial topology.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper demonstrates a method to grow strain-free, symmetric InGaAs quantum dots that emit in the telecommunications C-band. By using local droplet etching on GaAs(111)A substrates, the researchers achieved high-quality single-photon emission with low asymmetry. This is a significant step toward building long-distance quantum communication networks using existing fiber technology.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a scalable material platform for generating high-purity single photons at the telecom C-band, which is essential for integrating quantum nodes into existing fiber-optic infrastructure.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for high-quality, strain-free single-photon emitters operating in the telecommunication C-band (1550 nm) for low-loss, long-distance quantum communication networks.

**Main result.** Successful fabrication of strain-free, symmetric InGaAs/InAlAs quantum dots on GaAs(111)A substrates that emit in the 1400-1600 nm range with high single-photon purity (g2(0) = 0.141).

**Method.** The researchers used Local Droplet Etching (LDE) via Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) to create nanoholes, followed by filling them with InGaAs, and validated the results using 8-band k.p simulations and optical spectroscopy.

**Model / system.** Strain-free, C3v-symmetric In0.7Ga0.3As/In0.7Al0.3As quantum dots grown on a thin metamorphic buffer layer on vicinal GaAs(111)A substrates.

**Key observables.** Emission wavelength (1400-1600 nm), exciton lifetimes (1.3-1.9 ns), linewidths (approx. 300 ueV), second-order autocorrelation g2(0), and degree of linear polarization (DOLP).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Target wavelength of 1550 nm, substrate temperature of 450-500 C, and QD height of approximately 6 nm.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the envelope function approximation (EFA) within an eight-band k.p framework to model electronic properties.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the QD fabrication schematic; Figure 2 displays AFM morphology of nanoholes and simulated emission properties; Figure 3 shows micro-photoluminescence spectra and photon statistics.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of C-band emission, describes the LDE growth process and metamorphic buffer layer, presents morphological characterization via AFM, details the k.p simulations, and concludes with optical characterization of the emitters.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Non-classical photon sources made of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) emitting in the telecommunication C-band are crucial components for low-loss, long-distance photonic quantum communication networks. Here we designed and fabricated strain--free In$_{0.7}$Ga$_{0.3}$As/In$_{0.7}$Al$_{0.3}$As QDs grown on GaAs(111)A substrates working as single-photon emitters in the 1550 nm window. The QDs were grown via local droplet etching method in a molecular beam epitaxy environment, employing a thin In$_{0.7}$Al$_{0.3}$As metamorphic buffer layer with the same lattice constant of the QD material, thus allowing for a completely strain--free self-assembly of the QDs. The QDs exhibit a C$_{3v}$ symmetry with a ground state emission in the 1400--1600 nm range. The exciton lifetimes of $\approx$ 1.3--1.9 ns and linewidths as low as $\approx$ 300 $μ$eV show the good quality of the fabricated QDs. Second-order autocorrelation measurements under pulsed excitation confirmed the single-photon purity of the emitters, yielding a $g^{(2)}(0)$ value of $0.141 \pm 0.027$

</details>

**Authors:** Thi D. Hoang, Fateme Mahdikhany, Zixuan Wang, Richard Mirin, Kevin Silverman, Poolad Imany, Shuo Sun  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16694v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** The researchers have created a tiny, vibration-resistant mechanical assembly for open microcavities that works reliably in standard cryostats. This platform allows for easy tuning of cavity resonance and has successfully demonstrated strong coupling with quantum dots. It offers a scalable path toward portable quantum light sources and quantum computing components.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a highly stable and scalable hardware platform for cavity QED, which is essential for implementing spin-photon interfaces and quantum networks in solid-state systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Operating open microcavities at cryogenic temperatures is challenging due to extreme sensitivity to mechanical vibrations and the need for bulky, custom vibration-mitigation setups.

**Main result.** The authors developed a compact, robust, and tunable mechanical host for an open microcavity that shows no vibration-induced broadening in a standard cryostat and achieves cooperativity exceeding unity with an InGaAs quantum dot.

**Method.** The platform uses a piezo-driven assembly to tune cavity resonance in situ and employs cross-polarized transmission spectroscopy and photoluminescence to characterize the emitter-cavity coupling.

**Model / system.** The system is a plano-concave Fabry-Perot resonator consisting of a GaAs/AlAs DBR bottom mirror and a microscale concave top mirror, coupled to InGaAs quantum dots embedded in a p-i-n junction.

**Key observables.** Cavity resonance, quantum-dot-induced transmission dips (dipole-induced transparency), cavity finesse, and cooperativity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Finesse > 1,000, coupling strength g/2pi = 1.37 GHz, cooperativity C > 1, and a tuning range of 3 nm.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theoretical model for fitting spectra omits inhomogeneous spectral diffusion, and the model assumes the weak excitation limit.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the microcavity schematic and electric field profile; Figure 2 shows PL spectra and cavity modes; Figure 3 displays transmission spectra showing QD-induced dips, saturation, and Fano-like features; Supplemental figures show device yield and Stark tuning.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the vibration-mitigation challenge, describes the fabrication and architecture of the microcavity and mechanical host, presents characterization of cavity stability and tuning, demonstrates quantum dot coupling and nonlinear response, and concludes with scalability and device yield analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Open microcavities provide a powerful platform for studying cavity quantum electrodynamics in solid-state systems. However, operating open microcavities at cryogenic temperatures, as required for many solid-state quantum emitters, typically demands bulky and cryostat-specific vibration-mitigation setups. Here we report a compact, robust, and tunable mechanical host for an open microcavity. The complete mechanical assembly fits within a footprint of $1'' \times 1'' \times 0.5''$. Using this mechanical host, we observe no vibration-induced cavity broadening for an open microcavity with finesse exceeding 1,000 without cryostat customization or active locking. The assembly also enables in situ tuning of the cavity resonance over 3 nm, and the resonance of the same cavity remains within this range across multiple cooldowns. To further showcase the capability of this assembly, we demonstrate coupling between an InGaAs quantum dot and an open microcavity with a cooperativity exceeding unity. This platform provides a versatile testbed for fundamental cavity quantum electrodynamics and a scalable route to portable quantum light sources and spin-photon interfaces for quantum repeaters, quantum networks, and photonic quantum computing.

</details>

### [Demonstration of transport in an ion trap design for two-dimensional lattices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16543v1)

**Authors:** Michael Pfeifer, Marco Valentini, Matthias Dietl, Fabian Anmasser, Simon Schey, Jakob Wahl, Philip C. Holz, Clemens Rössler, Yves Colombe, Philipp Schindler  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16543v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Rf minimum paths (orange dashed lines) and ion chains (turquoise balls) are forming a two- dimensional lattice for implementing a QSA architecture [1]. The ion chains interact via the Coulomb interaction (purple zig-zag lines) in the “interaction zones” (green areas) and can be shuttled, using dc electrodes, along the rf minimum paths through “transition zones” (red areas) between interaction zones. They can interact in the radial direction between lat- tice sites, as shown on the left of the figure, or axially within a lattice site, as shown on the right.</sub>

The markings correspond to the potential cross-sections in the figures on the right. The line “center” marks the center of the trap at z = 0. The lines “outer region” mark the positions z = ±800 µm. Subfigures b, c are rf pseudo-potential cross-sections of the trap in the linear transition zone configuration in a) at different axial positions z....</sub>

</details>

The values are along the rf minimum path ⃗ξ(z) = (x0(z), y0(z), z). All calculations assume 194.5 V rf amplitude and an rf drive frequency of 2π × 31.91 MHz. a) Tangential pseudo-potential along the rf minimum path. b) Secular frequencies of the eigenmodes, considering only rf electrode contributions. c) Horizontal position of the minimum x0, which is half the ion-ion distance s0 in the central double-well. d) The ion-surface separation y0 as function of the axial position z.</sub>

**Summary.** The paper presents a new ion trap chip design that allows for the tuning of radial coupling in 2D ion lattices using only DC voltages. By optimizing electrode geometry, the authors successfully demonstrated reliable ion transport through transition zones with low heating rates. This approach provides a scalable path toward large-scale quantum computing using complex 2D architectures.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a scalable hardware solution for 2D ion trap architectures, which is crucial for implementing large-scale quantum simulations and many-body dynamics in multidimensional ion crystals.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of controlling radial separation between ion chains in Quantum Spring Array (QSA) architectures without using RF voltage modulation, which causes high heating rates.

**Main result.** Demonstration of a new ion trap design that enables radial transport and tuning of ion separation using only DC voltages, significantly reducing pseudo-potential barriers and heating.

**Method.** The researchers used an optimization strategy (Nelder-Mead solver) to design curved RF electrodes and transition zones, then fabricated a prototype on a fused silica substrate and characterized it using 40Ca+ ions.

**Model / system.** A microfabricated surface ion trap chip featuring a Quantum Spring Array (QSA) architecture, where ion chains are arranged in a 2D lattice and interact via the Coulomb force.

**Key observables.** Radial transport fidelity, ion heating rates, stray electric fields, micromotion modulation index, and radial secular frequencies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** RF drive frequency of 31.91 MHz, RF amplitude of 194.5 V, ion-surface separation of ~90 micrometers, and heating rates of 18 +/- 3 ph/s.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The design uses the gapless plane approximation, neglecting the physical gaps between RF and DC electrodes.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the trap geometry and coupling modes; Figure 2 compares linear vs. optimized electrode transition zones; Figure 3 illustrates the reduction in pseudo-potential barriers and frequency variation; Figure 8 and 9 show microscopy of the multi-layer trap and its vias.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QSA architecture and the limitations of RF-based tuning, presents the optimized curved electrode design and its simulation, demonstrates experimental transport and characterization of a single-layer prototype, and concludes with a scaling path using a multi-layer fabrication approach.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Microfabricated ion trap chips are at the core of some of the most advanced quantum computers. How a large number of ions is arranged and controlled on an ion trap chip depends on the chosen trap architecture. One such architecture is the quantum spring array (QSA). In the QSA architecture, ion chains are arranged in a two-dimensional lattice and interact with ion chains in neighboring sites in the radial and axial directions of the respective chain. This interaction, or coupling, is mediated by the Coulomb force while keeping ions in separate trapping sites, and scales inversely with the third power of the separation. The capability to control the distance between ions in the lattice is thus essential. In previous works, the radial separation between ions was tuned by controlling the rf pseudo-potential, which revealed to be experimentally challenging to realize while maintaining low heating rates. In this work, we present an ion trap chip design that allows tuning of the radial distance between ions using only dc voltages. The radial transport is executed between different interaction zones, designated for quantum operations, through specifically designed transition zones. A prototype of this type of ion trap chip was microfabricated on fused silica substrate. Its functionality is characterized by demonstrating dc-controlled radial transport of a single ion through a transition zone and measuring stray fields and ion heating rates in the center of the trap. Moreover, the fabrication of a multi-metal layer version of such a trap is presented as a scaling path for the presented chip design.

</details>

### [Closed-form Bayesian quantum estimation of Gaussian states](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16978v1)

**Authors:** Edward Gandar, Jesús Rubio  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16978v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

Similarly, the dashed and solid blue curves show the relat- ive MSL of the {1, ˆq2 ϕ} subspace at homodyne angles ϕ = 0 or π/2—chosen to minimise the MSL Eq. (67)—with the constrained [Eq. (68)] and PM estimator [Eq. (19)] respectively. The grey dot- dashed curve indicates...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a closed-form method for Bayesian quantum estimation of Gaussian states by approximating the optimal operator within a finite-dimensional polynomial subspace. It transforms a complex, infinite-dimensional optimization problem into a tractable linear algebra problem. The framework allows for the design of near-optimal, experimentally feasible measurement strategies for tasks like displacement and squeezing estimation.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a practical, analytical tool for optimizing continuous-variable quantum metrology, bridging the gap between complex numerical Bayesian methods and experimentally implementable Gaussian operations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The computational intractability of Bayesian quantum estimation for continuous-variable Gaussian states due to infinite-dimensional optimization and complex parameter integrals.

**Main result.** A variational framework that reduces the optimization of measurements and estimators to a finite-dimensional linear problem with closed-form solutions using polynomial subspaces.

**Method.** The authors use a variational approach restricting operators to a polynomial subspace of canonical quadratures, transforming the Mean Square Loss minimization into a solvable linear system involving a Gram matrix.

**Model / system.** Continuous-variable (CV) quantum systems consisting of N bosonic modes, specifically focusing on Gaussian states characterized by mean vectors and covariance matrices.

**Key observables.** Canonical quadratures (q and p), Symmetric Posterior Mean (SPM) operator, and Weyl symbols.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Displacement parameter, squeezing parameter, prior variance (Gaussian and uniform), and thermal photon number.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The input states are Gaussian; the optimization is restricted to a polynomial subspace; the Gaussian state is assumed to be faithful.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares relative Mean Squared Loss for different polynomial estimators (linear, cubic) and the posterior mean in displacement estimation. Figure 2 shows the convergence of constrained and posterior mean estimators toward the global minimum for squeezing estimation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the computational bottleneck in Bayesian QET, proposes a variational framework using polynomial subspaces, provides a geometric interpretation of the solution as an orthogonal projection, derives optimality conditions, and validates the method with single-shot examples of displacement and squeezing estimation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Bayesian quantum estimation provides a robust framework for quantum technologies, especially in scenarios with limited data and minimal prior information. Yet, its application to continuous-variable Gaussian systems has remained limited and largely numerical due to the complexity of the underlying parameter integrals. Here, we introduce a variational framework reducing the optimisation over measurements and estimators to a finite-dimensional linear problem and admitting closed-form solutions. This is achieved by restricting the analysis to operators polynomial in the canonical quadratures, leading to solutions with a geometric interpretation as orthogonal projections of the global optimum. We further derive a necessary and sufficient condition for global optimality. Through single-shot examples, we show that the framework yields experimentally feasible strategies based on Gaussian operations and quadrature measurements that are either optimal or near-optimal, and that replacing the induced estimator with the posterior mean further improves performance towards the global optimum.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.16935"></a>
### [Exact Entanglement-Depth Speed Frontier for Complete Quantum Charging](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16935v1)

**Authors:** Wenlong Sun, Gang Lu, Yuanfeng Jin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16935v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.16935_figures/2605.16935_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Exact integer speed–depth frontier for coherent com- plete charging, shown for N = 20. The staircase gives the cer- tified trajectory entanglement depth Dcert(η) = ⌈N/⌊η−2⌋⌉ from an observed QSL-normalized rate η = τQSL/T. The threshold η &gt; 1/ √</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proves that the speed of charging a quantum battery is fundamentally limited by the amount of multipartite entanglement present in the system. It establishes an exact 'staircase' relationship where faster charging rates directly certify higher levels of entanglement depth, including genuine N-partite entanglement.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a model-independent, orbit-level diagnostic for quantifying many-body entanglement resources based solely on the observed speed of a dynamical process.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the fundamental relationship between the speed of a complete quantum charging process and the amount of multipartite entanglement depth required to achieve that speed.

**Main result.** The authors establish an exact integer staircase frontier relating the QSL-normalized rate to entanglement depth, proving that crossing the threshold eta > 1/sqrt(2) certifies genuine N-partite entanglement.

**Method.** The study uses Quantum Speed Limit (QSL) geometry, specifically the Mandelstam-Tamm bound, combined with Minkowski's integral inequality and a Baire category argument to relate path speed to block orthogonalization.

**Model / system.** A closed N-qubit quantum battery undergoing unitary evolution from an all-down state to an all-up state under a time-independent Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** QSL-normalized rate (eta), entanglement depth (Ent[U]), and the number of independent blocks (m).

**Important parameters / regimes.** N-qubit system size, entanglement depth constraint (k), and the threshold eta = 1/sqrt(2) for N-partite entanglement.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The results apply to finite-dimensional pure-state complete charging under a closed, time-independent Hamiltonian with exact endpoint conditions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 displays the exact integer staircase frontier for N=20, plotting the charging rate against the lower bound on entanglement depth.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of quantum charging speed, defines the QSL-normalized rate, provides the mathematical derivation of the speed-depth frontier using block orthogonalization, constructs a saturated model to prove sharpness, and concludes with entanglement certification thresholds.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Complete quantum charging provides a sharp setting in which to ask how much multipartite entanglement is forced by speed itself. For a closed \(N\)-qubit battery evolving from \(\ket{\downarrow}^{\otimes N}\) to \(\ket{\uparrow}^{\otimes N}\) under a time-independent Hamiltonian, we exactly solve the pure-state depth-constrained speed problem. If the realized trajectory has entanglement depth at most \(k\), then the largest possible QSL-normalized rate \(η=τ_{\rm QSL}/T\) is \(η_{\max}(k)=\lceil N/k\rceil^{-1/2}\). Conversely, an observed rate \(η\) certifies trajectory entanglement depth at least \(\bigl\lceil N/\lfloor η^{-2}\rfloor\bigr\rceil\). The mechanism is block orthogonalization: under a fixed product partition, complete charging forces all blocks to orthogonalize simultaneously, and the quantum speed limit converts this counting constraint into the speed bound. Balanced cluster-flip evolutions saturate the bound, establishing an exact integer staircase frontier. Thus fast complete charging cannot be explained by many small independently charging blocks; in particular, crossing the threshold \(η>1/\sqrt2\) certifies, for \(N>1\), the generation of genuine \(N\)-partite entanglement.

</details>

### [Krylov complexity and fidelity susceptibility in two-band Hamiltonians](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18594v1)

**Authors:** Rishav Chaudhuri, Ayush Raj, Soham Ray, Sai Satyam Samal  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18594v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

The spread complexity is linearly dependent on t2 in the trivial phase and shows a constant plateau in the topological phase, recovering earlier results [40]. (b) The spread complexity is a constant across both the trivial and the topological phase for ˆnref(k) = (0, −1, 0). (c) With ˆnref(k) = (−1, 0, 0), the derivative of the spread complexity with respect to t2 shows a logarithmic...</sub>

</details>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the derivative of Krylov spread complexity can detect any gap-closing event in two-band models, not just topological transitions. By establishing a mathematical bound related to fidelity susceptibility, the authors show that complexity captures the non-analyticity of the system's energy spectrum. They also uncover a universal scaling ratio and a duality between topological and trivial phases in the SSH model.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a new geometric tool to detect criticality and explores the fundamental relationship between quantum complexity and the sensitivity of the ground state to parameter changes.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether Krylov spread complexity can serve as a diagnostic for phase transitions and gap-closing events in two-band Hamiltonians.

**Main result.** The authors prove that the derivative of Krylov spread complexity is bounded by fidelity susceptibility, meaning it is sensitive to both topological and trivial gap closings, and they identify a universal ratio between these two quantities.

**Method.** The study uses a geometric formulation based on Bloch sphere data to calculate complexity without constructing a circuit Hamiltonian, alongside analytical integration using elliptic functions.

**Model / system.** The analysis focuses on two-band Hamiltonians, specifically the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model for topological transitions and the massive Dirac Hamiltonian for trivial gap closings.

**Key observables.** Krylov spread complexity, fidelity susceptibility, winding number (Zak phase), and the ratio between the complexity derivative and the square root of fidelity susceptibility.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hopping amplitudes (t1, t2), mass parameter (mu), Bloch sphere angles (theta, phi), and the duality parameter (r).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derived inequality assumes a momentum-independent reference state, and the scope is primarily limited to two-band Hamiltonian systems.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the SSH model lattice structure, plots of Krylov complexity and its derivative exhibiting logarithmic divergence and plateaus, and the symmetry/saturation of the ratio R between complexity and fidelity susceptibility.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of complexity as a phase probe, develops a geometric formulation for two-band models, applies this to the SSH and Dirac models to compare topological vs. trivial transitions, establishes mathematical bounds and duality, and extends the analysis to non-Hermitian systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate Krylov spread complexity for the ground state of two-band Hamiltonians, where the reference state is a generic state on the Bloch sphere. The spread complexity is obtained by using a purely geometric formulation in terms of Bloch sphere data without constructing the circuit Hamiltonian. For generic reference states, the derivative of the spread complexity is logarithmically divergent at the topological phase transition in the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model. We demonstrate that the derivative of the spread complexity is bounded by fidelity susceptibility for general two-band models, indicating the sensitivity of the spread complexity to any gap closing (topological or trivial). This is illustrated in the massive Dirac Hamiltonian with a trivial gap closing. Finally, we introduce a non-unitary duality in the SSH model between the topological and trivial phases, which manifests itself in the spread complexity and fidelity susceptibility.

</details>

**Authors:** Jiajie Guo, Shuheng Liu, Matteo Fadel, Qiongyi He  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18605v1>

States with ENG(ρ) &gt; 1 are certified as non-Gaussian entangled, and larger d witnesses higher non-Gaussian entanglement dimensions. (b) For ∆A = 0, KA = 1, χ = 1 and different loss rates γL = {0, 0.1, 0.5}, we compare the entanglement witnesses E(ρ) and ENG(ρ) and the distilled entanglement E(ρd) as the functions of times t.</sub>

(b) The Gaussian-entanglable threshold fG for different N. If the de- tection statistics f lies in the red regions, f &gt; fG, certifies genuine non-Gaussian entanglement.</sub>

<sub>FIG. 5. The complexity of pure state learning in a finite-energy truncation with local dimension D = 20. The red solid line, Ncore(d) = 2d(2D −d) −2, provides a lower bound on the number of real parameters required to learn the pure state, while the black dashed line, Nfull = 2D2 −2, shows the full tomography complexity. The witnessed integer d = ⌈ENG⌉therefore reflects the complexity of learning two- mode pure states.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper establishes a new hierarchy for non-Gaussian entanglement in bosonic systems using a quantitative witness based on the Schmidt number. It connects the level of non-Gaussianity to the complexity of quantum state learning and proves that such entanglement can be distilled using only Gaussian operations. This provides a practical tool for identifying and utilizing non-Gaussian resources in quantum optical and circuit-QED platforms.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous framework for classifying non-Gaussian resources in continuous-variable systems, directly linking entanglement dimensionality to the practical difficulty of quantum state tomography and the utility of Gaussian-based distillation.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of quantifying and characterizing non-Gaussian entanglement in bipartite bosonic systems and establishing a hierarchy that connects entanglement dimension to state-learning complexity.

**Main result.** The authors introduce a quantitative witness E_NG that defines a natural hierarchy of non-Gaussian entanglement based on the Schmidt number, and demonstrate that this entanglement can serve as a resource for distillation using only Gaussian operations.

**Method.** The study uses the trace norm of the partial transpose minimized over Gaussian unitaries to define the witness, alongside complexity analysis for state reconstruction and a two-copy distillation protocol.

**Model / system.** Bipartite bosonic systems, specifically focusing on continuous-variable states such as NOON states, squeezed Kerr states, and photon-subtracted Gaussian states, applicable to platforms like circuit-QED and quantum acoustics.

**Key observables.** The non-Gaussian entanglement witness E_NG, the Schmidt number hierarchy d, and the expectation value of a NOON-type witness operator F.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Squeezing strength, nonlinearity (Kerr parameter), loss rates, and the Gaussian-entanglable threshold f_G.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The optimization of the witness is performed over an effective set of Gaussian unitaries, and the distillation protocol assumes the availability of local homodyne measurements and classical communication.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the hierarchy of GE and non-Gaussian entangled states, linking the integer d to entanglement levels and state-learning complexity; Figure 5 shows the scaling of reconstruction complexity as a function of the entanglement integer d.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of non-Gaussian entanglement, defines the E_NG witness and its hierarchy, provides mathematical proofs for its properties, benchmarks the framework with specific states (NOON, Kerr), analyzes robustness to loss, proposes an experimental NOON-type witness, and demonstrates entanglement distillation protocols.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Non-Gaussian entanglement is a promising resource in various quantum tasks. A recently defined class identifies entanglement that cannot be generated by applying Gaussian operations to separable inputs. To further explore the entanglement in this context, we introduce a quantitative witness $E_{\rm NG}$ in bipartite bosonic systems, which satisfies $E_{\rm NG}=1$ for all Gaussian-entanglable states, while $E_{\rm NG}>1$ certifies non-Gaussian entanglement. Its ceiling $d=\lceil E_{\rm NG}\rceil$ provides a lower bound on the Schmidt number irreducible by Gaussian transformations, thereby defining a natural hierarchy of non-Gaussian entanglement. For pure states, the condition is sharp and the hierarchy reflects the complexity of state learning. We benchmark the framework with some paradigmatic non-Gaussian states, such as NOON states and squeezed Kerr states, and analyze its robustness against loss. Moreover, we construct an experimentally economical NOON-type witness requiring only four projective measurements, where an analytical Gaussian-entanglable threshold is derived. These results establish an operationally meaningful and experimentally accessible framework for identifying non-Gaussian entanglement resources in continuous-variable quantum platforms.

</details>

### [Quantum randomness beyond projective measurements](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18291v1)

**Authors:** Fionnuala Curran  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18291v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

</details>

<sub>Figure 5: The space of average post-measurement states ⃗R for unbiased MICs in dimension two.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how much randomness can be extracted from non-projective quantum measurements. It proves that a new class of 'skewed SIC' measurements can achieve the theoretical maximum of randomness and identifies the SIC measurement as the least random among certain qubit measurement classes.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for quantum optics and quantum information as it provides fundamental bounds on randomness extraction and characterizes the security of measurement-based protocols against eavesdroppers.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Quantifying the amount of intrinsic quantum randomness generated by extremal measurements beyond standard projective measurements, specifically determining which measurements maximize or minimize the guessing probability of an eavesdropper.

**Main result.** The paper identifies that SIC measurements are the least random among unbiased qubit measurements and introduces 'skewed SIC' measurements that can generate the maximal $2 \log d$ bits of randomness in dimensions where SICs exist.

**Method.** The author uses semidefinite programming (SDP) dualities, geometric analysis of polytopes in the Bloch ball, and KKT optimality conditions to characterize guessing probabilities and randomness.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on finite-dimensional quantum systems (qubits, qutrits, and ququarts) using Positive Operator-Valued Measures (POVMs), specifically rank-one extremal and Symmetric Informationally Complete (SIC) measurements.

**Key observables.** Conditional min-entropy $H_{\min}(ho, M)$, optimal guessing probability $P_{	ext{guess}}$, and quantum fidelity $F(ho, \sigma)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimension $d$, scaling parameter $\gamma$ for skewed SICs, noise parameter $\epsilon$, and angular parameters $\delta$ and $\alpha$ for qubit/qutrit measurement families.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis primarily focuses on rank-one extremal POVMs and assumes a device-dependent or source-device-independent framework.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate geometric regions in the Bloch ball, the comparison between SIC and 'scissors' POVMs, and the space of average post-measurement states.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of intrinsic randomness, moves to characterizing unbiased extremal qubit measurements, introduces the skewed SIC family and its randomness-generating properties, and concludes with higher-dimensional constructions for qutrits and ququarts.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The unpredictability of quantum physics gives rise to intrinsic randomness. In an adversarial scenario, any additional degrees of freedom must be attributed to an eavesdropper with correlations to the measurement set-up. The true randomness is then quantified by the probability that she correctly guesses the measurement outcomes, optimised over all possible strategies. Extremal measurements are appealing here, since they do not allow information to leak to such an eavesdropper. Beyond projective measurements, however, a simple question remains open: how much intrinsic randomness can be generated by a given extremal measurement? In a step towards solving it, we characterise the randomness generated by any unbiased extremal rank-one measurement acting on any state, solving the problem explicitly in dimension two. Four-outcome qubit measurements of this type are tomographic, so these results hold for fully source-device-dependent randomness too. The tetrahedral symmetric informationally complete (SIC) measurement, we find, has the least intrinsic randomness within this class. We also present the skewed SIC family of measurements, and use them to partially solve an open problem: we prove that $2 \log d$ bits of randomness, the maximal amount, can be generated device-dependently (or source-device-independently) in any dimension in which there exists a SIC measurement.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how different annealing schedules affect the distribution of energy basins in D-Wave quantum annealers. It demonstrates that while schedules can redistribute probability between local minima, they do not provide a universal quantum advantage over classical parallel tempering, and it corrects previous claims regarding the effectiveness of multi-pause schedules.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the shape of reverse-anneal schedules in a quantum annealer controls the final sampling distribution of energy basins beyond simple temperature rescaling, and determining if these effects are genuine quantum signatures or reproducible by classical Monte Carlo processes.

**Main result.** The study finds that for certain instances, the QPU dynamics sit strictly between localized parallel tempering and delocalified path-integral annealing, and that schedule engineering can redistribute probability among basins but does not provide a universal quantum advantage, refuting previous claims of a 'two-pause enhancement'.

**Method.** The authors used a cycled reverse-anneal protocol on D-Wave Advantage2 and Advantage system6.4 hardware, comparing results against classical parallel tempering (PT) and path-integral simulated quantum annealing (SQA) using a bootstrap-based statistical framework.

**Model / system.** 12-qubit Ising instances with mixed frustration on random 3-regular logical graphs, implemented on D-Wave Advantage2 (Zephyr) and Advantage system6.4 (Pegasus) quantum annealers.

**Key observables.** Subsystem configuration autocorrelation C(k), memory order parameter M, endpoint distances to classical/quantum Gibbs targets (D_cl, D_q), and basin occupation/weight redistribution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Effective temperature (beta_eff approx 7.22 and 4.29), annealing schedules (single-pause, two-pause, quench-and-hold), and transverse-field scale (A approx 1.38 GHz).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the observed 'unmatched' signals are not artifacts of minor-embedding, which is supported by native-graph control experiments.

**Figures summary.** Figure 5(a) compares QPU autocorrelation against PT benchmarks across various schedules; Figure 6 shows native-graph control experiments on induced subgraphs of the Zephyr hardware graph.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of schedule-dependent dynamics, describes the hardware and Ising models used, details the reverse-anneal and classical comparison protocols, presents results on basin occupation and the 'bracketing' effect, provides a native-graph control to rule out embedding artifacts, and concludes by refuting previous claims of universal quantum enhancement via two-pause schedules.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

On a mixed-frustration 12-qubit Ising instance (seed 14029) run on two D-Wave generations, Advantage2 Zephyr and Advantage_system6.4 Pegasus, the late-time subsystem autocorrelation under cycled reverse annealing sits strictly between two equilibrium reference processes at the device-calibrated effective temperature: localized parallel tempering, and delocalized equilibrated path-integral simulated quantum annealing at a fixed Advantage2 pause-point transverse-field scale. The bracket holds on all three tested schedules and at both hardware calibrations. We obtain this result through two ingredients: a cycled reverse-anneal protocol (reinitialize_state=False, 50 cycles per submission) used as a Markov-chain probe of the device's pause-point dynamics, and a parallel-tempering falsification framework with bias-corrected and accelerated bootstrap 95% confidence intervals. Of eighteen tested (seed, schedule) combinations on Advantage2, three are PT-unmatched and correspond to two distinct Ising instances; an independent native-graph control with no minor embedding on a third mixed-frustration instance reproduces the same direction of mismatch. Among twenty random training instances, schedule shape modulates basin occupation on six of the thirteen multi-basin-in-readout instances, with dominant-configuration shifts of up to 38 percentage points including changes of the dominant configuration. A pre-registered linear predictor of schedule sensitivity from exhaustively computable landscape features fails on ten held-out instances, indicating that schedule sensitivity is not captured by simple linear functions of the tested landscape moments. The bracketing result revises an earlier two-pause-enhancement claim and reframes reverse-anneal schedules as instance-specific basin-occupation probes rather than universal quantum-enhancement knobs.

</details>

### [Cryogenic Time-Division-Multiplexed Voltage Control for Scalable Trapped-Ion Quantum Processors](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17824v1)

**Authors:** Ryutaro Ohira, Shinichi Morisaka, Yoshinori Kurimoto, Toshiaki Inada, Ippei Nakamura, Takefumi Miyoshi, Atsushi Noguchi  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17824v1>

A DAC at room temperature generates a TDM waveform at an up- date rate of Nf Hz, where N represents the number of trap electrodes. The waveform is sent to a demultiplexer (denoted as DEMUX) located at the 4-K stage, which distributes the signal to the individual electrodes. A holding capacitor con- nected to each demultiplexer output stores the voltage for the corresponding electrode via charging during the selected time slot. Consequently, each electrode is updated...</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17824_figures/2605.17824_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. TDM-based voltage control system for static elec- trodes. (a) Schematic of the system architecture. (b) Cryo- genic measurement setup with the vacuum chamber removed. (c) TDM signal generation board and DEMUX board. The TDM signal generation board, shown at the top, includes an FPGA, a DAC, operational amplifiers, and relay switches. The 32-channel DEMUX board, shown at the bottom, is in- stalled on the cryogenic stage.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17824_figures/2605.17824_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Measured DC output voltages of the 32-channel mul- tiplexed system at approximately 27 K.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17824_figures/2605.17824_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. TDM-based voltage control system for dynamic elec- trodes. (a) Schematic of system architecture. (b) Cryogenic measurement setup with the vacuum chamber removed. (c) TDM signal generation board and DEMUX board, connected by a flexible printed circuit (FPC) cable. (d) Measured out- put waveforms from the four-channel demultiplexed system at approximately 14 K.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a cryogenic voltage control system using time-division multiplexing to reduce the number of wires needed for large-scale trapped-ion quantum processors. By successfully operating at 14 K and 27 K, the authors show that both static and dynamic electrode requirements can be met with significantly fewer vacuum feedthroughs. This approach provides a scalable path for managing the massive electrode counts required by QCCD architectures.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a practical engineering solution to the scalability limits of trapped-ion processors, which is crucial for the realization of large-scale quantum computing architectures and complex many-body simulations in ion traps.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The scaling of trapped-ion quantum computers using the QCCD architecture is limited by a 'wiring bottleneck,' where the massive number of electrodes required leads to an unsustainable number of vacuum feedthroughs and room-temperature DAC channels.

**Main result.** The researchers successfully demonstrated a cryogenic Time-Division-Multiplexed (TDM) voltage control system that achieves both static and dynamic electrode control at 14 K and 27 K, significantly reducing the required physical connections.

**Method.** A TDM-based architecture was implemented using a room-temperature FPGA-driven DAC and a cryogenic demultiplexer (DEMUX) board, utilizing a holding capacitor at each output to retain voltage between update cycles.

**Model / system.** The experimental platform is a cryogenic ion-trap system (utilizing 40-K and 4-K stages) designed for the Quantum Charge-Coupled Device (QCCD) architecture, featuring both static electrodes for potential compensation and dynamic electrodes for ion shuttling.

**Key observables.** Effective voltage update rates, voltage drop (leakage) over time, crosstalk between channels, and output voltage ranges.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Update rates of 37.5 kHz (static) and 1 MHz (dynamic); voltage range of +/- 10 V; operating temperatures of 14 K and 27 K; crosstalk levels around -12 to -35 dB.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The RC time constant of the output nodes must allow for DAC output settling and capacitor charging within the allocated time slot.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the TDM system schematic and architecture for electrode classes; Figure 2 details the physical cryogenic setup and hardware components; Figure 3 displays measured DC output voltages for 32 channels; Figure 4 shows the dynamic electrode setup and measured sinusoidal waveforms.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the wiring bottleneck, describes the TDM architecture and hardware implementation, presents experimental validation for both static and dynamic electrode classes at cryogenic temperatures, and discusses scaling implications and hardware limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Trapped-ion quantum computers based on the quantum charge-coupled device architecture require on the order of ten trap electrodes per qubit, making the number of vacuum feedthroughs a bottleneck at the system scale. Time-division multiplexed (TDM)-based voltage control for trap electrodes provides a natural route to alleviate this constraint. However, previous studies have been limited to architectural proposals for static trap-potential compensation and room-temperature demonstrations of dynamic-electrode control, leaving cryogenic operation of TDM-based voltage control for static and dynamic electrodes experimentally unexplored. In this study, we develop and cryogenically validate TDM-based voltage control schemes for two distinct electrode classes. For static electrodes used in trap-potential compensation, we implement a 32-channel demultiplexed system operating at approximately 27~K, achieving an effective voltage update rate of 37.5~kHz with an output range of $\pm10~\mathrm{V}$ per channel. For dynamic electrodes used in ion operations, such as shuttling, we implement a four-channel demultiplexed system operating at approximately 14~K, achieving an effective voltage update rate of 1~MHz with a comparable output range. These results establish TDM-based voltage control as a practical approach for both electrode classes, providing a path for mitigating the vacuum feedthrough bottleneck in scalable trapped-ion quantum processors.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18002v1>

**Summary.** This paper provides an exact derivation of the density matrix for the Kitaev spin liquid, revealing how the entanglement entropy splits into distinct gauge and matter components. It establishes the microscopic link between spin and Majorana fermion representations and explains the high degeneracy in the entanglement spectrum through the lens of emergent gauge symmetries.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to derive an explicit, physically transparent form of the density matrix for the Kitaev spin liquid in the spin representation to understand the microscopic origin of its entanglement structure.

**Main result.** The authors derive an exact form of the density matrix and prove that the reduced density matrix is block-diagonal, leading to the separability of entanglement entropy into gauge and matter parts and explaining the extensive degeneracy in the entanglement spectrum.

**Method.** The study employs an algebraic approach using Majorana fermion representations, Gutzwiller projection, and the study of string operator equivalence classes, alongside symmetry analysis of the Gauss law and 1-form Wilson symmetry.

**Model / system.** The Kitaev honeycomb model, an exactly solvable quantum spin system featuring a spin-1/2 liquid ground state with fractionalized Majorana fermion excitations and an emergent Z2 gauge field.

**Key observables.** Reduced density matrix (RDM), entanglement entropy (EE), entanglement spectrum (ES), spin correlation functions, and fermion parity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Subregion size (even vs. odd number of lattice sites), boundary length, and the flux-free sector (fixed Z2 fluxes).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The subregion is assumed to be simply-connected, and the Z2 fluxes are assumed to be static (flux-free sector).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows equivalent edge configurations and Wilson loops; Figure 2 illustrates the linking of Majorana fermions via gauge-field strings; Figure 6 shows 't Hooft loop operators; Figure 8 shows the duality between triangular and honeycomb lattices.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by deriving the exact density matrix in the spin representation, moves to analyzing the block-diagonal structure of the RDM using gauge symmetries, extends the formalism to odd-sized subsystems, and concludes with a geometric derivation of the entanglement entropy components using lattice duality.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We give an exact form of the density matrix of the spin-1/2 Kitaev spin liquid represented in terms of spin operators and study the entanglement structures of the Kitaev honeycomb model within the spin framework. We show that the density matrix is naturally organized by equivalence classes of string operators associated with the underlying gauge structure of the model. With the explicit form of the density matrix, plus the exact Gauss law of the emergent gauge theory and the exact 1-form Wilson symmetry in the Kitaev model, we demonstrate the existence of the underlying symmetry-resolved block-diagonal structure of the reduced density matrix, which gives rise to the extensive degeneracy in the entanglement spectrum. The block-diagonal structure is then proven to be responsible for the separability of the entanglement entropy into the gauge and matter parts. Furthermore, we extend the formalism to subsystems with an odd number of lattice sites, revealing a relation between the entanglement spectrum and the fermion parity that is seldom mentioned in the literature.

</details>

**Authors:** Santanu Sarkar, George Biswas, Jun-Yi Wu, Anindya Biswas  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18391v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5**
