**Summary.** This paper evaluates the effectiveness of using Stabilizer Renyi Entropy as a local probe for quantum criticality. It demonstrates that while purity-corrected SRE is excellent for detecting certain transitions in frustrated models, the raw SRE may be more reliable in low-frustration regimes. The study provides a new way to use quantum magic as a signature of phase transitions.

**Why it may be interesting.** It explores how non-stabilizer resources (quantum magic) can act as a complementary diagnostic tool to traditional entanglement entropy for identifying phase transitions in frustrated many-body systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the Stabilizer Renyi Entropy (SRE) and its purity-corrected version can serve as effective local probes for detecting quantum phase transitions.

**Main result.** The purity-corrected SRE successfully detects the antiphase-floating transition in the high-frustration TANNNI model and the first-order transition in the Quantum Compass Model, while the raw SRE is more accurate for the low-frustration TANNNI regime.

**Method.** The authors use Exact Diagonalization for the TANNNI model and Jordan-Wigner/Bogoliubov transformations for the QCM, applying finite-size scaling to the derivatives of the SRE.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the 1D Transverse Axial Next-Nearest-Neighbor Ising (TANNNI) model and the 1D Quantum Compass Model (QCM).

**Key observables.** Second-order Stabilizer Renyi Entropy (SRE), purity-corrected SRE, and their first derivatives with respect to control parameters.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Transverse field strength (gamma), frustration ratio (J2/J1), and anisotropy ratio (Jx/Jz).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The purity-corrected SRE is used to mitigate the effects of mixedness in reduced density matrices, though it is noted not to be a true magic monotone for mixed states.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the TANNNI phase diagram; Figures 2-6 display SRE and its derivatives for various TANNNI regimes; Figure 7 compares extracted transition lines to established boundaries; Figure 13 shows SRE for the QCM.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the SRE as a probe, describes the TANNNI and QCM models, details the analytical and numerical methods (JW transformation, BdG, ED), presents results for high and low frustration regimes in TANNNI, analyzes the QCM transition, and concludes with a comparison of SRE types.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the effectiveness of the stabilizer Rényi entropy (SRE), a quantifier associated with non-stabilizer resources (quantum magic), as an indicator of quantum phase transitions. Specifically, we analyze the behavior of the purity-corrected SRE of reduced density matrices in the ground states of two one-dimensional spin models: the transverse axial next-nearest-neighbor Ising (TANNNI) model and the quantum compass model (QCM). The ground state of the TANNNI model is obtained using exact diagonalization techniques, while the QCM is analyzed using the Jordan--Wigner (JW) transformation followed by Bogoliubov diagonalization of the resulting quadratic fermionic Hamiltonian. For the TANNNI model, the purity-corrected SRE successfully detects the antiphase--floating phase transition in the high-frustration regime, while in the low-frustration regime the raw (purity-uncorrected) SRE reproduces the known ferromagnetic--paramagnetic phase boundaries more accurately. For the QCM, the purity-corrected SRE exhibits a clear signature near the isotropic point \(J_x/J_z=1\), where the system undergoes a first-order quantum phase transition. Our results establish SRE of reduced states as a complementary probe of quantum criticality and provide further insight into the role of non-stabilizer resources in many-body quantum phase transitions.

</details>

**Authors:** Luis Lozano  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17623v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5**

<img src="2605.17623_figures/2605.17623_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Formulation choice for D-Wave portfolio optimization. The constraint-native CQM path preserves sparsity, requires no penalty tuning, and achieves optimal solutions at the min- imum budget. </details>

**Summary.** This paper audits D-Wave's hybrid solver, revealing that the observed performance gains in portfolio optimization stem from a classical preprocessing pipeline rather than quantum sampling. It demonstrates that the constraint-native CQM interface is significantly more efficient than the BQM approach because it avoids unnecessary graph densification.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Auditing the actual quantum contribution of D-Wave's hybrid service in portfolio optimization and investigating the performance gap between constraint-native (CQM) and penalty-encoded (BQM) formulations.

**Main result.** The study finds that the reported hybrid advantage is primarily due to a classical decomposition pipeline rather than quantum sampling, as QPU access time accounts for only ~0.7% of the total runtime. Additionally, the CQM interface is superior because it preserves sparsity and avoids the 'density-axis collapse' caused by penalty encoding.

**Method.** A comparative performance audit using D-Wave hybrid solvers (LeapHybrid-CQM and LeapHybrid-BQM) against classical baselines (Gurobi MIQP and Simulated Annealing) on synthetic and real-world financial datasets.

**Model / system.** The problem is a cardinality-constrained mean-variance-turnover (MVT) portfolio optimization model, implemented on D-Wave Advantage systems using Pegasus and Zephyr topologies.

**Key observables.** QPU access time, relative gap to Gurobi optimality, chain-break fractions, embedding overhead, and solution determinism/variance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Problem size (N = 10 to 640), wall-clock budgets (5s to 300s), covariance density (diagonal, block-diagonal, dense), and penalty weight (A).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes a fixed penalty weight (A=4.0) for BQM based on prior literature and focuses on a specific class of portfolio optimization problems.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the mechanism of constraint-induced density increase; Figure 2 summarizes the advantages of CQM over BQM; Figure 6 and 7 show the divergence of BQM and SA from the optimal Gurobi baseline as problem size increases.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with an audit of QPU contribution, followed by a structural analysis of density-axis collapse in BQM, an evaluation of formulation superiority (CQM vs BQM), a study of budget saturation and determinism, and concludes with recommendations for benchmarking quantum-finance applications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We audit how much of D-Wave's hybrid quantum-classical portfolio-optimization service is actually quantum. On cardinality-constrained mean-variance-turnover instances spanning N equal to 10 to 640 with a Gurobi MIQP optimality anchor, the constraint-native LeapHybridCQM service matches Gurobi's proven optimum on all 54 instances where Gurobi proves optimality, but the mean QPU access time is only 0.034 seconds out of a 5-second wall-clock budget, roughly 0.7 percent of the run. The remaining roughly 99 percent is the service's classical decomposition, sub-problem assembly, and feasibility-aware reassembly, so the reported D-Wave hybrid win on this problem class is a constraint-native classical pipeline with a small QPU contribution rather than a quantum-sampling win. Two structural results sharpen this audit. First, the cardinality penalty contributes a dense rank-one term that makes the penalty-encoded logical graph fully connected regardless of the original covariance density, collapsing the intended density benchmark axis for all penalty-encoded paths while leaving the constraint-native sparsity intact. Second, the constraint-native service returns identical solutions at every tested wall-clock budget from 5 to 300 seconds and across 10 repeated calls, a determinism property of the service on this problem class. Together with two classical baselines, namely Gurobi MIQP and simulated annealing, and a comparison against the penalty-encoded hybrid interface, these results extend the prior constraint-native versus penalty-encoded observation of Sakuler et al. from the statement that the constraint-native interface handles constraints natively to the operational decomposition of where the win actually originates, a finding that reframes how D-Wave hybrid performance should be reported in quantum-finance benchmarks.

</details>

### [Positive-rate PCA and IPS with stationary Bernoulli measures are rapidly forgetful](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16904v1)

**Authors:** Irène Marcovici, Siamak Taati  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16904v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5** · `scars & prethermalization` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.16904_figures/2605.16904_page2.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

</details>

<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 4</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.16904_figures/2605.16904_page5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 5</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.16904_figures/2605.16904_page6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proves that stochastic lattice models like PCA and IPS converge exponentially fast to their stationary Bernoulli measures if the transition rules are strictly positive. It also demonstrates that in two or more dimensions, it is computationally impossible to distinguish between systems that have such a stationary measure and those that do not.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and open quantum systems, as it provides rigorous bounds on how local noise and information exchange drive a system toward equilibrium (forgetting initial conditions) and explores the complexity of characterizing steady states.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the ergodicity, convergence properties, and algorithmic complexity of Probabilistic Cellular Automata (PCA) and Interacting Particle Systems (IPS) that admit stationary Bernoulli measures.

**Main result.** The authors prove that positive-rate PCA and IPS admitting stationary Bernoulli measures are exponentially ergodic with logarithmic mixing times, and show that in dimensions d >= 2, these systems are algorithmically indistinguishable from those that do not admit such measures.

**Method.** An information-theoretic approach using relative entropy (Kullback-Leibler divergence) as a Lyapunov function, combined with noise decomposition and strong data processing inequalities.

**Model / system.** Discrete-time Probabilistic Cellular Automable (PCA) with synchronous updates and continuous-time Interacting Particle Systems (IPS) with asynchronous updates on a d-dimensional lattice.

**Key observables.** Relative entropy (Kullback-Leibler divergence), total variation distance, and mixing time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Positive transition rates (noise strength), lattice dimension d, and the diameter of the observed region n.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The systems must have strictly positive transition probabilities (positive-rate) and admit a stationary Bernoulli measure.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of ergodicity in PCA/IPS, develops a noise decomposition framework, proves entropy contraction for both synchronous and asynchronous updates, establishes exponential ergodicity via a bootstrap argument, and concludes with a proof of algorithmic undecidability in higher dimensions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We prove that every probabilistic cellular automaton with strictly positive transition probabilities that admits a stationary Bernoulli measure is exponentially ergodic. Moreover, the mixing time of any finite region in such a system is logarithmic in the diameter of the region. A similar result holds in continuous time for positive-rate, finite-range interacting particle systems. The proofs use entropy, and rely on a representation of the system as a perturbation of another system with noise. The ergodic behaviour results from a competition between the accumulation of randomness due to noise and the diffusion of randomness due to local information exchange. We show that, in two and higher dimensions, the positive-rate probabilistic cellular automata that admit stationary Bernoulli measures are algorithmically indistinguishable from those that do not.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper establishes a rigorous field-theoretic method to derive the effective motion of an interface from the underlying bulk dynamics. It corrects widespread errors in applying mean-field approximations to active matter and provides a systematic way to compute non-linear interface evolution.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of a controlled, systematic field-theoretical framework to derive interface dynamics from bulk dynamics, particularly for non-equilibrium and active systems where common mean-field approximations are mathematically unjustified.

**Main result.** The authors derive a unified framework that recovers known equilibrium results (like the Edwards-Wilkinson equation) and extends to non-equilibrium models, demonstrating that a popular mean-field ansatz is uncontrolled for active theories and can lead to incorrect predictions.

**Method.** The study uses the dynamical-action formalism (Janssen-De Dominicis action) and a geometric projection operator to integrate out bulk fluctuations, employing perturbation theory and spectral analysis of the linearized operator.

**Model / system.** The paper focuses on interfaces separating two stable phases in scalar field theories, specifically examining Model A (non-conserved) and Model B (conserved) dynamics, as well as active versions of these models.

**Key observables.** Interface height field h(r, t), interface relaxation rate (dispersion relation), drift velocity, and bulk fluctuation field chi.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (or noise amplitude) T, surface tension sigma, interface width xi, and wavevector q.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a low-temperature/weak-noise limit, a large-wavelength limit (q xi << 1), and focuses on weakly deformed interfaces.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a cartoon of a fluctuating interface with bulk deformations; Figure 2 shows relaxation dynamics; Figure 4 shows the exponential decay of Fourier amplitudes of excited modes.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by identifying the methodological gap and criticizing the mean-field ansatz, then introduces a new geometric definition for the interface, proceeds to derive the linear dynamics for Model A and Model B, and concludes by discussing non-linear terms and active matter applications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the dynamics of weakly deformed interfaces separating two stable phases, starting from the fluctuating hydrodynamics of the phase-separating fields. Using a well-chosen definition for the interface and the dynamical-action formalism to represent path probabilities, we derive the linear relaxation of the interface and the fluctuations around it for a large class of models. Our method applies to equilibrium dynamics, where it recovers and complements existing results, but also extends to their non-equilibrium counterparts. We explain how non-linear terms can be systematically computed and illustrate their derivations in the case of (active) model A. We highlight the danger of a popular ansatz used to derive interface dynamics, which was rigorously established in equilibrium but is uncontrolled for active field theories.

</details>

### [Path-Extrema Upper Bounds on Mean Entropy Production](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17074v1)

**Authors:** Surachate Limkumnerd  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17074v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

Panel (b) shows the cor- responding exponential process Mt = e−Σt, whose running minimum and maximum define the realized martingale enve- lope [LT , HT ]; the terminal value MT lies inside this interval. Panel (c) shows the local convexity geometry: for a fixed en- velope, the convex function −ln m is bounded above by the secant joining (LT , −ln LT ) and (HT , −ln HT ). This secant construction represents the conditional terminal mean by an effective endpoint mixture on the...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17074_figures/2605.17074_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

Each point represents a realized en- velope class in cost-gain coordinates (d, c), where moving ter- minal weight from H to L gives entropy gain c = ln(H/L) and consumes martingale cost d = H −L. Classes are ranked by ρ = c/d. The selected classes fill the martingale-cost budget in decreasing order of ρ, with a possible fractional threshold class at the boundary.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17074_figures/2605.17074_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

Panel (b) shows the two nonnegative gap terms, the allocation gap A and the curvature gap C. Panel (c) re- ports the numerical closure error in the decomposition. Simu- lations use k+ = 3, k−= 1, 2×105 trajectories per T, 80×80 envelope bins, and 20 independent batches. At T = 12, the raw estimate Araw = −5.74 × 10−4 is within two batch stan- dard errors of zero (SE = 1.24×10−3), so the plotted reported value is clipped to zero while the raw diagnostic remains in the summary table.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17074_figures/2605.17074_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Appendix-level diagnostic showing the empirical dis- tribution of realized envelope classes in the Markov-jump ex- ample. Each occupied bin is plotted in entropy-extrema co- ordinates (AT , BT ), with color indicating the efficiency ratio ρ and marker area indicating empirical weight. Open circles mark bins selected by the knapsack sorting, and the red dia- mond marks the threshold bin. This diagnostic supports the interpretation of Fig. 2 and shows that the upper-envelope estimate is built from occupied envelope bins rather than a formal continuum.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper establishes a theoretical framework for upper-bounding mean entropy production using the running extrema of the entropy-production martingale. It introduces a novel decomposition that quantifies how dynamical processes deviate from the theoretical maximum through allocation and curvature gaps. This provides a complementary tool to the standard fluctuation relations used to find lower bounds on dissipation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a new way to constrain dissipation and quantify the thermodynamic cost of fluctuations using path-level information.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to determine the upper bounds on mean entropy production in stochastic thermodynamic processes using information from path extrema, complementing the well-known lower bounds provided by fluctuation relations.

**Main result.** The author derives a path-extrema upper envelope (U_ext) and provides an exact identity decomposing the gap between the actual mean entropy production and this bound into an allocation gap (A) and a curvature gap (C).

**Method.** The study uses martingale stochastic thermodynamics, framing the derivation of the upper envelope as a continuous knapsack optimization problem and employing convexity/secant-line arguments.

**Model / system.** The framework applies to steady-state stochastic processes where the exponential entropy production is a positive martingale, specifically including stationary Markov jump processes and diffusion processes.

**Key observables.** Total stochastic entropy production (Sigma_T), running extrema of the martingale (L_T, H_T), and the efficiency ratio (rho_T).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The martingale-cost budget (K), the efficiency ratio (rho), and the entropy-production excursions (A_T, B_T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The results assume the process is a positive martingale (M_t = e^-Sigma_t) and that the integral fluctuation theorem holds.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 illustrates the continuous knapsack problem via cost vs. gain plots; Figure 3 shows the empirical decomposition of the entropy production gap into A and C components; Figure 4 displays the distribution of envelope classes in a Markov-jump process.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by demonstrating the insufficiency of one-sided bounds, moves to deriving deterministic two-sided bounds, introduces the random path-extrema upper envelope via a knapsack optimization, and concludes with a gap decomposition verified by numerical simulations of Markov jump processes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Fluctuation relations imply the second-law inequality $\langleΣ_T\rangle\ge0$, but path extrema can also constrain how large the mean entropy production can be. For steady-state processes with entropy-production martingale $M_t=e^{-Σ_t}$, we show that knowing only the positive running maximum of $Σ_t$ gives no improvement over the trivial endpoint bound: rare negative entropy-production excursions can still carry the exponential weight required by the fluctuation relation. Using the running extrema $L_T=\inf M_t$ and $H_T=\sup M_t$, we derive a path-extrema upper envelope $\mathcal{U}_{\rm ext}$. The relaxed envelope problem ranks realized intervals by the entropy gain per martingale cost, $\ln(H_T/L_T)/(H_T-L_T)$, giving a continuous knapsack problem. The actual mean satisfies the exact identity $\langleΣ_T\rangle=\mathcal{U}_{\rm ext}-\mathcal{A}-\mathcal{C}$, where $\mathcal{A}$ is an allocation gap across realized envelopes and $\mathcal{C}$ is a curvature gap within each envelope. Thus path extrema set the upper envelope, while the two gaps quantify how actual dynamics allocate terminal outcomes across envelope classes and place terminal values within each realized envelope. This turns path-extrema information into a quantitative upper-bound theory for entropy production, complementary to the usual lower-bound role of fluctuation relations.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.17621"></a>
### [Variational Boundary Fluctuations as a First-Principles Origin of Langevin Noise](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17621v1)

**Authors:** Francisco Monroy  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17621v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17621_figures/2605.17621_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Variational origin of boundary-induced tra- jectory fluctuations. (A) With fixed endpoints, the admis- sible variational set A selects a reference extremal trajectory between states A and B, satisfying δA = 0. (B) Environmen- tal uncertainty in preparation and termination makes end- point constraints fluctuate, shown by red halos ηi, ηf. This selects neighbouring admissible shells, schematically repre- sented by e A, and generates a family of nearby extremals, shown as dashed trajectories. The bulk dynamics remains deterministic for each realization: endpoint fluctuations in- duce a boundary variation of Hamilton’s principal function, which is then propagated by the Hamilton–Jacobi...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes that Langevin noise is not an external input but emerges from fluctuations in the initial and final boundary conditions of a system's action. By using Hamilton-Jacobi theory, the author shows how these boundary uncertainties propagate to create a state-dependent effective force. This provides a first-principles link between deterministic variational mechanics and stochastic dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** This provides a fundamental derivation of stochasticity from deterministic variational principles, offering a new way to view the emergence of open quantum system dynamics and noise from boundary-driven uncertainty.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to identify a first-principles, variational origin for Langevin noise, rather than treating it as a phenomenological or externally imposed stochastic force.

**Main result.** The author demonstrates that stochastic forces can emerge from fluctuations in Hamilton's principle boundary/endpoint data, resulting in a state-dependent, multiplicative noise that recovers standard additive Langevin noise only in the Markovian coarse-grained limit.

**Method.** A variational framework using the first variation of the Lagrangian action and Hamilton-Jacobi propagation to transport boundary-induced perturbations through the bulk dynamics.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on mechanical Hamiltonians of the form H(q, p) = p^2/2m + V(q), specifically using a harmonic oscillator as a benchmark, and discusses extensions to field theories.

**Key observables.** The effective Langevin force xi(t), which is found to be multiplicative, anisotropic, and state-dependent.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The Hessian of Hamilton's principal function (acting as a geometric filter), the overdamped limit, and the Markovian coarse-grained limit.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes a single-valued Hamilton-Jacobi branch and smooth boundary fluctuations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the distinction between fixed endpoints (a single reference extremal) and fluctuating endpoints (a family of nearby extremals due to environmental uncertainty).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of noise origins, develops a variational framework based on Hamilton's principle, uses Hamilton-Jacobi theory to derive the effective force, benchmarks the theory with a harmonic oscillator, and discusses limits and field-theoretic extensions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Stochastic forces are usually postulated or obtained by eliminating environmental degrees of freedom. Here we identify a variational origin: fluctuating endpoint data in Hamilton's principle induce fluctuations of the on-shell action. Hamilton--Jacobi propagation transports this boundary imprint, whose gradient generates an effective Langevin force inherited from boundary-action fluctuations. The resulting force is not freely specifiable: its amplitude is filtered by the Hessian of Hamilton's principal function, yielding multiplicative and state-dependent noise. Homogeneous additive Langevin forcing is recovered only as a Markovian coarse-grained limit.

</details>

**Authors:** Ankan Banerjee, Manuel Santos Gutierrez, John Moroney, Valerio Lucarini  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16574v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

Filled green cir- cles with errorbars (indicating the 95% confidence interval), are the estimation of mean exit times, as computed from en- sembles. Dashed black lines are linear fit of these estimators. Shaded regions denote 95% confidence interval of the fit. Solid orange lines are theoretical estimation of exit times - half of mean transition times. Inverse of the modulus of second eigen value of the Koopman operator estimated by EDMD (solid red triangles), inverse of the second eigenvalue of the Markov matrix computed using the Ulam’s method (hollow inverted blue triangles) are also...</sub>

</details>

<sub>FIG. 5. Crossing points (blue and red crosses) for different noise strengths α and rotation rate µ are plotted together with contours of φ2. The blue circle, the red circle, and the green hollow star represent two attractors and the saddle point, re- spectively. Red and blue colour shadings correspond to dif- ferent level sets. The green line is the 0-line, which defines the basin boundary.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a data-driven approach using Koopman operator theory to identify metastable states and quantify transition rates in stochastic systems. It demonstrates that the subdominant Koopman mode can reconstruct basin boundaries and that its decay rate accurately predicts escape times. The method is robust for both equilibrium and non-equilibrium dynamics, even in the presence of rotational forces.

**Why it may be interesting.** The method provides a way to characterize transitions and metastable states in high-dimensional, non-equilibrium systems using only data, which is highly relevant for studying dissipation and decoherence in open quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a purely data-driven, geometry-agnostic method to study noise-induced transitions and metastability in complex stochastic systems where governing equations are unknown.

**Main result.** The Koopman operator framework accurately reconstructs basin boundaries and predicts escape time statistics, showing strong agreement with Large Deviation Theory in both equilibrium and non-equilibrium regimes.

**Method.** The authors use Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) to approximate the Koopman and Perron-Frobenius operators from time-series data, validated against Ulam's method and analytical Large Deviation Theory.

**Model / system.** A 2D stochastic bistable system (Rotating Double Well Potential) described by an Ito diffusion process, featuring two attractors, a saddle point, and a rotational component that drives the system out of equilibrium.

**Key observables.** Mean exit time, subdominant Koopman modes (for basin reconstruction), Koopman eigenvalues (decay rates), and the 0-level set of the subdominant mode.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Noise intensity (alpha), rotation strength (mu), and the weak-noise limit (alpha -> 0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The noise is assumed to be non-degenerate (invertible covariance), and the dictionary of basis functions must sufficiently span the relevant subspace.

**Figures summary.** Plots of Koopman eigenvalues and spectral gaps; heat maps of Koopman modes overlaid with true basin boundaries; time-series visualizations of mode-based transition identification; and eigenfunctions of the Fokker-Planck operator.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of noise-induced tipping, presents the Koopman-based data-driven framework, applies it to a 2D rotating bistable model, validates results against Large Deviation Theory, and explores the role of saddle dynamics and rotation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the metastability properties of a simple prototypical bistable system using the formalism of the Koopman operator. Instead of studying noise-induced transitions by following the trajectories of the system, we track them by studying the time evolution and the decay rate of the subdominant mode of the Koopman operator, thus in a geometry-agnostic framework. We find agreement with the predictions - both the exponential and subexponential ones - of large deviation theory in the weak-noise limit for the statistics of escape time, both in equilibrium and nonequilibrium conditions. The subdominant Koopman mode also allows for an accurate reconstruction of the competing basins of attraction. Going deeper in the Koopman spectrum, we are able to recognise modes that are associated with intrawell variability as well as with the escape of trajectories from the saddle towards the attractor, both in the equilibrium and nonequilibrium case. Our methodology, being grounded in purely data-driven techniques, could be helpful for studying high-dimensional metastable systems.

</details>

### [Dynamically Enabled Robustness of Geometric Phases and Entanglement in the Nonlinear Jaynes-Cummings Model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17016v1)

**Authors:** Ali Martin Zynda, Paula I. Villar, Fernando C. Lombardo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17016v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

Finally, in blue, is the eigenvector |ψ+(t)⟩which accumulates GP in the later case. Here, |0⟩≡|e0⟩and |1⟩≡|g1⟩.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that in nonlinear light-matter systems, protecting quantum features like geometric phases requires more than just resonance; it requires the dissipation to align with the system's unitary rotation. By showing that environmental action can reshape state-space geometry, the study offers new principles for engineering robust evolution in noisy quantum technologies.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a new geometric principle for engineering decoherence-resilient quantum states by focusing on the alignment of coherent and dissipative trajectories, which is highly relevant for designing stable open quantum platforms.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether standard conditions like resonance or geodesic evolution are sufficient to protect geometric phases and entanglement from environmental decoherence in nonlinear light-matter systems.

**Main result.** The authors find that resonance and geodesic evolution are necessary but not sufficient for robustness; true protection requires a 'dynamically enabled' alignment where dissipation preserves the plane of the unitary trajectory.

**Method.** Analytical diagonalization of the Hamiltonian in the low-excitation regime combined with numerical analysis of Lindblad master equation dynamics.

**Model / system.** A nonlinear Jaynes-Cummings model featuring Kerr-type nonlinearity, describing a two-level atom coupled to a quantized bosonic mode subject to cavity loss, atomic relaxation, and dephasing.

**Key observables.** Geometric phase (GP), Negativity (entanglement), and population dynamics (Rabi oscillations).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Detuning (Delta), Kerr nonlinearity (chi), coupling strength (g), and dissipation rates (gamma, Gamma, Gamma_z).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Zero-temperature environment, validity of the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA), and restriction to the low-excitation regime.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares Negativity evolution in closed vs. open systems; Figure 2 shows Negativity for various nonlinearity values; Figure 3 quantifies GP sensitivity to initial conditions; Figure 4 shows GP deviation vs. detuning; Figure 5 illustrates the alignment of trajectories on the Bloch sphere for on-resonance vs. off-resonance cases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the nonlinear JCM and dissipation model, defines the geometric phase and entanglement metrics, analyzes the necessity of geodesic paths, demonstrates the insufficiency of resonance alone, and concludes with a general geometric criterion for robustness.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Robustness in dissipative light-matter systems has recently been associated with resonance conditions or geodesic evolution. We show that, in the nonlinear Jaynes-Cummings model, these conditions are necessary but not sufficient. Using a Kerr-type extension together with a Lindblad description of cavity losses and atomic decoherence, we identify a dynamically enabled mechanism in which the stability of geometric phases and entanglement is governed by the alignment between coherent and dissipative trajectories in Hilbert space. Our results reveal that environmental action does not merely suppress quantum features, but reshapes the geometry of state-space evolution: protection emerges only when dissipation preserves the structure of the underlying unitary dynamics. This establishes a general geometric criterion for decoherence resilience in nonlinear light-matter systems and provides guiding principles for engineering protected evolution in open quantum platforms.

</details>

### [A geometric Fano--Procrustes framework for purification-based distances and quantum channels analysis](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18485v1)

**Authors:** Tristán M. Osán  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18485v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<sub>FIG. 2. Purification misalignment angle Θ(ρ, ΦBF p (ρ)) as a function of the bit-flip probability p, for the same configu- rations as in Fig. 1. The angle captures the geometric re- alignment of the optimal purification frames induced by the anisotropic channel action.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.18485_figures/2605.18485_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. The metric DN(ρ, ΦPF p (ρ)) as a function of the phase- flip probability p, for fixed Bloch angles and different values of the Bloch-vector length.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new geometric framework to analyze quantum state distinguishability by looking beyond scalar fidelity. By reducing purification optimization to a Procrustes problem, it identifies a misalignment angle that captures the geometric reorientation of quantum channels. This allows for a more detailed distinction between purely dissipative and anisotropic noise processes.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a new geometric diagnostic (Theta) for characterizing the anisotropic and non-radial components of quantum noise, which is highly relevant for characterizing open quantum systems and control errors.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the limitation of existing fidelity-based distance measures that provide only a scalar value for distinguishability, discarding the underlying geometric structure of optimal purifications.

**Main result.** The authors develop a Fano-Procrustes framework that reduces purification-overlap optimization to an orthogonal Procrustes problem on SO(3), introducing a new geometric misalignment angle (Theta) that complements scalar metrics.

**Method.** The work uses the Fano representation of two-qubit pure states and applies Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to solve the orthogonal Procrustes problem on the Lie group SO(3).

**Model / system.** The framework is applied to mixed qubit states and various quantum channels, including depolarizing, bit-flip, phase-flip, and amplitude-damping channels, as well as an imperfect quantum NOT gate.

**Key observables.** Maximal purification overlap (g*), entropic metric (DN), purification misalignment angle (Theta), and the optimal Procrustes rotation (S*).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Channel error probabilities (p), damping probability (gamma), and Bloch vector length (r).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The current framework is specifically developed and restricted to the qubit (two-level) case.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the behavior of the DN metric and the misalignment angle Theta as functions of error probabilities and input state orientations for various noise channels.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of purification geometry, develops the Fano-Procrustes mathematical framework, applies it to specific qubit channels to demonstrate the separation of radial and anisotropic effects, and concludes with discussions on the operational interpretation and limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work we reformulate the Uhlmann purification-overlap optimization and develop a purification-based geometric framework for the analysis of mixed qubit states and qubit channels. Using the Fano representation of two-qubit pure states, a purification is described in terms of the Bloch vector of the system, the ancilla Bloch vector, and a real correlation matrix. For a fixed one-qubit mixed state, the freedom in the choice of purification can be parametrized by proper rotations acting on the ancillary degrees of freedom. As a result, the optimization over purifications entering the definition of the metric \(D_N\) introduced in Ref.~\cite{Lamberti2009} is reduced to an orthogonal Procrustes problem on the Lie group \(SO(3)\). This reduction yields not only the maximal purification overlap, but also the optimal rotation relating the purification frames. From this rotation we define a purification misalignment angle \(Θ\), which provides geometric information not contained in scalar fidelity-based distinguishability measures.   The formalism is applied to representative qubit channels, including depolarizing, bit-flip, phase-flip, amplitude-damping channels, and an imperfect quantum NOT gate. For symmetry-adapted evolutions preserving the Bloch-vector direction, the optimal rotation is trivial and \(Θ=0\), whereas noncollinear channel actions generate a nonzero misalignment. The pair \((D_N,Θ)\) therefore separates the magnitude of the maximal purification overlap from the geometric reorientation of the optimal purification frames. Since the optimal Procrustes rotation can be lifted to a local unitary acting on the ancilla, the construction also provides an operational interpretation of the optimal purification in terms of an ancilla-side transformation.

</details>

**Authors:** Ronnie Slowinski, Gaël Servignat, Frédéric Chevy, Carlos Lobo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18405v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The vacuum two-body T-matrix (T2) enters the dimer propagator since d is a composite particle.</sub>

The upper bound corresponds to the point dimer limit and the lower bound corresponds to the unitary dimer limit, with the shaded gray areas indicating the regions where trimers cannot form for any combination of a2 and aAD. The remaining results have been found by numerically solving Eq. (19) with effective range fixed so R = 0.1. We find that by varying 1/a2 the trimer so- lutions interpolate smoothly between these two limits.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper uses effective field theory to study the transition between dimer and trimer states in a three-component Fermi gas. It reveals that external driving can control the crossover between these states and that many-body effects can create trimer states that do not exist in a vacuum. This work is significant for understanding the stability and manipulation of complex clusters in ultracold gases.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a theoretical framework for controlling many-body bound states using external drives, offering new insights into how the transition between different types of many-body clusters (like polarons and molecules) can be manipulated in ultracold atomic systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the crossover between dimer and trimer states in a three-component Fermi gas, specifically how external drives and many-body effects influence the stability and binding energies of these states.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates a guaranteed crossover from a dimer to a trimer ground state as the atom-dimer interaction is tuned, and shows that many-body effects can induce trimer solutions (Cooper pairs) even in regimes where they are absent in vacuum.

**Method.** The authors use an Effective Field Theory (EFT) and a variational approach, employing Feynman rules, the T-matrix formalism, and renormalization to solve self-consistent energy equations.

**Model / system.** A three-component Fermi gas consisting of three distinguishable atomic species (a, b, and c). Species a and b interact via an external drive to form a non-universal closed-channel dimer, which then interacts with a Fermi sea of the third species (c) via a contact interaction.

**Key observables.** Two-body and three-body binding energies, atom-dimer scattering length, and the energy difference between the dimer and trimer branches.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Atom-dimer scattering length (1/a_AD), two-body scattering length (a_2), effective range (R), and Fermi momentum (k_F).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The dimer is assumed to be non-universal, and the third species is treated as a spinless fermion forming an unperturbed Fermi sea.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows Feynman rules for the effective Hamiltonian; Fig 2 shows Feynman diagrams for two-body interactions; Fig 4 shows trimer energy bounds; Fig 9 illustrates the energy difference between trimer and dimer branches across different interaction strengths.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the EFT framework and Hamiltonian, develops the two-body sector (vacuum and medium), extends the theory to the three-body sector (trimer binding energies), analyzes the dimer-trimer crossover, and discusses the implications of external driving.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop an Effective Field Theory (EFT) for a system with three distinguishable atomic species and present a variational calculation of the two and three-body binding energies in vacuum and in the presence of a single Fermi sea. Specifically, we consider the case where the interaction between first two atomic species is externally driven so as to produce a (non-universal) closed-channel dimer whose coupling can be controlled independently of all other interactions. We then model the remaining interactions as a contact interaction between the dimer and a third atomic species which forms the medium. We derive analytical expressions for the dimer and trimer binding energies in vacuum and in medium, and in the latter case we predict a crossing between the dimer and trimer branches as a function of the atom-dimer scattering length, analogous to the usual polaron and molecule problem. Furthermore, we show that the position of this crossing can be controlled by varying the atom-atom coupling that results from the external drive and we discuss the implications of these findings.

</details>

### [Global resetting and emergent correlations: exit statistics in an interval](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17906v1)

**Authors:** Paul C Bressloff  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17906v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

At a random sequence of times Tn the positions of both particles are reset to the origin. The inter-event times τn = Tn+1 −Tn are exponentially distributed. (b) Pair of Brownian particles in the interval [0, L] with an open boundary at x = 0 and a stochastically gated boundary at x = L. The latter switches between an open state (blue) and a closed state (red) according to a two-state Markov chain. Also shown is the exit of one particle through the open gate.</sub>

We take x0 = (x, x) and xr = (xr, xr) with (a) x = 0.1, xr = 0.9 and (b) x = 0.9, xr = 0.1. The solid curves correspond to π(2) loc, whereas the discrete points sample π(2). We set L = 1 and truncate the double summation of the Green’s function in (4.3) by taking 1 ≤n, m ≤N with N = 1000.</sub>

The solid curve corresponds to π(2) loc, whereas the discrete points sample π(2). We also set L = 1, r = 100 and N = 1000. The inset shows π(2) for N = 1000 (solid red circle) and N = 1100 (indicated by x) and π(2) loc (solid black circle) for x = 0.5.</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.17906_figures/2605.17906_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

(b) Local gating: each particle independently switches between two conﬁgurations, only one of which can exit the boundary at x = L. (2D representation is for illustrative purposes.)</sub>

</details>

In addition, a 50,000-dimensional version of the inﬁnite-dimensional matrix equation (A.30) is solved to estimate the Fourier coeﬃcients. Other parameters are L = 1 and ξ = p</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores how a shared stochastic resetting protocol induces correlations between independent Brownian particles. It develops a mathematical framework using nested boundary value problems to show that these particles no longer behave independently. The study establishes a direct link between multi-particle exit statistics and the moments of a stochastic diffusion equation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a mathematical framework for understanding how shared environmental noise (common-mode noise) generates entanglement-like statistical correlations in otherwise independent constituents.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how global stochastic resetting induces statistical correlations between non-interacting Brownian particles and determining the resulting joint exit (splitting) probabilities.

**Main result.** The paper proves that global resetting creates emergent correlations such that the joint splitting probability cannot be decomposed into independent single-particle probabilities, and establishes a mapping between these probabilities and the moments of a stochastic diffusion equation.

**Method.** The author uses generalized Ito's lemma, backward Kolmogorov equations, and Green's function methods to derive a hierarchy of nested boundary value problems (BVPs).

**Model / system.** A population of M non-interacting Brownian particles in a 1D interval [0, L] with absorbing boundaries, subject to a global Poissonian resetting process to a common position.

**Key observables.** Splitting probability (probability of exiting through a specific boundary), joint splitting probability, and survival probability.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Resetting rate (r), diffusion coefficient (D), interval length (L), and the characteristic scale xi = sqrt(r/D).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Particles are non-interacting (no physical collisions); resetting is global (all particles reset simultaneously); boundaries are strictly absorbing.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the global resetting mechanism; Figure 2 plots joint splitting probability vs. reset rate; Figure 4 compares global vs. local gating; Figure 5 compares joint splitting probabilities for global and local gating scenarios.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of emergent correlations, derives the BVP hierarchy using Ito's lemma, provides an analytical solution for the two-particle case, establishes a mapping to SPDE moment equations, and concludes with a comparison between global and local gating dynamics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

There is considerable current interest in the emergence of statistical correlations within a population of otherwise non-interacting Brownian particles subject to a common fluctuating environment or drive. Examples include global stochastic resetting, switching confining potentials, fluctuating diffusivities, and stochastically gated boundaries. Most studies have focused on the analytical structure of the stationary joint probability density (assuming it exists). In this paper, we extend previous work on the exit statistics of multiple particles in stochastically gated domains to the case of global resetting in an interval with absorbing boundaries at both ends. First, we use a generalised Itô's lemma to derive a hierarchy of boundary value problems (BVPs) for the joint splitting probability that all particles exit from the same end of the interval. The BVPs form a nested sequence with respect to the initial number of particles $M$. We explicitly solve the BVP for a pair of particles ($M=2$) and use this to illustrate the emergence of pairwise correlations. Second, we show how the BVP for the splitting probability of $M$ Brownian particles can be mapped onto the $M$th order moment equation of a stochastic diffusion equation with resetting. We thus establish a general mathematical framework to study exit problems for globally-driven particle systems.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.18196"></a>
### [Localization of a quantum particle in a classical one-component plasma. II. Dynamic Disorder and Temporal Decorrelation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18196v1)

**Authors:** Yury A. Budkov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18196v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<img src="2605.18196_figures/2605.18196_page6.jpg" width="500"><br>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how the movement of ions in a plasma affects the localization of a quantum particle. It demonstrates that while fast particles behave according to static theory, ultra-slow particles experience a suppression of disorder that prevents exponential localization.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental look at how environmental dynamics (temporal decorrelation) can destroy localization, which is highly relevant to understanding decoherence and transport in many-body open quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Extending the theory of Anderson localization from a static random potential to a dynamic, time-dependent potential caused by ionic density fluctuations in a plasma.

**Main result.** The study finds that for slow particles, temporal decorrelation suppresses the Coulomb logarithm and leads to a scaling of the localization length where $\ell \propto k^{-1/3}$, implying that ultra-slow particles are not exponentially localized.

**Method.** The author uses a path-integral formalism combined with the eikonal approximation, the Random Phase Approximation (RPA), and the fluctuation-dissipation theorem to derive the decay of the Green function.

**Model / system.** A non-relativistic quantum particle with charge $q_0$ and mass $m$ moving in a classical one-component plasma (OCPS) of mobile ions, where the potential is a Gaussian random field generated by ionic density fluctuations.

**Key observables.** Averaged retarded Green function, localization length ($\ell$), decay rate ($\Gamma$), and effective dynamic disorder strength ($G_{dyn}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Particle velocity ($v$) vs. ion thermal speed ($v_{th}$), Debye screening length ($\kappa^{-1}$), and ion plasma frequency ($\omega_{pi}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The eikonal (straight-line) approximation is used, neglecting quantum fluctuations of the path, and the plasma is treated within the Random Phase Approximation (RPA).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the dynamic problem, establishes the mathematical framework using path integrals and RPA, analyzes the two velocity regimes (fast vs. slow), and discusses the physical implications and scaling laws.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We extend the static theory of disorder-induced exponential decay of the averaged Green function of a quantum charged particle in a classical one-component plasma to the dynamic regime by incorporating the temporal evolution of the ionic density fluctuations within the random phase approximation. The dynamic potential correlator is derived from the fluctuation-dissipation theorem and the Kramers--Kronig relations. Within the eikonal (straight-line) approximation, the effective disorder strength is expressed through the dielectric function of the ion plasma. For particles moving faster than the ion thermal speed, the static Coulomb logarithm is recovered, with the large-distance cutoff replaced by the dynamic scale $v/ω_{pi}$. For slow particles, the Coulomb logarithm disappears completely and the disorder strength becomes proportional to the velocity, leading to a fundamentally different scaling of the localization length. In particular, the strong-disorder length diverges as $k^{-1/3}$ for $v\ll v_{\mathrm{th}}$, whereas it saturates in the static limit, indicating that ultra-slow particles are not exponentially localized in a dynamic plasma. A crossover between the quasi-static and dynamic regimes occurs when the particle speed becomes comparable to the ion thermal speed.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.16662"></a>
### [Euler-Maruyama method for non-Wiener processes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16662v1)

**Authors:** Richard D. J. G. Ho  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16662v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.16662_figures/2605.16662_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.16662_figures/2605.16662_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a generalized Euler-Maruyama method for simulating stochastic differential equations driven by non-Wiener, non-Gaussian processes. By using increments from Lévy processes like the Gamma distribution, the method more accurately captures the physical reality of systems with skewed fluctuations. It demonstrates that this approach avoids the inaccuracies introduced by standard Gaussian approximations in modeling decay and diffusion.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics who need to simulate non-equilibrium processes where environmental noise is non-Gaussian or heavy-tailed.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the limitations of the standard Euler-Maruyama method when modeling complex systems that exhibit non-Gaussian (non-Wiener) fluctuations.

**Main result.** The author develops a 'relaxed' Euler-Maruyama method using Lévy processes (such as Gamma processes) that accurately captures the tails and skewness of non-Gaussian noise, providing more physically accurate results for decay processes than standard Brownian motion.

**Method.** A generalized Euler-Maruyama discretization scheme where increments are drawn from non-Gaussian, infinitely divisible distributions, validated via Kramers-Moyal expansion and comparison with the Central Limit Theorem approach.

**Model / system.** The method is applied to stochastic differential equations (SDEs) featuring both additive and multiplicative noise, specifically modeling noisy exponential decay and particle decay processes.

**Key observables.** Probability density functions (PDFs), mean lifetime, variance, skewness, and kurtosis.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Simulation time step (delta t), decay rates (nu and lambda), and noise variance (sigma^2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the noise follows a subset of Lévy processes that are infinitely divisible; numerical implementation of the Master equation requires truncation of the Kramers-Moyal expansion.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares PDFs of different processes across methods; Figure 2 shows decay trajectories and the distribution of mean lifetimes; Figure 3 compares the Master equation solution against the exact EM method solution.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the mathematical requirements for a relaxed EM method, moves to testing the method with additive noise models, and concludes with applications to multiplicative noise and validation via the Kramers-Moyal expansion.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Descriptions of complex physical or biological systems often include stochastic contributions, and these are commonly simulated using Wiener processes. In many cases however, non-Gaussian fluctuations may originate from non-Wiener processes which remain less explored. The Euler-Maruyama method of discretising stochastic differential equations to non-Wiener processes is generalised. Non-Gaussian noise generated from a subset of Lévy processes can be used simply and often with more physical justification, for both additive and multiplicative noise. An example of this is provided that gives superior physical results compared to using geometric Brownian motion. Finally the results of the additive noise are shown to be equivalent to a derived master equation via the Kramers-Moyal expansion.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

The black dashed line indicates the low-energy predic- tion −ℏ2/(mIa2 eff). Energies are in units of Fermi energy EF and temperatures in units of the Fermi temperature TF.</sub>

In panels (e)–(h), the left vertical axis denotes the calculated nafter/ninitial, while the right vertical axis denotes the experimentally measured loss signal from Ref. [49]. The blue solid lines are obtained from the high- temperature virial expansion with local density approximation (LDA), and the black dotted lines show the corresponding results without LDA. Red symbols represent the experimental data. Panels (a) and...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes that temperature can be used to tune single-channel resonances in heteronuclear quantum gas mixtures by reshaping the effective interaction between impurities. This 'Temperature-Controlled Resonance' mechanism explains observed temperature-dependent atom loss in Bose-Fermi mixtures. The study demonstrates that temperature serves as a simple control knob for manipulating effective scattering properties.

**Why it may be interesting.** It introduces a novel, experimentally accessible way to tune interactions in ultracold gases using temperature rather than magnetic fields, which has implications for controlling many-body dynamics and impurity physics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty in systematically controlling single-channel resonances in atomic collisions, which are usually incidental and lack a tuning mechanism like Feshbach resonances.

**Main result.** The discovery of a Temperature-Controlled Resonance (TCR) where thermal smearing of the Fermi surface reshapes the effective potential, allowing temperature to act as a control knob for resonances.

**Method.** The authors use a grand potential approach to calculate Casimir-like mediated interactions, the variable-phase equation for scattering theory, and a high-temperature virial expansion to study quench dynamics.

**Model / system.** A heteronuclear quantum gas mixture consisting of heavy impurities (e.g., 133Cs) immersed in a light, single-component Fermi gas (e.g., 6Li), modeled using the Born-Oppenheimer approximation and a contact pseudopotical.

**Key observables.** Effective scattering length (a_eff), impurity bound-state energy (E_imp,b), and momentum distribution depletion (delta n_k) following an interaction quench.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (T), Fermi temperature (T_F), interspecies scattering length (a_IF), mass ratio (eta), and the short-range cutoff (R0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The impurities are assumed to be dilute and static, the Born-Oppenheimer approximation is applied, and the interaction is modeled as a contact s-wave potential.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the temperature-dependent Casimir-like interaction; Figure 2 plots the effective scattering length as a function of interaction strength and temperature, highlighting resonance boundaries.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of resonance control, proposes the TCR mechanism via thermal modification of the Fermi sea, provides a theoretical derivation of the effective potential, validates the mechanism against experimental loss data, and explores the dynamical consequences via interaction quenches.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Single-channel resonances are fundamental processes in scattering of atoms, yet their occurrence is largely incidental and lacks systematic control. In this Letter, we propose a mechanism to realize a continuously tunable single-channel resonance by controlling the temperature of the heteronuclear mixture. By extending the Casimir-like mediated interaction to finite temperature, we demonstrate that thermal smearing of the Fermi surface reshapes the effective potential between impurities, giving rise to a temperature-controlled resonance (TCR) over a wide parameter range. As a direct consequence, the resonance position shifts systematically with temperature variation, providing a clear experimental signature of this mechanism. We further investigate the quench dynamics of a Bose gas immersed in a Fermi sea and demonstrate that the observed temperature-dependent loss features in recent experiments are consistent with the TCR mechanism. Our results establish temperature as a simple and experimentally accessible control knob for single-channel resonances in ultracold quantum gases.

</details>

### [Localization of a quantum particle in a classical one-component plasma.III. Mutual coherence and coherence degradation in Coulomb-disordered media](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18559v1)

**Authors:** Yury A. Budkov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18559v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

</details>

**Summary.** This paper derives a universal scaling law relating the transverse coherence length of an electron beam to the longitudinal localization length in a Coulomb-disordered medium. It demonstrates that phase decorrelation is driven by the same disorder correlator as single-particle localization. These findings have significant implications for understanding image contrast degradation in liquid-cell electron microscopy.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper establishes a formal connection between transverse coherence decay and longitudinal localization, providing a fundamental link between transport phenomena and wave-front decorrelation in disordered media.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the loss of mutual coherence and phase decorrelation in electron beams propagating through Coulomb-disordered media, such as electrolytes and plasmas.

**Main result.** The derivation of a universal scaling relation for the coherence length rho_c ~ lambda_D * sqrt(ell/L), linking transverse coherence decay to longitudinal localization phenomena.

**Method.** The study employs the Efimov path-integral formalism, the eikonal approximation, and the saddle-point approximation to evaluate the mutual coherence function.

**Model / system.** A quantum particle (electron) interacting with a random potential originating from the thermal fluctuations of a classical one-component plasma (Coulomb-disordered medium).

**Key observables.** Mutual coherence function (gamma), coherence length (rho_c), localization length (ell), and phase structure function (D_phi).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Debye length (lambda_D), sample thickness (L), electron momentum (k), and the distinction between static and dynamic disorder regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes a Gaussian random potential, uses the eikonal (straight-line) approximation, neglects strong multiple scattering and inelastic channels, and assumes a statistically homogeneous medium.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the coherence function via the Green function, applies the Efimov path-integral and disorder averaging, derives scaling laws for static and dynamic regimes, and concludes with an extension to the relativistic regime in an appendix.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We derive the mutual coherence function of an electron beam propagating through a static or dynamic Coulomb-disordered medium and show that its decay introduces an intrinsic coherence-reduction mechanism relevant for electron microscopy in Coulomb-disordered media. Using the Efimov path-integral formalism, the coherence length $ρ_c$ is expressed through the same disorder correlator that governs the single-particle localization length $\ell$. For both a static electrolyte and a dynamic plasma we obtain a universal relation $ρ_c \sim λ_D \sqrt{\ell/L}$, where $λ_D$ is the Debye length and $L$ the sample thickness. In the static case $\ell\propto k^{2}$ (electron momentum), whereas in the dynamic slow-particle regime $\ell\propto k$, leading to qualitatively different energy dependences of the coherence scale. The ion thermal velocity cancels out in the final expression, demonstrating a formal connection between transverse coherence decay and longitudinal localization phenomena. Exact analytical results are given for the phase structure function of a model electrolyte, and numerical estimates indicate that disorder-induced phase decorrelation may contribute appreciably to the attenuation of high-spatial-frequency contrast under experimentally relevant liquid-cell electron microscopy conditions. Possible implications for cryo-EM, disordered liquids, soft condensed matter, and biological media are discussed. In an appendix we extend the theory to the relativistic regime relevant for transmission electron microscopy.

</details>

**Authors:** Abhishek Das, Neelesh Kumar Vij, Demitry Farfurnik

(b) The far-field emission pattern of the inverse-designed cavity normalized to the peak intensity. **Summary.** The researchers developed an inverse-design method to create a new type of circular photonic cavity that breaks the trade-off between high photon confinement and efficient light collection. The resulting design significantly improves the Q-factor compared to standard periodic structures while remaining robust to manufacturing imperfections. This is a key step toward building efficient interfaces for quantum information processing.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful computational tool for designing high-cooperativity interfaces, which are critical for scalable quantum networks and light-matter interaction in cavity QED.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The simultaneous optimization of the quality factor (Q-factor) and far-field emission mode in photonic cavities to enable efficient spin-photon interfaces.

**Main result.** An optimized non-periodic circular cavity achieves a Q-factor approximately ten times higher than traditional bullseye cavities while maintaining a Gaussian-like far-field profile and robustness to fabrication errors.

**Method.** A gradient-based inverse-design framework using the Guided-Mode Expansion (GME) method combined with automatic differentiation, validated by FDTD simulations.

**Model / system.** A non-periodic circular ring cavity consisting of a central disk and concentric rings, implemented on a GaAs substrate designed for coupling to InAs quantum dots.

**Key observables.** Q-factor, far-field mode profile (Gaussian overlap), and collection efficiency.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Central disk radius, ring widths, ring gaps, and fabrication tolerance (up to +/- 6 nm).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The GME method is an approximation requiring FDTD validation; the primary demonstration assumes a GaAs material platform.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the bullseye cavity geometry and compares GME/FDTD far-field modes; Figure 2 shows the optimization evolution, resulting geometry, and far-field pattern; Figure 3 presents FDTD resonance spectra and validation; Figure 4 displays single-parameter fabrication tolerance analysis.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the motivation for spin-photon interfaces, validates the GME method against FDTD, describes the multi-objective gradient-based optimization process, presents the final optimized cavity performance, and concludes with a sensitivity analysis of fabrication errors.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Spin-photon interfaces, realized by coupling optically active spin systems to photonic cavities, are essential for quantum networking and quantum information processing. Implementing such an interface for polarization-encoded photons requires a cavity that supports arbitrary polarization, provides efficient optical access through its far-field mode, and maintains sufficiently high quality factors to enable high cooperativity with the system's optical transitions. However, inherent trade-offs between the Q-factor and far-field emission mode make the simultaneous optimization of these parameters toward the realization of spin-photon interfaces challenging. In this work, we implement a gradient-based inverse-design framework using guided-mode expansion with automatic differentiation to obtain the geometrical features of a circular ring cavity that supports arbitrary polarization while simultaneously optimizing the cavity quality factor and far-field mode profile. The resulting optimized non-periodic cavity achieves a quality factor of approximately $9,000$, about an order-of-magnitude higher than that of a periodic ("bullseye") cavity while preserving a Gaussian-like far-field emission pattern. Furthermore, by varying the cavity geometry within a $\pm 6$ nm fabrication tolerance, we demonstrate the robustness of the design against fabrication errors and identify the innermost ring width and central disk radius as the parameters with the greatest impact on the quality factor and far-field mode. These results establish guided mode expansion-based inverse design as a powerful and computationally efficient approach for developing high-cooperativity spin-photon interfaces for quantum photonic applications.

</details>

### [Quantum optics of frequency comb metrology](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16702v1)

**Authors:** Dong-Chel Shin, Edwin Ng, Myoung-Gyun Suh, Vivishek Sudhir  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16702v1>

Top: the optical frequency comb carries vacuum fluctuations (colored boxes) at each comb line, spaced by Ωr; diagonal lines connecting adjacent pairs indicate the beating that produces the microwave tone. Bottom left: the resulting microwave spectrum at Ωr, with contributions from multiple adjacent-pair beatnotes shown in matching colors. Bottom right: decomposition into amplitude-modulation (AM) and phase-modulation (PM)...</sub>

(b) Frequency-domain vacuum noise folding in DCS. Top (optical): the signal comb (black, line spacing Ωr) is shown together with the LO comb (blue, spacing Ωr + ∆Ωr), with colored regions marking the signal-comb vacuum fluctuations at offsets Ω0 + n∆Ωr relative to the LO comb lines. Bottom (electrical): beating during photodetection maps each pair to an RF comb tone spaced by ∆Ωr, and the inset...</sub>

Time-domain photocurrent variance Var[I(t)] (left axis, solid lines) oscillates between the squeezed level 1/G and the anti-squeezed level G for gains G = 1 (dark blue), G = 5 (medium blue), and G = 10 (light blue), shown together with the mean photocurrent ⟨I(t)⟩(right axis, gray dashed). The variance is up- and down-modulated at twice the beat frequencies Ωn, time-averaging to a noise floor that is increased by (G + 1/G)/2 ≥1 above the vacuum SQL.</sub>
