**Summary.** This study demonstrates how applied pressure can tune the magnetic and structural phases of the heavy-fermion material CeMnSi. It reveals that suppressing Mn-3d magnetism leads to non-Fermi-liquid behavior and that a structural transition at high pressure modifies the electronic state. The findings highlight the strong coupling between Ce-4f electrons and the crystal lattice.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the pressure-induced evolution of electronic, magnetic, and structural properties in the heavy-fermion antiferromagnet CeMnSi, specifically the interplay between Ce-4f and Mn-3d electrons.

**Main result.** Pressure suppresses Mn antiferromagnetic order at 1.3 GPa, inducing non-Fermi-liquid behavior and a new magnetic anomaly, followed by a structural transition to a monoclinic phase at 5.7 GPa.

**Method.** High-pressure electrical resistivity measurements using piston-cylinder and cubic-anvil apparatus, and synchrotron powder X-ray diffraction.

**Model / system.** The physical system is CeMnSi, a heavy-fermion antiferromagnet with a tetragonal CeFeSi-type structure, compared against the non-4f reference compound LaMnSi.

**Key observables.** Néel temperature (TN), pressure-induced anomaly temperature (TM), structural transition pressure (Ps), resistivity exponent (n), and lattice constants (a, c).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Critical pressures Pc ~ 1.3 GPa and Ps ~ 5.7 GPa; temperature scales for Mn-AFM order and heavy-fermion formation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The structural transition is driven by lattice instabilities associated with Ce-4f electrons rather than pure valence instability.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Crystal structure; Fig 2: Temperature dependence of resistivity under pressure; Fig 3: Resistivity power-law fits and exponent n; Fig 4: XRD patterns and lattice constants; Fig 5: High-pressure XRD patterns.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the CeMnSi system, details the experimental pressure/temperature setup, presents transport and structural results showing magnetic and structural transitions, and concludes with a discussion on the 3d-4f hybridization mechanism.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigated pressure effects on the nontrivial heavy-fermion antiferromagnet CeMnSi by means of electrical resistivity and powder X-ray diffraction. With increasing pressure, the antiferromagnetic order of Mn (T_N ~ 240 K at ambient pressure) is rapidly suppressed and disappears at P_c ~ 1.3 GPa. Instead, a pressure-induced anomaly appears at T_M ~ 97 K and shifts to higher temperatures with increasing pressure. The switching of the Mn magnetic state may reflect a modification of the magnetic symmetry of the system, which could influence the stability of the heavy-fermion state. In the low-pressure region, non-Fermi-liquid-like behavior characterized by nearly T-linear resistivity is observed around 0.7 GPa. In addition, the resistivity shows a marked reduction below T_M and a qualitative change toward more metallic behavior above the structural transition pressure P_s ~ 5.7 GPa. At ambient pressure, CeMnSi exhibits negative thermal expansion below ~40 K, which is absent in LaMnSi, supporting the formation of a heavy-fermion ground state.

</details>

### [QLIF-CAST: Quantum Leaky-Integrate-and-Fire for Time-Series Weather Forecasting](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18333v1)

**Authors:** Alberto Marchisio, Aayan Ebrahim, Nouhaila Innan, Muhammad Kashif, Muhammad Shafique  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18333v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18333_figures/2605.18333_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. QLIF neuron model. The qubit is initialized in |0⟩, rotated by Rx(ϕ) to encode the current excitation state, then rotated by Rx(θinput) to integrate the input signal. Measuring the qubit yields the updated excitation probability αnew = sin2( ϕ+θinput</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18333_figures/2605.18333_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Classical LIF neuron model. The membrane potential U integrates synaptic input Iin and decays exponentially between spikes (Unew = βUprev + (1−β)Iin, β = e−1/τ). A spike is emitted when U exceeds the threshold, after which U resets to zero.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18333_figures/2605.18333_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. QLSTM architecture. Each of the four classical LSTM gates (forget, input, cell, output) is replaced by a multi-qubit variational quantum circuit (VQC), with gate angles trained via parameter-shift gradient rules.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.18333_figures/2605.18333_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. LSTM-QNN architecture. A classical LSTM encoder performs temporal feature extraction, whose output is passed to a variational quantum neural network (QNN) before the regression head.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.18333_figures/2605.18333_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5. Overview of the QLIF-CAST methodology. Three datasets (D1-D3) used across the two evaluation phases, with shared preprocessing steps. QLIF-CAST and Classical LIF architectures; the two pipelines are structurally identical, while only Layer 2 (the neuronal update rule) differs. Two-phase evaluation design and key results, with QPU hardware validation on IBM Marrakesh.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces QLIF-CAST, a lightweight hybrid quantum neural network designed for time-series forecasting. By using shallow single-qubit circuits to model neuronal dynamics, the model significantly reduces training time compared to deeper quantum models while outperforming classical counterparts in prediction accuracy.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of developing efficient and accurate quantum-classical hybrid architectures for multivariate time-series weather forecasting, specifically addressing the high computational cost of deep variational quantum models.

**Main result.** The QLIF-CAST model achieves 15.4% lower MSE than a classical LIF baseline and converges up to 94% faster than state-of-the-art QLSTM and QNN models, while maintaining hardware reliability on a 156-qubit QPU.

**Method.** The authors use a hybrid recurrent architecture with surrogate gradient training (arctan approximation) and a vectorized circuit execution strategy to achieve a 500x speedup in simulation.

**Model / system.** A hybrid quantum-classical recurrent architecture (QLIF-CAST) utilizing a depth-2, single-qubit circuit where neuron excitation states are encoded as quantum superpositions driven by Rx rotations and T1 relaxation decay.

**Key observables.** Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and R-squared (R2).

**Important parameters / regimes.** T1 relaxation constant of 10.0, spike threshold of 0.75, and 12-step context windows.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes that the higher error in certain benchmarks remains within operational tolerances for practical deployment and that the shallow circuit depth is sufficient to capture necessary dynamics.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 illustrates the QLIF neuron model; Fig 2 shows the classical LIF model; Fig 7 shows training/validation loss convergence; Fig 8 and 9 show forecasting performance for air quality and wind speed respectively.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QLIF-CAST model, describes the quantum neuronal dynamics and architecture, presents a controlled comparison against classical LIF, performs a cross-domain benchmark against QLSTM/QNN, and concludes with hardware verification on IBM Marrakesh.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Accurate and efficient time-series forecasting remains a challenging problem for both classical and quantum neural architectures, particularly in multivariate environmental settings. This work adapts the Quantum Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (QLIF) spiking neural network for time-series regression tasks, specifically short-term multivariate weather forecasting. We extend QLIF beyond classification and demonstrate its applicability to continuous-valued prediction problems.   The QLIF-CAST model encodes neuron excitation states as single-qubit quantum superpositions, driven by Rx rotation gates and T1 relaxation decay, and is embedded within a hybrid quantum-classical recurrent architecture. We conduct two distinct evaluations. First, a controlled comparison against a parameter-matched classical LIF baseline on a multivariate weather dataset shows that QLIF-CAST achieves 15.4% lower MSE and 4.4% lower MAE, demonstrating that quantum neuronal dynamics reduce prediction error over classical equivalents. Second, a cross-domain comparative analysis with state-of-the-art quantum LSTM (QLSTM) and quantum neural network (QNN) models on air quality and wind speed benchmarks reveals that QLIF-CAST converges in up to 94% less training time, occupying a distinct position in the speed-error trade-off space. Hardware verification on IBM Marrakesh (156-qubit QPU) confirms reliable circuit execution with only 1.2% average deviation from simulation.

</details>

### [Quantum Sidecar Architectures for Hybrid AI Training and Inference: Stateful Protected Registers, Stateless Reset-and-Reprepare Circuits and Quantum Weight-State Outlook](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18031v1)

**Authors:** Y. Mo, G. D. Su  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18031v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18031_figures/2605.18031_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: High-level quantum sidecar interface for LLM systems. The classical LLM system keeps the full model state, while the quantum sidecar returns bounded control signals such as expert selection, routing hints, and candidate gradient/update proposals.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18031_figures/2605.18031_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Stateful protected-register QND-style readout for 8 protected qubits plus one ancilla. The ideal case is stable; noise causes gradual degradation.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18031_figures/2605.18031_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Stateful scaling under depolarizing noise after 50 readout rounds.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.18031_figures/2605.18031_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Stateless sidecar candidate-update search. The sidecar-style sampler selects top-4 update directions more often than random or noisy classical baselines in this synthetic landscape.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.18031_figures/2605.18031_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Mean regret for candidate update-direction selection. Lower is better.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a hybrid framework where quantum processors serve as 'sidecars' to classical AI models, providing bounded signals for tasks like routing or weight updates. It explores two modes of operation: a stateful mode that preserves quantum resources and a stateless mode based on frequent resets. The study shows how these quantum primitives can act as useful priors for classical optimization and selection processes.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Addressing the scaling bottlenecks of large-scale classical AI by proposing a hybrid 'quantum sidecar' architecture that acts as a bounded signal generator for classical pipelines.

**Main result.** The paper demonstrates that quantum sidecars can operate in two modes—stateful (preserving observables via protected registers) and stateless (reset-and-reprepare)—to provide useful priors for tasks like routing and parameter updates.

**Method.** The authors use density-matrix and statevector simulations of small-scale quantum circuits (4-8 qubits) under depolarizing noise, cross-checked with Qiskit utilities.

**Model / system.** The architecture involves a quantum co-processor interacting with a classical LLM, utilizing either a protected register with QND-style parity readout or a parameterized QAOA-style circuit for sampling.

**Key observables.** Parity accuracy (q_t), fidelity (F_t), and probability mass of top-k update directions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Qubit counts (n=4, 6, 8), depolarizing noise levels, and reset overhead times (20 ns to 1200 ns).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The work does not claim quantum advantage; simulations are synthetic and do not represent actual hardware performance; the 'weight-state' is a speculative long-term vision.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the high-level sidecar interface; Fig 2 and 3 illustrate the stability and noise-driven degradation of the 8-qubit QND readout; Fig 11 illustrates a classical software interface for a router.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies AI bottlenecks, defines the sidecar architecture and its two operational modes, simulates the performance of both modes under noise, and concludes with a speculative outlook on quantum weight-states.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a quantum sidecar architecture family for future hybrid AI training and inference. The central idea is not to store an entire Transformer in a small quantum memory, nor to claim one-shot collapse into a fully trained model or an optimal answer. Instead, we identify two physically distinct operating modes for quantum co-processors attached to classical large-model pipelines. The first is a stateful protected-register mode, in which a protected register stores a reusable quantum resource while an ancilla or temporary register performs QND-style readout. The second is a stateless reset-and-reprepare mode, in which each query prepares a task-conditioned quantum circuit, evolves over bounded training or inference control variables, measures candidate signals, resets the qubits, and repeats. We simulate the stateful mode using 2/4/6/8 protected-qubit density-matrix QND-style parity readout with one ancilla and a Qiskit cross-check. For the stateless mode, we include both an abstract candidate-update sampler and a circuit-level QAOA-style statevector sampler over structured candidate landscapes, followed by reset-overhead sensitivity analysis. The resulting framework positions quantum sidecars as bounded signal generators for optimizer-side sampling, adapter or expert selection, retrieval, routing, and reasoning-path proposal. As a speculative outlook, we introduce quantum weight-state sidecars: restricted quantum representations over model-control variables, not direct encodings of complete classical weight tensors.

</details>

### [Sparse Mamba Decoder for Quantum Error Correction: Efficient Defect-Centric Processing of Surface Code Syndromes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17156v1)

**Authors:** Samira Sayedsalehi, Nader Bagherzadeh, Maxim Shcherbakov, Jean-Luc Gaudiot  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17156v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **2/5**

White circles represent data qubits, while blue circles • and pink circles • represent Z-type and X-type stabi- lizer measurement qubits, respectively. b) Circuit representation of the X-type (top) and Z-type (bottom) stabilizer measurements. Open circles (◦) denote the four data qubits coupled to each measurement qubit.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17156_figures/2605.17156_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Sparse Mamba Decoder architecture. (a) Sparse defect extraction from a (d2−1) × R syndrome volume to k defect tokens d1, . . . , dk (k ≪d2R at physically relevant error rates). (b) 13-dimensional feature vector per defect: spatial coordinates (x, y), normalized time t/R, stabilizer type τ, spatial and temporal neighbor flags, boundary distances bZ, bX, and the reconstructed measurement mi,t from cumulative XOR. (c) Pipeline: per-token embedder R13 →Rdmodel, L mixer layers, masked mean pool, ResBlock readout to logical observable ˆλ. (d) One mixer layer: RMSNorm + Mamba selective scan (dstate=16, dconv=4), then RMSNorm + Gated Dense (SiLU gating, widening wgate), both with residual...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17156_figures/2605.17156_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Logical error rate under depolarizing noise with perfect stabilizer measurements. The Sparse Mamba Decoder (solid lines) with dual prediction heads for λZ and λX is compared against MWPM (dotted), the Tesseract near-MLD decoder [8] (dashed), and Belief Matching [18] (dash- dotted) at distances d ∈{3, 5, 7, 9, 11}. Each data point is evaluated over 106–3 × 106 fresh samples. Inset: Log-log plot of the low-p region showing the widening gap between Mamba and all classical decoders with increasing distance.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17156_figures/2605.17156_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Logical error rate under uniform circuit-level noise with R = d stabilizer measurement rounds. SMD (dotted circles) vs MWPM (solid squares) at distances d ∈{3, 5, 7, 9} and error rates p ∈[0.001, 0.005]. SMD outperforms MWPM at all distances and rates.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.17156_figures/2605.17156_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Mean logical error per round on the Google Sycamore experimental dataset at code distances 3 and 5. Results are averaged across X and Z bases. Error bars indicate the spread between bases. The proposed Sparse Mamba Decoder (blue, hatched) matches or outperforms the dense Mamba decoder of Varbanov et al. [12] (red) at both distances, despite processing only active defects with O(k) complexity compared to O(d2R) for dense approaches.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new neural decoder for surface codes that exploits the sparsity of error syndromes to achieve much faster decoding speeds. By using a Mamba state-space model to process only active defects, it maintains high accuracy while scaling linearly with the number of errors rather than the total lattice size.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Existing neural decoders for quantum error correction (QEC) are computationally inefficient because they process dense syndrome arrays regardless of the actual error rate, leading to high latency as code distance increases.

**Main result.** The Sparse Mamba Decoder (SMD) achieves O(k) complexity by processing only active detection events, providing a 95-467x speedup over near-MLD decoders and significantly reducing logical error rates under SI1000 and Sycamore experimental noise.

**Method.** The authors introduce a defect-centric neural decoder using a Mamba state-space model backbone that processes a 13-dimensional feature vector for each active syndrome defect.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the rotated surface code under various noise models, including depolarizing, uniform circuit-level, and SI1000 noise, as well as experimental data from the Google Sycamore processor.

**Key observables.** Logical error rate (LER), Frame Error Rate (FER), and decoding latency.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Code distance (d = 3 to 11), number of measurement rounds (R), and error rate (p approx 0.1%).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The effectiveness of the decoder relies on the inherent sparsity of syndromes at physically relevant, low error rates.

**Figures summary.** Figure 4 shows SMD outperforming MWPM under uniform noise; Figure 5 compares LER on Sycamore data; Figure 6 and 7 illustrate the speed-accuracy Pareto front under different noise regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the inefficiency of dense decoders, describes the SMD architecture and feature engineering, details the training protocols (curriculum learning), presents performance benchmarks against classical and neural baselines, and concludes with future directions for scaling.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum error correction (QEC) is essential for building fault-tolerant quantum computers, requiring decoders that are simultaneously accurate, fast, and scalable. Most state-of-the-art neural decoders achieve high accuracy but process the full dense syndrome array of size $O(d^2 R) $regardless of the actual error rate, where d is the code distance and R is the number of measurement rounds. At physically relevant error rates (p ~ 0.1%), fewer than 5% of syndrome entries contain active detection events -- yet existing decoders process the entire syndrome volume. We introduce the Sparse Mamba Decoder (SMD), a defect-centric neural decoder that processes only the k active detection events using a 13-dimensional feature representation per defect and a Mamba state-space backbone, achieving $O(k)$ complexity. Across depolarizing, uniform circuit-level, SI1000, and Google Sycamore experimental benchmarks, SMD reduces the MWPM logical error rate by up to 49% at $d \le 5$ under SI1000 noise, runs 95-467x faster than the Tesseract near-MLD decoder and 232-463x faster than Belief Matching, and maintains nearly constant latency (24-57 us) across d = 3-9 under uniform circuit-level noise. On the Sycamore experimental dataset, the SMD ensemble matches or slightly surpasses the dense Mamba decoder of Varbanov et al. All results are obtained on commodity NVIDIA GPUs with 7.5-16M parameters, without specialized accelerators.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.16678"></a>
### [Spatially Adaptive Detection for Satellite-based QKD under Atmospheric Turbulence Channel](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16678v1)

**Authors:** Yaoxuan Yang, Ivi Afxenti, Majid Safari  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16678v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.16678_figures/2605.16678_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Statistical characterization of focal-plane intensity at different detector positions. (a) Corner element, (b) edge element, and (c) center element show the histogram fitting results. The horizontal axis represents the received intensity, and the vertical axis represents the occurrence frequency (histogram counts). (d) Schematic illustration of detector positions within the array.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.16678_figures/2605.16678_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Example realizations of focal-plane speckle intensity distributions under different turbulence conditions. (a) Weak, (b) moderate, and (c) strong turbulence for a focal length of f = 0.5 m, and (d) weak turbulence with an increased focal length of f = 4 m. The horizontal and vertical axes represent spatial coordinates on the focal plane, and the color scale indicates intensity. The white grid denotes the detector array layout.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.16678_figures/2605.16678_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Performance comparison of different detector-selection strategies under different turbulence conditions. (a) QBER and (b) SKR under weak turbulence; (c) QBER and (d) SKR under moderate turbulence; (e) QBER and (f) SKR under strong turbulence. The horizontal axis represents the noise level nB, and the vertical axis denotes QBER or SKR (Hz), respectively.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes an adaptive receiver design for satellite-based quantum key distribution that uses detector arrays to filter out noise-heavy regions caused by atmospheric turbulence. By selectively activating only the most promising detector elements, the system significantly improves the secret key rate. This approach is particularly effective in moderate turbulence conditions where spatial signal fluctuations are most pronounced.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it addresses the practical challenge of maintaining entanglement/signal integrity in free-space quantum communication channels subject to decoherence-like atmospheric effects.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Atmospheric turbulence in satellite-to-ground QKD causes spatial beam distortion and intensity fluctuations, leading to high background noise interference in single-element detectors.

**Main result.** A spatially adaptive detector activation strategy effectively reduces the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER) and improves the Secret Key Rate (SKR), with the highest performance gains observed in moderate turbulence regimes.

**Method.** The authors propose a threshold-based selection method and a 'Best-K' strategy to selectively activate detector elements with high signal probability, evaluated using Monte Carlo simulations and the Split-Step Fourier Method.

**Model / system.** A satellite-to-ground downlink QKD system using an 8x8 Single Photon Detector (SPD) array at 850 nm, modeling a 1200 km link with atmospheric turbulence via the Hufnagel-Valley model and Kolmogorov statistics.

**Key observables.** Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), Secret Key Rate (SKR), Sifted Bit Rate (SBR), and Error Bit Rate (EBR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Turbulence strength (weak, moderate, strong), noise level (background and dark counts), focal length, and detector array size.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The channel is quasi-static within each clock period, the transmitter is an ideal single-photon source, and background noise is spatially uniform.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows statistical model fitting (Gamma, lognormal, Gamma-Gamma) to intensity distributions; Fig 2 illustrates focal-plane speckle patterns under different turbulence and focal lengths; Fig 3 compares QBER and SKR for different strategies across turbulence regimes.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the SatQKD problem, description of the physical system and turbulence model, presentation of the proposed adaptive detection strategies, statistical analysis of intensity distributions, performance evaluation via Monte Carlo simulations, and discussion of results and limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum key distribution (QKD) provides information-theoretic security and satellite-based quantum key distribution (SatQKD) has demonstrated the potential to extend this communication security to intercontinental scales. However, atmospheric turbulence induces significant distortion in the spatial distribution of received optical beams, while background noise remains approximately uniform across the detector plane. As a result, single-element qubit (quantum bit) detection can be frequently dominated by noise due to the random spatial pattern of the imaged wavefront, thereby degrading the system performance. To address this limitation, we propose to exploit the spatial degrees of freedom of single-photon detector arrays to reject the excessive noise while adapting to channel variations induced by turbulence. We develop a threshold-based selection method that only activates detector elements that have higher probability of registering qubits. We evaluate the performance of the proposed noise-rejection QKD schemes using Monte Carlo simulations considering the impact of diffraction and atmospheric turbulence on the transmitted optical beam in the presence of background and dark noise. The results show that, compared to conventional schemes, the proposed noise-rejection strategy effectively reduces the quantum bit error rate (QBER) and improves the secret key rate (SKR) performance, while the performance gains depend on the turbulence condition. These findings demonstrate the potential of adaptive array receiver design to enhance the robustness of the SatQKD system under realistic atmospheric conditions.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.18051"></a>
### [Structural $f$-divergence: Tight universal bounds for cost function moments and gradients in parameterized quantum circuits](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18051v1)

**Authors:** Tomohiro Nishiyama, Yoshihiko Hasegawa  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18051v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18051_figures/2605.18051_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Conceptual diagram illustrating that the structural f-divergence Dstr f (P, Q) bounds both the difference in the k- th moments of the cost function and the difference in the expected gradient magnitudes between probability measures P and Q on either the parameter space Θ or the unitary group U. Here, ⟨O⟩denotes the cost function defined in Eq. (2) and ˆO = O/∥O∥∞denotes the operator normalized by the operator norm.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new information-theoretic metric called structural f-divergence to quantify how differences in parameter distributions affect the landscape of quantum cost functions. It provides tight, universal bounds on gradient vanishing and cost concentration, offering new design principles for optimizing parameterized quantum circuits.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The vanishing of cost-function gradients (barren plateaus) in variational quantum algorithms is typically analyzed using high-randomness assumptions, which lack design principles for choosing realistic probability measures on parameter spaces.

**Main result.** The authors introduce 'structural f-divergence' and establish tight, universal analytical bounds that relate the discrepancy between probability distributions to differences in expected gradient magnitudes and cost-function moments.

**Method.** The study utilizes information-geometric tools, specifically a new symmetric, normalized f-divergence-based measure, to derive trade-off inequalities using properties like the data processing inequality and Hölder's inequality.

**Model / system.** The framework is applied to Parameterized Quantum Circuits (PQCs) consisting of n qubits and L layers of alternating gates, using unitary operators $U(	heta)$ and Hermitian cost function operators $O$.

**Key observables.** Expected gradient magnitude, cost-function moments (even and odd), and cost concentration.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Probability measures on the parameter space (P and Q), number of qubits (n), number of layers (L), and the structural f-divergence metric.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The function f must be twice-differentiable, strictly convex, and satisfy f(1)=0; equality conditions are demonstrated using a minimal one-qubit, one-layer ansatz.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a conceptual diagram showing how structural f-divergence bounds the differences in cost function moments and expected gradient magnitudes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the barren plateau problem, defines the new structural f-divergence metric, establishes analytical trade-off inequalities for gradients and moments, proves the tightness of these bounds using specific ansatz examples, and applies the framework to derive conditions for avoiding barren plateaus and suppressing noise.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The barren plateau phenomenon, in which cost-function gradients of variational quantum algorithms vanish exponentially, remains a central obstacle for near-term quantum computing. Existing analyses typically depend on t-design or Haar-random assumptions and bound quantities at the level of unitary distributions, offering limited insight for designing probability measures on the parameter space of parameterized quantum circuits. In this paper, we introduce the structural $f$-divergence, a symmetric $f$-divergence-based measure between probability distributions on the parameter space. We establish analytically trade-off inequalities that bound the discrepancies in the expected gradient magnitude and in the cost-function moments between a distribution on PQC and a reference distribution; equality is attained by a minimal one-qubit, one-layer ansatz. As applications, we derive necessary conditions on probability measures for avoiding BPs and cost concentration, and sufficient conditions that suppress noise-induced deviations.

</details>

**Authors:** Antik Sihi, Caden Ginter, Kristjan Haule, Subhasish Mandal  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16771v1>

Panels (a)–(c) show the eDMFT total density of states (TDOS) for SrMnO3, LaCrO3, and LaMnO3, respectively, computed in the paramagnetic (PM, black dashed lines) and antiferromagnetic (AFM, blue solid lines) phases. Experimental x-ray photoemission spec- troscopy (XPS, red), x-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS, dark green), and bremsstrahlung isochromat spectroscopy (BIS, light green) data are reproduced from the literature [100–103, 112].</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper shows that using the embedded DMFT (eDMFT) framework allows researchers to perform high-throughput calculations of correlated oxides using fixed, universal interaction parameters. By treating screening self-consistently, the method avoids the need for material-specific tuning and accurately reproduces experimental spectral features. This paves the way for creating large-scale, predictive databases for strongly correlated materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** High-throughput electronic-structure discovery for strongly correlated materials is hindered by the need for material-specific, non-transferable Coulomb interaction parameters (U).

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that the eDMFT framework allows for a parameter-tuning-free approach using uniform U values (6 eV for metals, 10 eV for insulators) that achieves excellent agreement with experimental spectroscopy.

**Method.** High-throughput charge self-consistent embedded Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (eDMFT) using CTQMC for the impurity problem and the Maximum Entropy Method for analytical continuation.

**Model / system.** A series of 12 3d transition-metal perovskite oxides with the formula ABO3, where A = Ca, Sr, La and B = V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni.

**Key observables.** Total density of states (TDOS), momentum-resolved spectral functions, bandwidth renormalization, and Mott gaps.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fixed Hubbard U (6 eV for metals, 10 eV for insulators), Hund's coupling J = 0.8 eV, and magnetic phases (paramagnetic and antiferromagnetic).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The spectral properties are primarily governed by the dynamical self-energy rather than static interaction-induced energy shifts, allowing for a fixed-U approach.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Overview of the 12 compounds and their properties; Fig 2: Comparison of eDMFT vs ARPES/XPS/BIS for Vanadium oxides; Fig 3/4/5: Comparison of eDMFT TDOS with experimental XPS, XAS, and BIS for various Mn, Fe, and Co oxides in PM and AFM phases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of parameter-dependency in DFT+DMFT, presents the eDMFT framework and the fixed-U hypothesis, details the systematic study of 3d perovskites, benchmarks results against various spectroscopic experiments, and concludes with the implications for high-throughput materials databases.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Strongly correlated transition-metal perovskite oxides pose a fundamental challenge for electronic-structure theory and for large-scale, data-driven materials discovery. While DFT+DMFT provides a quantitatively accurate description of such systems, its high-throughput application is hindered by the need to determine material-specific Coulomb interaction parameters ($U$). First-principles approaches such as the cRPA predict a highly nonlinear and non-transferable evolution of the interaction strength across chemically similar ABO$_3$ perovskites. Here we show that this paradigm does not extend to the large-energy-window eDMFT, which employs highly localized orbitals and treats electronic correlations and screening self-consistently within the same many-body framework. As a result, spectral properties are governed primarily by the dynamical self-energy rather than by static interaction-induced energy shifts. Recent constrained-eDMFT calculations demonstrated that, for broad classes of $3d$ transition-metal oxides, the self-consistently screened Coulomb interactions naturally fall within relatively narrow ranges for correlated metals and insulators. Motivated by these findings, we implement a high-throughput eDMFT framework employing physically derived interaction values of $U=6$ eV for metals and $U=10$ eV for insulators together with $exact$ double counting. We test this framework using systematic high-throughput eDMFT calculations for ABO$_3$ compounds (A = Ca, Sr, La; B = V--Ni) and benchmark the resulting spectral functions against photoemission experiments, where we find overall excellent agreement. Our results establish that charge self-consistent eDMFT enables robust, parameter-tuning-free high-throughput many-body calculations for correlated oxides, opening a practical pathway toward predictive electronic-structure databases for strongly correlated materials.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.18148"></a>
### [Taming the 3D Wilson-Fisher Fixed Point via Nonlocal Effective Action](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18148v1)

**Authors:** Hyeon Jung Kim, Seung-Jong Yoo, Jinmo Bok, Lemuel John Sese, Semin Park, Ki-Seok Kim  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18148v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18148_figures/2605.18148_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. The intersection of the independent field flow equa- tions γϕ = 0 and γφ = 0 on the (∆ϕ, ∆φ) plane. The un- constrained physical non-trivial root cleanly stabilizes at the crossing point (0.981, 0.415), isolating the true local Wilson- Fisher critical coordinates without requiring any fine-tuned kinematic matching constraints.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The authors propose a new Renormalization Group framework that uses a nonlocal effective action to solve the 3D Wilson-Fisher fixed point problem. By treating the scaling dimensions of both primary and auxiliary fields as independent variables, they eliminate systematic truncation errors. This approach yields critical exponents that are highly accurate when compared to established numerical benchmarks.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of accurately characterizing the 3D Wilson-Fisher quantum critical point due to severe truncation errors and unphysical fixed-point divergence in standard non-perturbative Renormalization Group frameworks.

**Main result.** The identification of a robust, non-trivial physical fixed point with critical exponents (eta ~ 0.038, nu ~ 0.6317) that show exceptional agreement with Quantum Monte Carlo and conformal bootstrap benchmarks.

**Method.** A multi-loop Renormalization Group approach using a nonlocal effective action ansatz and a two-variable master equation system evaluated up to three-loop order.

**Model / system.** A 3D relativistic phi^4 theory (Wilson-Fisher universality class) where the quartic interaction is decoupled via a Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation into a primary field and an auxiliary field.

**Key observables.** Kinematic anomalous dimension (eta_phi), energy operator dimension (Delta_phi^2), and thermal correlation length exponent (nu).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Scaling dimensions (Delta_phi and Delta_phi_tilde) treated as independent dynamical variables, and the dimensionless coupling (g_tilde^2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes a scale-invariant action at the critical point and utilizes a three-loop truncation of the effective action.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the intersection of the two independent field flow equations (gamma_phi = 0 and gamma_phi_tilde = 0) on the scaling dimension plane, marking the stable physical root.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of truncation errors in RG, presents a nonlocal effective action ansatz using Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, details the multi-loop diagrammatic calculations, demonstrates the numerical solution of the two-variable master equations, and validates the results against benchmarked data.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a novel Renormalization Group (RG) framework based on a nonlocal effective action ansatz to tame the strong coupling dynamics of the three-dimensional relativistic $φ^{4}$ theory. By implementing a Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, we decouple the quartic interaction into a system of the primary field $φ$ and an auxiliary field $\varphi \sim φ^2$. Rather than freezing the intermediate scaling dimensions, the nonlocality of our effective action allows both exponents $Δ_φ$ and $Δ_{\varphi}$ to act as fully independent, unconstrained dynamical variables.This nonlocal propagator framework plays a critical role in the RG flow: evaluating self-energies and vertex fluctuations up to the three-loop order, the nonlocality drives precise structural cross-cancellations among multi-loop fluctuations near the Gaussian limit. Solving the resulting closed two-variable master equations isolates a robust, non-trivial physical fixed point at $Δ_φ^{*} \approx 0.981$ and $Δ_{\varphi}^{*} \approx 0.415$. These dynamic exponents yield a kinematic anomalous dimension $η_φ \approx 0.038$, an energy operator dimension $Δ_{φ^2} \approx 1.417$, and-via mass deformation-a thermal correlation length exponent $ν\approx 0.6317$, demonstrating exceptional quantitative agreement with high-precision Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) and conformal bootstrap benchmarks. Our results rigorously confirm that unfreezing the nonlocal degrees of freedom successfully eliminates the systematic truncation errors inherent to conventional local ansatz treatments, simultaneously resolving both the static scaling and thermodynamic flows of the Wilson-Fisher universality class.

</details>

### [The problem of time: a path integral view](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17935v1)

**Authors:** Juan Manuel Diaz, Alejandro Perez  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17935v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17935_figures/2605.17935_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: The results in a picture: In a timeless closed system where all the allowed quantum states have the same total energy E there is no external time evolution. Assuming that the Hamiltonian is H = Hc + HR where Hc is the Hamiltonian of an internal (clock) degree of freedom, one can write the (boundary) states, for which the transition amplitudes will be computed, as ⟨ψ′ c; xout i | (with ψ′ c and ψc denoting the clock states and xi the rest of the universe degrees of freedom). When the boundary clock states are in suitable semiclassical states, their relative features encode internal time τ. In the picture, these states are represented by the clock cartoon inside the Dirac ket and bra...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17935_figures/2605.17935_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2: Same data give rise to different spacetime developments depending on the arbitrary fields N(x) and N i(x). The two spacetime developements are solutions of Einstein’s equations related by a diffeomorphism.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper addresses the 'problem of time' in quantum gravity by showing that time evolution is an emergent phenomenon. By using a path integral view, the authors demonstrate that a semiclassical clock state can resolve the ambiguity of backward-propagating amplitudes, effectively recovering standard Schrödinger dynamics from a fundamentally timeless theory.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** How time evolution and a directed arrow of time emerge from a fundamentally timeless, generally covariant quantum system, and how to resolve the 'cosine problem' in path integral representations.

**Main result.** The authors show that Schrödinger evolution and forward-propagating amplitudes emerge naturally when a clock degree of freedom is prepared in a semiclassical 'good-clock state', which breaks time-reversal symmetry.

**Method.** A path integral approach using a non-relativistic toy model and semiclassical approximations (stationary phase) to analyze transition amplitudes.

**Model / system.** A non-relativistic, closed, background-independent quantum system with a fixed total energy E, decomposed into a clock subsystem (harmonic oscillator or free particle) and the rest of the universe.

**Key observables.** Dirac observables (gauge-invariant quantities), relational observables, and the emergent physical time parameter.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The semiclassical limit (|z| >> 1), the large energy regime, and the energy-time uncertainty relation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes a non-interacting clock and a fixed energy eigenvalue; it also assumes the existence of suitable semiclassical 'good-clock' states.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of how clock boundary states select forward-propagating amplitudes; Figure 2 illustrates different spacetime developments related by 4D diffeomorphisms.

**Paper structure.** The paper reviews general covariance and timelessness, critiques existing classical and quantum clock approaches, defines the cosine problem, and then uses a non-relativistic model to demonstrate the emergence of time via path integrals.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that the emergence of time evolution in an otherwise timeless nonrelativistic closed quantum system -- viewed as a poor man's model of generally covariant quantum theory -- can be understood from the perspective of the path integral representation. As often happens in the functional integral approach, this viewpoint offers a more intuitive account of features that become cumbersome in the operator/Hilbert-space formulation. We show how Schrödinger evolution emerges once a clock degree of freedom is identified and placed in a suitable semiclassical `good-clock state'.   Our analysis has a consequence that extends to path integral formulations of generally covariant systems with action $S$ (including gravity). In such theories certain transition amplitudes take the form $\exp(iS/\hbar)+\exp(-iS/\hbar)$ rather than the expected `forward propagating' $\exp(iS/\hbar)$. This feature, known as the {\em cosine problem}, appears in concrete regularizations of the path integral, for example in the spin foam representation defining the physical inner product between spin network states in loop quantum gravity. Both formally and in explicit regularizations, this apparent difficulty has led some authors to seek modifications of the basic amplitudes to eliminate backward propagation.   Our model shows that the cosine problem is instead a natural consequence of time-reversal invariance of the fundamental dynamics together with the time-neutral boundary states commonly used in transition amplitudes. When a suitable clock system is identified and placed in a semiclassical `good-clock state', it introduces a time arrow selecting the `forward propagating' $\exp(iS/\hbar)$, without modifying the fundamental dynamics.   The analysis clarifies how time emerges under suitable conditions and emphasizes that, in the canonical formulation, quantum gravity is fundamentally timeless.

</details>

### [Discovering Data Encoding Strategies for Quantum-Classical Neural Networks Using Monte Carlo Tree Search](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18540v1)

**Authors:** Lena Tokuhiro, Amine Bentellis, Jeanette Miriam Lorenz  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18540v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

The MCTS-derived 2×2 circuit is compared against baseline encodings using RX, RY , and higher-order gates with 1 to 3 re-uploading layers; only the best-performing configuration for each baseline is shown. The two angle encodings (RX and RY ) achieve identical performance and therefore overlap. Results are averaged over five random seeds, with error bars indicating standard deviation.</sub>

</details>

The effective rank is computed on a subset of 20 images, with error bars indicating standard deviation. Pearson correlation: r = 0.87 (BreastMNIST) and r = 0.82 (PneumoniaMNIST).</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to automatically find high-performing quantum data encoding circuits for hybrid neural networks. It discovers that the diversity of the feature map, measured by effective rank, is a key predictor of success, providing a way to accelerate the search for effective quantum machine learning models.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of discovering optimal data encoding strategies for Quantum-Classical Convolutional Neural Networks (QCCNNs) and identifying which circuit properties predict classification performance.

**Main result.** MCTS-discovered encoding circuits outperform standard strategies, and the effective rank of feature maps was identified as a strong predictor of encoding performance, whereas entanglement and Fourier properties were not.

**Method.** Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with progressive widening is used to automate the discovery of encoding circuits, combined with an analysis of feature map diversity via effective rank.

**Model / system.** A hybrid Quantum-Classical Convolutional Neural Network (QCCNN) architecture using non-variational quantum blocks for feature extraction on medical imaging datasets (BreastMNIST and PneumoniaMNIST).

**Key observables.** Classification accuracy, Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC), effective rank of feature maps, entanglement capability, and Fourier spectrum.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Patch sizes (2x2 and 3x3 qubits), scaling factor (f), and MCTS exploration constant (c).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes a noise-free simulation regime and does not explicitly account for NISQ-era hardware noise or classical simulation hardness.

**Figures summary.** Schematic of QCCNN architecture, visual representation of discovered circuits, performance comparisons against baseline encodings, training metrics (loss/accuracy), and correlations between effective rank and AUC.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QCCNN problem, describes the MCTS-based search methodology, presents the discovered circuits and their performance on medical datasets, analyzes the correlation between circuit metrics and accuracy, and discusses limitations regarding noise and quantum advantage.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum machine learning (QML) has attracted considerable research interest, yet whether it offers practical benefits over classical approaches remains an open question. The choice of data encoding significantly influences QML performance, but why certain encodings outperform others remains poorly understood. We employ Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to discover optimal data encoding circuits for a quantum-classical convolutional neural network (QCCNN) combining a non-variational quantum block for feature extraction with a classical classifier. Evaluating on two medical imaging datasets, the discovered circuits outperform commonly used encoding strategies while showing competitive results compared to purely classical counterparts. We further analyze metrics to identify predictors of encoding performance. Entanglement capability and Fourier decomposition provide minimal insight, whereas the effective rank of the feature maps exhibits meaningful correlation and can serve as a threshold criterion to accelerate the search for high-performing encodings. Our findings provide both a practical method for encoding discovery and new insights into what makes data encodings effective in QML.

</details>

### [Incommensurate Antiferromagnetic Order in the Fe-substituted Bi-2201 Cuprate in the Heavily Overdoped Regime](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16988v1)

**Authors:** Y. Komiyama, Y. Ikeda, T. Taniguchi, T. J. Williams, M. Matsuda, S. Asai, T. Masuda, H. Kuwahara, H. Kuroe, T. Kawamata, I. Watanabe, M. Fujita, T. Adachi

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that incommensurate antiferromagnetic order exists in the heavily overdoped, non-superconducting regime of Bi-2201 cuprates when stabilized by iron substitution. The findings suggest that robust antiferromagnetic correlations persist even beyond the superconducting dome. The observed magnetism appears to be a glassy state induced by the pinning of intrinsic fluctuations by Fe impurities.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates whether incommensurate antiferromagnetic (AFM) order or fluctuations persist in the non-superconducting, heavily overdoped regime of Bi-2201 cuprates.

**Main result.** The researchers discovered incommensurate AFM peaks in 5% Fe-substituted Bi-2201 in the heavily overdoped regime, suggesting that Fe-substitution stabilizes existing AFM fluctuations into a glassy magnetic state.

**Method.** Elastic neutron scattering experiments were performed using triple-axis spectrometers (TOPAN, HER, and HB-3) on single crystals, supplemented by magnetic susceptibility and muon spin rotation (muSR) measurements.

**Model / system.** The physical system is Fe-substituted Bi-2201 cuprate (Bi1.74Pb0.38Sr1.88Cu1-yFeyO6+d) in the heavily overdoped, non-superconducting regime (hole concentration p approx 0.29 to 0.31).

**Key observables.** Incommensurability (delta), magnetic correlation length (xi_mag), magnetic transition temperature (Tm), and hole concentration (p).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fe-substitution level (y = 0.05), hole concentration (p approx 0.29-0.31), and temperature.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the observed magnetic order is induced/stabilized by Fe impurities acting on intrinsic AFM fluctuations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the difference profile of elastic neutron scattering and the scan trajectory; Figure 2 shows the temperature dependence of net counts; Figure 3 shows the hole-concentration dependence of incommensurability across various cuprates.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of magnetism in the heavily overdoped regime, describes the experimental setup and sample preparation, presents neutron scattering results showing incommensurate peaks, discusses the temperature dependence and glassy nature of the magnetism, and concludes with a discussion on the mechanism of Fe-induced stabilization.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Elastic neutron scattering experiments showed incommensurate antiferromagnetic peaks in 5% Fe-substituted Bi-2201 cuprate in the non-superconducting heavily overdoped regime. The incommensurability delta~0.21 is comparable to that observed in Fe-substituted Bi-2201 in the overdoped regime. [Hiraka et al., Phys. Rev. B 81, 144501 (2010).] The magnetic correlation length is comparable between the overdoped and non-superconducting heavily overdoped regimes. It is plausible that incommensurate antiferromagnetic order is induced and stabilized by Fe in the heavily overdoped regime, which suggests a robust antiferromagnetic correlation beyond the superconducting dome in the phase diagram.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.17769"></a>
### [InterQ: Communication-Aware Scheduling Across Modular QPUs with Classical and Quantum Links](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17769v1)

**Authors:** Vinooth Kulkarni, Jaehyun Lee, Lauren Li, Aaron Orenstein, Xinpeng Li, Shuai Xu, Daniel Blankenberg, Vipin Chaudhary  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17769v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Rydberg arrays` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17769_figures/2605.17769_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1: Execution semantics of circuit cutting. In LO, cut fragments execute independently and are stitched through offline reconstruction. In LOCC, upstream measurement outcomes are transmitted over a classical link and trigger downstream conditional operations, creating explicit precedence between fragments.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17769_figures/2605.17769_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2: QComm abstraction used by InterQ. Remote execution across modules consumes Bell-pair resources managed by a shared Bell-pair distribution service, so latency depends on Bell-pair generation, success probability, and contention from concurrent link requests.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17769_figures/2605.17769_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3: Execution schedule comparison of IBM LOCC, Atom QComm, and Ionq QComm architectures for the 11-circuit workload.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17769_figures/2605.17769_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4: InterQ schedules for the MQT and QUEKO bench- mark workload under two communication models: (a) IonQ QComm, where scheduling is influenced by remote-operation demand and quantum-link availability, and (b) IBM adaptive LOCC mode, where no circuits are partitioned because all circuits fit within the available IBM QPU capacities.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.17769_figures/2605.17769_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5 compares the execution schedules, and Table III reports the corresponding makespan and fidelity-related met- rics. Serial Round Robin achieves better queue length, work- load changes, TiIF, and LPST, whereas InterQ reduces queue time, runtime, and wait time while improving TRF and TiRF through adaptive LOCC partitioning. The 142-qubit circuit cannot be placed directly on any 127-qubit backend and is therefore omitted from the Serial Round Robin sched- ule; InterQ partitions it into LOCC-compatible upstream and downstream fragments and schedules them across the modular backend pool.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper presents InterQ, a scheduler designed to manage quantum jobs across modular processors with different communication technologies. It demonstrates how to adaptively partition circuits to balance the costs of communication latency and sampling overhead. The study highlights how different hardware platforms like superconducting and trapped-ion systems offer distinct trade-offs between speed and fidelity.

**Why it may be interesting.** It addresses the critical scaling bottleneck of moving from monolithic to modular quantum computing, specifically focusing on the interplay between hardware-specific interconnects (photonic vs. classical) and computational throughput.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Efficiently scheduling and partitioning quantum workloads across modular QPU architectures that use heterogeneous communication links (classical vs. quantum).

**Main result.** The InterQ scheduler optimizes the trade-off between runtime, fidelity, and communication overhead, revealing that superconducting systems minimize runtime while neutral-atom systems achieve the highest fidelity.

**Method.** A communication-aware, iterative scheduling framework (InterQ) that jointly optimizes job placement, adaptive circuit cutting, and communication mode selection (LO, LOCC, or QComm) using a discrete-event simulation.

**Model / system.** Modular quantum architectures including superconducting (LOCC-based), trapped-ion (QComm-based), and neutral-atom (hierarchical/optical link) systems.

**Key observables.** Throughput, latency (makespan), fidelity (LPST, TiIF), queue time, and communication costs (Bell-pair consumption, synchronization delay).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Qubit capacity, communication bandwidth, entanglement generation rates, sampling overhead (scaling with number of cuts), and synchronization constraints.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes a shared quantum cloud environment where jobs compete for limited communication resources and treats entanglement generation as a resource-constrained service.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 contrasts LO vs. LOCC execution semantics; Figure 2 illustrates the QComm abstraction with shared Bell-pair resources.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of modular scheduling, defines the InterQ framework and its optimization objectives, details the hardware-specific communication models (LO, LOCC, QComm), presents a simulation-based evaluation across various hardware platforms and workloads, and concludes with an analysis of architecture-dependent trade-offs.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

As quantum computing scales toward practical workloads, future systems are expected to move beyond single monolithic processors toward modular architectures that connect multiple QPUs. Different platforms realize this modularity through different communication models: superconducting systems rely on real-time classical links and dynamic-circuit coordination, trapped-ion systems use photonic interconnects for remote entanglement, and neutral-atom systems provide strong intra-core connectivity with proposed optical links for inter-core communication. This heterogeneity makes communication-aware scheduling essential for shared modular quantum cloud environments.   We present InterQ, a communication-aware scheduler for modular QPU architectures with heterogeneous communication models. InterQ jointly considers qubit capacity, placement, parallel execution, and communication-driven dependencies across distributed subcircuits, while enabling adaptive circuit cutting to reduce makespan while balancing fidelity and communication overhead. The framework distinguishes classical-link execution, where measurement and feedforward impose synchronization constraints, from quantum-link execution, where entanglement distribution and state transfer determine coordination cost.   Using a unified simulation framework to compare superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral-atom modular systems, InterQ shows how communication models and scheduler-driven cutting decisions affect throughput, latency, and fidelity. Across evaluated workloads, InterQ exposes an architecture-dependent tradeoff: neutral-atom modular QPUs achieve the highest fidelity, superconducting systems minimize runtime, and trapped-ion systems provide a balanced intermediate profile across fidelity and makespan.

</details>

### [qstack: Compositional End-to-End Compilation for Fault-Tolerant Quantum Programs](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16595v1)

**Authors:** Andres Paz, Dan Grossman  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16595v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.16595_figures/2605.16595_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Distribution of measurement outcomes from 1000 simulation runs of the repeat-until-success program. Bitstrings of length n occur with probability 1/2n, confirming the expected geometric decay.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.16595_figures/2605.16595_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Composability of two compiler passes. Left: the wrapping structure after pass 1 and pass 2 each wrap the original callback. Right: runtime data flow when a measurement triggers the outermost wrapper. Decoders run outside-in (pass 2 then pass 1); if the callback returns a kernel, compilers run inside-out (pass 1 then pass 2).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.16595_figures/2605.16595_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5. Compilation graph and example pipelines. Each edge is a compiler pass. QEC passes (loop) preserve the Cliffords ISA and can be applied repeatedly for concatenation.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces qstack, a new compiler framework designed to automate the complex task of composing quantum error correction layers with hardware-specific instructions. By treating classical feedback as opaque callbacks, it allows for a seamless, automated pipeline that handles the transition from high-level logical gates to physical hardware operations. This approach significantly reduces the manual effort required to design and implement multi-layered fault-tolerant quantum programs.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of composing multiple layers of quantum error correction (QEC) and hardware-specific ISA translations without manual, error-prone adaptation of the classical feedback logic required for syndrome measurement.

**Main result.** The development of qstack, a compiler framework that uses a callback wrapping mechanism to enable automated, compositional, end-to-end compilation from high-level gates to hardware-native instructions.

**Method.** A kernel-based intermediate representation (IR) where classical logic is accessed via opaque callbacks, which are automatically wrapped by compiler passes to decode measurement results and re-compile dynamically generated kernels.

**Model / system.** A fault-tolerant quantum computing framework supporting various gate sets (Toy, Clifford) and error-correcting codes (bit-flip, phase-flip, Steane, and Shor codes) targeting platforms like trapped-ion and neutral-atom hardware.

**Key observables.** Measurement stack (classical outcomes) and kernel stack (hierarchy of nested kernels and qubit allocations).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The classical logic is treated as an opaque callback; the framework assumes a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) discipline for measurements.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows gate definitions; Figure 2 shows a histogram of a repeat-until-success program; Figure 3 illustrates the wrapper structure and runtime data flow; Figure 4 defines the operational semantics; Figure 5 shows a compilation graph.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of hybrid quantum-classical compilation, presents the qstack framework and its callback wrapping mechanism, provides formal operational semantics, demonstrates end-to-end compilation pipelines including concatenated QEC codes, and discusses limitations and future integration with OpenQASM 3.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Compiling quantum programs for fault-tolerant execution requires transforming high-level operations through multiple abstraction layers: from logical gates to error-corrected encodings to hardware-native instructions. A key challenge is that quantum error correction turns purely quantum programs into hybrid quantum-classical programs, where classical feedback from syndrome measurements drives quantum corrections at runtime. Existing compilation frameworks handle these quantum and classical components separately, requiring manual adaptation of classical logic at each compilation stage, all while preserving program semantics. We present qstack, a compiler framework built around a purely quantum intermediate representation in which classical logic is accessed only through opaque callbacks, written in any classical language. The framework's central mechanism, callback wrapping, enables compositional compilation: each compiler pass automatically adapts both quantum operations and their associated classical callbacks, and any kernel dynamically generated by a callback is compiled through the full pipeline. This allows ISA translation and quantum error correction to be expressed as composable compiler passes, including concatenation of error-correcting codes, without manual intervention. We demonstrate end-to-end compilation from a high-level gate set through Clifford gates to trapped-ion native operations, with bit-flip and phase-flip repetition codes, the Steane code, and the Shor code obtained by composing two repetition passes.

</details>

**Authors:** Hussain Gohar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18551v1>

**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

**Summary.** This paper provides a unified geometric origin for various modified black hole entropy models by linking them to specific scalar-tensor gravity theories. It shows that these information-theoretic entropy proposals correspond to a varying effective gravitational coupling and specific scalar potentials that drive distinct cosmological phenomena like inflation and dark energy.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the lack of a consistent geometric/gravitational theory for generalized black hole entropy functionals (such as Barrow or Tsallis-Cirto entropy) and seeks to resolve the thermodynamic inconsistencies that arise when these entropies are used without a proper gravitational framework.

**Main result.** The author establishes a direct correspondence between various generalized entropy proposals and scalar-tensor gravity theories, demonstrating that these entropies can be realized through a scale-dependent effective gravitational coupling and specific scalar potentials.

**Method.** The study uses conformal transformations between Jordan and Einstein frames, evaluates the Wald Noether-charge entropy, and maps the Misner-Sharp quasilocal mass to a generalized mass-to-horizon relation.

**Model / system.** The framework involves scalar-tensor gravity theories in both Jordan and Einstein frames, applied to spherically symmetric spacetimes and spatially flat FLRW cosmologies, incorporating entropy models like Barrow, Tsallis-Cirto, and entanglement-induced corrections.

**Key observables.** Spectral index (ns), tensor-to-scalar ratio (r), time variation of the gravitational constant (G-dot/G), and primordial power spectrum modifications.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Entropy parameters (m, beta, alpha, delta, Delta), the Brans-Dich parameter (omega), and the horizon scale (L or x).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes the validity of the mass-to-horizon relation and focuses on the quasi-static regime where scalar kinetic and matter energy density contributions are subdominant to the scalar potential.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the generalized entropy framework, moves to the geometric realization via scalar-tensor gravity, derives specific scalar potentials for different entropy models, analyzes the resulting cosmological implications (inflation and dark energy), and concludes with observational constraints.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a geometric realization of a broad class of generalized black hole entropy functionals by establishing their direct correspondence with the Misner$-$Sharp quasilocal mass and the Wald Noether$-$charge entropy in scalar$-$tensor theories of gravity. The resulting models feature a scale-dependent effective gravitational coupling, whose functional dependence is determined by the underlying entropy parameters. Within this framework, we derive explicit Einstein-frame scalar potentials: for Barrow entropy, a steep exponential potential; for Tsallis$-$Cirto entropy, an exponential potential governed by the nonextensivity parameter; and for quantum-gravity and entanglement$-$induced corrections, an approximately linear potential. These distinct potentials generate characteristic cosmological phenomenology, with implications for inflationary dynamics, late-time dark-energy behavior, and non-singular bouncing cosmologies. The framework is compatible with current constraints from solar-system tests, big-bang nucleosynthesis, and pulsar-timing observations, and it yields predictions that can be probed by forthcoming observational surveys. In this way, the analysis establishes a unified and geometrically grounded connection between information$-$theoretic entropy proposals and gravitational field theory.

</details>
