<a id="paper-2605.17217"></a>
### [Toward Near-Real-Time Marine Oil Spill Detection in SAR Imagery using Quantum-Assisted SVM](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17217v1)

**Authors:** Joseph Strauss, Jyotsna Sharma  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17217v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17217_figures/2605.17217_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Qualitative comparison of segmentation performance across classical and quantum architectures. Top row: High-fidelity segmentation on an easily separable spill. Middle row: Intermediate performance exhibiting partial boundary degradation. Bottom row: Poor segmentation demonstrating susceptibility to complex speckle noise and look-alikes. Columns from left to right: SAR Image, Ground Truth Mask, Classical SVM prediction, QSVM (Annealing) prediction, and QSVM (Gate-Based) prediction.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17217_figures/2605.17217_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Strait of Hormuz oil spill segmentation results. Columns from left to right: SAR Image, Ground Truth Mask, Classical SVM prediction, QSVM (Annealing) prediction, and QSVM (Gate-Based) prediction.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a quantum-assisted machine learning pipeline for the rapid detection of oil spills in satellite radar imagery. By using quantum annealing to optimize SVMs, the researchers achieved high-accuracy segmentation with much faster inference speeds than traditional gate-based quantum approaches. The method proved robust and transferable to geographically different maritime regions.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving near-real-time, low-latency marine oil spill detection in SAR imagery while overcoming the high computational costs and data requirements of deep learning.

**Main result.** A quantum-assisted SVM bagging ensemble achieved performance (IoU 0.60, balanced accuracy 0.89) comparable to classical baselines, with annealing-based approaches offering superior inference efficiency over gate-based methods.

**Method.** A pixel-wise bagging ensemble of SVMs where quantum annealing is used to optimize support vectors via a QUBO formulation, and gate-based quantum computing is used for quantum kernel methods.

**Model / system.** The framework uses a pixel-wise bagging ensemble of 500 base learners applied to 5D feature vectors from Sentinel-1 SAR imagery, utilizing D-Wave quantum annealing hardware and gate-based quantum circuit simulations.

**Key observables.** Intersection-over-Union (IoU), F1-score, balanced accuracy, inference time, and training time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 5D feature vectors (VV intensity, VH/VV ratio, local VV entropy, local VV standard deviation, and Sobel gradient magnitude); 256x256 image resolution.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes that small, randomly sampled subsets of data can effectively represent the full dataset through a bagging ensemble; notes the emulator-to-hardware gap due to noise and connectivity.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a qualitative comparison of segmentation masks (Ground Truth, Classical, Annealing, and Gate-based) across different prediction qualities; Figure 2 shows qualitative segmentation results for an independent oil spill in the Strait of Hormuz.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the environmental problem and limitations of deep learning, proposes a quantum-assisted SVM ensemble, details the annealing and gate-based implementations, evaluates performance on Sentinel-1 data, compares computational trade-offs, and tests generalization on a geographically distinct dataset.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Marine oil spills require rapid detection to mitigate severe ecological and economic damage. While satellite-based Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) provides essential all-weather monitoring, analyzing this data remains challenging. Deep learning models often require massive datasets and incur high latency. To address this, a pixel-wise quantum-assisted Support Vector Machine (QSVM) bagging ensemble is developed. Quantum annealing is leveraged to optimize the support vectors of individual weak SVMs on small data subsets, which are then classically aggregated.   The approach is evaluated on Sentinel-1 imagery using both quantum simulation and physical quantum annealing hardware. The quantum-assisted pipeline achieved performance comparable to a rigorous classical baseline, yielding an Intersection-over-Union (IoU) of 0.60 and a balanced accuracy of 0.89. Complementary experiments with gate-based quantum computing demonstrated similar segmentation accuracy, although the annealing approach offered superior inference efficiency. Generalization was further assessed on independent oil spill imagery from the Strait of Hormuz, demonstrating the potential transferability of the trained pipeline to geographically distinct spill events. These results establish the feasibility of quantum-assisted, segmentation pipelines for near-real-time environmental monitoring.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper reports the discovery of a 5/9 magnetization plateau in the kagome antiferromagnet YCOB using ultrahigh magnetic fields up to 120 T. By applying tensor-network simulations, the authors demonstrate that an anisotropic 3J-type Heisenberg model explains the observed magnetization and predicts a spin supersolid phase.

**Why it may be interesting.** The discovery of a spin supersolid phase and the study of fractional magnetization plateaus in a frustrated kagome lattice provide deep insights into many-body dynamics and the emergence of exotic broken-symmetry phases in strongly correlated systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to determine the high-field magnetic behavior of the kagome antiferromagnet YCOB and investigate the existence of higher-order fractional magnetization plateaus and exotic phases like spin supersolidity.

**Main result.** The researchers discovered a previously unobserved 5/9 fractional magnetization plateau and identified a spin supersolid phase between the 1/3 and 5/9 plateaus using an anisotropic 3J-type Heisenberg model.

**Method.** High-precision magnetization measurements were performed using the single-turn coil technique up to 120 T, and experimental data were modeled using DMRG and iPEPS tensor-network calculations.

**Model / system.** The system is the kagome lattice antiferromagnet YCu3(OD)7-xBr2+x (YCOB) with tunable Br-concentration. The model is an effective 3J-type Heisenberg Hamiltonian with three spatially anisotropic exchange couplings (JR, JG, JB).

**Key observables.** Magnetization (M) as a function of magnetic field (H), differential susceptibility (dM/dH), and local spin expectation values.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Br-concentration (x), exchange couplings (JR, JG, JB), magnetic field up to 120 T, and the g-factor (~2.2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is modeled using a 3J-type Heisenberg model to account for lattice distortions caused by OH/Br site mixing.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows magnetization and susceptibility curves; Fig 4 shows calibration of high-field data; Fig 5 displays loss maps for DMRG and iPEPS parameter space; Fig 6 compares experimental M-H curves with numerical results.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the YCOB system, presents high-field experimental magnetization data, describes the 3J-type model and numerical fitting procedure, discusses the composition-dependent evolution of plateaus, and proposes the spin supersolid phase.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We performed high-precision magnetization measurements up to 120~T on three compositions of the newly discovered kagome antiferromagnet YCu$_3$(OD)$_{7-x}$Br$_{2+x}$ (YCOB), revealing a previously unobserved 5/9 fractional magnetization plateau. All YCOB samples with different Br$^-$ concentrations exhibit nearly identical magnetization curves below 60~T, whereas the 5/9 plateau appears at markedly different fields in the ultrahigh-field regime. By modeling the experimental data using tensor-network calculations, we derive the effective spin Hamiltonians for the YCOB family with three spatially anisotropic Heisenberg couplings (the 3$J$-type model), which quantitatively reproduces the measured magnetization processes and captures the composition-dependent evolution of the 5/9 plateau. Furthermore, our theoretical analysis suggests the emergence of a spin supersolid phase in the field window between the 1/3 and 5/9 plateaus, which is sensitive to spin exchange parameters and accounts for the significant variation in the critical fields of the 5/9 plateau observed among different YCOB compositions.

</details>

### [A Fourier perspective on the learning dynamics of neural networks: from sample complexities to mechanistic insights](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16913v1)

**Authors:** Fabiola Ricci, Claudia Merger, Sebastian Goldt  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16913v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** The paper explains why deep neural networks can efficiently learn complex natural images despite the inherent difficulty of learning phase information. It shows that the power-law frequency spectrum of natural images acts as a mechanism to accelerate the learning of higher-order correlations.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the simplicity bias in neural network learning dynamics, specifically how networks prioritize learning amplitude (pair-wise) information before phase (higher-order) information.

**Main result.** Proves that learning phase information is computationally hard (cubic sample complexity) for isotropic inputs, but power-law decaying spectra in natural images dramatically accelerate this learning process.

**Method.** Uses a Fourier-based synthetic data model, Hermite polynomial expansions of the loss and likelihood ratio, and analysis of the stochastic dynamics of online SGD via the information exponent and population drift.

**Model / system.** A Fourier data model with translation-invariant, non-Gaussian inputs where class differences are encoded in the phases of the Discrete Fourier Transform, featuring both isotropic and power-law decaying covariance matrices.

**Key observables.** Test loss, average label assignment, overlaps with DFT phase vectors (alpha_u, alpha_v), and the information exponent (k*).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Sample complexity (n), dimension (N), power-law decay exponent (alpha), signal-to-noise ratio (beta), and learning rate (delta_N).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Activation functions are continuously differentiable and even; inputs are translation-invariant; analysis focuses on the high-dimensional limit (N to infinity).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 demonstrates phase importance via image reconstruction; Figure 2 shows SGD failure/success in isotropic regimes; Figure 4 illustrates the principal subspace and loss landscape changes in power-law regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Fourier perspective, establishes the hardness of phase learning in isotropic settings, introduces the power-law model to show learning acceleration, and provides theoretical proofs and experimental validation on ImageNet/CIFAR.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Neural networks trained with gradient-based methods exhibit a strong simplicity bias: they learn simpler statistical features of their data before moving to more complex features. Previous analyses of this phenomenon have largely focused on settings with (quasi-)isotropic inputs. In this work, we study the simplicity bias from a Fourier perspective, which allows us to include two key features of natural images in the analysis: approximate translation-invariance and power-law spectra. We first show experimentally that simple neural networks trained on image classification tasks first rely on amplitude information -- related to pair-wise correlations between pixels -- before exploiting phase information, which encodes edges and higher-order correlations. In view of this, we introduce a synthetic data model for translation-invariant inputs that allows precise control over amplitudes and phases while remaining tractable. We rigorously establish that for isotropic and high-dimensional inputs, classification based on phase information alone is a genuinely hard task: online stochastic gradient descent (SGD) cannot distinguish the structured inputs from noise within $n \ll N^3$ steps, but needs at least $n \gg N^3 \log^2{N}$ steps. In contrast, we show both experimentally and theoretically that power-law spectra can dramatically accelerate the speed of learning phase information, even if the spectra do not help with classification. Simulations with two-layer networks trained on textures and with deep convolutional networks on ImageNet and CIFAR100 confirm this non-trivial interaction between amplitudes and phases, providing mechanistic insights into how deep neural networks can learn natural image distributions efficiently.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17944v1>

**Summary.** This paper proposes new algorithms for managing distributed quantum computing tasks in the cloud. By optimizing for fidelity, time, and network costs, the methods significantly reduce delays and communication overhead in NISQ-era distributed networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Efficient resource allocation for distributed quantum workflows in cloud-based environments, specifically addressing the limitations of priority-based protocols in handling large-scale, multi-task applications.

**Main result.** The proposed SoftIso and RandomAware algorithms achieve significant improvements in execution time (~5%), communication overhead (~30%), and wait time (~40%) compared to existing methods like CloudQC.

**Method.** The study introduces SoftIso, an extended graph isomorphism-based matching algorithm with early stopping, and RandomAware, a randomized selection algorithm, to optimize a multi-objective cost function.

**Model / system.** A hybrid classical-quantum network architecture consisting of a Quantum Resource Manager and distributed quantum nodes (QPUs) with specific error models, connectivity, and quantum memory.

**Key observables.** Fidelity, execution time, communication overhead, wait time, and task completion percentage.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Qubit count, circuit depth, gate counts (1-qubit and 2-qubit), readout error, CLOPS, and network latency.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes a hybrid quantum-classical network rather than a purely quantum network and focuses on allocating standalone programs rather than partitioning single large circuits.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a GHZ state circuit; Figure 2 depicts the cloud service architecture; Table I compares the proposed method to others; Figures 5-9 show trends in communication cost, fidelity, workload distribution, and execution/wait times.

**Paper structure.** The paper defines the scientific problem, establishes the system architecture and mathematical model, introduces the proposed SoftIso and RandomAware algorithms, details the simulation framework using IBM hardware data, and concludes with performance analysis and scalability results.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Rapid advancements in cloud based platforms providing access to quantum computing capabilities have opened up several challenges for efficient usage of these highly delicate and costly devices. Although most of the current systems use a priority based access protocol, they are unable to fully support reliable, efficient, and scalable execution of larger-scale applications. To overcome this limitation, we propose a comprehensive solution for efficient allocation of quantum programs to appropriate quantum devices, considering all the relevant cost metrics into account including, fidelity, execution time and communication overhead. We also formulate use-cases for distributed quantum workflow and propose modified graph based algorithms to solve for allocation of such use-cases, assuming a hybrid classical-quantum network. Since hardware advancements in large standalone devices is an ongoing process, it is critical to investigate such distributed workflows to maximize the best utilization of current NISQ devices. Our empirical study shows that the proposed techniques perform better than state-of-the-art methods for almost all evaluation parameters, with average improvements of approximately $5\%$ in execution time, $30\%$ in communication overhead, $40\%$ in wait time and $2\%$ in fidelity, providing better solutions to efficient allocation strategies.

</details>

**Authors:** Tianyu Li, Xin Tang, Sheng Liu, Haiping Hu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17953v1>

For (a) and (b), the extracted critical disorder strength and correlation-length exponent are Wc = 12.9 ± 0.1 and ν = 1.54 ± 0.04, with spectral dimension dS = 2.89. For (c) and (d), corresponding to (η, p) = (0, 0.4), we obtain Wc = 10.2 ± 0.2 and ν = 1.59 ± 0.06, with dS = 2.90. The averages are taken over the central...</sub>

The blue and red curves represent quadratic fits to the respective datasets, with Wc = 2.83(dS − 2) + 13.11(dS −2)2 and Wc = 4.71(dS −2) + 9.23(dS −2)2</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores how the Anderson localization transition behaves in non-integer dimensions using a tunable 3D fractal lattice model. It demonstrates that the spectral dimension is the key driver of the transition's universality class and provides a quantitative scaling relation for the critical exponent. The findings offer a new framework for studying quantum criticality in complex, non-Euclidean geometries.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the nature of the Anderson transition and the existence of mobility edges in 3D fractal lattices with continuously tunable spectral dimensions.

**Main result.** The spectral dimension (ds) primarily determines the universality class of the Anderson transition, with the critical exponent (nu) following an approximate inverse dependence on ds.

**Method.** Large-scale finite-size scaling analysis of the level-spacing ratio and mean fractal dimension using numerical simulations of the discrete Laplace equation and Anderson Hamiltonian.

**Model / system.** A family of 3D fractal lattices generated via a dielectric breakdown model (NPW model) with a Hamiltonian featuring hopping amplitude t=1 and uniform onsite disorder W.

**Key observables.** Average level-spacing ratio <r>, mean fractal dimension <D2>, critical disorder strength Wc, and critical exponent nu.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spectral dimension (ds), Hausdorff dimension (dH), disorder strength (W), and growth parameters (eta and p).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is studied in the thermodynamic limit via finite-size scaling; the lattice is constructed using a specific dielectric breakdown model.

**Figures summary.** The figures illustrate the evolution of the level-spacing ratio and mean fractal dimension across different disorder strengths and lattice configurations, demonstrating the transition and mobility edges.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the fractal lattice construction, describes the numerical methods for dimension extraction and localization analysis, presents the results regarding the Anderson transition and mobility edges, and discusses the scaling relations and universality.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Anderson localization is fundamentally controlled by dimensionality, yet the nature of the Anderson transition in continuously tunable noninteger dimensions remains largely unexplored. Here, we introduce a family of three-dimensional fractal lattices with continuously tunable spectral dimension $d_s\in[2,3]$, providing a controlled platform for studying localization physics beyond integer dimensions and across the lower critical dimension $d_s=2$. Using large-scale finite-size scaling analysis, we systematically investigate the Anderson transition and identify mobility edges throughout the fractal family. The critical disorder strength evolves continuously from $0$ to $16.6$ as the spectral dimension increases from $2$ to $3$. We show that the spectral dimension predominantly governs the universality class of the transition, while the precise critical point is additionally influenced by microscopic geometric details of the underlying fractal lattice. The critical exponent exhibits an approximate inverse dependence on $d_s$, providing quantitative insight into scaling theory in noninteger dimensions. Our results establish tunable fractal lattices as a versatile framework for exploring localization and quantum critical phenomena beyond conventional integer-dimensional systems.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.17338"></a>
### [At Most Two Infinite Blue Clusters in the CMR Representation of the Edwards-Anderson Spin Glass](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17338v1)

**Authors:** Yan Ru Pei  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17338v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17338_figures/2605.17338_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: The box-resampling lemma. The event A3(Λ) is expressed using exterior arms and is therefore measurable from FΛc. Conditional on this outside event, Bs(Λ) has positive probability and merges all exterior blue components in the overlap class q = s that touch ∂Λ. The dotted blue arms indicate exterior blue components in the opposite overlap class; they are shown only as context for the chosen q = s resampling.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous mathematical upper bound on the number of infinite clusters in the geometric representation of a spin glass. It proves that the number of infinite blue clusters is at most two, supporting the two-cluster picture suggested by mean-field theory and numerical simulations.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the geometric structure and the maximum number of infinite blue clusters in the Chayes-Machta-Redner (CMR) representation of the Edwards-Anderson spin glass.

**Main result.** Proves that there are at most two infinite blue clusters in the CMR representation; if two exist, they must belong to opposite overlap-parity classes and reside within the same infinite grey cluster.

**Method.** Rigorous mathematical proof using Bayesian resampling of couplings, finite-box merge operations, mass-transport bounds on ends of subgraphs, and Peierls-type estimates.

**Model / system.** The Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass model on a hypercubic lattice Z^d, characterized by random i.i.d. couplings J_e that are continuous at zero and two-sided.

**Key observables.** Number of infinite blue clusters, overlap parity classes, grey cluster percolation, and spin overlap parameter q.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Inverse temperature beta, dimension d, and the overlap sign s in {+1, -1}.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes translation-invariant joint Gibbs measures; the results provide an upper bound on cluster multiplicity but do not prove the existence of a spin-glass phase transition.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the Box-resampling lemma, showing how exterior blue components in a specific overlap class are merged through a resampled finite box.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes a joint Gibbs measure for the CMR representation, uses Bayesian resampling to prove grey cluster percolation and uniqueness, and then applies a box-resampling lemma and mass-transport bounds to constrain the number of blue clusters.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The two-replica Chayes-Machta-Redner (CMR) representation is one of the main proposed geometric signatures of spin-glass order in the short-range Edwards-Anderson model. Mean-field arguments and recent numerics suggest that the low-temperature phase should exhibit two macroscopic blue clusters carrying opposite overlap signs. We prove a rigorous structural constraint in this direction. For any translation-invariant joint Gibbs measure on disorder, two spin replicas, and CMR bond variables on Z^d, the blue subgraph contains at most two infinite connected components; if two exist, then they lie in a common infinite grey cluster and belong to opposite overlap-parity classes. The main obstacle is that the blue-bond process is neither insertion-tolerant nor positively associated, so the usual Burton-Keane and random-cluster arguments do not apply. We circumvent this by working in the full joint measure and using a finite-box merge operation together with the mass-transport bound on ends of translation-invariant subgraphs. As auxiliary input, we establish finite energy and a percolation transition for the grey subgraph via Bayesian resampling of couplings and a parity-based Peierls estimate. These results do not prove the existence of infinite blue clusters or a spin-glass phase transition, but they give a rigorous upper bound compatible with the two-cluster picture for short-range spin glasses.

</details>

### [Basis-free neural-network geminal and Jastrow factors for variational Monte Carlo](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17083v1)

**Authors:** Jan Kessler, Thomas D. Kühne  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17083v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<sub>Figure 2b resolves the remaining deviations. The rela- tive error increases below 2 Bohr, i.e., around and below the equilibrium distance of about 1.4 Bohr, reaching ap- proximately 0.0025% or 0.025 mHa. At larger separations the error remains close to 0.001%. This behavior dif- fers from many conventional single-reference electronic- structure methods, which are often accurate near equi- librium but deteriorate for stretched H2 as static correla- tion becomes important [19]. For a two-electron singlet, however, this distinction is less severe than in larger sys- tems: in a finite orbital basis, the coupled-cluster singles- and-doubles excitation manifold already spans the full-CI space...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17083_figures/2605.17083_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. H2: VMC energies (a) and relative energy differences (b) of NNAGP + all-body-NNJF for different H–H distances, compared with reference energies [44].</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.17083_figures/2605.17083_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. H4: VMC energies of NNWFs for different H–H distances in the x direction, with ry = 2.4 Bohr. FCI energies [5] are shown as reference values.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The authors develop a new 'basis-free' neural-network wave function that uses neural networks to replace traditional basis sets in an AGP-Jastrow ansatz. This method allows researchers to clearly distinguish between errors caused by imperfect nodal surfaces and those caused by insufficient dynamical correlation. It achieves near-exact accuracy for simple systems and provides a scalable framework for studying electronic correlation.

**Why it may be interesting.** It presents a highly interpretable neural-network wave function that avoids the 'black-box' nature of other architectures by explicitly separating the nodal surface from correlation recovery, providing a diagnostic tool for many-body wave function accuracy.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of accurately describing electronic ground-state wave functions by balancing the treatment of fermionic antisymmetry, dynamical correlation, and static (multireference) correlation without the computational bottleneck of traditional basis-set expansions.

**Main result.** The proposed basis-free Jastrow-AGP ansatz achieves sub-millihartree accuracy for H2 and compact H4 systems, successfully distinguishing between errors from the Jastrow factor (dynamical) and the AGP nodal surface (static).

**Method.** Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) optimization of a neural-network wave function that replaces conventional basis sets with feed-forward neural networks for both the geminal (AGP) and Jastrow components.

**Model / system.** The study uses the hydrogen molecule (H2) and the rectangular hydrogen tetramer (H4) as benchmark systems to test the balance of dynamical and static correlation.

**Key observables.** Electronic ground-state energies, relative energy differences compared to reference data (FCI, DMC, FermiNet), and CPU time for optimization.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Neural network hidden units (N_G, N_J), nuclear coordinates (bond distances r_x, r_y), and cusp-correction parameters (b_ee, b_en).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The accuracy is limited by the fixed nodal surface of the AGP part; the all-body Jastrow factor relies on an approximate exchange symmetry that emerges during minimization.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the optimization protocol; Figure 2 displays H2 dissociation energy curves; Figure 3 illustrates the H4 geometry; Figure 4 shows H4 energy as a function of distance; Figure 6 compares energy errors vs. network size; Figure 7 shows CPU time scaling with network size.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a new neural-network wave function ansatz, describes the architecture and optimization protocol, presents validation on H2, uses H4 as a benchmark for correlation types, analyzes scaling and efficiency, and discusses limitations regarding nodal flexibility and size consistency.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Neural-network quantum states offer a flexible route to compact many-electron wave functions, but their practical accuracy depends strongly on how fermionic antisymmetry, electron correlation, and optimization noise are treated. Here we combine an antisymmetrized geminal power (AGP) determinant with feed-forward neural networks that replace conventional basis-set expansions in the geminal and in two Jastrow-factor constructions. The resulting basis-free Jastrow--AGP ansatz is optimized by variational Monte Carlo and is designed to separate two tasks: the AGP part defines the nodal surface, while the neural-network Jastrow factor recovers dynamical correlation at fixed nodes. This separation makes it possible to distinguish errors associated with dynamical correlation from those caused by static, multireference correlation. Applications to the hydrogen molecule and the rectangular hydrogen tetramer show sub-millihartree accuracy when the AGP nodes are adequate, and expose the residual nodal limitation near the large-radius square geometry of the hydrogen tetramer. These results clarify where neural-network building blocks can improve a compact geminal ansatz and where additional nodal flexibility is required.

</details>

### [Bipolaronic High-Temperature Superconductivity from Phonon-Modulated Hopping: A Perspective](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16625v1)

**Authors:** John Sous  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16625v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.16625_figures/2605.16625_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Schematic illustration of the breakdown of Migdal–Eliashberg theory at inter- mediate λ and the resulting bound Tc ≲Ω/10 on phonon-mediated superconductivity out of a Fermi liquid.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.16625_figures/2605.16625_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Density coupling: a phonon ˆXi attached to each lattice site couples to the local electron density ˆni. Adding an electron displaces the oscillator and digs a deep self-trapping well; the resulting polaron, and the bipolaron formed from two of them, is heavy.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.16625_figures/2605.16625_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Bipolaron effective mass m⋆ BP/(2me) in the one-dimensional site-Peierls model (blue) compared with the Holstein model (red), as a function of the dimensionless electron– phonon coupling λ at Ω∼W. The Peierls bipolaron remains light up to and beyond λ = 2, while the Holstein bipolaron is exponentially heavy. Adapted from Ref.19</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.16625_figures/2605.16625_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Cartoon of the phonon-mediated electron-pair-hopping process generated by the site-Peierls coupling of Eq. (3) in the antiadiabatic limit Ω/t ≫1. A virtual phonon emitted by one electron during a hop is absorbed by a neighboring electron during a second hop, with the net effect of moving a singlet pair as a unit.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.16625_figures/2605.16625_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Left: Cartoon of the bond-Peierls model: oscillators sit on the bonds of the square lattice (open ions), and their displacements ˆX⟨i,j⟩modulate the hopping of electrons between neighboring sites (filled circles). Right: The bond-Peierls coupling in the iron pnictides. Pnictogen oscillations out of the Fe plane modulate the electron tunneling between neighboring iron atoms; destructive interference between the direct dxy–dxy hop and the indirect Fe–pnictogen–Fe path produces a small net hopping with a large coupling to the pnictogen displacement. Adapted from Refs.32</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This review explores a mechanism for high-temperature superconductivity where phonons modulate electron hopping instead of density. By using the bond-Peierls model, the author shows that superconductivity can bypass the traditional temperature limits because the resulting bipolarons remain light and mobile. This provides a theoretical framework for understanding high-Tc in materials like iron-based pnictides.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether phonon-mediated superconductivity can exceed the conventional transition temperature ceiling (Tc < Omega/10) by avoiding the formation of heavy, immobile bipolarons.

**Main result.** The author demonstrates that in the bond-Peierls model, where phonons modulate electron hopping rather than density, light bipolarons form an s-wave superconductor with a Tc that significantly exceeds the conventional bound and is robust against Coulomb repulsion.

**Method.** The study utilizes sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo simulations, semi-classical instanton analysis to calculate effective mass, and second-order perturbation theory in the antiadiabatic limit.

**Model / system.** The primary model is the bond-Peierls (or SSH) model on square and cubic lattices, where lattice distortions modulate the electron hopping amplitude, contrasted with the standard Holstein density-coupled model.

**Key observables.** Transition temperature (Tc), bipolaron effective mass (m*BP), bipolaron binding energy (Delta_BP), and bipolaron mean squared radius (R2_BP).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimensionless electron-phonon coupling (lambda), phonon frequency (Omega), hopping amplitude (t), adiabaticity ratio (t/Omega), and Hubbard repulsion (U).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a dilute liquid of bipolarons and uses a linearized expansion of hopping about equilibrium; it neglects higher-order interaction terms that might affect the density limit.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the breakdown of Migdal-Eliashberg theory; Figure 2 illustrates the Holstein model; Figure 3 compares bipolaron effective mass; Figure 5 provides a cartoon of the bond-Peierls model applied to iron pnictides.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the conventional Tc ceiling, introduces the Peierls/bond-Peierls model as a solution, presents numerical QMC results showing light bipolarons and high Tc, discusses the role of Coulomb repulsion, and concludes with material design principles.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Phonon-mediated superconductivity is conventionally thought to be capped at a transition temperature $T_{\mathrm{c}}$ no larger than roughly one-tenth of the phonon frequency $Ω$, a bound rooted in the breakdown of Migdal-Eliashberg theory at intermediate coupling and in the heaviness of bipolarons formed in standard models with phonons that couple to the electron density. In this review I describe a route to phonon-mediated high-$T_{\mathrm{c}}$ superconductivity that bypasses this bound. The key ingredient is a class of electron-phonon couplings in which lattice distortions modulate the electron hopping and therefore its kinetic energy rather than its potential energy, known as the Peierls model (also known as Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model). In these models phonon exchange generates an interaction that binds two electrons into a small but unusually light bipolaron. Using sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo simulations of a bond-Peierls model on the square and cubic lattices, my collaborators and I have shown that a dilute liquid of such bipolarons forms an $s$-wave superconductor with a $T_{\mathrm{c}}/Ω$ that significantly exceeds the conventional bound, that this conclusion is robust against screened Coulomb repulsion, and that $T_{\mathrm{c}}/Ω$ -- despite being reduced -- remains above bound in presence of strong long-range Coulomb repulsion. A semi-classical instanton analysis explains why, at strong coupling, bipolarons in models with phonon-modulated hopping are lighter than their density-coupled (Holstein) counterparts. I close with a discussion of materials in which this physics may be operative, in particular the iron-based pnictide superconductors, and of design principles that follow from it.

</details>

### [Bogoliubov sum rules and the Knight-shift ellipsoid in noncentrosymmetric superconductors](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17030v1)

**Authors:** Yi Zhou  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17030v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17030_figures/2605.17030_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

The faint dashed sphere is the normal-state KN reference; the blue solid ellipsoid is the T = 0 shape with semi-axes Ki(0)/KN = 1 −πi, so πi = 1 collapses the ellipsoid along axis i and πi = 0 leaves it touching the reference sphere. (a) Singlet with strong 3D SOC at the simplex barycenter, isotropically suppressed to 2</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17030_figures/2605.17030_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Simplex of allowed T = 0 Knight-shift ellipsoids in the strong-locking regime, parametrized by the eigenvalues (π1, π2, π3) of Π with P</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper introduces a geometric framework to classify pairing symmetries in noncentrosymmetric superconductors by relating the zero-temperature Knight shift to the spin-locking texture of the Fermi surface. It provides a set of experimental protocols to distinguish between various singlet and triplet pairing states. The theory is validated by analyzing NMR data on K2Cr3As3, revealing a specific c-axis spin-locking mechanism.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the pairing symmetry and spin-orbit coupling (SOC) texture in noncentrosymmetric superconductors using the residual T=0 Knight shift.

**Main result.** Derivation of a central theorem where the T=0 Knight shift tensor is determined solely by the Fermi-surface average of the spin-locking direction, independent of pairing symmetry or gap magnitude, and application to K2Cr3As3 to identify a c-axis locking texture.

**Method.** Use of a Bogoliubov sum rule within a BdG framework to establish geometric identities and the development of six experimental protocols for diagnosing pairing classes via a Knight-shift ellipsoid.

**Model / system.** Noncentrosymmetric superconductors, specifically the K2Cr3As3 system, modeled using a single-band unitary Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) framework with antisymmetric spin-orbit coupling.

**Key observables.** Residual T=0 Knight shift tensor, spin susceptibility, spin-lattice relaxation rate (1/T1), and the Knight-shift ellipsoid.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spin-locking direction (n_k), SOC strength (lambda), Zeeman field (h), and the projection tensor (Pi_munu).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary derivation assumes the strong-locking regime and unitary pairing; the application assumes a single-band model and hyperfine-invariant coupling.

**Figures summary.** Figure 4 shows a numerical cross-check of the derived closed-form theory against digitized endpoints of s-wave curves, demonstrating high precision.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes a central Bogoliubov sum rule, derives static bounds and the Knight-shift ellipsoid geometry, explores departures in intermediate SOC and finite Zeeman fields, develops dynamical counterparts (FGT sum rule), and concludes with experimental protocols applied to K2Cr3As3 data.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that the residual $T=0$ Knight shift of a noncentrosymmetric superconductor in the strong-locking regime is completely determined by a single Fermi-surface average -- the projector $Π_{μν}=\langle\hat n_μ\hat n_ν\rangle_{\rm FS}$ of the spin-locking direction $\hat n_{\mathbf{k}}$ -- giving the tensor identity $χ_{μν}(0)=χ_{N}[δ_{μν}-Π_{μν}]$ independently of pairing symmetry, gap magnitude, and Fermi-surface shape. Because $\mathrm{Tr}\,Π=1$, the three principal Knight shifts at $T=0$ lie on a two-dimensional simplex of locking textures, the \emph{Knight-shift ellipsoid}, whose vertices, edges, and interior classify every canonical pairing class. The identity follows from a Bogoliubov sum rule, $\sum|M_{ph,O}|^{2}+\sum|M_{pp,O}|^{2}=\mathrm{Tr}_{s}(O^{2})$, valid at every momentum for every Hermitian single-particle operator $O$ as the BdG-doubled form of unitary invariance. Around the central theorem we develop controlled departures (a closed-form $s$-wave SOC interpolation, a finite-field strong-locking identity), a dynamical counterpart (a spin Ferrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule and a rigorous vanishing-projection theorem for $1/T_{1}$), and a decoupled-pocket multiband baseline, packaged into six experimental protocols. Applied to the $^{75}$As NMR data on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, the observed ellipsoid sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ and saturates the trace bound; the decoupled-pocket SOC-texture baseline is excluded by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized units, requiring a common $\hat c$-axis locking on all three pockets, and the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ is identified, via the vanishing-projection theorem, as a fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ ferromagnetic spin-fluctuation gap formation.

</details>

**Authors:** Younes Javanmard, Sina Kazemian  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16749v1>

<img src="2605.16749_figures/2605.16749_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<img src="2605.16749_figures/2605.16749_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Compressed open-boundary block from zero-padding. The size-M QFT circuit is periodic on the padded register, while compression back to the first N coordinates gives (A(N) α,h + E(M))/λmax.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.16749_figures/2605.16749_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Matrix structure for N = 64, h = 1, and α = 1.5. The third panel shows the absolute aliasing error on a logarithmic color scale; the highlighted corners are the wrap-around regions.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.16749_figures/2605.16749_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5 gives a direct functional comparison using three bounded-domain test functions also used in Ref. [19]. We compare the open-boundary Toeplitz target, the native QFT-circulant surrogate, and the zero-padded compressed operator. The top row overlays the operator outputs, while the bottom row shows the pointwise absolute error on a logarithmic scale. The native QFT curve plays the role of a periodic Fourier method and displays boundary artifacts, whereas the padded construction follows the open-boundary target.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a way to use the Quantum Fourier Transform to simulate fractional diffusion processes on bounded domains without the errors caused by periodic wrap-around. By padding the quantum register with zeros, the authors show how to effectively implement open boundary conditions for the fractional Laplacian. This serves as a fundamental building block for more complex quantum algorithms for solving partial differential equations.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Standard Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) circuits naturally implement periodic boundary conditions, which introduces artificial 'wrap-around' aliasing errors when trying to simulate the fractional Laplacian on bounded domains with open boundaries.

**Main result.** The authors prove that by zero-padding the input state into a larger M-point QFT register and then compressing it back, one can implement the open-boundary Toeplitz operator with a controllable error that vanishes as the padding increases.

**Method.** The method uses a padding-and-compression mechanism combined with a block-encoding of the Fourier symbol via a diagonal spectral oracle and controlled Ry rotations.

**Model / system.** The semi-discrete fractional Laplacian operator (with symbol |xi|^alpha) on bounded domains with open, zero-extension boundary conditions.

**Key observables.** spectral norm of the error matrix E(M), gate complexity, and the decay of the convolution kernel tail.

**Important parameters / regimes.** fractional order alpha, grid spacing h, physical dimension N, padded dimension M, and the decay rate r_alpha = min(2, 1 + alpha).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes a sufficiently large padding (M >= 2N) and relies on the specific decay properties of the kernel coefficients to bound the error.

**Figures summary.** Figure 4 compares the matrix structures of Open Toeplitz vs. QFT circulant operators highlighting aliasing; Figure 5 shows the functional accuracy of the padded method compared to the target operator.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the boundary mismatch problem, provides a mathematical proof of the aliasing identity, introduces the padding-and-compression construction, performs error analysis using the Schur test, and demonstrates a concrete quantum oracle implementation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the quantum Fourier transform (QFT) block-encoding of the semi-discrete fractional Laplacian on bounded domains with open, zero-extension boundary conditions. In the notation of the main construction, the target operator is the finite Toeplitz truncation \(A^{(N)}_{α,h}\) obtained from the full-lattice semi-discrete operator with symbol \(|ξ|^α\). A finite QFT register, however, diagonalizes circulant matrices rather than Toeplitz truncations. The native QFT circuit therefore implements a periodic surrogate \(\widetilde A^{(N)}_{α,h}\), not the open-boundary operator. We identify this mismatch through an exact Toeplitz-to-circulant aliasing identity.   To recover the open-boundary action, we zero-pad the state into a larger \(M\)-point QFT register, apply the same Fourier-symbol block-encoding, and compress back to the physical subspace. The resulting compressed block satisfies \(P_{N\to M}^{\dagger}\widetilde A^{(M)}_{α,h}P_{N\to M} = A^{(N)}_{α,h}+E^{(M)}\), where \(E^{(M)}\) is controlled by the tail of the semi-discrete convolution kernel. Thus, the QFT layer implements the fractional symbol, while zero-padding supplies the open-boundary geometry. The construction is an operator-compilation primitive for boundary-aware quantum simulation rather than a complete PDE solver.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces CausalHealth, a framework that uses causal inference and information theory to detect early-stage lithium-ion battery degradation. By tracking changes in the directed information flow between battery parameters, the method provides highly reliable warnings long before traditional capacity-based thresholds are reached. The approach is physically validated by correlating statistical transfer entropy with electrochemical charge-transfer resistance.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a robust, automated pipeline for the early detection of lithium-ion battery degradation that is physically interpretable and computable from routine telemetry without needing access to the degradation region.

**Main result.** The CausalHealth framework achieves 100% detection across various battery chemistries (LFP, LCO, NMC) and provides a significant prognostic lead time of up to 402 cycles before capacity failure, with the RWMHI fusion improving lead time by 15-52 cycles.

**Method.** The framework uses PCMCI for causal discovery and KSG-type Transfer Entropy to quantify information flow between voltage, current, temperature, and resistance, then applies a Reliability-Weighted Master Health Index (RWHMI) to fuse multiple anomaly detectors.

**Model / system.** The study analyzes lithium-ion batteries with LFP, LCO, and NMC chemistries using datasets from MIT-Stanford MATR, NASA PCoE, and CALCE CS2, with physical validation performed via Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) on NMC prismatic cells.

**Key observables.** Terminal voltage (V), current (I), temperature (T), resistance (R), Transfer Entropy (TE), charge-transfer resistance (Rct), and capacity State-of-Health (SOH).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Commissioning fraction (fc) between 5-30%, CUSUM drift (delta) and threshold (theta), and activation energy (Ea) of approximately 39.4 kJ/mol.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The commissioning window must be large enough to provide a stable reference (minimum 10 cycles); the method infers statistical dependencies rather than proving physical causation; SOC acts as a latent confounder.

**Figures summary.** Figures include PCMCI causal structure recovery, EIS cross-validation (Nyquist spectra), and plots showing the first-alarm cycle relative to the commissioning fraction and detector bundles.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of early degradation detection, describes the CausalHealth pipeline (causal discovery, feature engineering, and detector bundles), presents the RWHMI fusion method, validates the results against battery datasets, and provides physical grounding via EIS and Arrhenius analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Reliable early detection of lithium-ion battery degradation requires health indicators that are physically interpretable and computable from routine cycler telemetry without access to the degradation region. We introduce \textsc{CausalHealth}, a framework that applies causal graph discovery and $k$-nearest-neighbour transfer entropy to per-cycle voltage, current, temperature, and resistance time series, and organises twelve resulting anomaly scores into three signal-class bundles (Magnitude-shift, Predictive-residual, Complexity-entropy) -- with Isolation Forest reported separately as it falls below the bundle reliability threshold -- to characterise detection sensitivity across ten commissioning fractions (5--30\,\%). The Magnitude-shift class achieves 100\,\% detection across all seven tested cells spanning LFP (MIT--Stanford MATR) and LCO (NASA PCoE, CALCE CS2) chemistries, with a lead time of up to 402 cycles before conventional capacity-threshold failure on gradual-fade cells. A Reliability-Weighted Master Health Index (RWMHI) -- a cross-bundle fusion of five high-reliability detectors weighted by inverse coefficient of variation -- improves lead time by 15--52 cycles over the class median on long-lived cells while maintaining 100\,\% detection. Validation against electrochemical impedance spectroscopy on an NMC prismatic cell provides independent physical grounding: transfer entropy $\mathrm{TE}(R \!\to\! V)$ correlates with charge-transfer resistance $R_{\mathrm{ct}}$ (pooled $r = +0.990$; temperature-controlled partial $r = +0.898$), and an Arrhenius analysis of both quantities yields an activation energy consistent with published NMC charge-transfer kinetics. These results are evaluated on seven cells across three benchmark datasets.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18089v1>

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous topological description of Laughlin quasihole states on arbitrary Riemann surfaces. By computing the Chern character of the quasihole bundle, the authors mathematically link the topological invariants of the bundle to the physical fractional statistics and Aharonov-Bohm phases observed in the fractional quantum Hall effect.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to compute the Chern character and Chern classes of the Laughlin quasihole bundle over the quasihole moduli space on Riemann surfaces of arbitrary genus.

**Main result.** The authors derive a general formula for the Chern character of the quasihole bundle and show that in the completely filled state, the bundle is compatible with projective flatness, with results matching the predicted Berry phase decomposition.

**Method.** The study employs the Grothendieck-Riemann-Roch theorem, the Abel-Jacobi map, and the Chern-Weil theory, alongside explicit verification in genus 0 and 1 using the Chern connection and theta functions.

**Model / system.** The system consists of single-layer and multi-layer Laughlin fractional quantum Hall states on Riemann surfaces of genus g, featuring particles and quasiholes with specific vanishing orders (b and c) and interaction matrices (K and C).

**Key observables.** Chern character, Chern classes, Berry phase (Aharonov-Bohm and fractional statistical contributions), and Hall conductance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Genus (g), number of particles (n), number of quasiholes (m), particle-particle vanishing order (b), particle-quasihole vanishing order (c), and magnetic line bundle degree (d).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The calculation assumes the wave-functions can be analytically continued and that the chosen frame of sections is holomorphic in the quasihole positions.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the geometric construction of the quasihole bundle, proceeds to derive the Chern character using the Grothendieck-Riemann-Roch theorem, provides explicit low-genus verifications, and concludes with a generalization to multi-layer systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study fractional quantum Hall states with quasihole excitations, on Riemann surfaces of arbitrary genus. For configurations with $m$ quasiholes we construct a vector bundle above the $m$-th symmetric power of the curve so that the fiber at a point $\lbrace w_1,\dots,w_m \rbrace$ corresponds to the state with quasiholes localized at these positions. We determine the Chern character of this bundle via the Grothendieck-Riemann-Roch theorem and show that in the completely filled state, i.e. when the number of particles is maximal, the vector bundle is compatible with the condition of projective flatness. Furthermore, we obtain a generalization of this result to the case of multiple layers and multiple quasihole types. In genus zero and one, we construct explicit wave-functions and verify that the curvature of the associated Chern connection reproduces the predicted Chern classes. The Chern classes obtained match, term by term, the predicted decomposition of the Berry phase under quasihole exchange, into an extensive Aharonov--Bohm contribution and a fractional statistical contribution.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper studies how polymer droplets merge when moving across a surface with varying stiffness. It reveals that the merging process transitions between different physical regimes (capillarity vs. viscoelasticity) and is highly sensitive to the chemical attractions between the droplets and the surface.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the coalescence dynamics of two viscoelastic polymer droplets moving on a soft substrate with a stiffness gradient (durotaxis).

**Main result.** The study identifies a crossover from a capillarity-dominated to a viscoelasticity-dominated regime in bridge growth and shows that coalescence morphology and mixing depend strongly on inter- and intra-droplet attraction strengths.

**Method.** Coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations using the LAMMPS package with a Langevin thermostat and Velocity-Verlet integration.

**Model / system.** Two viscoelastic polymer droplets (modeable via FENE potential and LJ interactions) moving on a deformable substrate with a stiffness gradient implemented via a harmonic potential.

**Key observables.** Temporal growth of bridge height (h) and radius (r), power-law exponents (alpha and beta), contact angle, and degree of droplet mixing.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Substrate stiffness gradient, velocity ratios of droplets, droplet-surface attraction strength, and droplet-droplet/intra-droplet interaction strengths.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is modeled using coarse-grained particles; the effect of droplet bending rigidity is not considered.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the experimental setup of durotaxis motion; Figure 8 shows the distance between droplet centers of mass; Figure 9 shows time-sequenced snapshots of the 'enveloping' morphology.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of durotactic coalescence, describes the molecular dynamics model and simulation setup, compares stationary vs. moving droplets, analyzes the effect of various interaction parameters, and discusses biological implications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Here, we study the coalescence of two droplets that are moving in the same direction on a soft surface; the motion of the droplets is caused by a gradient in the surface stiffness. As reference, stationary coalescence of the same droplets is also studied on the corresponding uniform surfaces for different stiffness values. To describe the coalescence phenomenon on a surface with stiffness gradient, a relevant range of velocity ratios of the leading and the trailing droplet was considered to elucidate the effect of this parameter on coalescence. Moreover, to analyze the dynamics of the process, the temporal growth of the bridge height $(h)$ was investigated, which follows a power law $(h \sim t^α)$, before eventually attaining a constant value. The obtained values of $α$ show a transition from a higher to a lower value as a function of time, pointing to the presence of two distinct power-law growth regimes, where the transition signifies the crossover from the capillarity-dominated regime to the viscoelasticty-dominated regime of coalescence. In addition, varying attractive strengths for droplet--droplet and intra-droplet interactions were considered. The results indicate that both the dynamics and the degree of the coalescence strongly depend on these interaction parameters. Thus, we anticipate that our results will shed more light on the durotaxis-driven coalescence of polymeric droplets for various relevant system parameters, which will have practical implications for applications ranging from microfluidics to ink-jet printing, where substrate properties may vary. In addition, results may add to the fundamental understanding of the interactions among multicellular aggregates moving on biological surfaces.

</details>

**Authors:** Zhenhao Wang, Weijing Bao, Jia-Rui Luo, Gentaro Watanabe, Kui-Tian Xi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18139v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Second col- umn: Two-dimensional Fourier power spectrum F(kx, ky) in the range kx,y ∈[−2π/ℓ, 2π/ℓ]. The FFT satisfies Parseval’s theorem: R F(kρ)dkρ = R |δn2(ρ)|2dρ. **Summary.** This paper explores how axial confinement and population imbalance control the formation of complex density patterns in a two-component dipolar BEC. It demonstrates that these quantum patterns mimic microphase-separated structures in polymers and that their characteristic length scales are tunable via the confinement geometry.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a controllable quantum fluid platform to study microphase separation and pattern formation, drawing a direct structural correspondence between quantum gases and soft-matter systems like diblock copolymers.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating ground-state density pattern selection and morphological transitions in a population-imbalanced, two-component dipolar Bose-Einstein condensate confined in a circular quasi-2D box.

**Main result.** The study identifies a sequence of morphologies (pancake, droplet arrays, concentric rings) where the population ratio acts as an effective volume fraction, and discovers that the pattern spacing scales linearly with the axial confinement length.

**Method.** A mean-field approach using coupled Gross-Pita_Pitaevskii equations (GPEs) minimized via the preconditioned conjugate-gradient method and evaluated using Fast Fourier Transforms.

**Model / system.** A binary (two-component) dipolar Bose-Einstein condensate of 52Cr atoms in a quasi-2D circular box with tight axial harmonic confinement.

**Key observables.** 2D column density profiles, density contrast (C2), structure factors (radial and 2D Fourier power spectrum), and characteristic wave vectors.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Population ratio (N2/N), axial confinement strength (omega_z), interaction asymmetry (a22/a11), and box radius (R0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Zero temperature, mean-field approximation (neglecting Lee-Huang-Yang fluctuations), and rigid/hard-wall-like boundary conditions for the box potential.

**Figures summary.** Figures show real-space density patterns for different phases, density contrast (C2) as a function of population ratio and axial confinement, and scaling analysis of pattern spacing.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the system and its analogy to block copolymers, describes the mean-field model and numerical methods, presents phase diagrams and morphological transitions, analyzes scaling laws and geometric frustration, and concludes with implications for quantum fluid pattern selection.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the ground-state density patterns of a population-imbalanced two-component dipolar Bose-Einstein condensate confined in a circular quasi-two-dimensional box. Using a mean-field model, we map out phase diagrams as functions of the axial confinement, interaction imbalance, and population ratio. The system supports a rich sequence of stationary morphologies, including a nearly uniform pancake state, pancake-droplet and ring-droplet coexistence states, droplet arrays, and concentric rings. These patterns show a close structural correspondence to microphase-separated morphologies in diblock-copolymer systems, with the population imbalance acting as an effective volume fraction that selects the pattern topology. Analysis of the density profiles and structure factors reveals that the modulated states possess an intrinsic nonzero characteristic wave vector, which remains essentially unchanged when the box size is varied. We also find that the characteristic pattern spacing scales linearly with the axial confinement length, indicating that the transverse thickness of the condensate controls the effective in-plane length scale. In a finite circular box, this smooth scaling is interrupted by discrete steps, reflecting geometric frustration and the integer locking of the number of rings or droplets. Our results show that box-trapped dipolar mixtures provide a controllable platform for studying finite-size pattern selection and nonlocal microphase formation in quantum fluids.

</details>

### [Correlation-Driven Spin Reorientation via Competing Anisotropy Channels in CrPS4](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16541v1)

**Authors:** Raju Baral, Harald O. Jeschke, Igor I. Mazin, Jue Liu, David Mandrus, Stuart Calder  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16541v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.16541_figures/2605.16541_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Magnetic structure of CrPS4, where the Cr spins are canted by 12◦from the c axis at 2 K. The six nearest-neighbor Cr–Cr shells (n1–n6) used in the correlation analysis are la- beled, with n1,n2 forming the dominant intrachain bonds.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.16541_figures/2605.16541_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Temperature evolution of the (a) local magnetic order parameter, (b) spin angle, (c) correlation length for T &gt; TN, and (d) shell-resolved spin-spin correlation function, ⟨S0 · Sn⟩, of the first six nearest neighbors for T &gt; TN. Positive values correspond to net ferromagnetic (FM) and negative to antiferromagnetic (AFM) correlations. The persistence of positive n = 1, 2 correlations to 100 K demonstrates that the paramagnetic state remains locally chain-like even after the collapse of long-range correlations.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.16541_figures/2605.16541_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. (a) Correlation-renormalization factor ξ(T) from the moment-based estimate ξ = 2m2/(1 + m4) (blue circles, filled for T ≤TN, open above TN) and the directly measured intrachain correlation ξexp = ⟨S0 ·S1⟩from mPDF analysis (used as model input for T &gt; TN). The mPDF correlation length ξmPDF is shown on the right axis. Agreement near TN supports the use of the Gaussian estimator in the ordered phase. (b) Model-predicted equilibrium spin angle θmin(T) (triangles) compared with the experimentally refined spin angle θexp(T). Below TN, θmin is computed using the moment-based ξ and above TN it is computed using the directly measured ξexp. The dashed horizontal line marks the DFT+U...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the spin reorientation in CrPS4 is caused by the temperature-dependent decay of magnetic correlations, which affects exchange anisotropy differently than single-ion anisotropy. By combining neutron scattering with ab initio calculations, the authors provide a quantitative microscopic description of how low-dimensional correlations control magnetic anisotropy.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Identifying the mechanism behind the temperature-induced spin reorientation transition (SRT) in the quasi-1D van der Waals antiferromagnet CrPS4.

**Main result.** The SRT is driven by a differential renormalization where decaying intersite correlations suppress exchange-anisotropy channels while single-ion anisotropy remains local, causing the effective easy axis to rotate.

**Method.** The study combines magnetic pair distribution function (mPDF) analysis of neutron total scattering with DFT+U calculations for energy mapping and classical Monte Carlo simulations.

**Model / system.** The system is CrPS4, a quasi-1D van der Waals antiferromagnetic semiconductor with A-type magnetic order. The model uses a microscopic spin Hamiltonian including isotropic exchange, exchange-anisotropy, and single-ion anisotropy terms.

**Key observables.** Magnetic pair distribution function (mPDF), local magnetic order parameter (m), spin polar angle (theta), correlation length (xi), and Cr-Cr bond distances (d1, d2).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Neeel temperature (TN = 37 K), Hubbard U (2 eV), exchange couplings (J1, J2), and spin-spin correlation functions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model uses a dominant-chain approximation focusing on n=1 and n=2 bonds and neglects Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions due to inversion symmetry.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the system; Figure 3 compares the correlation-renormalization factor and predicted spin angles against experimental data, and shows structural anomalies in Cr-Cr bond distances at TN.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the spin reorientation problem, describes the mPDF and DFT+U methodology, presents the results of local correlation measurements, develops the anisotropy renormalization model, and discusses the connection to structural anomalies.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We identify a correlation-driven mechanism for the temperature-induced spin reorientation in the quasi-one-dimensional van der Waals antiferromagnet CrPS4. Magnetic pair distribution function (mPDF) analysis resolves the local spin direction and shows that ferromagnetic intrachain correlations persist far above TN. Combining these correlations with a DFT-derived spin Hamiltonian reveals competing single-ion and exchange-anisotropy channels, with single-ion anisotropy remaining local while exchange anisotropy is renormalized as intersite correlations decay. This differential renormalization rotates the effective easy axis and captures the ordered-state canting. Above TN, the continued rotation beyond the model prediction delineates the limits of the dominant-chain approximation. These results establish mPDF-derived correlations as direct inputs to microscopic Hamiltonians and show how low-dimensional correlations can control magnetic anisotropy.

</details>

### [Direct On-Wafer Measurements of Noise Parameters in C- and X-bands at $T=4$ K](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17073v1)

**Authors:** Daniil Frolov, Jean-Olivier Plouchart, Utku Soylu  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17073v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The VNA equivalent circuit includes: RF source 1; terminated switches 2,10,11,18; SPDT switch 20; heterodyne receivers R1...R4, A,B,C,D; noise receiver 21; and directional couplers 3,6,9,14,17,19. The RF hardware inside the cryo- stat includes: 4-12 GHz triple junction ferrite isolator 23, 3.4-12.3 GHz circulator 28, 0.3-14 GHz low noise amplifier (Low noise factory) 30, 30 dB directional coupler 24, 0.2-18 GHz...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new method for measuring the noise properties of semiconductor transistors directly on a wafer at 4 K. By using a cryogenic preamplifier and a specialized calibration technique, the researchers can measure low-gain devices that would otherwise be hidden by room-temperature noise. This is crucial for developing the readout electronics needed for quantum computing and radio astronomy.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of performing accurate, direct on-wafer noise parameter measurements of low-gain transistors at cryogenic temperatures (4 K) because their output noise power is often below the room-temperature instrumentation noise floor.

**Main result.** The development of a vector-corrected measurement framework that enables simultaneous S-parameter and noise parameter extraction for 14nm FinFETs and LNAs at 4 K, successfully overcoming the room-temperature noise floor using a cryogenic preamplifier.

**Method.** A combination of the 'source-pull' and 'cold-source' methods using a four-state cryogenic tuner, integrated with a TRL (thru-reflect-line) calibration scheme and a cryogenic preamplification chain.

**Model / system.** A cryogenic measurement platform consisting of a 4 K cryostat with wafer probes, a four-port VNA, a cryogenic impedance generator (tuner), and a preamplification chain, used to characterize 14nm FinFETs and LNA dies.

**Key observables.** S-parameters (S11, S21, S12, S22), noise parameters (minimum noise temperature Te, minimum noise factor Fmin, optimal reflection coefficient Gamma_opt, and Lange invariant 4N), and I-V curves.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature of 4 K; frequency range of 5-12 GHz (C- and X-bands); 14nm FinFET technology.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes S21 = S12 reciprocity for the probe; assumes high circulator isolation and good matching to simplify the mismatch coefficient.

**Figures summary.** Schematics of the measurement stand and electrical signal paths; physical layout of cryogenic hardware; signal flow graphs and calibration planes; and plots of FET/LNA S-parameters, noise temperatures, and optimal impedances.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific challenge, describes the hardware architecture and signal path, details the operational modes (S-parameter and noise parameter), explains the TRL calibration and de-embedding procedure, presents the characterization of the FETs and LNAs, and concludes with validation and future improvements.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper describes the setup and the results of the direct on-wafer measurements of a FET noise parameters obtained with a source-pull method at temperatures down to T=4K and in the 5-12 GHz frequency range. The setup consists of a cryostat with wafer probes, two reflectometers, a programmable impedance generator, wideband isolators and bias tees and low noise preamplifier, all cooled to cryogenic temperatures, allowing to perform a full vector error-corrected wafer-level measurements of the discrete transistors and amplifier dies. The setup and its calibration procedure are designed in a such way that allows simultaneous calibration, S-parameters, noise parameters and I-V curve measurements of several FETs all in one cooldown. Using the described setup we perform first measurements of 14nm FinFETs and also measure noise parameters of an LNA based on these FETs. Resulting noise temperature values are compared against those obtained using independent and alternative measurement techniques.

</details>
