### [LEAD: A Local Ensemble-Assisted Parallel Decoding Framework for Quantum Tanner Codes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17796v1)

**Authors:** Zhuo-Yan Xiao, Sha Shi, Chen-Peng Huang, Dong-Sheng Wang, Yun-Jiang Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17796v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17796_figures/2605.17796_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. As described in the main text, each face is composed of four edges, where the horizontal edges (A-edges) and vertical edges (B-edges) correspond to the group elements a ∈A and b ∈B, respectively [8].</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17796_figures/2605.17796_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Flowchart of the LEAD decoding algorithm.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17796_figures/2605.17796_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Performance comparison of the LEAD framework against baseline decoders for various random quantum Tanner codes. Panels (a)–(c) illustrate the LER versus physical error probability p, and panels (d)–(f) present the corresponding average number of decoding iterations. The code parameters are: (a, d) [[392, 48]], (b, e) [[392, 8]], and (c, f) [[252, 8]]. Across all instances, the LEAD architecture (solid lines) consistently achieves superior error suppression and lower computational latency compared to the standalone BP–OSD and BP–LSD baselines.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17796_figures/2605.17796_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. Decoding performance comparison for various quantum Tanner codes derived from [2, 3]. Panels (a)–(c) present the LER as a function of physical error probability p, while (d)–(f) illustrate the corresponding average number of decoding iterations. The code parameters are: (a, d) [[36, 8, 3]], (b, e) [[80, 2, 10]], and (c, f) [[144, 8, 12]]. Across all configurations, the LEAD framework (solid lines) consistently reduces decoding latency compared to standalone BP-LSD and BP-OSD baselines, while maintaining or improving error-correction accuracy.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.17796_figures/2605.17796_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5. Analysis of LEAD decoding gain (∆log) vs. subcode FER for different quantum Tanner code instances at p = 0.005. The horizontal dash- dotted line at 0 denotes the baseline; points below it indicate performance degradation. The results illustrate that local decoding accuracy alone is insufficient to predict global performance improvement, highlighting that local overconfidence—rather than raw subcode error rate—is the primary driver of performance bottleneck in the global belief propagation process.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces LEAD, a new decoding framework for Quantum Tanner Codes that uses local, parallelized decoding to estimate error probabilities. By regularizing these local estimates to prevent overconfidence, the method significantly improves error correction performance and reduces decoding time compared to traditional methods.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Existing decoding algorithms for Quantum Tanner Codes face a trade-off between exponential computational complexity in local decoders and poor performance in structure-blind iterative decoders due to high degeneracy and short cycles.

**Main result.** The LEAD framework achieves significantly lower logical error rates and reduced decoding latency compared to standard BP-OSD and BP-LSD baselines by leveraging the code's topological structure.

**Method.** A two-stage parallel decoding framework that projects the global code onto overlapping local subcodes, aggregates local error probability estimates using a soft-information regularization mechanism, and performs final global decoding.

**Model / system.** Quantum Tanner Codes, which are a class of quantum LDPC codes constructed via a left-right Cayley complex using a CSS-type structure on vertices, edges, and faces.

**Key observables.** Logical Error Rate (LER), Frame Error Rate (FER), decoding iterations, and decoding gain (delta_log).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The scaling parameter alpha (used for regularization), physical error probability p, and the local neighborhood size Delta.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes a depolarizing noise model and relies on the decomposable structure of the underlying Cayley complex.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the quadrangle structure of the Cayley complex; Figure 2 provides a flowchart of the LEAD algorithm; Figure 3 compares LER and decoding iterations for various codes; Figure 5 shows the relationship between decoding gain and subcode FER; Figure 6 demonstrates the impact of the alpha parameter on performance.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of decoding Quantum Tanner Codes, describes the geometric structure of the Cayley complex, presents the LEAD decoding framework (local decoding, regularization, and global decoding), provides complexity analysis, and validates the method through numerical simulations and error rate comparisons.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum Tanner codes are a recently developed family of quantum error-correcting codes characterized by favorable asymptotic performance characteristics. Despite their theoretical potential, practical decoding algorithms that effectively leverage their structural properties remain limited. This work introduces LEAD (Local Ensemble-Assisted Decoder), a structure-aware decoding framework tailored for quantum Tanner codes. The proposed scheme leverages the decomposable structure of Cayley complexes to project the global code onto overlapping local subcodes defined by vertex neighborhoods, where error probabilities are estimated in parallel. To ensure global consistency, LEAD utilizes the inherent topological symmetry of the complex and introduces a soft-information regularization mechanism to mitigate local overconfidence during information aggregation. This framework enables highly parallelized, low-complexity decoding that is intrinsically compatible with various local search heuristics. Simulation results demonstrate that LEAD achieves significantly lower logical error rates than standard decoding framework while substantially reducing the average decoding latency and iteration count.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.18064"></a>
### [Localization Transitions in a Half-Filled Helical Aubry-André Model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18064v1)

**Authors:** Taylan Yildiz, B. Tanatar, Balázs Hetényi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18064v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18064_figures/2605.18064_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. (a) Schematic illustration of a one-dimensional lattice embedded on a toroidal geometry. The sites follow a helical path wrapped around the torus, such that periodic boundary conditions are naturally realized by the closed topology. The shaded region highlights a local segment of the lattice. (b) Effective representation of the helical structure, where sites on neighboring turns become spatially close. Solid lines de- note nearest-neighbor hopping J, while dotted links indicate longer-range hopping JN emerging from the geometric em- bedding.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18064_figures/2605.18064_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Geometric Binder cumulant U4 as a function of quasiperiodic potential strength ∆for L = 987 and N = 40, shown for several Nth-neighbor hopping amplitudes JN (panel labels). Horizontal dotted lines indicate U4 = 1/3, 1/2 and U4 = 0. The vertical dotted line marks the critical po- tential ∆c defined by the zero crossing of U4(∆).</sub>

</details>

Left panel: JN = 0.5; right panel: JN = 1.0. Some pronounced spikes occur at N values with gcd(N, L) &gt; 1the , inset graph corresponds to gcd(N, L) vs N.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper explores how long-range, helical connections modify the localization transition in a quasiperiodic system. It reveals that helical hopping stabilizes the delocalized phase and that the transition point fluctuates significantly due to resonance effects between the hopping range and the lattice potential.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how long-range helical hopping and the winding range N influence the delocalization-localization transition in a 1D quasiperiodic lattice.

**Main result.** Increasing helical hopping strength stabilizes the extended phase, and the critical potential exhibits non-monotonic spikes due to resonant helical sampling of the quasiperiodic landscape.

**Method.** The authors use the modern theory of polarization to construct a geometric Binder cumulant and employ a Zeckendorf-shift construction with Fibonacci system sizes to perform controlled thermodynamic limit extrapolations.

**Model / system.** A one-dimensional helical Aubry-André model featuring an incommensurate onsite potential and an Nth-neighbor hopping term that connects successive windings of a helical chain.

**Key observables.** Geometric Binder cumulant (U4), polarization amplitudes (Zq), normalized Fermi gap, and single-particle energy spectrum.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Quasiperiodic potential strength (Delta), Nth-neighbor hopping strength (JN), helical range (N), and system size (L).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on non-interacting fermions at half-filling and utilizes periodic boundary conditions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the helical lattice geometry; Figure 6 displays the Binder cumulant and Fermi gap for different hopping strengths; Figure 7 illustrates the energy spectrum changes; Figure 8 shows the Binder cumulant for various Fibonacci sizes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the helical Aubry-André model, details the polarization-based diagnostic methods and thermodynamic limit construction, presents results on phase stabilization and commensurability-induced spikes, and concludes with a robustness analysis via finite-size scaling.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study localization in a one-dimensional quasiperiodic lattice obtained by extending the Aubry-André model with an additional $N$th-neighbor hopping term of strength $J_{N}$. This long-range tunneling couples successive windings of an effective helical chain and introduces a second control parameter beyond the quasiperiodic potential strength $Δ$. Working with noninteracting fermions (typically at half filling), we diagnose the delocalization-localization transition using extensions of the modern theory of polarization. Specifically, we compute the polarization amplitudes of the many-body Slater-determinant ground state and construct a geometric Binder cumulant from polarization amplitudes. The critical potential where the localization transition happens is extracted from the sign change (zero crossing) of the geometric Binder cumulant. We map critical potential as a function of $J_N$ and the helical range $N$, finding that stronger helical hopping generally stabilizes the extended phase (shifting critical potential upward), while the $N$-dependence can display pronounced commensurability-induced spikes. We further compare the geometric Binder cumulant with the Fermi gap, which remains near zero at small values of potential and opens in the same parameter regime where the geometric Binder cumulant departs from extended phase. Finally, to take a controlled thermodynamic limit along Fibonacci system sizes, we employ a Zeckendorf-shift construction that fixes the many-body sector consistently as system size goes to infinity.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new generative model for growing simplicial complexes using a preferential attachment mechanism that acts on different dimensions. It demonstrates how the model can produce power-law degree distributions and reveals how the interplay between different dimensional components can trigger abrupt transitions in the network's scaling properties.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for generative models of higher-order networks (simplicial complexes) that can reproduce the topological and spectral features found in empirical many-body interaction data.

**Main result.** The authors develop a dimension-wise preferential attachment model that produces power-law degree distributions and identify an abrupt transition in scaling exponents when moving between different dimensional attachment probabilities.

**Method.** The study uses algebraic topology (Hodge-Laplacian, boundary operators), continuous differential equation approximations for growth dynamics, and spectral analysis of the $d$-Laplacian.

**Model / system.** A growing simplicial complex where new nodes and higher-dimensional simplices (links, triangles, tetrahedra) are added at each time step based on dimension-specific attachment probabilities $p_d$.

**Key observables.** Generalized degree distribution, spectral dimension ($d_s$), Betti numbers ($eta_d$), Fiedler eigenvalue, and scaling exponents ($eta, \gamma$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Maximum dimension $D$, attachment probabilities $p_1, \dots, p_D$, and a flavor parameter $s$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The growth process assumes a specific probabilistic attachment rule where the probability of selecting a simplex is proportional to its generalized degree.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the basic building blocks (simplices) and their assembly; Figure 2 illustrates the growth process; Figure 4 shows the transition of the growth exponent $eta_0$ as a function of $p_1$; Figure 6 and 7 visualize the hierarchical construction and the relationship between node and link degrees.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the higher-order network framework, introduces the dimension-wise preferential attachment growth algorithm, provides analytical derivations for degree evolution and scaling exponents, explores specific dimensional regimes and phase transitions, and concludes with numerical validation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Network science is a powerful framework allowing to model complex systems, it is capable to describe and take into account the intricate web of connections existing among the constituting basic element of the system. Recently scholars have brought to the fore the relevance of higher-order networks, namely structures allowing to encode for many-body interaction, differently from the pairwise case handled by networks. This novel research field opens new avenues of research with applications ranging from neurosciences to social sciences; there is thus a need for generative models of higher-order network capable to reproduce features present in empirical data. In this work we present a model for growing simplicial complex rooted on a preferential attachment process acting dimension-wise, i.e., returning a power law distribution for the generalized degree of simplexes of different dimension.

</details>

**Authors:** Ming-Xing Luo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17404v1>

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous mathematical analysis of the security of module lattices used in post-quantum cryptography. It demonstrates that the structured nature of these lattices allows for much tighter error bounds than previously assumed, significantly reducing the estimated approximation factor for certain attacks on ML-KEM.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the security of module lattices in post-quantum cryptography, specifically determining the CVP distance on the log-unit lattice and its impact on the ML-KEM (Kyber) scheme.

**Main result.** Proves that the L2 CVP distance converges to a specific constant times sqrt(n) and reduces the Short Generator Problem approximation factor from exponential to sub-polynomial.

**Method.** Uses probabilistic methods including the Central Limit Theorem, sub-Gaussian concentration inequalities, and analytic tools like the Trigamma function and QR decomposition.

**Model / system.** The mathematical framework involves cyclotomic fields Q(zeta_{2^k}), the log-unit lattice, and module lattices of rank d over the ring of integers.

**Key observables.** L2 and L-infinity CVP distances, approximation factor gamma, and the variance of the log-determinant sigma_{g0}.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimension n = 2^{k-1}, module rank d, and the coefficient variance sigma_c^2.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes ring elements have i.i.d. bounded coefficients and utilizes the Gaussian limit for large dimensions.

**Figures summary.** Table 4 summarizes the cumulative progress of the CDPR attack analysis across three parts of the paper, comparing prior states to the current work.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from analyzing L2 and L-infinity distances on the log-unit lattice to evaluating Babai's algorithm performance, then extends the analysis to module determinant ideals via the Trigamma Theorem, and concludes with security implications for ML-KEM.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We prove that the $L^2$ CVP distance from a random short ring element to the log-unit lattice of $\Q(ζ_{2^k})$ converges to $\fracπ{2\sqrt{6}}\sqrt{n}$ as $n=2^{k-1}\to\infty$. We then show that this target lies inside the Voronoi cell of the origin for $k\ge 4$. For the $L^\infty$ norm, the maximum over $n$ sub-Gaussian coordinates yields $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ which translates into a sub-polynomial approximation factor for the Short Generator Problem. We show a Coarse Lattice Theorem that Babai's algorithm returns zero for all structured targets, yet exactly recovers unit perturbations of arbitrary size. For module determinant ideals, we further prove the Trigamma Theorem that proves an intrinsic imbalance $σ_{g_0}=O(1)$ independent of the modulus $q$. Finally, combined with Parts I and II, we reduce the CDPR factor for ML-KEM from $\exp(\tO(\sqrt{n}))$ to a sub-polynomial value.

</details>

### [Module Lattice Security (Part IV): Probabilistic Polynomial Quantum Attack on Module-LWE over 2-Power Cyclotomics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17412v1)

**Authors:** Ming-Xing Luo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17412v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a quantum algorithm that breaks several post-quantum cryptographic standards, including ML-KEM and Falcon, by solving the Principal Ideal Problem in polynomial time. It achieves this by exploiting the algebraic structure of 2-power cyclotomic rings through a tower decomposition. The results suggest that the security of these lattice-based primitives is significantly lower than previously assumed under quantum attack.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a probabilistic polynomial-time quantum attack on Module-LWE (MLWE) schemes, specifically targeting ML-KEM, Falcon, Hawk, and NTRU over 2-power cyclotomic rings.

**Main result.** The paper presents a quantum algorithm that reduces the Principal Ideal Problem (PIP) complexity to polynomial time, effectively breaking the standardized parameter sets of ML-KEM, Falcon, Hawk, and NTRU.

**Method.** The attack uses an extended CDPR pipeline involving module-to-ideal reduction, a quantum tower decomposition of the Principal Ideal Problem via the Abelian Hidden Subgroup Problem, and classical log-unit CVP using Babai's algorithm.

**Model / system.** The mathematical framework involves module lattices over 2-power cyclotomic rings R = Z[zeta], utilizing the algebraic structure of cyclotomic towers and the log-unit lattice.

**Key observables.** The approximation factor (gamma), quantum gate complexity, qubit requirements, and the CVP residual.

**Important parameters / regimes.** ML-KEM-1024 parameters (n=256, q=3329), ring degree (n), module rank (d), and the approximation factor threshold (q/2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The attack relies on the validity of the Hidden Subgroup Problem (HSP) solution, the use of the tower decomposition, and certain empirical/numerical results from previous parts of the series.

**Figures summary.** The notes mention a table summarizing quantum resource costs for ML-KEM-1024 and a table comparing theoretical vs. simulated approximation factors for Falcon.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a logical progression from module-to-ideal reduction to a recursive quantum tower PIP algorithm, followed by complexity analysis, security evaluation of specific NIST standards, and a summary of cumulative improvements over previous works.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a quantum attack on ML-KEM and related 2-power cyclotomic lattice schemes. Combining with Parts I-III, we provide an algorithm and verify the resulting approximation factor satisfies $γ\le 21 < q/2=1665$ for ML-KEM-1024, with a success probability $\ge 0.99$. We apply a tower decomposition of the Principal Ideal Problem (PIP) through the chain $\Q \subset \Q(ζ_8) \subset \cdots \subset \Q(ζ_{2^k})$ which yields a polynomial-time quantum algorithm costing $O(n^3 \log^2 n)$ gates, $O(n^2 \log n)$ qubits, and $\mathrm{poly}(n)$ classical bit operations. We extend the analysis to Falcon, Hawk, and NTRU over 2-power cyclotomic rings. This means that ML-KEM, Falcon, Hawk, NTRU-HPS, and NTRU-HRSS with all standardized parameter sets are broken under quantum attack.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a method for compressing quantum time-evolution circuits with guaranteed convergence to near-optimal gate complexity. By using a specific initialization strategy, the authors show that small-scale optimized circuits can be effectively scaled to simulate large, complex systems like the Kagome Heisenberg antiferromagnet. This approach significantly reduces the gate overhead required for digital quantum simulation.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous mathematical bridge between variational quantum algorithms and efficient digital simulation, offering a provable way to scale quantum simulators toward the classical complexity limit.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of implementing efficient, large-scale quantum time evolution circuits that achieve near-optimal gate complexity while providing rigorous global convergence guarantees for variational compression.

**Main result.** The authors provide a recipe for choosing an initial optimization point that guarantees convergence to a near-optimal circuit with gate complexity O(N t polylog(N t/epsilon)) for local, translationally invariant Hamiltonians.

**Method.** A variational compression strategy (TICC) using a Trotterization-based 'warm start' and a bootstrapping approach to extend small-time-step optimized gates to larger evolution times.

**Model / system.** Local and translationally invariant Hamiltonians, specifically demonstrated on a Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a Kagome lattice, with proposed implementation on trapped-ion platforms using B-gates.

**Key observables.** Evolution infidelity, gate complexity (gate count), and local infidelity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size (N), evolution time (t), error tolerance (epsilon), and the critical time threshold (t_crit) for convergence.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Hamiltonian is local and translationally invariant; the optimization manifold is real-analytic; the time step must be sufficiently small (below t_crit).

**Figures summary.** Visualizations of the optimization protocol, comparisons of error scaling between Trotterization and optimized circuits, transferability of gates to larger systems, and gate decomposition/performance in noisy ion-trap models.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the scaling challenge, theoretical proof of convergence and convexity, demonstration of the TICC protocol on various lattices, verification of transferability to large systems, and hardware-specific gate implementation analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Variational compression can significantly lower implementation overheads for encoding the time evolution of Hamiltonians into quantum circuits. However, they usually lack global convergence guarantees and well-established scaling behavior. In this work, we provide a recipe for choosing the initial point of such variational optimizations that guarantees convergence to a quantum circuit with near-optimal gate complexity $\mathcal{O}\left( N \, t \, \text{polylog}(N \, t/ε) \right)$ for all local and translationally invariant Hamiltonians. We demonstrate our method by encoding the globally controlled time evolution of a Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a Kagome lattice. For $N = 48$ sites, evolution time $t=0.1$ and infidelity $ε\approx1\%$, the controlled time-evolution circuit requires 960 two-qubit B gates, for which we propose a straightforward implementation scheme for ion-trap setups. Thereby, our recipe extends digital quantum simulators toward system sizes and geometries that are challenging for classical computation.

</details>

### [Nonlinear response of the chiral magnetic effect in the D3/D7 holographic model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16931v1)

**Authors:** Masataka Matsumoto, Mirmani Mirjalali, Ali Vahedi

**Summary.** This paper explores how the chiral magnetic effect behaves in a highly nonlinear regime using holographic duality. It reveals that the relationship between current and magnetic field can be multi-valued and shows how external fields can stabilize certain phases of the system.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the nonlinear response of the chiral magnetic current to an external magnetic field and the dynamical stability of the system near the phase boundary between insulating and CME phases.

**Main result.** The researchers found a multi-valued, highly nonlinear dependence of the chiral magnetic current on the magnetic field and demonstrated that the axial chemical potential and magnetic field cooperatively stabilize the insulating phase.

**Method.** The study uses the Dirac-Born-Infeld (DBI) and Wess-Zumino (WZ) actions within a holographic framework, employing the shooting method and determinant method to solve nonlinear ODEs and analyze meson spectra.

**Model / system.** A D3/D7 holographic brane system in an AdS5 x S5 background, representing a dual field theory with axial chemical potential and an external magnetic field.

**Key observables.** Chiral magnetic current (Jz), pseudo-scalar condensate, chiral condensate (mass operator), and meson mass spectrum (M^2).

**Important parameters / regimes.** External magnetic field (B), axial chemical potential (mu5 or angular frequency omega), temperature (T), and quark mass (m).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study operates in the probe limit (Nf << Nc) and assumes large Nc and large 't Hooft coupling.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the current and condensate vs. magnetic field for different embeddings; Figure 4 plots the meson mass squared to identify stability/tachyonic regimes; Figure 5 shows the current response at finite temperature.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the holographic setup, derives equations of motion using the DBI action, analyzes the nonlinear current-magnetic field relation, investigates the stability of the meson spectrum, and concludes with finite temperature effects.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the nonlinear response of the chiral magnetic current to an external magnetic field in a holographic setup. Using the D3/D7 brane system, where the chiral magnetic effect (CME) can be realized by considering rotating probe D7-branes, corresponding to introducing an axial chemical potential, we analyze the current-magnetic field relation beyond the linear regime. Focusing on the vicinity of the phase boundary between the insulating phase and the CME phase, we find that the chiral magnetic current exhibits a multi-valued dependence on the magnetic field, indicating a highly nonlinear response characteristic of this model. We further study the dynamical stability of the insulating phase near the transition point, and show that the presence of both an axial chemical potential and an external magnetic field cooperatively stabilize the system. Our results clarify the interplay between the axial chemical potential and the magnetic field in determining the phase structure and stability of the system, and reveal new nonlinear aspects of chiral transport in holographic gauge theories.

</details>

**Authors:** Yashwant Singh  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18326v1>

**Summary.** This paper provides a comprehensive review of advanced Density Functional Theory methods for describing phase transitions in molecular systems. It focuses on overcoming the limitations of previous approximations by accurately capturing how correlation functions change when a system breaks symmetry. The work demonstrates high accuracy in predicting the stability of complex phases like liquid crystals and crystalline solids.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of accurately applying classical Density Functional Theory (DFT) to broken symmetry phases in molecular systems, specifically regarding the determination of pair correlation functions (PCFs) and the relative stability of phases.

**Main result.** The development of an 'exact DFT' and an Effective DFT (EDFT) approach that accounts for new contributions to correlation functions during symmetry breaking, providing accurate predictions for phase boundaries (e.g., fluid-solid, bcc-fcc) and thermodynamic stability.

**Method.** The use of functional calculus, Legendre transformations, and integral equation theories (such as RY and ZH closures) to relate the grand potential and intrinsic free energy to density distributions and direct correlation functions.

**Model / system.** Classical many-body systems of rigid or asymmetric molecules (including isotropic fluids, liquid crystals, and crystalline solids) interacting via pairwise additive potentials, such as Lennard-Jones or power-law potentials.

**Key observables.** One-particle density, pair correlation functions (PCFs), direct correlation functions (DCFs), order parameters (positional and orientational), and the structure factor S(q).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature, pressure, chemical potential, molecular anisotropy, softness parameter (1/n), and the degree of particle localization (alpha).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is treated in the classical limit with pairwise additive intermolecular potentials; specific models are limited to rigid linear molecules or specific symmetries.

**Figures summary.** The figures show the variation of free energy difference as a function of density and localization, phase diagrams in the softness vs. density plane, and density-temperature phase diagrams.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the basics of statistical mechanics, moves to the derivation of the grand potential and intrinsic free energy in terms of correlation functions, reviews recent developments in calculating PCFs for broken symmetry phases, and concludes with applications to various phase transitions and comparisons with simulation data.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Although the classical density functional theory (DFT) of inhomogeneous fluids was formulated more than four decades ago, its application to broken symmetry phases of molecular systems remained a challenge. Approximate free energy functionals proposed in the past failed to give accurate description of relative stability of phases, phase transitions, and of properties arising due to broken symmetry. In a DFT pair correlation functions (PCFs) play a fundamental role. While in the case of homogeneous fluids, PCFs are routinely determined using experimental, theoretical or simulation methods, determination of PCFs of broken symmetry phases remained a problem. Breaking of symmetry at the transition point gives rise a new contribution to correlation functions which may differ significantly from that of the coexisting higher symmetry phase. We review methods which have been developed in the last few years to calculate PCFs of broken symmetry phases and their inclusion in the expressions of the grand potential and the intrinsic free-energy. This leads to formulation of an exact DFT. We describe application of the theory to freezing of variety of fluids into ordered phases and transition from an ordered phase of higher symmetry to a phase of lower symmetry. Comparison of results found from different versions of DFT and simulations reveal their accuracy. A brief description of basics of statistical mechanics is included to make the article self-contained.

</details>

### [Parameterized 4-Qubit EWL Quantum Game Circuits with Dirac-Solow-Swan Hamiltonian Integration for Quadruple Helix Disruptive Innovation Recommender Systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18080v1)

**Authors:** Agung Trisetyarso, Fithra Faisal Hastiadi, Kridanto Surendro  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18080v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The circuit is constructed for the representative CORDIS Horizon Europe project COVend (ID 101045956). Each qubit corresponds to one helix actor (q0 = Academia, q1 = Industry, q2 = Government, q3 = Civil Society). Local strategy operators are realized as single-qubit rotations Ry(θi) with angles θi = 2 arcsin(√pi) derived directly from nor- malized participant funding dominance weights (see Table 1). The upper panel shows the forward circuit (initial Hadamard layer, multi-qubit EWL entangler, and parameterized...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a quantum computing framework for predicting innovation trends by mapping real-world economic funding data onto a 4-qubit quantum game circuit. By integrating these quantum results into a relativistic economic Hamiltonian, the authors can simulate complex capital accumulation and disruptive growth dynamics. The approach offers a computationally efficient, NISQ-compatible tool for strategic decision-making in complex ecosystems.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of modeling and forecasting disruptive innovation within complex 'quadruple helix' socio-economic ecosystems using classical methods.

**Main result.** The development of a NISQ-compatible 4-qubit quantum circuit that successfully forecasts disruptive capital trajectories and reproduces capital bifurcation dynamics.

**Method.** A parameterized Eisert-Wilkens-Lewenstein (EWL) quantum game circuit is tuned using real-world CORDIS funding data and integrated with a Dirac-Solow-Swan Hamiltonian for time-evolution simulation.

**Model / system.** A 4-qubit EWL quantum game circuit representing four helix actors (academia, industry, government, and civil society), governed by a Dirac-Solow-Swan Hamiltonian where measurement probabilities act as a diagonal Dirac potential.

**Key observables.** Post-game measurement probabilities (recommender scores) and the time-evolution of disruptive capital probability.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Normalized dominance weights (p_i) from funding data, rotation angles (theta_i), and circuit complexity (22 gates, depth 11).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Local strategies are represented by single Ry rotations, and economic dynamics can be modeled via a simplified diagonal Dirac potential.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the time-evolution trajectories of the economic model, specifically demonstrating capital bifurcation and exponential growth.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the failure of classical models, presents a quantum-enhanced circuit design, details the integration of empirical data and relativistic Hamiltonians, and concludes with complexity analysis and NISQ compatibility.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a novel parameterized 4-qubit Eisert-Wilkens-Lewenstein (EWL) quantum game circuit for recommender systems in quadruple helix innovation ecosystems (academia, industry, government, and civil society). The local strategy operators $U_{i} = R_y(θ_{i})$ for each helix actor are directly tuned by normalized dominance weights extracted from real participant funding data (\texit{ecContribution}) in the European Commission CORDIS Horizon Europe database (project COVend, ID 101045956). The circuit employs a multi-qubit EWL entangler followed by parameterized local rotations, inverse entangler, and full measurement, achieving only 22 gates and circuit depth 11 while scaling as $O(n)$ for $n$-round helix communications. Measurement probabilities after the quantum game serve as recommender scores for disruptive versus sustaining innovation trends. These scores are subsequently mapped into the diagonal Dirac potential of a Dirac-Solow-Swan Hamiltonian, enabling time-evolution simulation of capital accumulation and bifurcation dynamics under disruptive innovation. Numerical experiments on real CORDIS quadruple-helix collaboration networks demonstrate the circuit's NISQ compatibility and its ability to forecast disruptive capital trajectories with high fidelity.   The proposed framework bridges quantum game theory, parameterized quantum circuits, and relativistic economic growth models, offering a computationally efficient tool for innovation policy and strategic decision-making in complex socio-economic ecosystems. Complexity analysis and reproducibility are provided through open Qiskit implementations.

</details>

### [Physics in the Public Square: University Extension as a Strategy for Integrating Physics Education and Science Communication](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18384v1)

**Authors:** Andre A. A. Marinho, Gisele B. Freitas, Camila B. C. da Silva  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18384v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18384_figures/2605.18384_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18384_figures/2605.18384_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Some experiments presented by our team to the public.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper evaluates a university extension program that uses low-cost, hands-on physics experiments to bridge the gap between academia and the public. It demonstrates that such outreach effectively promotes scientific literacy in the community and provides essential professional training for future physics teachers.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The gap between academic physics and society caused by the perception of physics as abstract and the lack of experimental infrastructure in basic education.

**Main result.** The outreach activity significantly improved visitor understanding (96.9%) and engagement, while successfully developing communication and pedagogical skills in pre-service teachers.

**Method.** An outreach event 'Physics in the Public Square' was conducted using 12 low-cost didactic experiments, with data collected via structured questionnaires from 52 visitors.

**Model / system.** An experimental platform consisting of 12 didactic experiments built from low-cost materials (PET bottles, wires, magnets, etc.) covering classical mechanics, electromagnetism, and optics.

**Key observables.** Visitor understanding of phenomena, clarity of communication ratings, level of engagement, and familiarity with experiments.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is limited by a relatively small sample size (n = 52) and focuses on a specific regional context in Brazil.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the research team and project logo; Figure 2 displays photographs of the team presenting experiments to the public.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of science communication, describes the design and implementation of the outreach activity, details the pedagogical framework and data collection methods, presents the quantitative and qualitative results, and discusses limitations and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

University extension activities play a fundamental role in bridging the gap between academia and society by fostering the socialization of scientific knowledge. This study reports and analyzes an outreach activity conducted in a public space, involving undergraduate students enrolled in Physics I, Physics III, and Physics IV courses within the Physics Teacher Education Program at the State University of the Tocantina Region of Maranhao (UEMASUL). The activity was developed through the design and presentation of didactic experiments using low-cost materials. Its main objectives were to disseminate fundamental physics concepts to the community, stimulate public interest in science, and provide pre-service teachers with a formative experience integrating theory, practice, and social responsibility. Data were collected from questionnaires adminisvelopment of communication skills, and the strengthening of the university's social role, while also fostering scientific curitered to visitors (n = 52). The results indicate that the activity significantly contributed to student learning, the deosity among participants.

</details>

### [Pointwise Generalization in Deep Neural Networks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18598v1)

**Authors:** Shaojie Li, Yunbei Xu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18598v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18598_figures/2605.18598_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Hierarchical construction of the data-independent prior π and its role in the pointwise- dimension bound (one single-layer case).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18598_figures/2605.18598_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Effective Rank evolutions of FCNs on MNIST (left) and ResNets on CIFAR-10 (right) across the training</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18598_figures/2605.18598_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Riemannian Dimension evolutions of FCNs on MNIST (left) and ResNets on CIFAR-10 (right) across the training</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes a new theory of generalization for deep learning that moves away from worst-case global complexity to a localized, feature-dependent measure. By focusing on the effective dimension of learned representations, it explains how overparameterization actually aids generalization through feature compression. This provides a much tighter mathematical bound on the performance of neural networks than traditional methods.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Explaining why massive, overparameterized deep neural networks generalize well in practice despite classical theory predicting severe overfitting.

**Main result.** Established a pointwise generalization theory where the generalization gap is controlled by a 'Riemannian Dimension' derived from the eigenvalues of learned feature representations, providing bounds orders of magnitude tighter than previous methods.

**Method.** A non-perturbative, representation-aware framework using hierarchical covering on Grassmannian manifolds and a mixed empirical-ghost metric to analyze the geometry of the feature space.

**Model / system.** Fully connected (feed-forward) Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) with L layers, widths d_l, and 1-Lipschitz nonlinear activations like ReLU.

**Key observables.** Generalization gap, pointwise Riemannian Dimension, effective dimension (d_eff), and feature compression/rank.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Overparameterization regime, feature-scale resolution (epsilon), and spectral decay of the feature Gram matrix.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Relies on verifiable spectral conditions on learned features and assumes 1-Lipschitz activation functions where sigma(0)=0.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a pointwise generalization framework, develops a non-perturbative mathematical calculus for DNNs, establishes upper and lower bounds on the generalization gap using Riemannian Dimension, and provides empirical evidence of feature compression and optimizer bias.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We address the fundamental question of why deep neural networks generalize by establishing a pointwise generalization theory for fully connected networks. This framework resolves long-standing barriers to characterizing the rich nonlinear feature-learning regime and builds a new statistical foundation for representation learning. For each trained model, we characterize the hypothesis via a pointwise Riemannian Dimension, derived from the eigenvalues of the learned feature representations across layers. This establishes a principled framework for deriving hypothesis-dependent, representation-aware generalization bounds. These bounds offer a systematic upgrade over approaches based on model size, products of norms, and infinite-width linearizations, yielding guarantees that are orders of magnitude tighter in both theory and experiment. Analytically, we identify the structural properties and mathematical principles that explain the tractability of deep networks. Empirically, the pointwise Riemannian Dimension exhibits substantial feature compression, decreases with increased over-parameterization, and captures the implicit bias of optimizers. Taken together, our results indicate that deep networks are mathematically tractable in practical regimes and that their generalization is sharply explained by pointwise, feature-spectrum-aware complexity.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.17840"></a>
### [Pole Skipping, Avoided Crossing, and Resonant Excitation in Kerr Quasinormal Modes near Algebraically Special Frequencies](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17840v1)

**Authors:** Kei-ichiro Kubota, Hayato Motohash  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17840v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17840_figures/2605.17840_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Prograde Kerr QNM frequencies for (s, l, m) = (−2, 2, 2) over −1 &lt; a/M &lt; 1. The black dot marks the Schwarzschild limit a/M = 0.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17840_figures/2605.17840_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Riemann-surface structure of the Schwarzschild Green’s function, visualized by the color-coded phase arg(1/AT in). Black crosses and dots mark QNM poles and Matsubara zeros, respectively. The prograde and retrograde QNMs lie on the lower and upper sheets, respectively.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17840_figures/2605.17840_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Near-AS QNM and MM frequencies (upper left), absolute values of the excitation factors (upper right), and excitation- factor trajectories in the complex plane (lower) for the 80 and 81 branches. Solid curves denote QNM poles and dashed curves denote Matsubara zeros. The white circle marks the pole-skipping point, and thick curves highlight the resonant-excitation region 7.0 × 10−5 ≲a/M ≲2.4 × 10−4.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper resolves decades-old puzzles regarding the behavior of black hole ringdown modes near specific frequencies. It shows that what appeared to be disappearing or splitting modes are actually the result of complex interactions between poles and zeros on different Riemann sheets. This provides a continuous and physically consistent description of the transition from rotating to non-rotating black holes.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Resolving long-standing anomalies in Kerr quasinormal mode (QNM) spectra near algebraically special frequencies, specifically the apparent bifurcation, disappearance, and non-smooth connection to the Schwarzschild limit.

**Main result.** The paper demonstrates that the apparent bifurcation is due to an avoided crossing with resonant excitation, while the mode disappearance is caused by pole skipping via the cancellation of a QNM pole by a Matsubara-mode zero.

**Method.** The authors use Green's function analysis, the Teukolsky equation, and the Mano-Suzuki-Takasugi (MST) representation to track poles and zeros across different Riemann sheets.

**Model / system.** The system consists of rotating Kerr black holes and their non-rotating Schwarzschild limit, specifically focusing on gravitational perturbations of the (l, m) = (2, 2) multipole.

**Key observables.** Quasinormal mode frequencies, excitation factors (BnT), gravitational-wave strain, and the scattering matrix.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Black hole mass (M), spin parameter (a/M), Hawking temperature (TH), and horizon angular velocity (OH).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is based on linear perturbation theory in General Relativity.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 visualizes the Riemann-surface structure of the Schwarzschild Green's function; Figure 2 shows the trajectory of Kerr QNM poles as spin varies; Figure 3 displays the magnitude and trajectories of excitation factors and the identification of Matsubara zeros.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the long-standing anomalies in the Kerr/Schwarzschild limit, introduces a pole-zero tracking framework using the MST method, provides the physical resolution via avoided crossings and pole skipping, and discusses the universality and finiteness of the physical waveforms.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Kerr quasinormal modes near algebraically special frequencies exhibit anomalous behavior, including apparent bifurcation, disappearance, and a nonsmooth connection to the Schwarzschild limit, which has remained puzzling for decades. Tracking poles and zeros of Green-function building blocks across different Riemann sheets, we show that the bifurcation is due to an avoided crossing accompanied by resonant excitation, while the disappearance is due to pole skipping caused by cancellation of a quasinormal-mode pole by a Matsubara-mode zero. This resolves the physical origin of these long-standing anomalies.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.18207"></a>
### [Positivity of the effective range for finite range attractive potentials with a repulsive core](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18207v1)

**Authors:** Davide Germani  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18207v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18207_figures/2605.18207_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Repulsive potential. The function 𝑢0 is shown in black, while the dashed line represents the asymptotic expression. The scattering length is positive, as the line intersects the positive 𝑟−axis, and lies between 0 and 𝑅.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18207_figures/2605.18207_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Attractive potential not strong enough to support a bound state. The function 𝑢0 is shown in black, while the dashed line represents the asymptotic expression. The scattering length is negative, as the line intersects the negative 𝑟-axis.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18207_figures/2605.18207_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Attractive potential that supports a shallow bound state. The function 𝑢0 is shown in black, while the dashed line represents the asymptotic expression. The scattering length is positive and 𝑎≫𝑅.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.18207_figures/2605.18207_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. The wave function 𝑢𝐵of the shallow bound state is shown in black. The spatial extent of the wave function is much larger than the range of the potential. The dashed line represents the exponential behavior of the asymptotic expression.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.18207_figures/2605.18207_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Solid line: zero-energy wavefunction for the potential in Eq. (35) with the parameters listed in Tab. I. The dashed line indicates the solution for the purely attractive problem. The dash-dotted line represents the asymptotic solution 1 −¯𝑟/ ¯𝑎, which is common to both solutions since they share the same scattering length. The vertical dotted lines indicate the end of the repulsive region (¯𝑟= ¯𝑟1) and the range of the potential (¯𝑟= 1), respectively.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proves that adding a repulsive core to an attractive finite-range potential cannot produce a negative effective range if the scattering length is larger than the potential range. This result implies that the negative effective ranges used to identify compact exotic hadrons cannot be explained by simple single-channel local potentials and instead require more complex dynamics like coupled-channels.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether a single-channel local potential with an inner repulsive core and an outer attractive tail can produce a negative effective range ($r_0 < 0$).

**Main result.** The author rigorously proves that for such finite-range potentials, the effective range remains strictly positive provided the scattering length is greater than the potential range ($a > R$).

**Method.** The study uses the radial Schrödinger equation, effective range expansion (ERE), and an integral representation of the phase shift to derive a mathematical proof based on the convexity of the wave function.

**Model / system.** The system consists of single-channel local, spherically symmetric, finite-range potentials, specifically modeled using a piecewise continuous spherical barrier/well and a truncated superposition of Yukawa potentials.

**Key observables.** Scattering length ($a$), effective range ($r_0$), and phase shift ($\delta_0$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Potential range ($R$), repulsive core radius ($r_1$), scattering length ($a$), and the regime where $a > R$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The proof assumes the potentials are real, local, and finite-range, and specifically focuses on the condition $a > R$.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a repulsive potential and its wave function; Figures 2, 3, and 4 illustrate the zero-energy wave function and its asymptotic form, demonstrating the spatial extent of the potential's influence.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the phenomenological problem of $r_0$ in hadron physics, defines the mathematical model of the potential, provides a rigorous proof of the positivity of $r_0$, verifies the result with numerical models (barrier/well and Yukawa), and discusses the implications for multiquark configurations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In the phenomenological study of exotic hadrons, the sign of the effective range, $r_0$, is invoked as a criterion to distinguish between compact multiquark configurations (associated with $r_0 < 0$) and loosely bound hadronic molecules ($r_0 > 0$). Motivated by this, we investigate the fundamental constraints on the sign of the effective range for single-channel local interactions. We rigorously prove that for finite-range potentials, characterized by an inner repulsive core and an outer attractive tail, the effective range remains strictly positive provided that the scattering length is greater than the range of the potential ($a > R$).

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.18501"></a>
### [Quantum Emitters at Telecommunication Wavelengths based on Carbon Defects in Transition Metal Dichalcogenides](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18501v1)

**Authors:** Chanaprom Cholsuk, Sujin Suwanna, Tobias Vogl  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18501v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18501_figures/2605.18501_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1. Geometries and charge stabilities. a The 7×7×1 supercell structures where the carbon is doped on top or middle layers. b - e Defect formation energies of WS2, WSe2, MoS2, and MoSe2, respectively, under chalcogen-rich and chalcogen-poor conditions as a function of Fermi level. Finite-size charge corrections were taken into account. The Fermi level represents the chemical potential, which varies across the bandgap. The crossing point between the formation energy lines of different charge states (q) defines the charge transition level. Solid lines correspond to structures with C doped at the topmost layer, while dashed lines correspond to structures with C doped at the middle layer.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18501_figures/2605.18501_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2. Kohn–Sham electronic transitions at the Γ point for carbon substitution in the top layer. a and b for WS2 doped with CS and C−1 S . c and d for WSe2 doped with CSe and C−1 Se . e and f for MoS2 doped with CS and C−1 S . g and h for MoSe2 doped with CSe and C−1 Se . The green arrows represent the electronic transition responsible for the computed ZPLs. Note that the electronic transitions for the doping at the middle layer remain unchanged, as can be seen from Supplementary S2.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18501_figures/2605.18501_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3. Simulated photoluminescense spectra. a and b for WS2 doped with CS and C−1 S . c and d for WSe2 doped with CSe and C−1 Se . e and f for MoS2 doped with CS and C−1 S . g and h for MoSe2 doped with CSe and C−1 Se . The green and pink shaded regions indicate the telecommunication wavelength ranges for the O-band (1260-1360 nm) and C-band (1530-1565 nm), respectively.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes using carbon-doped TMD bilayers as a way to create stable, room-temperature quantum emitters. By using the indirect bandgap of bilayers to suppress background noise, the authors show that carbon defects can emit single photons specifically in the telecommunication wavelength bands.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work identifies a scalable, solid-state platform for generating single photons at telecom wavelengths, which is critical for developing integrated quantum networks and photonic quantum computing.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving robust, room-temperature single-photon emission in 2D materials by overcoming the photoluminescence quenching found in TMD monolayers.

**Main result.** Carbon-doped TMD bilayers provide a stable platform for quantum emitters at telecommunication wavelengths (O- and C-bands) due to the suppression of excitonic background by the indirect bandgap.

**Method.** Hybrid-functional Density Functional Theory (HSE06) combined with the delta-SCF method for excited states and the Pyphotonics package for phonon-mediated luminescence modeling.

**Model / system.** Substitutional carbon defects at chalcogen sites (S and Se) within transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) bilayers, specifically WS2, WSe2, MoS2, and MoSe2.

**Key observables.** Zero-phonon line (ZPL) wavelengths, Huang-Rhys factor, Debye-Waller factor, radiative lifetime, and transition dipole orientation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** O-band (1260-1360 nm) and C-band (1530-1565 nm) telecommunication windows; neutral vs. singly negative charge states.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the stability of the 2H stacking phase and focuses on the electronic/optical properties of the defect-induced two-level system without investigating intersystem crossing.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the supercell geometries for top and middle layer carbon doping and the corresponding defect formation energies.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of PL quenching, describes the computational methodology (DFT and spectroscopic modeling), presents results on charge stability and optical properties (ZPL, lifetime, dipole), and concludes with experimental identification fingerprints.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Low-dimensional materials have emerged as promising hosts for quantum emitters, whose emission typically arises from either strain-induced band bending or defect-induced two-level systems. Among these materials, transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) monolayers have attracted particular attention; however, their performance is limited by strong photoluminescence (PL) quenching at room temperature. As TMDs transition from a direct to an indirect bandgap when moving from monolayers to multilayers, we herein propose a strategy to overcome this quenching limitation by exploiting the indirect bandgap of TMD bilayers in combination with a point defect doping. The indirect gap suppresses excitonic PL, while specific defects enable robust defect-mediated quantum emission. Using hybrid-functional density functional theory, we investigate substitutional carbon defects at chalcogen sites (S and Se) in WS2, WSe2, MoS2, and MoSe2 bilayers and comprehensively characterize their optical properties. Both neutral and singly negative charge states are found to be thermodynamically stable. Neutral defects exhibit singlet configurations with emission in the O- and C-band telecommunication windows, whereas negatively charged defects adopt doublet configurations featuring spin-selective transitions and near-infrared emission. The electron-phonon coupling strength, radiative lifetime, and dipole orientation are found to depend sensitively on both the host material and defect site, providing distinct fingerprints for experimental identification. Our findings, therefore, establish carbon-doped TMD bilayers as promising platforms for room-temperature defect-based quantum emitters operating at telecommunication wavelengths.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.18277"></a>
### [Quantum geometry induced anomalous chiral transport and hidden symmetry breaking in centrosymmetric 2M-WS2](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18277v1)

**Authors:** Hang Cui, Shao-Bo Liu, Erqing Wang, Mingxiang Pan, Yuqiang Fang, Ning Ma, Wenlong Liu, Di Chen, Yu Zhang, Yuanjun Song, Tingting Hao, Jiankun Li, Jian Cui, Ya Feng, Haiwen Liu, Fuqiang Huang, Huaqing Huang, X. -C. Xie, Jian-Hao Chen  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18277v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18277_figures/2605.18277_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Nonreciprocal transport under magnetic field in non-centrosymmetric crystals. (a) Spatial</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18277_figures/2605.18277_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Electrical magnetochiral anisotropy (eMChA) in centrosymmetric 2M-WS2 flake. (a)-(b) The</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18277_figures/2605.18277_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Angular, temperature, and field dependence of the electrical magnetochiral anisotropy</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.18277_figures/2605.18277_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Hidden symmetry breaking and intertwined physics in the normal state of 2M-WS2 around T ~ 25 K. (a) Two solid red lines represent temperature dependent second-harmonic voltage</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper reports the discovery of unexpected chiral transport in the centrosymmetric material 2M-WS2. The researchers show that this nonlinear response, along with a large Nernst effect, emerges simultaneously with a transition from a Fermi liquid to a strange metal. These phenomena are attributed to nontrivial quantum geometry and potential hidden symmetry breaking.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the origin of unexpected nonreciprocal transport (eMChA) and hidden symmetry breaking in the centrosymmetric material 2M-WS2, and understanding its connection to the Fermi liquid to strange metal transition.

**Main result.** The discovery of remarkable electronic magnetochiral anisotropy (eMChA) in centrosymmetric 2M-WS2 that coincides with an anomalous Nernst response and a crossover from Fermi liquid to strange metal at 25 K, driven by nontrivial quantum geometry.

**Method.** Experimental transport measurements using second-harmonic generation (SHG) and lock-in techniques, combined with first-principles DFT calculations and phenomenological modeling of nonreciprocal transport.

**Model / system.** The physical system is 2M-WS2, a metastable, centrosymmetric candidate topological superconductor characterized by a crossover from Fermi liquid to strange metal behavior.

**Key observables.** Second-harmonic voltage (V2w), first-harmonic voltage (V1w), eMChA coefficient (gamma), Nernst coefficient (nu), scattering rate (Gamma_tr), and Hall coefficient (RH).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Crossover temperature TFL approx 25 K, superconducting transition temperature Tc = 8.8 K, and magnetic field (B) and current (I) amplitudes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The existence of a thick-layer-sliding mechanism as a source for nontrivial quantum geometry.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Concept of symmetry breaking and nonreciprocal transport; Fig 2: 2M-WS2 device geometry, symmetry, and voltage dependence on B and I; Fig 3: Angular, temperature, and field dependence of eMChA; Fig 4: Phase diagram correlating eMChA, Nernst effect, and scattering rates.

**Paper structure.** Introduction to symmetry breaking and eMChA; Experimental setup and device fabrication; Observation of eMChA in 2M-WS2; Correlation of eMChA with Nernst effect and FL-SM transition; Theoretical analysis of quantum geometry and structural origin; Conclusion.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Chirality, a widely existing material property in nature involving the breaking of the left-right symmetry, has profound influences in various fields of natural sciences. Nonlinear response, such as electronic magnetochiral anisotropy (eMChA), has been recognized as a sensitive probe for the effects of symmetry breaking and nontrivial quantum geometries in solids. So far, observations of eMChA have primarily been limited to inversion-symmetry broken materials. Here, we report a remarkable chiral transport in centrosymmetric candidate topological superconductor 2M-WS2 flakes observed via second-harmonic generation under an out-of-plane magnetic field. More importantly, the eMChA becomes significant around the crossover temperature TFL ~ 25 K from the Fermi liquid (FL) to strange metal (SM) in the normal state, which interestingly echoes with the anomalously large Nernst response at the same temperature in bulk 2M-WS2. These observations reveal a direct correspondence between the nonlinear response, Nernst response, and FL-SM transition in 2M-WS2. Theoretical analysis indicates that nontrivial quantum geometry is behind the simultaneous response of eMChA and Nernst effects in 2M-WS2 and the contribution from the orbital magnetic moment at the Fermi surface becomes significant during the FL-SM transition. Based on first-principles calculations, a thick-layer-sliding mechanism with minimal energy gain in 2M-WS2 provides one possibility for the generation of such nontrivial quantum geometry. The intertwined physics of remarkable eMChA, Nernst response, and FL-SM transition make 2M-WS2 a rare quantum platform to study the chiral transport and unexplored phenomena in strange metals, which may shed light on the trans-century, unresolved scientific issue in unconventional high-temperature superconductivity.

</details>

### [Quantum Model for CVRPTW](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18393v1)

**Authors:** Imran Meghazi, Éric Bourreau  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18393v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18393_figures/2605.18393_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Quantum circuit for capacity constraint</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18393_figures/2605.18393_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Quantum circuit for time constraint</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18393_figures/2605.18393_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. Number of qubits relative to the number of customers for the oracle with the maximum capacity and number of time windows both ﬁxed to 8.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new quantum algorithm for solving complex vehicle routing problems with capacity and time constraints. By using a 'split-inspired' technique, the authors significantly reduce the number of extra qubits needed compared to previous methods, making the problem more accessible to near-term quantum hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing an efficient quantum algorithm to solve the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (CVRPTW), which is an NP-hard optimization problem.

**Main result.** The authors propose a qubit-efficient quantum model that adds only a linear number of decision qubits compared to standard TSP formulations, providing an optimal approach for the NISQ era.

**Method.** The approach uses a Grover Adaptive Search (GAS) framework based on the Dürr and Høyer algorithm, employing a 'route-first, cluster-second' strategy with a split-inspired model.

**Model / system.** The model is designed for gate-based quantum computers in the NISQ era, utilizing a position-based encoding and an oracle that checks capacity and time window constraints via reversible logic.

**Key observables.** Total travel cost (distance) and the validity of constraints (capacity and time windows).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of customers (n), maximum vehicle capacity (Cmax), delivery time windows [ai, bi], and the distance matrix (D).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes each customer is assigned a single time window and is limited by the gate complexity and qubit counts available on NISQ-era hardware.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the giant tour and the auxiliary graph used for the split procedure; Figure 2 and 3 show quantum circuit diagrams for capacity and time window constraints; Figure 4 shows qubit scaling relative to the number of customers.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the CVRPTW problem, proposes a quantum algorithm based on Grover Search, details the qubit-efficient encoding and oracle construction (including capacity and time constraints), analyzes the complexity, and discusses hardware limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper proposes a quantum algorithm for the capacitated vehicle routing problem with time windows (CVRPTW) based on Grover Search framework. This problem is often faced by Postal services in the context of package delivery or other time-sensitive operations. We provide an implementation on gate based quantum computer of a model inspired by classical route first, cluster second technique. The quantum paradigm allows to overcome suboptimality inherent property of this decomposition. In the current NISQ (Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum) era, the most important limitation is the number of available qubits which makes time windows and capacity constraints hard to tackle. We introduce a qubit-efficient split-inspired modeling which adds only a linear number of decision qubits to standard quantum formulations for Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP).

</details>
