### [quantum-safe: Bridging the Post-Quantum Production Gap with a Hybrid-by-Default Python Cryptography Library](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17061v1)

**Authors:** Animesh Shaw  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17061v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17061_figures/2605.17061_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Production readiness matrix for nine PQC libraries across eight dimensions. F = Full, P = Partial, N = None. Red borders highlight the three critical gaps: Hybrid KEM, Migration Path, and Protocol Layer. quantum-safe scores Full on all eight dimensions.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17061_figures/2605.17061_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Module architecture of quantum-safe. Application code interacts only with HybridKEM and HybridSign. Protocol helpers (tls, x509, envelope) and the audit module sit above a backend abstraction layer that currently exposes liboqs-python and RustCrypto.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17061_figures/2605.17061_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Lines of code required for a complete hybrid KEM operation. liboqs- python and noble-post-quantum require manual combiner implementation. cloudflare/circl (Go) is concise but has no Python binding. quantum-safe requires three lines; hybrid is the default.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17061_figures/2605.17061_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. Stacked bar decomposition of hybrid KEM latency. The Python combiner (HKDF-SHA256, PEM/CBOR serialisation, key wrapping) accounts for 37–64 µs per operation — comparable to or exceeding the underlying cryptographic primitive cost in ENV-2.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.17061_figures/2605.17061_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5. (a) Throughput vs concurrency; (b) per-user latency vs concurrency. ENV-2 and ENV-1 show nearly identical throughput despite a 2.4× per- operation latency difference, confirming GIL release.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper addresses the practical deployment challenges of post-quantum cryptography by introducing a Python library designed for 'hybrid-by-default' use. It quantifies the lack of production-ready tools in the current ecosystem and demonstrates that the new library provides high-performance, low-complexity implementations of NIST-standardized algorithms.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The 'production gap' in post-quantum cryptography (PQC), where despite standardized algorithms, there is a lack of practical tools for hybrid key exchange, migration, and protocol integration.

**Main result.** The authors introduce 'quantum-safe', a Python library that simplifies hybrid KEM implementation from 45 to 3 lines of code, and demonstrate that its performance overhead is negligible compared to TLS 1.3 budgets.

**Method.** A systematic evaluation of nine PQC libraries across eight production-readiness dimensions, combined with rigorous performance benchmarking and the introduction of the Coefficient of Variation (CoV) as a timing side-channel proxy.

**Model / system.** A software-based experimental platform using Python 3.12, liboqs, and Docker/Linux environments to evaluate cryptographic primitives like ML-KEM and ML-DSA.

**Key observables.** Latency (microseconds), throughput (ops/s), Coefficient of Variation (CoV) for timing stability, and Lines of Code (LOC) for API complexity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** X25519 + ML-KEM-768 handshake latency, 5,000 concurrent users, and the AES-256-GCM noise floor for timing analysis.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The CoV methodology is treated as a necessary but not sufficient condition for constant-time verification; results are specific to the Python 3.12 environment.

**Figures summary.** The figures include a production-readiness heatmap of existing libraries, a module architecture diagram for the new library, a comparison of code complexity (LOC), and charts showing timing stability (CoV) and latency percentiles.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the production gap, presents the 'quantum-safe' library design, provides a comparative analysis of the existing ecosystem, details the performance and timing stability benchmarks, and concludes with the introduction of new auditing tools.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The August 2024 finalisation of FIPS 203 (ML-KEM), FIPS 204 (ML-DSA), and FIPS 205 (SLH-DSA) closed the algorithmic gap in post-quantum cryptography (PQC). The production gap -- hybrid combiners, versioned key formats, protocol helpers, and migration tooling -- remains open. We present quantum-safe, a Python library that closes all three critical gaps we identify, and a systematic evaluation of the nine-library ecosystem that quantifies them.   We score nine PQC libraries across eight production-readiness dimensions. Three dimensions have coverage below 35%: hybrid KEM support (11%), migration tooling (22%), and protocol integration (33%). quantum-safe scores Full on all eight. The full API reduces the hybrid KEM task from 45 lines of manual combiner code to three lines, directly lowering the risk of insecure combiner implementations.   We report the first statistically rigorous per-operation overhead measurement for a Python hybrid PQC library (3,000 iterations, CPU-pinned, bootstrapped 95% confidence intervals). A full X25519 + ML-KEM-768 handshake completes in 243 μs under Docker/Linux -- 0.5--2.5% of a typical TLS 1.3 round-trip budget. At 5,000 concurrent users, throughput holds at 2,848 ops/s with only 4.9% degradation versus the single-user baseline, confirming that liboqs releases the Python GIL during C-level operations.   We introduce Coefficient of Variation (CoV) as a practical timing side-channel proxy across all FIPS 203/204 operations. ML-KEM-768 decapsulation achieves CoV = 3.9%, within the AES-256-GCM noise floor (2.1%). ML-DSA-65 signing shows CoV = 51.5%, expected from FIPS 204 rejection sampling, not a side-channel. This CoV methodology has not previously been applied to PQC library evaluation and provides a lightweight complement to formal constant-time verification tools. All results are reproducible via a single Docker command.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.16509"></a>
### [Quokka#: Quantum Computing with #SAT](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16509v1)

**Authors:** Jingyi Mei, Dekel Zak, Muhammad Osama, Tim Coopmans, Alfons Laarman  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16509v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.16509_figures/2605.16509_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. The architecture of Quokka#.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** Quokka# is a new Python library that treats quantum circuit analysis tasks as weighted model counting (#SAT) problems. It provides a unified framework for simulating, verifying, and synthesizing quantum circuits, offering high efficiency and the ability to handle arbitrary gate sets through approximate equivalence checking.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for a versatile, open-source tool capable of performing various quantum circuit analysis tasks, including simulation, verification, equivalence checking, and synthesis, across universal gate sets.

**Main result.** The development of Quokka#, a Python library that reduces quantum tasks to weighted model counting (#SAT), demonstrating competitive performance in simulation and equivalence checking, and enabling depth-optimal circuit synthesis.

**Method.** The framework reduces quantum circuit operations to weighted #SAT problems using various encodings (computational-basis, Pauli-basis, and cyclic) and utilizes specialized engines for simulation, verification via quantum Hoare logic, and synthesis via Max#SAT.

**Model / system.** The system consists of n-qubit quantum circuits represented in QASM format, utilizing various gate sets such as Clifford+T, Toffoli+H, and Clifford+R, with states represented as vectors or density matrices.

**Key observables.** Measurement probabilities, Jamiołkowski fidelity, and circuit depth.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Precision parameter (epsilon) for approximate equivalence and synthesis, and complex-valued weights for amplitudes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The current implementation focuses on strong simulation (computational and Pauli-basis measurements) and is restricted to unitary quantum circuits without measurements or control-flow constructs.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides an architectural diagram of the Quokka# design, showing the interaction between the circuit encoder, CNF generator, and #SAT solver across the functional engines; Tables 1-4 compare Quokka# performance against other state-of-the-art tools and show the iterative progress of circuit synthesis.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Quokka# library, details its mathematical framework and various encodings, describes the four functional engines (Simulation, Verification, Equivalence Checking, and Synthesis), provides benchmarking results against existing tools, and discusses limitations and future work.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present Quokka#, a versatile, open-source Python library for quantum circuit analysis. Quokka# reduces various simulation, verification, and synthesis tasks to weighted model counting (#SAT). It supports universal quantum circuits and a wide variety of gates. Quokka# provides multiple encodings based on different algebraic bases and equivalence-checking methods, enabling key performance trade-offs. Moreover, the new version of Quokka# adds approximate equivalence checking, which is crucial in its synthesis algorithms, since it enables translation between arbitrary gate sets. Its synthesis engine is depth-optimal, making it well-suited to real-world quantum computing. This paper demonstrates the design, extensibility, and use of Quokka#.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.16666"></a>
### [Reducing the Complexity of Density-Matrix Functionals in a Real-Space-Decomposed DF+RDMF Scheme with the Adaptive Cluster Approximation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16666v1)

**Authors:** Konstantin Tamoev, Robert Schade, Thomas D. Kühne  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16666v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.16666_figures/2605.16666_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Convergence of the local density-matrix correction with ACA bath level. The data demonstrate the systematic reduction of the truncation error across the ACA(1)–ACA(4) hierarchy as additional effective bath levels are retained.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.16666_figures/2605.16666_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Bending potentials of C3O2 on a common relative energy scale. Both curves are shifted such that E(180◦) = 0. Semilocal PBE favors the linear structure, whereas the real-space-decomposed DF+RDMF/ACA selected-CI result stabilizes a bent geometry.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.16666_figures/2605.16666_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Switching function f(r) used in the real-space in- teraction decomposition. The origin is placed at the central carbon atom of C3O2. Vertical markers indicate the distances to the neighboring carbon atoms and terminal oxygen atoms.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new hybrid method that combines the efficiency of DFT for weak correlations with the accuracy of RDMFT for strong local correlations. By using real-space decomposition and the Adaptive Cluster Approximation, the authors reduce the computational complexity of the density-matrix functionals. The method successfully corrects the structural prediction of carbon suboxide from linear to bent, demonstrating its potential for studying spatially inhomogeneous, strongly correlated systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The exponential computational scaling of Reduced Density-Matrix Functional Theory (RDMFT) makes it difficult to treat large systems, and combining it with Density Functional Theory (DFT) without double-counting or prohibitive costs is a major challenge.

**Main result.** The DF+RDMF/ACA scheme successfully recovers the correct qualitative structural tendency (a bent configuration) for carbon suboxide, whereas standard semilocal PBE incorrectly predicts a linear structure.

**Method.** A real-space-decomposed DF+RDMF scheme is used to partition the Coulomb interaction, combined with the Adaptive Cluster Approximation (ACA) to compress local density-matrix functionals via unitary rotation of the bath subspace.

**Model / system.** The study uses the bending potential of carbon suboxide (C3O2), a quasilinear oxocarbon cumulene, as a molecular test case to evaluate local correlation effects.

**Key observables.** C-C-C bending potential energy E(alpha), equilibrium bond angle, and ground-state energy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The spectroscopic bending barrier scale (0.09-0.36 mHa) and the ACA(M) truncation hierarchy.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The exchange-correlation hole is assumed to depend on the local value of the decomposition function, and the interaction is partitioned using a bounded real-space decomposition function.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the smooth switching function f(r) used for the real-space interaction decomposition.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scaling bottleneck of RDMFT, proposes a real-space decomposition DF+RDMF scheme, details the ACA compression method, applies the method to the C3O2 bending potential, and discusses convergence and accuracy.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Reduced density-matrix functional theory (RDMFT) provides a variational route to electronic correlations beyond conventional density-functional approximations, but explicit evaluations of density-matrix functionals still scale exponentially with the number of active one-particle states. We formulate and assess a real-space-decomposed density-functional plus reduced-density-matrix-functional (DF+RDMF) scheme in which the Coulomb interaction is partitioned locally in real space and the RDMF correction is evaluated only for the strongly correlated part of the interaction. The resulting local density-matrix functionals are further compressed using the adaptive cluster approximation (ACA), which performs a unitary rotation of the bath subspace before truncation and therefore preserves the local interaction while reducing the number of explicitly correlated bath states. As a molecular test case, we consider the bending potential of carbon suboxide, C$_3$O$_2$. While semilocal PBE favors a linear molecule, the DF+RDMF/ACA correction stabilizes a bent configuration in qualitative agreement with the quasilinear behavior inferred from spectroscopy. The approach provides a systematic embedding hierarchy for combining density functionals with explicitly correlated density-matrix corrections in extended or spatially inhomogeneous systems.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.16580"></a>
### [Revealing Hund superdispersion with tunneling spectroscopy](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16580v1)

**Authors:** Luke C. Rhodes, Fabian B. Kugler, Olivier Gingras, Carolina Marques, Edgar Abarca Morales, Phil D. C. King, Antoine Georges, Peter Wahl  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16580v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.16580_figures/2605.16580_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Waterfalls and superdispersive spectral features. (a) Schematic of a waterfall (left), where vertical waterfall-like features connect the quasiparticle band with the Hubbard band, and the superdispersion found in a Hund metal (right), where a reversal of the dispersion originates from successive renormalization and ‘unrenormalization’ of the non-interacting bands. (b,d) Diagonal elements of the real (Σ′ mm(ω)) and imaginary (Σ′′ mm(ω)) parts of the self-energy for Sr2RuO4. In (b), we take (U, J) = (4.5, 0) eV, chosen to produce similar quasiparticle weights as for the physical case (U, J) = (2.3, 0.4) eV in (d). (c,e) Spectral functions of bulk Sr2RuO4 along the Γ-M-X-Γ path...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.16580_figures/2605.16580_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

The surface has a 9◦rotation of the RuO6 octahedral (grey squares) compared to the bulk. (b) Band structure of the 9◦rotated surface of Sr2RuO4 from DFT, with dominant orbital characters shown by red, green and blue for the dxz, dyz and dxy orbitals, respectively. The thin grey bands indicate the electronic structure of the unrotated two-atom unit cell of Sr2RuO4 (equivalent to the bulk structure) for comparison. (c) Comparison of ARPES data points (orange crosses) around the M point, extracted from Ref. 24, 25, with...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.16580_figures/2605.16580_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Correlation-enhanced spin-orbit coupling at the surface of Sr2RuO4. (a) Com- parison of the experimental QPI (left-hand side, taken from Ref. 32) with cQPI+DMFT calculations (right-hand side) at 4.2 mV bias. (b) Energy dispersion along the qy = 0 line shown in a large energy range, revealing the modification of the scattering intensity due to self-energy effects with significant broadening away from the Fermi energy. (c-g) Closeup of the blue box in (b) for models calculated with different strengths of the spin-orbit coupling λ (specified in the lower right corner). A hybridization gap opens around the Fermi level producing two split branches of the dispersion, as seen in experiment...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.16580_figures/2605.16580_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Signatures of Hund superdispersion in tunneling spectroscopy. (a, b) Spectral function of the surface of Sr2RuO4 when incorporating the DMFT bulk self-energy obtained with (a) artificial parameters (U, J) = (4.5, 0) eV and (b) the physical parameters (U, J) = (2.3, 0.4) eV. The parameters in (a) are chosen to produce similar quasiparticles weights as in (b). The difference between the two spectral functions demonstrates the influence of Hund coupling J. The red overlaid lines are the solutions to Eq. (2) underlining the superdispersive feature at 160 meV in (b) with a change in curvature of the dispersion. (c, d) Simulated tunneling spectra L(V ) [Eq. 5] for (c) J = 0 and (d) J &gt; 0....</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that Hund coupling in Sr2RuO4 creates a unique 'superdispersion' feature where band velocity reverses direction. By using tunneling spectroscopy to probe unoccupied states, the authors successfully match experimental conductance dips with theoretical predictions of non-monotonic self-energy. This provides a new experimental route to identifying Hund physics in multi-orbital materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of experimentally identifying and verifying the unique spectroscopic signature of Hund superdispersion in multi-orbital Hund metals, which is difficult to observe via ARPES due to its presence in unoccupied states.

**Main result.** The authors provide direct experimental evidence for Hund-induced superdispersion in Sr2RuO4 using tunneling spectroscopy, showing that Hund coupling induces a non-monotonic self-energy that causes band velocity to reverse direction.

**Method.** The study combines experimental STM/STS and ARPES with a theoretical framework involving DFT, DMFT (using NRG solver), and continuum local density of states (cLDOS) calculations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the Hund metal Sr2RuO4, modeled using a multi-orbital (t2g) tight-binding Hamiltonian that incorporates Coulomb repulsion (U), Hund coupling (J), and spin-orbit coupling (SOC).

**Key observables.** Differential conductance (dI/dV), tunneling spectra (Feenstra function L(V)), quasiparticle interference (QPI) patterns, and the real and imaginary parts of the self-energy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hund coupling (J = 0.4 eV), Hubbard repulsion (U = 2.3 eV), spin-orbit coupling (lambda approx 200 meV), and energy scales of superdispersion (approx 160 meV).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The bulk self-energy is assumed to be applicable to the surface electronic structure despite surface reconstruction (octahedral rotations).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares waterfall vs. superdispersion schematics and self-energy components; Figure 2 shows surface vs. bulk structures and DFT band structures; Figure 4 presents spectral functions and simulated tunneling spectra (Feenstra function) showing the 160 meV dip.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the distinction between Mott and Hund physics, describes the experimental STM/ARPES setup and theoretical DFT+DMFT framework, presents the identification of superdispersion in the unoccupied states, and validates the model through comparison of simulated and experimental tunneling spectra.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In cuprate superconductors, electron-electron repulsion results in characteristic spectroscopic features known as `waterfalls', where the sharp quasiparticle dispersion transitions into broad Hubbard bands. However, in multi-orbital systems, the additional Hund coupling results in behavior that defies the conventional Mott--Hubbard paradigm, creating qualitatively distinct `superdispersive' features in the spectral function. Here, we use tunneling spectroscopy to reveal this signature of Hund physics in Sr$_2$RuO$_4$. By combining density functional theory, dynamical mean-field theory, and continuum local density of states calculations, we show that the experimental features are in excellent agreement with theoretical predictions and intimately linked to the non-monotonous energy dependence of the real part of the self-energy in a Hund metal. Our results provide direct experimental evidence for Hund-induced spectroscopic features and open a new route to probing correlation effects in quantum materials.

</details>

### [Scaling Quantum Optimization for Unit Commitment via Pauli Correlation Encoding](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17145v1)

**Authors:** Kien X. Nguyen, Ilya Safro, Xiaoyuan Liu  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17145v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17145_figures/2605.17145_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Overview of our heuristic-assisted differentiable bilevel optimization on a 12-variable problem, which is encoded into 4 qubits. The quantum circuit produces PCE correlators that decode to a soft commitment schedule ˜y. A classical QP computes the dispatch p∗(and dual variables), which defines the training loss. Gradients are obtained by combining QP dual sensitivities with parameter-shift derivatives, and circuit parameters are updated with the Adam optimizer. After training, a threshold sweep produces a hard schedule and a final dispatch.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17145_figures/2605.17145_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Ablation study on the number of ansatz layers over the range {2, 3, . . . , 9} for both brickwork and EfficientSU2 ansatzes. We report the best operating cost across the five small-scale systems.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17145_figures/2605.17145_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Ablation study on the number of optimization steps over the range {100, 200, 500, 1000} for both brickwork and EfficientSU2 ansatzes. We report the best operating cost across the five small-scale systems.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17145_figures/2605.17145_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5. Ablation study on the number of optimization steps over the range {100, 200, 500, 1000} for both brickwork and EfficientSU2 ansatzes. We report the average operating cost across the five small-scale systems.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.17145_figures/2605.17145_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. Ablation study on the number of ansatz layers over the range {2, 3, . . . , 9} for both brickwork and EfficientSU2 ansatzes. We report the average operating cost across the five small-scale systems.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a way to solve large-scale power grid scheduling problems using much fewer qubits than previously possible. By using Pauli Correlation Encoding, the authors demonstrate that a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm can handle hundreds of decision variables, providing near-optimal solutions for complex energy management tasks.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Scaling the quantum optimization of the Unit Commitment (UC) problem, which is NP-hard, by addressing the prohibitive qubit requirements of traditional encoding methods for multi-period power system scheduling.

**Main result.** The proposed method scales to 312 binary variables using only 15 qubits and produces feasible schedules with operating costs within 1-7% of the classical CPLEX optimum for small scales and 13-16% for large scales.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical leader-follower architecture using Pauli Correlation Encoding (PCE) to compress variables into fewer qubits, combined with a differentiable bilevel optimization approach using a classical Quadratic Program (QP) solver.

**Model / system.** A mixed-integer quadratic bilevel optimization model for power system unit commitment, involving binary on/off decisions and continuous power dispatch subject to load balance, ramping, and reserve constraints.

**Key observables.** Operating cost gap, feasibility rate, constraint violation percentage, and optimization stability (standard deviation).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits (down to 4-6 for small and 15 for large scales), number of optimization steps, ansatz depth (layers), and PCE encoding parameters.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The cost function is assumed to be quadratic, and the optimization uses a smooth surrogate/relaxation of constraints to ensure differentiability.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the hybrid optimization architecture; Figure 2 and 3 show ablation studies on ansatz depth and optimization steps; Figure 4 and 5 show performance and scalability on large-scale instances.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the UC problem and scaling challenges, details the PCE encoding and hybrid bilevel architecture, describes the gradient-based optimization strategy, presents experimental results on small and large-scale power systems, and concludes with ablation studies and limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Unit commitment is an important optimization problem in power system operations, classified as NP-hard. This paper presents a hybrid quantum-classical method for the unit commitment problem with time-dependent constraints, where decisions must be made about which generators to turn on/off and how much power they should produce over a planning horizon. We use a hybrid quantum-classical optimization procedure to determine the on/off schedules of the generating units and the corresponding power dispatch that satisfies operational constraints such as load balance, generator limits, ramping, and reserve requirements. We frame the optimization loop as a leader-follower structure, where the quantum optimizer leads to give the on/off decisions, and the classical optimizer follows to produce the power level schedule. Leveraging Pauli-Correlation Encoding, our method scales to horizon-wide unit commitment schedules by encoding the binary variables with far fewer qubits. By combining these components, the method can handle multi-period settings while using far fewer qubits than straightforward quantum encodings that allocate one qubit per decision variable as in prior approaches. We evaluate the approach on both small- and large-scale instances, up to 312 binary variables, and show that it reliably produces feasible schedules with competitive operating costs.

</details>

### [Spatial statistics for screening molecular structures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17147v1)

**Authors:** Pranoy Ray, Surya R. Kalidindi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17147v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17147_figures/2605.17147_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Hierarchical structural complexity across material classes. Schematic illustrating (left - Si) perfectly periodic crystals with a small number of symmetry-inequivalent environments, (center - Wüstite: Fe1−xO) chemically disordered solid solutions with local lattice distortions and short-range order, and (right - PFOA) finite, non-periodic molecules with complex 3D conformations (helicity) and steric shielding (F atoms).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17147_figures/2605.17147_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Voxelization pipeline for continuous atomic environments. The discrete, coordinate-based point-cloud of a local atomic neighborhood is systematically mapped onto a standardized cubic voxel grid. During this transformation, each voxel channel encodes specific physical attributes, such as binary occupancy, intrinsic elemental properties, or continuous pseudo-charge densities via a defined smearing function. Voxels falling within a prescribed atomic radius capture the designated elemental values, while interstitial regions remain at zero, effectively transforming the discrete Lagrangian coordinates into a continuous, multi-channel field ready for deterministic spatial statistical...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17147_figures/2605.17147_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Sources of voxelized molecular structure data from DFT. Example shown for a refractory high-entropy alloy Al4Nb44Ti4Zr76, where the converged or pseudo charge density field is sampled on a regular grid to construct the voxelized representation used in the spatial feature engineering workflow.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17147_figures/2605.17147_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: End-to-end spatial feature engineering workflow. Discrete atomic coordinates are first embedded into voxelized eigen–microstructure and pseudo-density fields on a standardized grid. Directionally resolved two-point auto- and cross-correlations are then computed via FFTs to obtain translationally invariant structural fingerprints. Finally, PCA projects these high-dimensional statistics onto a low-dimensional, convex manifold that serves as input to lean CNNs or GPR models.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.17147_figures/2605.17147_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Broad applicability of the spatial statistics based feature engineering paradigms. The left panel[93] shows the Principal Component space of refractory high-entropy alloys, demonstrating the chemically agnostic grouping of elemental and multi-component systems. The right panel shows the correlation of the principal component scores with exact polarizability in PFAS, successfully isolating thermodynamic outliers and variations driven by the length of the perfluoroalkyl chain [94] .</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes replacing heavy, data-hungry deep learning architectures with a physics-informed approach using spatial statistics and FFTs. This method enables highly accurate material property prediction even with extremely small datasets and provides a convex latent space suitable for efficient molecular design.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Traditional deep learning models for materials discovery require massive DFT-labeled datasets and struggle with chemically disordered configurations, chiral geometries, and non-convex latent representations that hinder inverse design.

**Main result.** A physics-informed feature engineering framework using spatial statistics achieves sub-2% prediction error with as few as 10 training samples and provides strictly convex representations for efficient optimization.

**Method.** The method uses voxelized 3D scalar fields to compute two-point auto- and cross-correlations via Fast Fourier Transforms, followed by PCA for dimensionality reduction into low-dimensional manifolds.

**Model / system.** The framework is scale-agnostic and applicable to periodic crystals (e.g., Silicon), chemically disordered high-entropy alloys, and non-periodic organic molecules (e.g., PFAS).

**Key observables.** Two-point spatial correlations, pseudo-charge densities, and structural fingerprints like helicity and short-range order.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Data-scarce regimes (10-30 training samples), lean neural networks (<100k parameters), and computational complexity scaling as O(S log S).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of non-interacting pseudo-densities neglects explicit charge transfer and electron correlation effects; 2-point statistics may smooth over rare, localized topological anomalies.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows structural complexity across different regimes; Figure 2 illustrates the voxelization pipeline; Figure 3 shows voxelized data for a high-entropy alloy; Figure 4 depicts the end-to-end workflow; Figure 6 shows a closed-loop autonomous discovery pipeline.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the limitations of current GNNs, proposes a spatial statistics-based feature engineering workflow (voxelization, FFT-based correlations, PCA), demonstrates performance across various material classes (crystals, alloys, molecules), and discusses the utility for inverse design and active learning.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The dominant paradigm in computational materials discovery relies on heavily parameterized deep architectures, including message-passing graph networks and equivariant models, that require millions of DFT-labeled training structures and produce non-convex latent representations that complicate continuous optimization for inverse design. These architectures are impractical in data-scarce regimes, which is the typical case in molecular screening, and exhibit well-documented limitations in capturing chemically disordered configurations and chiral geometries. This review presents feature engineering based on spatial statistics as a physically rigorous and immediately deployable alternative. Molecular structures are encoded as voxelized scalar fields, and two-point auto- and cross-correlations are evaluated deterministically via Fast Fourier Transforms, explicitly transferring the burden of spatial pattern recognition from the learning algorithm to a closed-form, physics-informed operation. Principal component analysis of the resulting correlation maps yields low-dimensional, strictly convex representations that support lean neural networks (<100k trainable parameters) and non-parametric surrogate models, achieving sub-2% prediction error with as few as 10 training samples. Demonstrated across periodic crystals, chemically disordered high-entropy alloys, and non-periodic organic molecules, this framework enables Bayesian active learning and zero-shot extrapolation on commodity hardware, which current large-scale architectures cannot replicate at equivalent data budgets.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a numerical framework using Sine-Square Deformation to study the thermodynamic properties of frustrated quantum magnets. It successfully captures complex features like double-peak specific heat in kagome lattices, providing a way to mimic the thermodynamic limit using small-scale clusters.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of accurately calculating the low-temperature thermodynamic properties of frustrated 2D quantum magnets in the thermodynamic limit due to finite-size effects and the sign problem in traditional numerical methods.

**Main result.** The study identifies a double-peak structure in the specific heat of kagome and triangular lattices and shows that the kagome lattice exhibits a strong enhancement of magnetic susceptibility at low temperatures due to magnetic excited states.

**Method.** The Sine-Square Deformation (SSD) technique is applied to the Hamiltonian to create fictitious bulk states, combined with the Thermal Pure Quantum (TPQ) method for finite-temperature calculations.

**Model / system.** S=1/2 antiferromagnetic Heisenberg models on 1D chains, square, triangular, and kagome lattices.

**Key observables.** Energy density, specific heat (Cv), magnetic susceptibility (chi), and thermal entropy density.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (T/J), exchange interaction (J), and system size (N).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The SSD method assumes that the center of the deformed system effectively mimics the thermodynamic limit; kernel regression assumes a smooth temperature dependence for extrapolating data.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 defines the SSD function and its spatial distribution; Figure 2 compares specific heat and susceptibility across 1D, square, triangular, and kagome lattices; Figure 3 shows the single-particle density of states; Figure 4 demonstrates convergence of energy density; Figure 6 highlights the reduction of the finite-size gap in 1D chains.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the SSD method, validates it using free fermion and 1D chain benchmarks, applies it to 2D square lattices to compare with QMC, and finally investigates the thermodynamic properties of frustrated triangular and kagome lattices.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the temperature dependence of the thermodynamic properties of spin-1/2 antiferromagnets on two-dimensional lattices. Our analysis employs the sine-square deformation (SSD), in which a real-space envelope function is applied to the Hamiltonian so that the local energy scale is smoothly reduced to zero at the system boundaries. The quantum eigenstates of the SSD Hamiltonian exhibit bulk-like behavior near the system center, effectively mimicking the thermodynamic limit even in small finite-size calculations. Using these fictitious bulk states, we compute the energy density, specific heat, and magnetic susceptibility as functions of temperature. We find that both the triangular- and kagome-lattice antiferromagnets show either a shoulder or a pronounced double-peak structure in the low-temperature specific heat, whereas the kagome case particularly shows a strong enhancement of magnetic susceptibility down to the lowest temperature range. These direct comparisons, together with the square-lattice and one-dimensional cases, reveal that although both frustrated systems retain a substantial amount of entropy, the low-energy excitations below ~ 0.5J of the kagome lattice are predominantly governed by the magnetic excited states, whereas not much for the triangular lattice.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.17139"></a>
### [Stable minimum principles for scattering states](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17139v1)

**Authors:** Scott Lawrence, Yukari Yamauchi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17139v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17139_figures/2605.17139_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: A schematic of the potential defined by (95). The ingoing particle “slows down” dramatically in the interval x ∈(0, a), where E −V (x) is small, and the scattering wave-</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper introduces a new family of stable minimum principles for approximating quantum scattering states using the L1 norm of the Schrodinger equation violation. It proves that this metric allows for rigorous error bounds on the S-matrix and cross sections, making it a robust foundation for numerical scattering algorithms. The method is shown to be applicable to complex systems, including momentum-dependent and many-body potentials.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and numerical physics as it provides a rigorous error-bounding framework for using variational/machine-learning-based wavefunctions to compute scattering observables.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing stable variational minimum principles for approximating quantum-mechanical scattering states and bounding the error in the resulting S-matrix.

**Main result.** The authors establish that the L1 norm of the Schrodinger violation provides a stable metric for bounding errors in scattering amplitudes, phase shifts, and cross sections, unlike the L2 norm which can be unstable.

**Method.** Introduction of a family of stable minimum principles using the L1 norm of the Schrodinger violation, designed to be compatible with modern computational algorithms like Neural Quantum States and Variational Monte Carlo.

**Model / system.** Nonrelativistic quantum-mechanical scattering involving single-particle, many-body, and momentum-dependent potentials, including Coulomb interactions and scattering of bound states.

**Key observables.** S-matrix elements, scattering amplitudes, phase shifts, total cross section, and probability flux.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Energy (E), particle mass (M), potential strength and range, and the L1 norm of the Schrodinger violation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The approximate wavefunction is assumed to have the correct ingoing plane wave; potentials are generally assumed to be self-adjoint and decay sufficiently rapidly at large distances.

**Figures summary.** A schematic of the problem setup.

**Paper structure.** The paper presents the minimum principle, establishes stability estimates, extends results to general and momentum-dependent potentials, discusses many-body scattering, and provides counterexamples for unstable metrics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum-mechanical scattering states are energy eigenstates obeying particular boundary conditions, whose behavior at infinity encodes the S-matrix which defines the outcoming of scattering experiments. With an eye toward numerical algorithms for computing nonrelativistic S-matrices, we present a family of stable minimum principles for scattering states. States that approximately satisfy these minimum principles are shown to have a bounded difference with the true scattering states. These minimum principles and stability estimates can be used to obtain rigorous bounds on scattering amplitudes. We show that these minimum principles are applicable to momentum-dependent potentials, long-range (Coulomb) interactions, and elastic or inelastic scattering of bound states.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.18539"></a>
### [The QuaST Decision Tree: Achieving Automation With Data-Based Recommendations](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18539v1)

**Authors:** Benedikt Poggel, Lena Tokuhiro, Georg Kruse, Jeanette Miriam Lorenz  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18539v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18539_figures/2605.18539_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Components of the quast-decisiontree package. core contains the framework, the DecisionTree base class and its building blocks. nodes provides a basic instance covering steps from problem loading to algorithm execution. algorithms and problems define templates for algorithms and problem classes, respectively.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18539_figures/2605.18539_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: The algorithm selection part of a basic DecisionTree instance. Based on the selected algorithms, different choices follow: the definition of an ansatz (e.g., variational quantum eigensolver [49]), a depth and mixer (QAOA [23]), or skipping for a direct solver setup. Multiple nodes take part in the hyperparameter definitions. In the VQA case, the paths converge for the optimizer selection, which is not required in other cases (here: linear ramp QAOA [50] and a classical solver).</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces the QuaST Decision Tree, a modular software framework designed to automate the selection of quantum algorithms and parameters for combinatorial optimization. By using data-driven recommendations and scalability analysis, it reduces the need for manual trial-and-error and helps identify when quantum approaches are practically feasible compared to classical methods.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The quantum software stack lacks a high-level abstraction layer to automate the complex decision-making process involved in selecting optimal algorithms, encodings, and hyperparameters for hybrid quantum-classical optimization.

**Main result.** The authors present the QuaST Decision Tree (QDT), a modular framework that automates algorithm selection and provides a scalability analysis module to predict the feasibility of Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs).

**Method.** The QDT uses a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) architecture with modular nodes and a data-driven automation module that utilizes a database of precomputed benchmarks to estimate required measurement shots.

**Model / system.** The framework targets quantum-enhanced combinatorial optimization (CO) using models such as QUBO, Ising, and MaxCut, applicable to gate-based NISQ devices, quantum annealers, and future error-corrected systems.

**Key observables.** Finite sampling error, optimizer tolerance threshold, approximation ratios, and the quantum disadvantage boundary.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Problem size (number of variables n), number of measurement shots (n_shots), QUBO density, and optimizer convergence thresholds.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The scalability analysis assumes that the decline of optimizer tolerance follows specific scaling laws (exponential, power-law, or logarithmic).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the Python package hierarchy; Figure 2 illustrates the decision tree branching logic for algorithm selection; Table I and II detail the class methods for the QDT core; Table III and IV quantify execution overhead and integration effort.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of software stack gaps, describes the modular architecture and design principles of the QDT, presents a specific automation module for VQA feasibility, validates the framework's efficiency and integration ease, and discusses future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum computers are increasingly powerful. Software tools for the development of quantum-enhanced algorithms are maturing. However, the software stack still lacks the connection to applications that would enable hybrid algorithms combining classical and quantum computing steps. End users need to be assisted in choosing the best combination of preprocessing, postprocessing, classical and quantum algorithms options. The application-facing software stack is therefore required to cover problem modeling, encoding, algorithm selection and hyperparameter tuning. A variety of tools exist for specific recommendations. The QuaST Decision Tree reflects the complexity in combining individual decisions in its modular network structure, consisting of flexible computation nodes with modular recommendations. It can easily be configured to serve in an industrial solver, an HPC software stack, or for rapid prototyping in development. The key ingredient, automation, is delivered by modules. We present one such module judging the feasibility of variational algorithms based on a robust scalability analysis and classification of problem instances. The automation improves the performance of end-to-end solutions, highlights the benefit to be gained from the hybrid quantum solution, reduces expensive trial-and-error testing, and leads to an improved utilization of quantum devices for a practical benefit.

</details>

### [Theory of melting lines with a variable enthalpy of fusion](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17631v1)

**Authors:** Anthony N. Papathanassiou  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17631v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 5</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.17631_figures/2605.17631_page6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new theoretical framework for calculating melting lines by accounting for the variable enthalpy of fusion caused by solid-state anharmonicity. The resulting model predicts a universal parabolic trajectory for phase boundaries that is consistent with both recent phonon-based theories and experimental observations across diverse materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty in defining a universal analytical equation for melting lines in P-T phase diagrams due to the inadequacy of the constant latent heat approximation in the Clausius-Clapeyron relation.

**Main result.** The derivation of a universal parabolic scaling law for melting lines, which aligns with the Phonon Theory of Liquids and matches experimental data for various substances.

**Method.** A two-phase analytical model that modifies the Clausius-Clapeyron equation to include a variable enthalpy of fusion correlated to the specific volumes of coexisting phases.

**Model / system.** A thermodynamic model of the solid-liquid phase transition (melting) applicable to a wide range of condensed matter, including noble gases, molecular systems, metals, and semiconductors.

**Key observables.** Melting lines (P-T phase boundaries), enthalpy of fusion, and the slope of the melting curve.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Enthalpy of fusion (delta H), scaling parameter epsilon (Cp/beta), bulk moduli, thermal expansion coefficients, and specific volumes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The dominance of the anharmonic, volume-dependent component of heat capacity near the melting point and the assumption that the scaling parameter epsilon remains constant over narrow pressure ranges.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of melting line derivation, develops a modified Clausius-Clapeyron relation based on variable enthalpy, derives a second-order differential equation for the melting line, provides analytical parabolic solutions, and validates the results against existing theories and experimental data.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Conventional derivations of phase boundaries from the Clausius-Clapeyron (CC) relation often employ the constant latent heat approximation to maintain analytical functions of the sublimation and boiling curves. To address the complex thermodynamics of the solid-liquid transition, we develop a two-phase analytical model by modifying the CC equation to account for a variable enthalpy of fusion along the melting line (ML). Our methodology utilizes recent theoretical and experimental progress demonstrating that the isobaric heat capacity of crystalline solids near the melting point features a dominant anharmonic, volume-dependent component. Consequently, the latent heat is correlated to the specific volumes of the coexisting phases. Differentiation of this modified CC relation yields a second-order differential equation governing ML. By imposing appropriate e boundary conditions, physically acceptable approximate parabolic solutions are derived. The parameters of these analytic functions are defined exclusively by fundamental thermophysical properties, including the bulk moduli, thermal expansion coefficients, and specific volumes of the coexisting phases, as well as the isobaric heat capacity of the solid. Our derivation, rooted in solid-state anharmonicity, yields approximate parabolic scaling laws that corroborate with a recent universal model derived from the Phonon Theory of Liquids [K. Trachenko, Phys. Rev. E 109, 034122 (2024)], supporting the universal parabolic nature of melting curves from a completely distinct theoretical foundation.

</details>

### [Truncated-Binary Encoding: Spectral Degree Reduction of Combinatorial Optimization Problems for Quantum Hardware](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17143v1)

**Authors:** Tristan Zaborniak  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17143v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17143_figures/2605.17143_page2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 2</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.17143_figures/2605.17143_page3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 3</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.17143_figures/2605.17143_page4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 4</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.17143_figures/2605.17143_page5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 5</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.17143_figures/2605.17143_page6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new method called Truncated-Binary Encoding to reduce the complexity of optimization problems mapped to quantum hardware. By strategically dropping high-degree interactions in the Ising basis, it minimizes hardware overhead while providing mathematical guarantees that the essential features of the optimization landscape remain intact.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** High-order interactions in the encoding of discrete cost functions into Ising models create high-degree monomials that are incompatible with the limited native locality of current quantum annealing and gate-based hardware.

**Main result.** The paper introduces Truncated-Binary Encoding (TBE), proving that discarding high-degree Ising monomials preserves the global minimum and local minimum structure under specific energy gap and basin barrier conditions.

**Method.** The author uses Walsh-Hadamard spectral analysis to treat the encoding as a low-pass filter and employs the Lindeberg-Feller Central Limit Theorem to characterize the truncation error as a Gaussian noise floor.

**Model / system.** The model consists of Cost Function Networks (CFNs) mapped to Ising-basis Higher-Order Unconstrained Binary Optimization (HUBO) problems, specifically targeting quantum annealing and gate-based architectures like QAOA.

**Key observables.** Truncation error (L-infinity norm), spectral power (P_k), energy gap (Delta E*), and the single bit-flip basin barrier (delta f).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The truncation cutoff degree (k_max), the maximum monomial degree (k_full), and the smoothness of cost tables (Lipschitz constants).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes an Ising-basis formulation and relies on a random-coupling ensemble where omitted couplings are treated as independent zero-mean random variables.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of high-order interaction overhead, introduces the TBE method, establishes error bounds and preservation conditions for minima, performs a spectral analysis using Walsh transforms, and concludes with a diagnostic workflow for selecting the truncation cutoff.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Exact-binary encoding compiles a discrete cost function network (CFN) into a higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) problem whose maximum monomial degree grows with the cardinalities of the underlying CFN variables. Given that quantum optimization hardware generally favours quadratic unconstrained binary optimization or low-degree HUBO Hamiltonians, high-cardinality CFNs therefore incur substantial overhead in the form of circuit depth, or ancilla qubits when degree-reduction techniques are employed. To ameliorate these issues, we propose \textit{truncated-binary encoding} (TBE): a modification of exact-binary encoding in which Ising-basis monomials exceeding a chosen cutoff $k_\text{max}$ are dropped from the encoded cost. We establish a tight $L^\infty$ bound on the truncation error in terms of the omitted couplings, derive sufficient conditions on the energy gap and on the single bit-flip basin barrier under which TBE preserves the global minimum and its local-minimum structure, and characterize a noise floor condition on the spectral profile under which the truncation residual acts as a perturbative correction to the underlying landscape. We then express the encoded coefficients directly as Walsh transforms of the underlying CFN cost tables, and prove a bound under which smoothness of each cost table implies rapid decay of its high-degree Walsh mass. Together these results yield a principled \textit{a priori} criterion for selecting $k_\text{max}$ and for judging whether a given CFN admits a small-$k_\text{max}$ TBE.

</details>

**Authors:** Christian Copetti  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16482v1>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores how bulk anomalies dictate the physics of monodromy defects, demonstrating that these defects host protected edge modes and undergo spectral pumping. It provides a unified framework for understanding these defects as domain walls in both gapped SPT phases and gapless anomalous theories.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the properties of monodromy defects, which are codimension-2 defects that terminate topological symmetry operators, specifically focusing on how bulk 't Hooft anomalies impact their physics.

**Main result.** The author shows that in anomalous gapless theories, monodromy defects are well-defined as domain walls between a symmetry defect and a topological decoration, leading to protected chiral edge modes and adiabatic spectral pumping of gapless degrees of freedom.

**Method.** The study utilizes the anomaly inflow mechanism, transgression/slant product in group cohomology, radial quantization, and the Symmetry TFT (SymTFT) framework.

**Model / system.** The paper considers gapped Symmetry Protected Topological (SPT) phases and gapless theories with anomalous global symmetries, including (3+1)d Dirac fermions, (1+1)d compact bosons, and (2+1)d scalar QED.

**Key observables.** Protected chiral edge modes, spectral flow/pumping of gapless degrees of freedom, and topological invariants like the chiral central charge and Hall response.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The twist parameter (magnetic flux) $
u$, the symmetry group $G$ and its centralizer $C_G(g)$, and the $	heta$ angle periodicity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis of $	heta$ angle periodicity and gravitational corrections is specifically focused on closed $Spin^c$ manifolds.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the monodromy defect as a dynamical termination of a symmetry operator or as being sourced by magnetic flux; Figure 2 shows the defect as a domain wall between a symmetry defect and a topological decoration.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining monodromy defects, moves to using topological defects as probes of SPTs, extends these concepts to anomalous gapless theories, and concludes with specific applications to free Dirac fermions and lattice models.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Monodromy defects describe a dynamical termination of topological symmetry operators, and are sourced by a localized background magnetic flux. We study their properties in gapped SPT phases and, by inflow, in gapless theories with an anomalous symmetry. The background flux acts as a source for the anomaly, impacting the definition of the monodromy defect, which can only be defined as a domain wall between the symmetry generator and a topological order induced by the anomaly. The topological dressing has several consequences, such as the presence of protected chiral edge modes on the defect's worldvolume, and the adiabatic pumping of gapless degrees of freedom bound to the localized flux. We verify our predictions in several examples, focusing on monodromy defects for anomalous chiral symmetries, both in the continuum and on the lattice.

</details>

</details>

**Authors:** F. Calloni, R. Satariano, R. Ferraiuolo, H. G. Ahmad, D. Gatta, E. Raja, G. Santo, G. Serpico, R. Vydyasagar, D. Montemurro, N. Poccia, A. Vettoliere, G. Ausanio, C. Granata, L. Parlato, G. P. Pepe, A. Bruno, F. Tafuri, D. Massarotti  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18586v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **2/5**

**Summary.** This paper investigates the electrodynamic properties of tunnel ferromagnetic Josephson junctions. It shows that submicrometric Al-based junctions can achieve quantum phase diffusion regimes similar to transmon qubits, suggesting their viability for future superconducting quantum and classical digital circuits.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper explores the transition from thermal activation to quantum phase diffusion and the management of dissipation in hybrid superconducting-magnetic systems, which is highly relevant to the development of high-coherence hybrid quantum architectures.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the phase dynamics, dissipation mechanisms, and scalability of tunnel ferromagnetic Josephson junctions (SIsFS) for potential integration into superconducting quantum and classical circuits.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that submicrometric Al-based SIsFS junctions exhibit quantum phase diffusion and properties comparable to state-of-the-art transmons, establishing a robust framework for hybrid ferromagnetic Josephson junctions.

**Method.** The researchers used transport measurements (I-V characteristics and switching current distributions) and fitted the data using the Tunnel Junction Microscopic (TJM) model and the Bakurskiy model.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of SIsFS (Superconductor-Insulator-thin superconductor-Ferromagnet-Superbolconductor) multilayer junctions, specifically Nb-based and Al-based devices with varying dimensions and ferromagnetic interlayers.

**Key observables.** Critical current (Ic), gap voltage (Vgap), IcRN product, switching current probability density distribution P(I), and quality factors (Q0, Q1).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Josephson energy (Ej), charging energy (Ec), Ej/Ec ratio, thermal activation, macroscopic quantum tunneling (MQT), and quantum phase diffusion regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the validity of the Tunnel Junction Microscopic model and the Bakurskiy model for describing the series combination of SIs and sFS junctions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows normalized I-V characteristics for different samples and compares SIsFS junctions to non-magnetic SIS counterparts; Figure 3 shows temperature-dependent switching current distributions and mean switching current for Nb-based samples.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the SIsFS architecture, details the fabrication and experimental platform, presents transport measurements and modeling results for different material systems, and concludes with the implications for quantum and classical circuit integration.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate tunnel ferromagnetic Josephson junctions based on Superconductor-Insulator-thin superconductor-Ferromagnet-Superconductor multilayers. A comparative study of their electrodynamic properties is performed for junctions with niobium and aluminum (Al) electrodes, featuring different ferromagnetic interlayer materials and lateral dimensions ranging from the micrometric to the submicrometric scale. The parameters extracted from the fitting of the current-voltage characteristics using the tunnel junction microscopic model are found to be consistent with those independently estimated from switching current distribution measurements. Submicrometric Al-based devices exhibit electrodynamic properties comparable to those implemented in state-of-the-art transmon qubits and display clear signatures of quantum phase diffusion. The strong agreement between transport modelling and escape dynamics establishes a robust framework for describing hybrid ferromagnetic Josephson junctions consistent with their energy scales and supports their potential integration into superconducting quantum and classical digital circuits.

</details>

(9)] is switched on, and the alignment strength is changed to A = 2. The agents then condense into a dense ordered cluster near the target. **Summary.** This paper presents a microscopic model of active matter where alignment depends on the orientation of neighboring particles. It shows that this simple rule can lead to complex behaviors ranging from disordered gases to highly ordered, oscillating clusters.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study explores how local, anisotropic, and velocity-dependent rules drive phase transitions and emergent collective behaviors, which is highly relevant to understanding non-equilibrium phase transitions and driven many-body dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how a simple, local, orientation-weighted alignment rule can generate a diverse range of nonequilibrium collective dynamics, such as flocking, jamming, and clustering.

**Main result.** The model demonstrates that a single microscopic rule can produce a broad spectrum of states, including coherent flocking, active gases, and dense ordered clusters that exhibit periodic collective reversals.

**Method.** Direct numerical simulations of an agent-based model using the velocity Verlet integration algorithm.

**Model / system.** An agent-based model of N self-propelled particles in a 2D arena, featuring a Lennard-Jones-type potential for short-range interactions and a velocity-dependent, orientation-weighted alignment rule.

**Key observables.** Polar order parameter, Mean Squared Displacement (MSD), sixfold orientational order parameter, and orientational correlation function.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Alignment strength (A), target strength (C), Lennard-Jones interaction strength (Up), and maximum speed (vmax).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The alignment rule is local and anisotropic; speed is regulated by a saturation rule to prevent unbounded acceleration.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the interaction potentials and the force field of a single agent; Fig 2 illustrates speed truncation and velocity rescaling; Fig 3 compares flocking and active gas regimes; Fig 4 shows system evolution after target removal; Fig 5 displays structural order parameters.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the microscopic alignment rule and model components, explores different dynamical regimes by tuning alignment strength, investigates the effect of localized target forcing, and tests the robustness of these states against noise and perturbations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study an agent-based model of self-propelled particles with a velocity-dependent alignment rule. This interaction is orientation weighted and acts along the line connecting neighboring particles. Tuning the alignment strength produces several distinct collective regimes, including disordered gas-like motion, coherent flocking, jammed high-density states, and densely ordered moving clusters with active-crystal-like behavior. These results show that a simple local alignment rule can generate a broad range of nonequilibrium collective dynamics within a single microscopic model.

</details>

### [Dynamic Many-Body Theory: The dynamics of atomic impurities in $^4$He](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17069v1)

**Authors:** Eckhard Krotscheck

**Summary.** This paper develops a microscopic theory to study how different atomic impurities move through superfluid helium-4. It specifically highlights how the large zero-point motion of light particles like muonium leads to massive increases in effective mass and unique dispersion relations.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a sophisticated microscopic framework for understanding how a light impurity couples to the collective excitations (phonons/rotons) of a quantum fluid, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and impurity physics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to develop a Dynamic Many-Body Theory to describe the dynamics, energetics, and effective mass of various atomic impurities (such as muonium, antiprotonic helium, and hydrogen isotopes) embedded in a superfluid 4He host.

**Main result.** The theory predicts extreme mass enhancement for muonium (m*/m > 100) due to large zero-point motion and shows that the dispersion relations for muonic and antiprotonic helium pass under the roton minimum.

**Method.** The authors use a microscopic many-body approach based on the Jastrow-Feenberg ansatz, the Hypernetted chain (HNC)/Euler-Lagrange method, and a time-dependent generalization of the variational principle to derive impurity self-energy.

**Model / system.** A single atomic impurity (Mu, p-bar, H, D, T, or 3He) immersed in a bulk 4He quantum liquid, modeled with Aziz-type He-He potentials and impurity-host potentials characterized by a strong repulsive core.

**Key observables.** Chemical potential, effective mass, dispersion relations, impurity self-energy, pair distribution function, and particle lifetime.

**Important parameters / regimes.** deBoer parameter (quantumness), reduced mass, scattering length, and the phonon-roton spectrum of the host liquid.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theory utilizes the G0W approximation for dispersion relations and a three-phonon vertex approximation to preserve long-wavelength properties; the Feynman approximation for the host spectrum is noted to be a factor of 2 too high.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows interaction potentials and scattering wave functions; Figure 2 compares chemical potentials vs density; Figure 7 shows effective mass vs density; Figure 8 compares dispersion relations; Figure 9 shows self-energy and particle lifetime; Figure 10 shows the imaginary part of the self-energy.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the motivation regarding muonium experiments, defines the many-body Hamiltonian and variational framework, details the derivation of the Dynamic Many-Body Theory, presents results for static properties (chemical potential), and concludes with dynamic properties (effective mass and damping).

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We implement manifestly microscopic many-body methods to study the dynamics of atomic impurities in a host quantum fluid, specifically $^4$He. Our investigations are motivated by experiments of muonium atoms within $^4$He with the goal of testing the universality of free fall by neutral bound states using unstable particles.   Structure calculations are performed using standard semi-analytic methods; we extend here the calculations of our previos work (Journal of Low Temperature Physics {\bf 93}, 415-449 (1993)) to muonic \he4, antiprotonic \he4 and mounium atoms within \he4. We find that the muonium impurity probes, due to its large zero-point motion, the atomic interaction at rather short distances. Its chemical potential is estimated to be about 19 meV. Antiprotonic \he4 has, on the other hand, a negative chemical potential.   Dynamics is treated by making all correlation functions time-dependent. In analogy to the derivation of the dynamics of the background liquid, we derive a working formula for the impurity self-energy that includes the most relevant physical effects. Results for the effective mass of \he3 and hydrogen atoms agree well with available experiments. The dispersion relations of muonic \he4 and antiprotonic \he4 pass through under the roton minimum.

</details>

As expected, δT ∗is always negative and saturates at large Ω, giving δT ∗= −0.13 K, −0.86 K, and −1.32 K, respectively. These shifts can be strongly amplified by in- creasing the input power. For instance, keeping the same power-law parameters for Q and d0 = 88 nm, raising the input to Pin = 2 103 Wm−2 increases the unmodulated temperature to T0 = 542 K and yields a much larger Kapitza-like shift, δT ∗= −8.6 K. Figure 2(c) shows the temperature shift δT ∗versus d0 in the large-Ωlimit, where ∆stat(T0) dominates. The shift decreases...</sub>

**Summary.** The paper introduces a way to control near-field radiative heat transfer by rapidly modulating parameters like the vacuum gap. This process creates a thermal version of the Kapitza mechanism, allowing for predictable temperature shifts and the ability to stabilize thermal states. The predicted effects are large enough to be measured with current experimental technology.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper presents a fundamental way to control non-equilibrium thermal dynamics and stability through parametric driving, which is highly relevant to the control of open quantum systems and heat transport in nanophotonic platforms.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how rapid periodic modulation of a parameter controlling near-field radiative heat transfer (NFRHT) affects the slow thermal dynamics and stability of a system.

**Main result.** The study establishes a thermal analogue of the Kapitza mechanism, showing that fast modulation produces a quadratic, time-averaged temperature shift and a modified effective thermal conductance consisting of static and dynamic terms.

**Method.** A two-scale analysis and Kapitza-type averaging method are used to decompose temperature into slow and fast components and expand the radiative flux to second order in modulation amplitude.

**Model / system.** A lumped-body thermal model consisting of two bodies (e.g., SiC slabs) separated by a vacuum gap, where heat transfer occurs via photon tunneling (evanescent fields) and a background conductive channel.

**Key observables.** Steady-state temperature shift (delta T*), effective thermal conductance (G_eff), and the frequency-dependent temperature ripple.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Modulation frequency (Omega), modulation amplitude (a), vacuum gap distance (d), and the ratio of modulation frequency to thermal relaxation rate (Omega vs G/C).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Small modulation amplitude, clear separation of timescales between modulation and thermal relaxation, and electromagnetic quasi-instantaneity.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows the static and dynamic Kapitza corrections and temperature shifts as functions of modulation frequency and gap distance, alongside unmodulated temperature comparisons.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the thermal Kapitza mechanism, defines the physical model and governing thermal balance equations, applies two-scale averaging to derive slow dynamics, applies the theory to SiC slabs to predict measurable shifts, and discusses stability and future applications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce a Kapitza-like mechanism for the near-field radiative heat transfer and show that fast modulation of any parameter controlling the flux, such as the vacuum gap or a material response, produces a quadratic, time-averaged correction in the slow thermal dynamics. This correction splits into a frequency-independent static term and a low-pass dynamical term, yielding sizable modulation-induced temperature shifts and modified effective thermal conductances that can stabilize or destabilize the steady state. Applying the theory to gap modulation between SiC slabs, we derive analytical scaling laws and predict temperature shifts that are fully measurable with existing experimental platforms, requiring only readily accessible low modulation frequencies of order $Ω\approx 10^4~\mathrm{rad/s}$. Our results establish a thermal analogue of the Kapitza mechanism and provide a general route for controlling radiative heat flow in micro- and nanoscale platforms.

</details>
