**Summary.** This paper shows that an AC-driven gate voltage in a single molecule magnet transistor can be used to detect Berry-phase interference through conductance oscillations. This method is significant because it allows for the observation of these quantum interference effects using unpolarized leads.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how a time-periodic gate voltage and a transverse magnetic field induce Berry-phase interference in the electron transport through a single molecule magnet transistor.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that the AC-driven potential creates zero transmission resonances that oscillate with the transverse magnetic field due to Berry-phase interference, and that these oscillations can be detected using unpolarized leads.

**Method.** The authors combine Floquet theory to handle the time-periodic potential with the Landauer formalism to calculate conductance and transmission probability.

**Model / system.** A single molecule magnet (SMM) transistor connected to two leads, modeled using a one-dimensional tight-binding chain and the Giant Spin Approximation (GSA) for the SMM spin tunneling.

**Key observables.** Electron transmission probability, conductance, and zero transmission resonances.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Transverse magnetic field (Bx), gate voltage amplitude (Vg), driving frequency (omega), anisotropy constants (Kz, K), and coupling strength (beta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is in the strong Coulomb regime where only uncharged or singly charged states are considered, and the exchange coupling is highly anisotropic (J_perp = 0).

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the device setup; Fig 2 displays oscillations of zero transmission resonances; Fig 3 plots transmission probability vs electron energy; Figs 4 and 5 show conductance oscillations as a function of the transverse magnetic field.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the system setup and Hamiltonian definition, moves to the Floquet-Landauer transport formalism, presents analytical and numerical results for transmission and conductance, and concludes with the discussion of Berry-phase detection.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider the electron transport through a single molecule magnet transistor in the presence of a local transverse magnetic field and ac-driven gate voltage. We calculate the conductance as a function of the electron energy and transverse magnetic field by using the Floquet and Landauer formalism. We show that the time periodic potential causes zero transmission resonances that oscillate as a function of the transverse magnetic field due to the Berry phase interference associated with two quantum tunneling paths. We find that these Berry phase oscillations can be detected in the conductance as a function of the transverse magnetic field for an incoming electron with a specific energy.

</details>

### [Electronic mechanism of sub-100-fs demagnetization induced by a femtosecond light pulse](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18638v1)

**Authors:** Konrad J. Kapcia, Victor Tkachenko, Flavio Capotondi, Alexander Lichtenstein, Serguei Molodtsov, Przemysław Piekarz, Beata Ziaja  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18638v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.18638_figures/2605.18638_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1. Transient electronic and magnetic properties of Co predicted by XSPIN in an single magnetic domain under optical irradiation with 2 eV pulse or X-ray irradiation with 1000 eV pulse (as labeled), both of 70 fs FWHM duration. (a) number of deep-shell holes produced (per atom; spin-resolved), (b) number of low-energy electrons per atom (with energy below the cutoff of 15 eV, spin-resolved), (c) electronic temperature, (d) magnetization (normalized to its initial value m0 before the pulse), all plotted as a function of time. Temporal profile of the pulse triggering the dynamics is schematically depicted.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.18638_figures/2605.18638_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2. Transient electronic and magnetic properties of Co predicted by XSPIN in a single magnetic domain under optical irradiation with 2 eV pulse or X-ray irradiation with 1000 eV pulse (as labeled), both of 2 fs FWHM duration. Other simulation parameters are as in Fig. 1.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.18638_figures/2605.18638_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3. Transient electronic and magnetic properties of Ni predicted by XSPIN in a single magnetic domain under optical irradiation with 2 eV pulse or X-ray irradiation with 1000 eV pulse (as labeled), both of 70 fs FWHM duration. Other simulation parameters are as in Fig. 1.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.18638_figures/2605.18638_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical study demonstrates that demagnetization in Co and Ni occurs in less than 100 femtoseconds due to electronic redistribution in the 3d band. The mechanism is shown to be universal across optical and X-ray regimes, provided the photon energy is sufficient to excite the electronic system. These findings are critical for developing next-generation ultrafast magnetic storage and switching technologies.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study explores non-equilibrium dynamics and the relaxation of highly excited electronic systems, which is relevant to understanding how ultrafast energy deposition drives transient changes in many-body electronic states.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Identifying the specific physical processes that trigger ultrafast (sub-100 fs) radiation-induced demagnetization in magnetic materials across optical to X-ray photon energies.

**Main result.** Sub-100-fs demagnetization is driven by electronic excitation and subsequent electron redistribution within the 3d band, a mechanism that remains universal across different photon energies.

**Method.** The study uses the XSPIN simulation code, which combines VASP-calculated electronic structures with a Monte Carlo approach for high-energy electrons and a transient Fermi-Dirac distribution for low-energy electrons.

**Model / system.** The system consists of single magnetic domains of bulk fcc Nickel (Ni) and Cobalt (Co) under strongly non-equilibrium conditions following femtosecond light pulses.

**Key observables.** Spin-resolved number of deep-shell holes, spin-resolved number of low-energy electrons (below 15 eV), transient electronic temperature, and normalized magnetization (m/m0).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Photon energies from 2 eV to 10,000 eV; pulse durations of 2 fs and 7/0 fs FWHM; absorbed dose of 0.93 eV/atom.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The band structure remains static (no nuclei movement); phonon-mediated processes and inter-site Heisenberg exchange are negligible on these timescales; the electronic temperature and chemical potential are common for both spin populations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares Co under 2 eV and 1000 eV irradiation, showing the evolution of spin-resolved holes, low-energy electrons, electronic temperature, and normalized magnetization.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of ultrafast demagnetization, describes the XSPIN modeling framework and electronic structure calculations, presents results for various photon energies and pulse durations, and discusses the implications of electron cascading and experimental convolution effects.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

A quantitative understanding of the processes that trigger light-induced demagnetization on ultrashort timescales is crucial for achieving an ultrafast, radiation-controlled magnetic response in materials. This milestone is essential for developing next-generation magnetic storage devices and ultrafast magnetic switches. In this theoretical study, we investigated demagnetization triggered in a single magnetic domain by light pulses ranging from a few to a few tens of femtoseconds in duration, with photon energies spanning the optical and X-ray regimes, under strongly non-equilibrium conditions. We predicted a loss of magnetization in the sub-100-fs range in all cases, primarily due to the excitation of the electronic system and the subsequent redistribution of electrons within the magneto-sensitive band. The considered timescales were too short for phonon-mediated processes or inter-site Heisenberg exchange processes to contribute significantly. These findings pave the way for highly accurate, radiation-driven magnetization control in magnetic materials at sub-100-femtosecond timescales with potential practical applications.

</details>

For all theoretical curves, we use S = 5/2, gs = 2, ν0 = 1032Tc0, νso = 348Tc0, νs = 0.89Tc0, ζs = 1.25, pF d = 20, and Tc0 = 717 mK. We use...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper extends the theory of superconductivity in disordered films to show how a parallel magnetic field can enhance superconductivity by polarizing magnetic impurities. By reducing spin-flip scattering, the field can increase the critical temperature, increase the upper critical field, and even restore superconductivity in previously normal films.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to extend the Kharitonov and Feigelman (KF) theory to explain recent experimental observations of superconductivity enhancement, specifically addressing the enhancement of the perpendicular upper critical field and the suppression of the London penetration depth by a parallel magnetic field.

**Main result.** The authors theoretically demonstrate that a parallel magnetic field suppresses the London penetration depth and enhances the perpendicular upper critical field by polarizing magnetic impurities and reducing the effective exchange scattering rate. They also predict a gapless-to-gapped transition and the possibility of field-induced superconductivity restoration.

**Method.** The study utilizes Gor'kov's diagrammatic technique for dirty superconductors within the Matsubara representation, employing the self-consistent Born approximation and Dyson's equation to derive superconducting observables.

**Model / system.** The system consists of dirty (disordered) superconducting thin films containing potential, spin-orbit, and magnetic impurities. The model uses a Bogoliubov-de Gennes Hamiltonian that accounts for orbital and paramagnetic effects from external magnetic fields.

**Key observables.** Critical temperature (Tc), superconducting order parameter (Delta), superfluid density (nsc), London penetration depth (lambda_L), perpendicular upper critical field (Hc2_perp), and spectral gap (Eg).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange scattering rates (nu_s, nu_inf), spin-orbit scattering rate (nu_so), magnetic field components (h_parallel, h_perp), impurity spin (S), and film thickness (d).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The films are in the dirty limit; the model assumes strong spin-orbit scattering to suppress paramagnetic effects; the Kondo effect is neglected (T > TK); and impurities are assumed to be independent and uniformly distributed.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of a disordered film with different impurity types; Figure 2 displays self-energy diagrams for various scattering processes; Figure 4 shows numerical plots of nonmonotonic Tc, nsc, and Hc2_perp; Figure 5 compares theoretical curves with experimental data for Tc, Hc2_perp, and lambda_L.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the formulation of the Hamiltonian and the physical model, followed by the development of the theoretical framework using Gor'kov's technique. It then explores specific limits (unpolarized and fully polarized), analyzes the gapless-to-gapped transition, and concludes with numerical results and comparison to experimental data.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Dirty superconducting films with magnetic impurities can exhibit nontrivial behavior in a magnetic field that polarizes the impurity spins. As predicted by Kharitonov and Feigelman (KF) [JETP Lett. 82, 421 (2005)], this polarization reduces the exchange scattering rate. Consequently, a parallel magnetic field can enhance the critical temperature $T_c$ when magnetic-field pair breaking is weak, as realized for strong spin-orbit scattering and small film thickness. Recently, Llanos et al. [Nat. Phys. (2026)] observed a pronounced enhancement of $T_c$ consistent with the KF theory. The same experiment also reported an enhancement of the perpendicular upper critical field $H_{c2}^{\perp}$ and a suppression of the London penetration depth $λ_L$ by a parallel magnetic field. These quantities were not considered in the original KF theory. To address this gap, we develop a theoretical framework based on Gor'kov's diagrammatic technique for dirty superconductors. We extend the KF theory in two experimentally relevant directions: (i) to arbitrary temperatures $T<T_c$ and several superconducting observables, and (ii) to arbitrary magnetic-field orientations. As a result, we demonstrate theoretically the suppression of $λ_L$ and the enhancement of $H_{c2}^{\perp}$ by a parallel magnetic field, in agreement with experiment.

</details>

**Authors:** Liam Bonds, Shiang-Bin Chiu, Anton Andreev, Boris Spivak  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17667v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** The paper provides a theoretical description of SNS junctions where the normal metal has multiple conduction band valleys. It predicts that the separation of different relaxation time scales leads to unique nonmonotonic current peaks and highly nonlinear resistance.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how a hierarchy of relaxation time scales (intra-valley, inter-valley, and inelastic) affects the I-V characteristics of SNS junctions with a multivalley normal region.

**Main result.** The I-V characteristic is nonmonotonic, exhibiting two current peaks at voltages corresponding to the inter-valley and inelastic relaxation rates, and showing a sharp voltage jump in current-biased regimes.

**Method.** Generalization of the Larkin-Ovchinnikov equations using the Keldysh-Gor'kov-Nambu formalism, Usadel equations, and a zero-dimensional approximation for the junction.

**Model / system.** SNS (Superconductor-Normal-Superconductor) junctions where the normal region is a diffusive multivalley conductor, such as n-doped silicon or germanium.

**Key observables.** Current-voltage (I-V) characteristics, current peaks (I_max), critical current (I_c), and nonlinear resistance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Relaxation time hierarchy (tau, tau_v, tau_in), Thouless energy (E_T), and voltage scales (V1, V2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Diffusive regime, adiabatic approximation for slowly varying order parameters, and a zero-dimensional approximation for the low-bias regime.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a sketch of a multivalley SNS junction with scattering processes; Figure 2 plots average current vs. bias voltage; Figure 3 shows the I-V characteristic with a distinction between low-bias and high-bias regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical problem, develops a generalized kinetic framework for multivalley systems, analyzes voltage-biased junctions to find current peaks, and then investigates current-biased junctions to identify the voltage jump regime.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In multivalley conductors the inter-valley relaxation time $τ_v$ and the inelastic relaxation time $τ_{in}$ may be significantly longer than the intra-valley momentum relaxation time $τ$. We show that this separation of time scales has dramatic effects on the I-V characteristics of SNS junctions with a multivalley normal region. We generalize the Larkin-Ovchinnikov equations describing superconducting kinetics to the case of multivalley superconductors. We use this generalization to obtain a kinetic description of multivalley SNS junctions. We find that at constant voltage bias $V$, the current $I(V)$ is nonmonotonic; it exhibits two peaks of similar magnitude $I_\text{max,1} \sim I_\text{max,2}$ at $V_1 \sim \hbar(eτ_{in})^{-1}$ and $V_2\sim \hbar(eτ_v)^{-1}$, which may greatly exceed the critical current $I_c(T)$. At constant current bias $I$ we find that in a wide interval, $I_c(T) < I \lesssim I_{\text{jump}}$, the nonlinear resistance of the junction is controlled by the long relaxation times and may be several orders of magnitude smaller than the normal state resistance.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a relativistic theoretical framework to describe how spin and orbital angular momentum interact and redistribute in magnetic materials. It highlights how the choice of exchange-interaction model (general vs. atomistic) fundamentally changes the predicted conservation laws for total angular momentum during ultrafast processes.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to develop a complete relativistic theory for the coupled dynamics of spin and orbital angular momentum in magnetic systems, specifically addressing the conservation or non-conservation of total angular momentum under external electromagnetic fields.

**Main result.** The authors show that while total angular momentum (J) is conserved in the absence of a spin-polarized exchange field, it is not conserved under external electromagnetic fields (like laser pulses); however, under an atomistic Heisenberg approximation, J remains conserved even with external fields.

**Method.** The study employs a relativistic framework starting from the Dirac-Kohn-Sham Hamiltonian, applying a Foldy-Wouthuysen unitary transformation to derive an extended Pauli Hamiltonian and using Heisenberg equations of motion to derive angular momentum dynamics.

**Model / system.** The system consists of electrons in magnetic and nonmagnetic materials (including ferromagnets and alloys) subjected to external electromagnetic fields, modeled using the Dirac-Kohn-Sham and extended Pauli Hamiltonians.

**Key observables.** Spin angular momentum (S), orbital angular momentum (L), and total angular momentum (J = S + L).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Relativistic corrections (1/c^2 order), electromagnetic field strength (E and B fields), spin-polarized Kohn-Sham exchange field (Bxc), and ultrafast/femtosecond timescales.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation relies on a semirelativistic 1/c^2 expansion, a weak-field approximation, and compares results between a general Kohn-Sham exchange field and an atomistic Heisenberg approximation.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the derivation of the relativistic Hamiltonian via unitary transformation, proceeds to analyze the dynamics of L and S in nonmagnetic and magnetic systems, investigates conservation laws under external fields, and concludes with a comparison between general exchange and atomistic Heisenberg models.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a complete relativistic theory to describe the dynamics of electronic angular momentum including both spin (S) and orbital (L) contributions in magnetic systems. We start with the relativistic Dirac-Kohn-Sham Hamiltonian under the influence of an electromagnetic field and apply a unitary transformation to formulate the extended Pauli Hamiltonian. Using the transformed semirelativistic Hamiltonian, we derive the angular momentum dynamics for the orbital and spin angular momenta. Thereby, we formulate the coupled dynamics of orbital and spin moments consistent with the relativistic Dirac framework. Considering especially the conservation of the total angular momentum, J = S +L, we show first that J is conserved in the absence of a spin-polarized Kohn-Sham exchange field, but is no longer conserved under the application of an electromagnetic field, e.g., laser pulse, THz field, etc. Second, considering magnetic systems with atomic spin and orbital momenta, we derive the coupled equations of motion of angular momenta dynamics whilst making the atomistic Heisenberg approximation for the exchange interaction. Our results suggest that, under these assumptions, the total angular momentum remains conserved, even with electromagnetic field, but atomic spin and orbital angular momenta individually are not conserved.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces and analyzes the dynamics of dark soliton gases with arbitrary genus in the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger equation. It establishes how these complex soliton ensembles evolve over time, passing through various modulated and unmodulated states. The results are supported by rigorous Riemann-Hilbert analysis and numerical simulations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for understanding complex, interacting ensembles of solitons in many-body systems like BECs, specifically describing how higher-order structures decay into simpler planar or modulated regimes.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the construction and asymptotic behavior of arbitrary-genus dark soliton gases within the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger (dNLS) hydrodynamics.

**Main result.** The authors successfully derive the long-time evolution and large-space asymptotics of higher-genus dark soliton gases, demonstrating a cascade of behaviors through modulated and unmodulated regions.

**Method.** The study employs the Deift-Zhou nonlinear steepest descent approach, Riemann-Hilbert Problem (RHP) framework, Whitham modulation theory, and the g-function approach via a variational formulation.

**Model / system.** The system is the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger (dHS) equation, which describes physical contexts such as Bose-Einstein condensates (Gross-Pitaevskii equation) and electromagnetic wave envelopes in defocusing optical media.

**Key observables.** The amplitude/density of the dark soliton gas potential |q(x, t)|^2 and the phase functions of the soliton gas.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The self-similar variable xi = x/t, the genus N of the soliton gas, the velocity v of solitons, and the spectral parameters (eigenvalues) on the unit circle.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the system is integrable and follows the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger hydrodynamic model with finite density boundary conditions.

**Figures summary.** Figures include a qualitative map of the x-t evolution plane, numerical simulations of N=1 and N=2 cases, a visualization of the band-gap conversion mechanism, and snapshots of the soliton gas zero level sets.

**Paper structure.** The paper proceeds from the construction of the arbitrary-genus potential via an N-to-infinity limit, followed by the analysis of large-space asymptotics, the derivation of long-time evolution using RHP and Whitham theory, and finally numerical validation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger hydrodynamics supports exact dark solitons under finite density boundary conditions. However, the dark soliton gas, an interacting ensemble of dark solitons, has not yet been studied. In this work, we introduce an arbitrary-genus potential of dark soliton gases by considering the limit of the $\mathcal{N}$-dark soliton as $\mathcal{N}\to \infty$. The large-space asymptotics and long-time evolution of this dark soliton gas potential are analytically investigated through Deift-Zhou nonlinear steepest descent approach. The genus-$N$ dark soliton gas potential approaches the genus-$N$ finite-gap solution as $x \to -\infty$ and the background $1$ as $x \to +\infty$. In the long-time evolution, as the self-similar variable $ξ=x/t$ increases, the gas configuration exhibits a cascade of behaviours, passing from unmodulated and modulated genus-$N$ regions and progressively reducing the genus down to the planar region (unmodulated genus-$0$ region). Notably, the evolution of lower-genus soliton gases can be embedded within that of higher-genus gases, exhibiting identical dynamics within specific regimes. This phenomenon is encoded by the underlying spectra. We also include numerical validations, in perfect agreement with the theoretical predictions.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a theoretical framework to understand how moving domain walls in antiferromagnets create electrical impedance. It reveals that a unique mechanism involving the tilting of sublattices contributes to this impedance, offering a way to tune electrical responses in future antiferromagnetic spintronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the origin and nature of emergent impedance in antiferromagnets induced by the dynamics of domain walls under AC currents.

**Main result.** The authors identify two distinct mechanisms for emergent impedance: one from the translational motion of the domain wall (similar to spiral magnets) and a unique contribution from the time-dependent canting of sublattice magnetizations, which is inversely proportional to the AFM exchange coupling.

**Method.** The researchers use a perturbation expansion of the coupled Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equations up to the first order of the inverse AFM exchange coupling and apply an AC current density to derive analytical expressions for the complex impedance.

**Model / system.** The model consists of an antiferromagnetic metal with two sublattices (A and B) and equal saturation magnetization, described by a 4x4 one-body Hamiltonian for conduction electrons and coupled LLG equations for the Néel and canting vectors.

**Key observables.** Emergent impedance (Z_omega), spinmotive force (SMF), emergent electric field, and the canting moment.

**Important parameters / regimes.** AFM exchange coupling (omega_E), spin-transfer torque (STT) coefficients (u, beta), pinning potential frequency (omega_pin), and Gilbert damping (alpha).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes spatial uniformity in the yz plane (1D walls), small Gilbert damping, and the limit where AFM exchange is dominant (|n| approx 1 and |m| much less than 1).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the model system with a driven domain wall; Figure 2 illustrates the two coupled DW modes (translational and canting).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of emergent impedance, develops a theoretical formalism using LLG equations and perturbation theory, identifies the two competing dynamical mechanisms, analyzes the frequency-dependent impedance regimes, and concludes with implications for spintronics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We theoretically investigate emergent impedance induced by domain-wall dynamics in antiferromagnets. Emergent impedance, arising from a combined action of spin-transfer torque and spinmotive force, was previously predicted and observed in spiral magnets. Here we develop a formalism for the electrical response of an antiferromagnetic domain wall under ac currents, and obtain analytical expressions for the resulting emergent impedance. We find that two dynamical modes play separate roles in the emergent impedance: Translational motion of the domain-wall center generates a contribution proportional to its velocity, analogous to that arising from the corresponding motion of a spiral magnet. Another contribution, unique to antiferromagnetic domain walls, originates from the time-dependent canting of the sublattice magnetizations localized within the moving domain wall, whose magnitude is inversely proportional to the antiferromagnetic exchange coupling constant. The competition between these two distinct contributions determines the sign and magnitude of the imaginary part of the emergent impedance at sub-resonant frequencies. Our results provide a fundamental insight into electron transport in antiferromagnets, and open avenues for novel antiferromagnet-based spintronics devices.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

Bottom: low-temperature solitonic branch, fitted with (3.74) as a function of the distance from κv. The lower panels show the corresponding residuals.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper develops a field-theoretic description of a 2D holographic superconductor, showing that the complex phase diagram of the bulk gravity model can be recovered from a deformed 2D CFT. It specifically explains the emergence of fractional magnetic flux and the Little-Parks effect through a 1+1D gauge theory toy model. The work demonstrates the power of using boundary-side modularity and effective actions to understand holographic phase transitions.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a bridge between holographic gravity and effective field theories, demonstrating how universal features of phase transitions and topological effects like fractional flux can be captured by boundary-side statistical mechanics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to reconstruct the phase diagram and condensate behavior of a 2D holographic superconductor using only the boundary field theory description.

**Main result.** The authors successfully map the four-phase holographic structure (including Hawking-Page and superconducting transitions) using a deformed 2D CFT and provide a field-theoretic mechanism for fractional magnetic flux and the Little-Parks effect.

**Method.** The study employs large-c factorization, modular invariance, Hubbard-Stratonovich transformations, and an effective Ginzburg-Landau expansion to relate the bulk gravity solutions to the boundary CFT.

**Model / system.** The system consists of a 2D Conformal Field Theory (CFT) on a torus, subject to a relevant double-trace deformation, which is dual to Einstein gravity in AdS3 coupled to a U(1) gauge field and a charged scalar field.

**Key observables.** Scalar condensate, magnetic flux (integer and fractional), temperature, entropy, and zero-momentum susceptibility.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Central charge (c), scaling dimension (Delta), double-trace coupling (kappa or f), temperature (T), and winding number (n).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The large-c limit is assumed to suppress fluctuations and allow a mean-field description; the scalar mass is restricted to the range -1 < m^2 < -3/4.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the relationship between coupling and condensate, the reconstructed phase diagram in the T-kappa plane, and the suppression of the condensate as the winding number increases.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the holographic and dual field theory models, proceeds to reconstruct the phase diagram using modular invariance and effective action approaches, matches the near-critical behavior with Ginzburg-Landau theory, and concludes with a toy model explaining fractional flux and the Little-Parks effect.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate field theory models of holographic superconductors in which the condensation of the order parameter is induced by a Robin boundary condition. Assuming large-$c$ factorization, we study the phase diagram of a two-dimensional CFT deformed by a relevant double-trace perturbation. Using modular invariance, we relate the high- and low-temperature phases, reproducing analytically the results for the zero-winding sector of the holographic model. Moreover, we match the near-critical behaviour of the condensate with an effective Ginzburg--Landau field theory description. Another important feature of the holographic superconductor is the presence of vortices that carry fractional magnetic flux. We investigate a field theory toy model with similar properties and interpret it as a fractional Little--Parks effect.

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<sub>Fig. 3. Theoretical and numerical analyses on the SOT switching of the Néel vector. Schematic illustrations of Néel vector dynamics based on the analytical model with Eqs. (1) and (2) in the 𝜋-switching regime (a), the multi-rotational switching regime (c), and the coherent rotation regime (e). The unit of 𝑢(𝜃) is arbitrary. Numerical simulations for the time evolution of the Néel vector in the corresponding regimes are shown in (b), (d) and (f). In the simulation, the current pulse is applied from 5 ns to 15 ns and 𝜇&amp;𝐻$ = 100 mT.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.18009_figures/2605.18009_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. Numerical results for the probabilities of multiple rotations of the Néel vector. a-c, Probabilities of the Néel vector completing 𝜋 (a), 3𝜋 (b) and 5𝜋 (c) rotations, as functions of 𝐽!# and 𝜏O . d, Summation of the probabilities of 3𝜋, 5𝜋, 7𝜋, … rotations, corresponding to the probability of multi-rotational switching.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

</details>

**Summary.** The researchers discovered that in noncollinear antiferromagnets, spin-orbit torque can drive the magnetic order through multiple full rotations rather than a single flip. This 'multi-rotational switching' explains why the current required to switch the device back is unexpectedly independent of the pulse duration, providing a new way to control antiferromagnetic states in nano-devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding the microscopic dynamics of spin-orbit torque (SOT) driven switching in noncollinear antiferromagnets, specifically explaining the unconventional insensitivity of the switching-back threshold current density to pulse duration.

**Main result.** Discovery of a 'multi-rotational switching' mechanism where the Néel vector undergoes multiple rotations (3pi, 5pi, etc.) before reversal, and the establishment of a phase diagram containing pi-switching, multi-rotational, and coherent rotation regimes.

**Method.** Experimental measurement of anomalous Hall resistance and switching probabilities in Mn3Sn nanodots combined with numerical simulations using coupled Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equations with thermal fluctuations.

**Model / system.** A noncollinear antiferromagnetic Mn3Sn nanodot (Kagome lattice) in a W/Ta/Mn3Sn/MgO/Ru heterostructure, modeled using a three-sublattice LLG approach and a reduced equation of motion for the Néel vector in a tilted-washboard potential.

**Key observables.** Anomalous Hall resistance (Rxy), switching probability (P), and critical current densities (Js for switching and Jb for switching-back).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pulse duration (5 ns to 5 ms), current density (up to 55 MA/cm2), in-plane magnetic field (Hx), and nanodot diameter (120-200 nm).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The dynamics can be represented by a single Néel vector; the SOT is modeled via a Slonczewski-like torque; the system is treated within a three-sublattice model with equal saturation magnetization.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the device schematic, SEM image, and Hall resistance curves; supplementary figures show pulse waveforms, switching probability measurements, and the effect of nanodot diameter on critical currents.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the experimental platform and the observed switching anomaly, presents the experimental characterization of switching regimes, provides a theoretical/numerical explanation via multi-rotational dynamics, and concludes with the implications for spintronics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Spintronics has advanced through discoveries of various electrically-driven spin dynamics in nanomagnets. Here, we report a novel switching dynamics of spin systems driven by spin-orbit torque, using a noncollinear antiferromagnetic nanodot. With electric pulses spanning a wide range of durations and amplitudes, we find an unconventional insensitivity of a threshold current density to pulse duration in switch-back events. This observation is attributed to a previously unrecognized process, in which the noncollinear antiferromagnetic order undergoes multiple rotations before completing reversal, a phenomenon we term multi-rotational switching. Our theoretical analysis reveals that multi-rotational switching arises from the interplay of three key factors: current-driven coherent rotation of the noncollinear antiferromagnetic order, field-induced reorientation of the uncompensated net magnetization, and thermal fluctuations. These findings establish a microscopic mechanism governing current-induced switching in noncollinear antiferromagnets, a topic of growing interest for next-generation spintronics technologies, opening a new route to controlling antiferromagnetic order in nanodevices.

</details>

### [Nonreciprocal impurity scattering as a probe for pairing symmetries in kagome superconductors](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16943v1)

**Authors:** Hong-Min Jiang, Hao Du, Shun-Li Yu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16943v1>

The black cross marked I in panels (c) and (d) denotes the inver- sion center of the two impurities. (e) Impurity conﬁguration with two magnetic impurities (black dots) located on distinct A- and...</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.16943_figures/2605.16943_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

The left column [panels (a1)-(a3)] corresponds to conven- tional on-site s-wave superconductivity, while the left column [panels (b1)-(b3)] illustrates the dx2−y2 + idxy-wave pairing symmetry. (a1)/(b1), (a2)/(b2), and (a3)/(b3) present the results for V = 0.75Vc, V = Vc, and V = 1.25Vc, respectively. Here, Vc denotes the critical exchange interaction at which particle- and hole-like excitations meet at zero energy, as evi- denced in panels (c) and (d). Panels (c) and (d) illustrate the energy evolution of...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method to distinguish between s-wave and TRSB d-wave pairing in kagome superconductors by using the interference patterns of two magnetic impurities. It demonstrates that nonreciprocal impurity scattering provides a unique spectroscopic signature resolvable by STM, bypassing the ambiguities of conventional techniques.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Resolving the ambiguity in superconducting pairing symmetry and its link to time-reversal symmetry breaking (TRSB) in vanadium-based kagome superconductors.

**Main result.** The study shows that while single magnetic impurities cannot distinguish between s-wave and d-wave symmetries, two impurities reveal distinct LDOS patterns due to nonreciprocal scattering in the TRSB case.

**Method.** The authors use the Bogoliamb-de Gennes (BdG) framework, T-matrix formalism, and the Bethe-Salpeter equation to calculate the electronic Local Density of States (LDOS).

**Model / system.** A tight-binding kagome model representing the AV3Sb5 family, including chemical potential, nearest-neighbor hopping, and two superconducting order parameters: conventional on-site s-wave and TRSB d+id-wave.

**Key observables.** Local Density of States (LDOS) patterns, specifically the energy evolution and interference of Yu-Shiba-Rusinov (YSR) states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Impurity positions, impurity strength (U, V), superconducting gap magnitude (delta), and the van Hove filling regime.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Magnetic impurities are treated as classical, static variables; the study focuses on a specific model of two representative pairing symmetries.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the kagome lattice and impurity configurations; Fig 3 shows LDOS interference at the midpoint of two impurities; Fig 4 compares s-wave and d-wave LDOS on interstitial sites; Fig 5 shows s-wave LDOS outside the impurity boundary.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of pairing symmetry ambiguity, defines the kagome model and impurity setup, analyzes single-impurity vs. two-impurity scattering, demonstrates the nonreciprocal signatures of TRSB, and concludes with experimental implications for STM.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The superconducting (SC) pairing symmetry and its link to time-reversal symmetry breaking (TRSB) in the vanadium-based kagome superconductors remain unresolved, with ambiguities stemming from sublattice interference and charge-density-wave (CDW) entanglement with superconductivity. Using two representative SC pairings, i.e., the conventional on-site $s$-wave and the TRSB $d_{x^2-y^2}+id_{xy}$-wave, as a model study, we theoretically show that while single magnetic impurity yield qualitatively identical spectral behavior of local density of states (LDOS) for these two symmetries, two magnetic impurities give rise to distinct LDOS patterns. For the conventional on-site $s$-wave pairing, time-reversal symmetry (TRS) enforces equivalent forward and backward scattering between two impurities across all impurity configurations, leading to near disappearance of a Yu-Shiba-Rusinov (YSR) state pair along the line connecting the two impurities. However, for the TRSB $d_{x^2-y^2}+id_{xy}$-wave pairing, this scattering equivalence holds only for inversion-symmetric impurity configurations, with a pair of YSR disappearance restricted to this case. These distinct spectral features are resolvable in scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) experiments, providing a direct avenue to discriminate TRSB and non-TRSB SC pairing symmetries in kagome superconductors and an alternative method to probe SC nonreciprocity that circumvents the ambiguities of conventional critical current-based techniques.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a way to use electric fields to permanently and reversibly program the path of spin waves in a multiferroic material. By switching ferroelectric domains, the researchers created programmable magnonic waveguides and even simulated a frequency-splitting device. This provides a low-power, scalable platform for future magnonic-based logic and neuromorphic computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for energy-efficient, non-volatile, and scalable on-chip magnonic devices for signal processing and neuromorphic computing, overcoming the limitations of current-driven or strain-based tuning methods.

**Main result.** Demonstrated a non-volatile, voltage-controlled method to tune magnon dispersion in a multiferroic heterostructure, enabling programmable waveguides and frequency demultiplexing via ferroelectric domain engineering.

**Method.** The researchers used pulsed laser deposition to grow BFO/LSMO heterostructures, Piezoresponse Force Microscopy (PFM) for domain writing, and Brillouin Light Scattering (BLS) for imaging spin-wave propagation and dispersion.

**Model / system.** A multiferroic thin-film heterostructure consisting of a BiFeO3 (BFO) ferroelectric layer and a La0.67Sr0.33MnO3 (LSMO) ferromagnetic layer grown on a NdGaO3 (NGO) substrate.

**Key observables.** Magnon dispersion relations, frequency shifts (up to 150 MHz), effective magnetization, Gilbert damping, and ferroelectric domain configurations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Frequency shifts of ~150 MHz, effective magnetization M_eff = 336 mT, Gilbert damping alpha = 5.9 x 10^-3, and BFO layer thickness of 34 nm.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of an effective equivalent magnetization pattern in simulations to represent the magnetoelectric influence of ferroelectric domains.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Schematic of the heterostructure and BLS process. Fig 2: PFM images showing reproducible ferroelectric domain writing. Fig 3: 2D BLS scans, magnon dispersion, and phase maps of the waveguide. Fig 4: Results of the inverse design for a frequency demultiplexer, including simulated propagation and output intensity.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of magnonic control, describes the BFO/LSMO heterostructure and growth process, details the experimental characterization of domain writing and magnon dispersion, presents the results of the magnetoelectric tuning, and concludes with an inverse-design demonstration of a functional device.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The realization of fully reconfigurable, voltage-controlled, and programmable on-chip magnonic devices is essential to fully harness the potential of spin waves for signal processing, logic and neuromorphic computing. Yet, existing demonstrations of electrical tuning of magnonic responses are either volatile, current-driven and thus energy-inefficient, or rely on local strain modification limiting their scalability for wafer-scale integration. Here, we address this challenge using a BiFeO3/La0.67Sr0.33MnO3 multiferroic thin film heterostructure. We show that ferroelectric domain engineering in BiFeO3 enables deterministic tuning of the magnon dispersion of La0.67Sr0.33MnO3, producing frequency shifts up to $\sim 150 MHz$ and allowing reconfigurable waveguiding. Micro-focused Brillouin light scattering directly images these effects, revealing electrically defined magnonic waveguides and spatially programmable dispersion. Compared to conventional approaches, this method provides non-volatile and reversible control. Furthermore, using an inverse-design simulation code, we demonstrate the capability of our platform to perform advanced magnonic functions such as frequency demultiplexing. Our results open a new avenue for using magnetoelectric heterostructures for magnonic logic, with further applicability to reservoir and neuromorphic computing and AI driven magnonic devices.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that in CrSBr, the spin angular momentum of magnons is not fixed at h-bar but diverges when magnon modes soften under an external field. This divergence creates a clear, measurable signature in the Spin Seebeck effect, providing a way to detect soft magnons via spin-caloritronic measurements.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how non-universal magnon spin angular momentum (SAM) and field-induced magnon softening in 2D van der Waals magnets like CrSBr lead to distinct spin-caloritronic signatures.

**Main result.** The study finds that triaxial anisotropy and dipolar interactions cause magnon SAM to diverge during field-induced softening, resulting in a pronounced peak in the thermal and diffusive Spin Seebeck response.

**Method.** Theoretical approach using Holstein-Primakoff transformation, Bogoliulyov transformation to diagonalize the Hamiltonian, and the linearized Boltzmann equation to calculate spin transport.

**Model / system.** Monolayer and bilayer CrSBr, a van der Waals magnetic insulator with tetragonal crystal lattice, featuring intralayer ferromagnetic and interlayer antiferromagnetic coupling.

**Key observables.** Magnon spin angular momentum (SAM), thermal Spin Seebeck coefficient (SSC), diffusive SSC, and magnon thermal conductivity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Applied magnetic field (B0), temperature (1-5 K), magnetic phases (AFM, FM, Canted, Saturated), and anisotropy axes (easy axis b, intermediate axis a).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Non-interacting magnon approximation, lowest-order Holstein-Primakoff expansion, constant relaxation time approximation, and small-amplitude assumption (which breaks down near saturation).

**Figures summary.** Figures show the dependence of SAM, SSC, and thermal conductivity on the magnetic field for both monolayer and bilayer CrSBr, highlighting peaks and discontinuities at phase transitions and softening points.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of non-universal SAM, describes the CrSBr model and Hamiltonian, presents calculations for monolayer SAM and SSC, extends the analysis to bilayer systems, and concludes with temperature-dependent transport properties.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Spin transport in magnetic insulators is often treated by assuming that magnons carry a fixed spin angular momentum of $\hbar$, which does not hold in general, however. Here we calculate the magnon spin angular momentum of a layered antiferromagnet as a function of applied magnetic field and wave vector. We show that the triaxial anisotropy and intralayer dipolar interactions in bilayer CrSBr renormalize the magnon spin angular momentum, which diverges upon field-induced magnon softening. This divergence gives rise to a pronounced peak in the thermal spin Seebeck response and provides a clear spin-caloritronic signature of soft magnons.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Authors:** Seou Choi, Sachin Vaidya, Charles Roques-Carmes, Marin Soljačić  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17006v1>

Supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators can achieve similar FSIM score while using ∼×10 less X-ray dose. The thickness of the scintillators considered here is 270 µm. For the X-ray...</sub>

**Summary.** This paper proposes using 3D photonic crystals to engineer the direction of light emission in scintillators through supercollimation. By suppressing the lateral spreading of light, these structures allow for thicker, more efficient X-ray detectors without sacrificing image resolution. This breakthrough could enable high-quality medical imaging at significantly lower radiation doses.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The fundamental trade-off in X-ray scintillators where increasing thickness improves X-ray absorption but degrades spatial resolution due to diffraction-induced lateral light spreading.

**Main result.** 3D supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators can improve spatial resolution by up to an order of magnitude and reduce the required X-ray dose by approximately 10-fold while maintaining image quality.

**Method.** A multiscale modeling framework integrating nanophotonic band-structure simulations (MPB) with Monte Carlo particle transport (Geant4) to evaluate light propagation and X-ray imaging performance.

**Model / system.** A 3D single gyroid photonic crystal made of scintillating materials such as Lu2O3(Eu) or ZnSe(Te), designed with flat isofrequency contours to achieve supercollimation of emitted light.

**Key observables.** Spatial resolution (via Point Spread Function and Modulation Transfer Function), Detector Quantum Efficiency (DQE), Feature Similarity Index (FSIM), and X-ray dose.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Isofrequency contour (IFC) flatness, supercollimation bandwidth, X-ray energy (e.g., 30-33.5 keV), and refractive index contrast.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Light emitted outside the supercollimation bandwidth is assumed to be emitted isotropically; the detector is assumed to be an ideal silicon detector with 100% quantum efficiency.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 illustrates the concept of supercollimation vs. bulk spreading; Fig 2 shows band structures, IFCs, and MIT logo imaging; Fig 3 compares emission spectra and DQE for different materials; Fig 4 demonstrates kidney vasculature imaging and dose reduction via FSIM scores.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the resolution-efficiency trade-off, description of the 3D photonic crystal design and supercollimation mechanism, presentation of the multiscale simulation framework, results comparing performance to bulk scintillators, and demonstration of dose reduction in medical imaging simulations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Scintillators convert X-ray energy into visible or near-visible photons, enabling applications in high-energy particle detection and X-ray imaging. Increasing scintillator thickness improves X-ray absorption but degrades spatial resolution due to diffraction-induced lateral spreading of emitted light, resulting in a fundamental trade-off between detection efficiency and image resolution. Here, we propose a class of three-dimensional photonic crystal scintillators that overcomes this limitation through supercollimation, in which light propagates with suppressed diffraction. We develop a multiscale modeling framework that integrates nanophotonic band-structure simulations with Monte Carlo particle transport to quantitatively evaluate the performance of such scintillators. Our results show that supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators can enhance spatial resolution by up to an order of magnitude relative to conventional bulk scintillators of equal thickness. This improvement leads to a substantial increase in detector quantum efficiency (DQE), particularly at high spatial frequencies, enabling fine features to be preserved at reduced X-ray dose. We further demonstrate that comparable image quality can be achieved with approximately an order-of-magnitude lower X-ray dose. By directly engineering light transport within the bulk of the scintillator, this work establishes a nanophotonic route to simultaneously improving resolution and dose efficiency in X-ray imaging.

</details>

**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18682v1>

**Summary.** This paper explores how electrical currents can be used to manipulate the population of excitons and trions in a dense electron-hole plasma via Coulomb drag. By using Hall currents to drive charge accumulation, the researchers demonstrate a method for electrical control of optical responses in hydrodynamic systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how electric currents and Hall voltages affect the optical response and charge distribution of a dense, hydrodynamic electron-hole plasma.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that Hall currents induce Coulomb drag, which causes light hole accumulation and the emergence of a double photoluminescence line (excitons and trions), distinguishing this from simple electric field-induced energy shifts.

**Method.** Scanning magneto-photoluminescence microscopy was performed at 4 K on a GaAs channel under intense laser excitation and applied magnetic fields (up to 9T) and electric currents.

**Model / system.** A mesoscopic n-doped GaAs channel containing a 14 nm thick quantum well, operating in a hydrodynamic electron-hole plasma regime where interparticle momentum-conserving collisions dominate.

**Key observables.** Photoluminescence (PL) energy, Landau fan patterns, and the appearance of specific spectral lines for excitons and trions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field up to 9T, Hall voltage, Hall current, electron drift velocity, and hole diffusion length (~5-10 micrometers).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is in the hydrodynamic regime where electron-hole scattering is intense enough to suppress the quantum Hall regime.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows PL contour plots for unbiased and parallel current configurations showing Landau fans and double PL lines; Figure 2 provides a microscopic image and a schematic of hole injection and diffusion; Figure 3 shows PL contour plots for perpendicular current configurations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the experimental setup and the hydrodynamic regime, describes the measurement of PL under different current configurations, compares the effects of Hall voltage versus Hall current, and concludes with the implications for controlling exciton populations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The influence of the Hall voltage on the photoluminescence of a dense hydrodynamic electron-hole plasma laser generated in a mesoscopic n-doped GaAs channel under intense laser excitation is studied. Laser excitation induces an interband current determined by the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. As a result, background electrons drifting under the influence of the Hall voltage form an effective Hall current. The Coulomb drag caused by the Hall current causes the accumulation of light holes, leading to the appearance of a double photoluminescence line formed by the recombination of excitons and trions. In contrast, in the absence of a Hall current, the shift in the photoluminescence energy associated with heavy holes occurs due to the electric field created by the Hall potential difference.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper uses machine learning-based interatomic potentials and Monte Carlo simulations to show that mechanical strain stabilizes Ag-Cu alloys in solar cells. It resolves a long-standing conflict between theory and experiment by demonstrating that coherency strain prevents the predicted phase separation. This provides a framework for understanding stability in systems where chemical and mechanical effects compete.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Resolving the discrepancy between ab initio predictions of a Ag-Cu miscibility gap and experimental observations of stable, homogeneous Ag-Cu distribution in solar-cell absorbers.

**Main result.** Coherency strain in the Ag-Cu alloy stabilizes the mixed phase by providing a negative elastic enthalpy that overcomes the chemical driving force for phase separation.

**Method.** Development of a machine learning interatomic potential using the Atomic Cluster Expansion (ACE) formalism, combined with active learning and large-scale Monte Carlo simulations.

**Model / system.** The system is the Ag-Cu-Ga-Se (ACGS) chalcopyrite alloy, specifically focusing on the Ag-Cu substitution on the group-I cation sublattice in thin-film solar cells.

**Key observables.** Mixing enthalpy, Gibbs free energy of mixing, Warren-Cowley short-range order parameter, lattice parameters (a and c), and stiffness tensor components (Cij).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Ag concentration (x), temperature (T), and the distinction between coherent and incoherent interface boundary conditions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the primary stabilizing mechanism is coherency strain and focuses on the tetragonal phase during the initial active learning cycles.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows Ag-Cu structure vs. phase-separated structures; Figure 2 details the ML potential development workflow; Figure 3 shows structural and elastic properties vs. Ag content; Figure 5 presents phase diagrams and mixing energy; Figure 6 illustrates the time evolution of phase separation in coherent vs. incoherent interfaces; Figure 7 compares chemical and elastic enthalpy contributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the stability controversy, describes the ML potential development via active learning, validates the potential against DFT, applies it to large-scale Monte Carlo simulations to study phase stability, and concludes by comparing coherent and incoherent interface models.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Alloying Ag into Cu(In,Ga)Se$_2$ has enabled record solar-cell efficiencies ($\sim23.6\%$), yet their long-term stability remains in question because initio calculations predict a Ag-Cu miscibility gap near ambient temperature. By off-lattice Monte-Carlo simulations using a newly developed machine learning (ML) interatomic potential we show that the presence of coherency strain is resolving the controversy between experimental observations and the predicted phase stability. Incorporating elastic energy contributions present in a coherent setup results in complete Ag-Cu miscibility, whereas the expected phase separation occurs in the absence of coherency strains with respect to the end boundary phases, which are mimicked by an incoherent interface with misfit dislocations. The developed ML-MC framework provides a novel approach for resolving discrepancies in thermodynamic stability for systems where mechanical and chemical effects compete.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17649v1>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that p-wave magnetic textures do more than just suppress conventional superconductivity; they can actually stabilize and promote spin-triplet p-wave pairing. By analyzing different magnetic helix orientations, the authors identify specific regimes where different superconducting symmetries coexist or compete, suggesting that unconventional magnets are promising platforms for realizing robust triplet superconductivity.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the interplay, competition, and coexistence between unconventional p-wave magnetism and different superconducting symmetries (spin-singlet s-wave and spin-triplet p-wave).

**Main result.** The researchers found that p-wave magnetic order can actively promote and stabilize spin-triplet p-wave pairing, with specific magnetic helix orientations driving quantum phase transitions between different pairing symmetries.

**Method.** The study uses a self-consistent Bogoliubov-de-Gennes (BdG) approach to numerically solve the microscopic equations on a lattice.

**Model / system.** A p-wave magnet on a square lattice featuring a helical magnetic structure along the x-direction, including electron hopping, Zeeman-like magnetic coupling, and superconducting pairing terms.

**Key observables.** Pairing amplitudes for s-wave and p-wave channels (mixed-spin and equal-spin), total spin projection (Sz), and critical temperatures (Tc).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic coupling strength (J), magnetic helix orientation (pMzy vs. pMzx), temperature (T), and superconducting coupling strengths (Vs, Vp).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Mean-field approximation for superconductivity; periodic boundary conditions; square lattice geometry.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the two different magnetic helix configurations (pMzy and pMzx) on the square lattice.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the magnetic helix model, details the BdG methodology, analyzes the competition/coexistence of pairing symmetries under varying magnetic coupling, and discusses the implications for proximity effects and material realization.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the interplay between unconventional magnetism and superconductivity in a model of a $p$-wave magnet on a square lattice. Using a self-consistent Bogoliubov-de-Gennes approach, we analyze the pairing amplitudes, competition, and coexistence of spin-singlet $s$-wave and spin-triplet $p$-wave pairings in the presence of a magnetic texture with a helical structure along the $x$ direction that is repeated in the $y$ direction. We find that the magnetic helix selectively stabilizes different pairing symmetries depending on its orientation and strength. In particular, mixed-spin $p_x$-wave pairing is enhanced at intermediate magnetic couplings and equal-spin $p_y$-wave pairing is robust and insensitive to all coupling intensities. When the multiple order parameters are simultaneously considered, we find regimes of coexistence and competition. Increasing the magnetic coupling drives two quantum phase transitions. The first from dominant spin-singlet $s$-wave to mixed-spin triplet $p_x$-wave pairings in a regime of coexistence. The second from spin-singlet $s$-wave and mixed-spin triplet $p$-wave pairings with total spin projection $S_z=0$ to dominant equal-spin triplet $p_y$-wave pairings with $S_z=\pm1$ in a regime of mutually exclusive superconducting phases. Our results demonstrate that $p$-wave magnetic order does not merely diminish spin-singlet $s$-wave superconductivity but can actively promote and stabilize spin-triplet $p$-wave pairing, both intrinsically and in proximity to spin-singlet $s$-wave superconductors. These findings highlight unconventional magnets as promising materials for realizing robust triplet superconductivity.

</details>

**Authors:** Mahan Bakhshikhah, Jiří Liška, Rahul Kesarwani, Jindřich Mach, Ondřej Červinka, Petr Dub, Jiří Spousta, Jan Přibyl, Jana Kalbáčová Vejpravová, Tomáš Šikola

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a method to significantly and durably boost the photoluminescence of single-layer MoS2 using a UV laser under ambient conditions. The process works by using oxygen to heal sulfur vacancies and convert trions into bright neutral excitons. This provides a reliable way to create high-performance, stable 2D materials for nanophotonic applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving high-intensity, long-term stable photoluminescence (PL) in single-layer MoS2 by overcoming low quantum yield caused by sulfur vacancies and non-radiative recombination.

**Main result.** A non-destructive UV laser treatment under ambient conditions achieves up to an 8-fold enhancement in PL intensity by inducing oxygen chemisorption, which passivates defects and induces p-type doping.

**Method.** Continuous-wave UV laser irradiation (355 nm) of exfoliated and CVD-grown MoS2 flakes, combined with Raman spectroscopy, PL mapping, and controlled-atmosphere chamber studies.

**Model / system.** Single-layer molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) flakes, including both mechanically exfoliated and CVD-grown domains, placed on c-cut sapphire substrates.

**Key observables.** PL intensity (peak height and integrated area), PL spectral features (FWHM, trion-to-neutral exciton transition), Raman mode shifts (E2g and A1g), and surface roughness (RMS).

**Important parameters / regimes.** UV laser wavelength (355 nm), laser power (4 mW), atmospheric composition (O2, Ar, N2), and long-term stability duration (up to 72 days).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The enhancement is driven by oxygen-mediated passivation rather than purely thermal or structural damage effects.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows PL maps and spectra for exfoliated MoS2 demonstrating intensity increase and spectral narrowing; Figure 2 shows similar enhancement in CVD MoS2; Figure 3 displays Raman blue shifts; Figure 4 demonstrates spatial control of the treatment; Figure 5 shows the necessity of oxygen for enhancement; Figure 6 tracks long-term stability.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of low PL in MoS2, presents the UV treatment method and its dramatic enhancement effects, provides spectroscopic evidence for the mechanism (oxygen-induced p-doping and defect healing), demonstrates spatial precision and atmospheric dependence, and concludes with long-term stability and structural integrity analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Single-layer molybdenum disulfide ($MoS_2$) possesses significant potential for nanoscale optoelectronics, but achieving high-intensity, long-term-stable photoluminescence (PL) emission remains a challenge. In this work, we demonstrate a remarkably robust, more than 8-fold maximum enhancement in the PL intensity of exfoliated and CVD-grown single-layer $MoS_2$ via a non-destructive ultraviolet (UV) laser treatment method. This substantial increase in radiative efficiency is accompanied by a trion-to-neutral exciton transition in the PL signal and a corresponding blue shift of the Raman $E_{2g}^1$ and $A_{1g}$ vibrational modes, signaling successful electron depletion (p-doping) and formation of Mo-O bonds, respectively. Furthermore, we demonstrate precise spatial control over PL properties by confining PL treatment exclusively to the UV laser-treated area. Crucially, the enhanced PL performance shows exceptional longevity; the CVD sample and the exfoliated sample remained stable for the entire monitoring period (72 and 32 days, respectively) under ambient conditions. We further investigated UV laser treatment in a controlled-environment chamber under argon, nitrogen, and oxygen atmospheres, distinguishing the influence of oxygen as the PL treatment agent. These findings establish a reliable pathway for the permanent treatment of single-layer $MoS_2$ PL properties, an essential step toward practical, high-performance nanophotonic devices.

</details>

### [Formation of mechanical rogue waves](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18518v1)

**Authors:** Yasuhiro Miyazawa, Christopher Chong, Panayotis G. Kevrekidis, Jinkyu Yang  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.18518v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</details>

<img src="2605.18518_figures/2605.18518_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Experimental strain landscape in focusing and non-focusing scenarios. (a) Initial Gaussian profile of the</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.18518_figures/2605.18518_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Localization characterization. Normalized inverse participation ratio (IPR) for narrow (σ = 1.0) and wide</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.18518_figures/2605.18518_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. Parametric study of nonlinear wave focusing. (a) Variation of IPRmax in an initial condition parameter space</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper reports the first experimental realization of mechanical rogue waves in a 1D nonlinear metamaterial lattice. It reveals that extreme wave localization is a synergistic effect requiring both high nonlinearity and a sufficiently wide initial energy reservoir to overcome dispersion. These findings provide a platform for studying transient nonlinear wave focusing for applications like energy harvesting and signal processing.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The experimental realization of mechanical rogue waves has been elusive due to challenges in controlling nonlinearity and overcoming energy dissipation. The study specifically investigates whether extreme energy focusing requires a narrow seed or a wide spatial energy reservoir.

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrate that the formation of mechanical rogue waves requires a synergy between high nonlinearity and a broad spatial energy reservoir; neither high amplitude nor wide initial width alone is sufficient to overcome dispersion.

**Method.** The study uses a 1D mechanical metamaterial lattice with an electromagnetic release system to prescribe initial strain profiles. Experimental results are validated against numerical simulations using the Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (NLSE) and FPUT-type models.

**Model / system.** A 1D mechanical metamaterial lattice consisting of 41 nonlinear spring units (pucks) on a low-friction air-bearing support. The dynamics are modeled by the Fermi-Pasta-Ulam-Tsingou (FPUT) framework and the Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (NLSE).

**Key observables.** Spatiotemporal evolution of the lattice, instantaneous spatial strain profiles, wave packet envelope amplitude, and the Inverse Participation Ratio (IPTR) as a metric for localization.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Initial peak strain amplitude (P0), initial Gaussian width (sigma), and the zone-boundary mode (k = pi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is assumed to be in a Hamiltonian scenario where the dynamics can be approximated by the NLSE, and inherent dissipation is assumed to be low enough to allow for observable focusing.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the experimental setup and the asymmetric force-strain relationship. Fig 2 compares the spatiotemporal evolution and strain profiles of narrow vs. wide seeds. Fig 3 compares experimental IPR and lattice deformation with NLS predictions. Fig 4 presents a parametric study of IPR_max across the P0 and sigma parameter space.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the rogue wave problem, description of the experimental metamaterial platform and electromagnetic release system, presentation of the numerical model (NLSE/FPUT), results comparing different initial conditions (narrow vs. wide), a parametric study of the localization threshold, and discussion of applications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Rogue waves, characterized by their abrupt and extreme localization in space and time, have evolved from maritime folklore to subjects of intense study across diverse fields, from hydrodynamics and nonlinear optics to plasmas and condensed matter physics. In mechanical systems, however, experimental realization remains elusive despite theoretical and numerical predictions. This gap stems from the stringent requirements for controllable nonlinearity, the high-fidelity initialization of the system, and the necessity to overcome inherent energy dissipation. Here, we report the experimental formation of mechanical rogue waves in a precisely engineered one-dimensional metamaterial lattice with tailored nonlinearity and minimal dissipative losses. Using a precision electromagnetic release system, we prescribe initial strain profiles that trigger a transition from dispersive decay to extreme wave focusing. Our parametric analysis reveals that the emergence of these extreme events is strictly contingent upon a synergy between high nonlinearity and a broad spatial energy reservoir within the initial seed. Crucially, neither factor alone is sufficient to overcome dispersion and trigger the observed focusing. These findings establish a robust platform for studying transient nonlinear wave focusing phenomena in mechanical systems and offer insights for harnessing extreme wave localization for applications such as energy harvesting, waveguiding, and mechanical signal processing.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.17939"></a>
### [Geometric symmetry and size-dependent skyrmion phase transitions in magnetic nanostructures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17939v1)

**Authors:** J. Y. Wang, C. X. Zhao, Y. F. Duan, H. M. Dong  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17939v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17939_figures/2605.17939_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: The size-dependent magnetic topological structures with the diameter Ds of (a) a nanodisk, (b) a square, and (c) a rectangle. This diagram illus- trates the reconstruction of topological states induced by the magnetic field and highlights their key charac- teristics. The phase diagram features four distinct types of topological states: (i) Ferromagnetic state (I) prevails in regions with high magnetic fields (B &gt; 2.9 T) and small diameters (L &lt; 15 nm). In this state, the mag- netic moments are fully aligned with the magnetic field, thereby suppressing topological defects. (ii) The transi- tion from skyrmioniums to skyrmions (red) induced by</sub>

**Summary.** This study demonstrates how the shape and symmetry of magnetic nanostructures can be used to engineer the stability of topological textures. It shows that while disks allow for complex multi-state configurations, asymmetric structures like rectangles are better for stabilizing simple skyrmions, providing a design principle for next-generation spintronic memory.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how geometric symmetry, size, and external magnetic fields regulate the stability and phase transitions of topological magnetic states like skyrmions and skyrmioniums in nanostructures.

**Main result.** Nanodisks with rotational symmetry enable complex topological transitions (skyrmionium, multi-states), whereas squares and rectangles exhibit topological suppression due to corner-induced demagnetization, though they stabilize skyrmions over a broader parameter range.

**Method.** Micromagnetic simulations using the GPU-accelerable MuMax3 package with energy minimization via the conjugate-gradient-based Relax routine.

**Model / system.** Magnetic nanostructures (nanodisks, squares, and rectangles) with a 1 nm thickness, modeled using a Hamiltonian including exchange, DMI, magnetic anisotropy, Zeeman, and demagnetization energies.

**Key observables.** Topological charge (Q), total magnetic energy density, and topological phase diagrams (B-L plots).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic anisotropy (Ku = 6 MJ/m3), DMI constant (D = 4.1 mJ/m2), saturation magnetization (Ms = 914 kA/m), and nanostructure dimensions (L).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Simulations are performed at zero temperature (T = 0 K), and the material parameters represent a theoretical limit for high-performance spintronics.

**Figures summary.** Phase diagrams for squares and rectangles as a function of field and size; energy density variations across different geometries; and the variation of total magnetic energy density as a function of size.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of geometric influence on skyrmions, details the micromagnetic model and parameters, presents comparative phase diagrams for different shapes, analyzes energy density variations, and concludes with implications for spintronic applications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the interplay of geometric symmetry, size, and external magnetic fields in regulating individual skyrmion states within magnetic nanostructures. By analyzing nanodisks, nanosquares, and nanorectangles, we demonstrate that rotational symmetry in nanodisks enables rich topological phase transitions, from ferromagnetic states to skyrmions, skyrmioniums, and multi-states, as their diameter increases. In contrast, square and rectangular structures exhibit suppressed topological complexity due to corner-induced demagnetization effects and reduced symmetries. Under perpendicular magnetic fields, nanodisks show field-driven transitions between skyrmionium and skyrmion states. By leveraging asymmetry, square and rectangular nanostructures stabilize skyrmions over a broader parameter range than nanodisks. These findings highlight geometric symmetry as a critical design parameter for tailoring skyrmion stability and functionality in spintronic applications such as multi-state memory and reconfigurable logic devices.

</details>

### [Geometry-Driven Nonlinear Orbital Magnetoelectric Effect](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17462v1)

**Authors:** Jinxiong Jia, Zhenhua Qiao, Jian Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17462v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.17462_figures/2605.17462_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
